title: How to make a new molecule in an existing large molecule model without causing errors
body_text:
I have a large molecule model that I exported to MoBi. I am trying to add a new molecule but I get all the errors regarding its moleculeproperties

I understand that these parameters are trying to be calculated. However, is it possible to create a new molecule in MoBi within an existing model without forcing it to run these calculations? I don't need to calculate the partition coefficients or anything like that I just want a molecule. For example, LigandEndo is a molecule within the model but it doesn't require the calculation of all the other parameters seen in the picture above. How do I make a new molecule like that?

If your goal is to add a floating (non-stationary) molecule with a "full PBPK," you need to do so via the "Add PK-Sim Molecule" option.
 ➡️ 
Once added, you need to extend the initial conditions for the added molecule and your spatial structure

title: PBPK model performance evaluation for fractional parameters
body_text:
I've built a PBPK model that performs very well by traditional fold-error metrics of observed-vs-simulated plasma time point concentrations and Cmax/AUC. However, I'm trying to evaluate the performance of the model in predicting urinary fraction data compared to the observed data I have. All of the observed data from the literature give variable quality cumulative excretion data and intermittently and inconsistently calculated CLr. I can plot the time-adjust simulation-vs-observed fraction data, but the traditional fold-error lines e.g. 1.25-fold, are very wide because of the calculation applied to fractional values.
Is there a better way of evaluating fraction simulated-vs-predicted performance, where you do not have a CLr value to evaluate in the same way as e.g. Cmax and AUC?

comments.body_text:
🤖 Discussion Assistant: I found some similar discussions that might be helpful:

Predictive ability (statistical analysis) of PBPK Models (72% similar)
PBPK Model Problem:  Extremely Fast Elimination (70% similar)
IV furosemide PBPK model: Cmax underprediction and transporter (OAT1/OAT3/MRP4) parameterization in PK-Sim (68% similar)
elimination pathways (67% similar)
Problems in PBPK model of oral absorption (67% similar)

AI answer:
When dealing with fractional parameters (like fraction excreted in urine, $f_e$, which is strictly bounded between 0 and 1), traditional relative fold-error metrics (like 1.25-fold or 2-fold) often break down. For instance, a 1.25-fold limit on an observed fraction of 0.8 goes up to 1.0 (physically restricted) and down to 0.64, while the same fold-error on an observed fraction of 0.01 creates an extremely narrow absolute band (0.008 to 0.0125).
Because of this scaling issue, here are a few better approaches for evaluating simulated-vs-predicted performance for fractional data when CLr is unreliable or missing:
1. Use Absolute Error/Difference Limits
Instead of relative fold-errors, it is common practice to evaluate fractional data using absolute differences (Prediction Error, $PE = Pred - Obs$).
You can define a predefined acceptable absolute deviation boundary. For example, setting an acceptance criteria that the predicted fraction is within ±0.1 or ±0.15 of the observed fraction (i.e., within 10–15% of the administered dose). Plotting an identity line ($y=x$) with parallel boundary lines ($y = x \pm 0.1$) often provides a much more meaningful goodness-of-fit visual for bounded fractions than radiating fold-error wedges.
2. Logit Transformation
If you still want to apply proportional/confidence boundary concepts, you can apply a Logit transformation to your fractional data:
$$logit(p) = \ln\left(\frac{p}{1-p}\right)$$
This maps the bounded (0 to 1) domain to real numbers ($-\infty$ to $+\infty$). You can then evaluate the residuals of the logit-transformed predictions vs. logit-transformed observations, which linearizes the bounds and prevents the extreme skewing seen at the edges of fractional bounds.
3. Evaluate Cumulative Amount ($Ae$) instead of Fraction
If you have the time-course cumulative excretion data, you can convert the fractional data back to cumulative amount excreted ($Ae_{0-t} = f_e \times Dose$) and evaluate this as a mass. While this is mathematically proportional to the fraction for a given dose, looking at the absolute mass in a time-course simulation (using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) or Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)) often makes it easier to compare against the time-concentration curves of the parent drug in plasma.
4. Residual Plots over Time
Since you mentioned the observed cumulative excretion data quality is highly variable, standard scatter plots might be misleading. Plotting the absolute residuals ($Pred - Obs$) versus time for the fractional data can help you easily spot whether the model is systematically overpredicting or underpredicting excretion at specific phases (e.g., initial rapid excretion vs terminal phase), without being distorted by the mathematical limits of a fold-ratio.
Summary Recommendation
If you need to report summary metrics for the model alongside Cmax and AUC (which use Geometric Mean Fold Error), switch to Mean Absolute Error (MAE) or Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) for the urinary fraction ($f_e$) parameters, and replace the 1.25-fold lines on your simulated-vs-observed plots with absolute boundary lines (e.g., $\pm 0.1$).

title: Baboon PBPK modeling
body_text:
Hello OSP team,
I would like to build a baboon PBPK model. However, I noticed that baboon is not included in the current gene expression database release (v3.0.1) developed by H.Cordes.
Could you please advise me which species would be the most appropriate surrogate for baboon in PK-Sim / MoBi ?
Would macaque or monkey be recommended in this case, or should another species be selected for this purpose?
Thank you very much for your guidance.
Best regards,
Bach

comments.body_text:
🤖 Discussion Assistant: I found some similar discussions that might be helpful:

Monkey ABCB1 transporter (65% similar)

Dear @nguyen-tranbach,
for baboon there is currently no expression DB build, I would need to find the time to build that one and upload.
What you could try is use the code in https://github.com/[Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Gene-Expression-Databases](https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Gene-Expression-Databases) and build one for baboon yourself.
The hosting party for the data has experiments on RNA buld data for Papio anubis: https://archives.bgee.org/15-1/search/raw-data?species_id=9555
Let me know if you have trouble with the code or during build, I can try to help.
Best,
Henrik

title: Question regarding antibody partition coefficient
body_text:
Hi all,
I'm new to PKSIM for large molecule PBPK modeling.
Recently I modelled an antibody (tipical humanized IgG) at 20mg/kg in monkey. No target is associated (I just want to know at the target saturation level, what is the distribution of the antibody).
I simulate the antibody concentration in plasma and the interstitial in fat, spleen, and bone. Then I ploted the tissue-to-plasma concentration ratio.
As you can see in the figure, that the steady state bone-to-plasma ratio is 0.53 and spleen-to-plasma ratio is 0.78.
This is where I get really confused. According to the paper (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3893240/#sec9), the antibody biodistribution coefficient (ABC value) for Bone is 7.27% and for spleen is 12.8% without TMDD. The ratio I got is far higher than what should be partitioned into bone and spleen. Could someone help me clarify this? Thank you so much!

[pksim file for discussion.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/27175084/pksim.file.for.discussion.zip)

answer.body_text:
It seems what you have plotted are interstitial concentration / plasma concentration ratios with interstitial concentration = amount in interstitial space / volume of interstitial space. If we assume there is no residual blood in the tissue, tissue concentrations are (amount in interstitial space + amount inside cells) / (volume of interstitial space + volume of cellular space) which corresponds to the "Tissue" concentration observer in PK-Sim. Since there is no antibody inside cells, tissue concentrations are amount in interstitial space / (volume of interstitial space + volume of cellular space), i.e. smaller than the interstitial concentrations.
Experimentally observed tissue concentrations might also include contributions from residual blood. For this the "Whole Organ" observer can be used with an adjustable parameter of the fraction of residual blood.

comments.body_text:
🤖 Discussion Assistant: I found some similar discussions that might be helpful:

title: PAGE 2026 Workshop: Managing Complex Drug-Drug-Gene Interactions in Real World Patients with PBPK
body_text:
Dear All,
we will hold a whole-day PBPK DDGI workshop focusing on real-world patient cases at this year's PAGE meeting.
You will learn what happens when drugs, comedications, and genetics collide as well as how to handle complex drug-drug-gene interactions to derive dose adaptations in PK-Sim.
You will also learn how PBPK models can be translated into clinical practice through decision support systems.
The workshop will take place in person on June 2, 2026 and will be free of charge thanks to the SafePolyMed project.
Limited seats are available!
Register here.

title: OSP Molecules catalogue
body_text: Hi, I´m new in OSP and I think that a good idea for new users like me is to have a catalogue of molecules with preconfigured properties. I think that PK-Sim can be very usefull for clinical uses with this information.

comments.body_text: Great idea
I don´t have sufficient programming background kwnoledge but I´m very interested in this topic. How can I help? Ideas to lead and planning work?

@jgonlop3
The major task first of all is to compile this list of ever growing preconfigured molecules.
For each molecule, following parameters are required:

Name,
Lipophilicity
MolWeight
Solubility at Ref pH
Ref pH
PKAs

Then ideally, some processes describing how the drugs interacts with different enzymes, transporters etc..
The actual format could be an

a json file : here is an example of how a compound is read by pk-sim to perform batch simulations).

{
  "Name": "inhibitor",
  "Lipophilicity": 2,
  "FractionUnbound": 0.6,
  "MolWeight": 3.0E-7,
  "SolubilityAtRefpH": 1.0E-7,
  "RefpH": 9,
  "PkaTypes": [{
      "Type": "Acid",
      "Value": 8
  }],
   "PartialProcesses": [{
      "MoleculeName": "CYP3A4",
      "InternalName": "MixedInhibition",
      "DataSource": "Lab",
      "ParameterValues": {"Ki_c": 1,"Ki_u": 1}
  }],
  "CalculationMethods": ["Cellular partition coefficient method - Rodgers and Rowland"]
}


a template database file: It is possible to export a compound to a user template database
...

Having a json file with all the compound properties would offer many advantages. One of which would be that this file would be on github and anyone could participate and add/modify the catalogue of molecules
So far, no programming knowledge required. Only  a Text editor  :)
In PK-Sim,  a simple change would have to be implemented to allow for

Export of one or more compound to json (so that it can be added to the catalog)
Import a compound from the molecule catalog

Perfect! I think it´s easy to do. I start today! I make an LibreOffice Calc file with this properties. I think Calc is more easy for non-programmers than json. I agree with your comment, the perfect situation will be the PK-sim option to import/export properties of molecules.
@Yuri05 @TheiBa Do you have any suggestions ?

What about the idea to import this information, e.g. from
https://www.drugbank.ca/

I think that would offer accepted, referencable start values. Could involve 2
steps: 1. importing/extracting the information from above into a suitable
format that is 2. readable by PK-Sim?
and further qualified models will be developed and posted as discussed in #11

I fear that importing compound data without validating them may lead to
quite a lot of unusable substances.
Do you think we can use values from the drug bank in PK-Sim as is? It gets
even more interesting when we think about processes.

I think about starting with some of this references values and then validate the whole model in PK-Sim. The pack of properties for any molecule must be coordinate with adequate package of anatomy+physiological values...etc. In summary, we need to stablish an initial molecular values that works with the reference PBPK model implemmented in PK-Sim.

@jgonlop3

I make an LibreOffice Calc file with this properties

Be aware that the number of properties is not set. That's why an excel/calc table won't work.
For example, in the json text I pasted above, you can see that there is also a PartialProcess defined for CYP3A4 of type MixedInhibition.
A compound can have many different processes defined and as you can imagine, this will be very hard to model in a table.

Yes, I know the limitations of a table. I try to do a database (sqlite or libreoffice base are good options for this purpose). First of all I need to design the tables and relations. I think on spreadsheet like Calc previously because of its simplicity. But I think you are right and database is better option. I will make a database.

@jgonlop3 the problem with a database is that it is very hard to extend if you are non programmer.
Why not try the json file option? This is a simple text file that can be edited in almost any decent text editor.
The beauty in this approach is that anyone could submit a patch on github and it would be fairly easy to see what has change between two versions (text file)
At the moment however, you can start with a simple table as you suggested which would have to be converted to another format once somewhat ready. Thoughts?

Also another cool advantage is that pksim could load this file (in start or
on demand) and use it as a remote compound database (json files are ideal
for that...very lightweight)

Regarding solubility data: How can I write 2 solubilities at 2 different pHs? Can you give me an example?

@jgonlop3

Is it planned that PK-Sim can export data of molecules in json format?

yes: Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#168
This will be released in 7.2.0. As soon as the feature is implemented however, you'll be able to try it out with the pre release

Regarding solubility data: How can I write 2 solubilities at 2 different pHs? Can you give me an example?

This is not supported by PK-Sim. Instead, using RefpH and Ref Solubility and solubility gain per charge, PK-Sim predicts the Sol  = f(pH)
Here is an extract of Chapter 15 describing how PK-Sim handles solubility

The solubility can be specified together with the type of measurement or the medium used (first column, Experiment). The corresponding unit can be chosen from the drop-down menu in the second column (Solubility at Ref-pH). For charged compounds, the pH value at which the solubility of the compound was measured should be given in the third column (Ref-pH). In the fourth column, the Solubility gain per charge can be modified, which defines the factor by which the solubility increases with each ionization step. In order to calculate the charge of the molecule, the fraction of each microspecies is calculated according to the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a given pH. This is done across the entire pH-range such that the fractions are used to calculate the probability with which a molecule is in a certain ionization state. Based on this information, the pH-dependent solubility of molecules with one or more ionizable groups is calculated. By clicking on Show Graph, the pH-dependent solubility across the whole pH range calculated based on the experimental solubility at the defined pH is shown. For neutral compounds the input fields Ref-pH and Solubility gain per charge and the graph are irrelevant.
Thanks @msevestre

In PK-Sim I can select differents units but in the json file you sent me as example this is not specified. Where can I registered the units (example mg/L) of the solubility at Ref-pH inside json files?
"SolubilityAtRefpH": 0.177827,   <- Which units?????
"RefpH": 7.4,

This is a very good point. The json specification does not support unit at the moment. That means that for now, all values should be defined in base unit for a specific dimension.
PK-Sim and MoBi are using the following dimension and unit definition
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSPSuite.Dimensions/blob/master/OSPSuite.Dimensions.xml
For example, Concentration (molar) as a base unit of umol/l
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSPSuite.Dimensions/blob/master/OSPSuite.Dimensions.xml#L165
When the export is implemented in PK-Sim,. values will be exported in the correct unit by default
Does it make sense?
Understand. Is possible to add dime coment in json file with the units? Only like explanation. Ir nota is possible, dont worry. I understand It.

Why json? I am not a fan of XML, but wouldn't it be more consistent to use XML for compound definitions? OSPS uses XML for exporting model blocks and whole simulations (MatLab/R), so no new structure should be thought of. Furthermore, schemas could help in validating the molecules catalogue.
The advantage of json of being more lighweight should not play any role as the data should be used locally.

Defining a schema would be a good idea in any case.
JSON format also supports schema definition and validation can be performed even online.
http://www.jsonschemavalidator.net/

json is the format that we use for batch simulations (see links above). We already support this format out of the box
json is much more readable than xml
any new API now favors json over xml
and it will be loaded right from github.com (albeit with a fallback option local). We will use this file a standard api endpoint to retrieve the compound list online

That being said, the format we have now, that I copied as an example above, will probably need to be extended. More particularly, I think we need to add

unit for parameter value (this will serve for documentation purpose)
ValueDescription (text describing where the value is coming from. Absolute must so that this compound value can be trusted)
description of compound itself
...probably much more
Alternative values (a way to define more than one lipophilicity for example with different source)
fu: For which species ?

@jgonlop3 While json will remain, the actual data structure will change now that we go from an intern use to external use only. It might be wise to wait a bit until the structure is finalize

Dear all,
The proposal of an "incremental" approach made by TheiBa sounds reasonable. Drugbank is a good source to start with : I used it quite often as a starting point when building a new PBPK model from scratch on reference compounds, and the quality of information seems sufficient for a first version of a model. Then the compound file may be gradually completed/optimized to use other validated in vitro or ex vivo data found elsewhere. Up to a real "validation" against clinical data (first PK in healthy people, then in disease population etc.). This approach may require to have a dedicated and standardized field within the compound file indicating the degree/scale of quality/validation of the file, starting with the lowest grade (pure in silico data) to the highest grade (file validated with various clinical datasets). This way people could know what degree of confidence they can put into their simulations.
Best regards,
@jgonlop3 , do you have any updates on this topic? Did you manage to create a database?

title: How can I see what percentage of the metabolic process a particular enzyme occupies?
body_text:
I would like to know which one I should choose if I wish to see the percentage of a particular enzyme, occupied in the metabolism of a drug? Organism or liver？
If the Liver tab is selected, which sub-tab should I continue to select?Blood cell？Interstitial？Or intarcellular？
What is the difference between the meanings represented by the Organism and the sub-options in liver options?
And，In pksim, is the sum of the proportions of the elimination processes in a model equal 100% by default?

answer.body_text:
Hi,
if you want to see fraction metabolized by CYP3A4 in the whole organism. (e.g. in liver plus intestine) select Organism. If you want to see what is metabolized in Liver only : select liver intracellular. That is where the enzyme CYP3A4 is located. Interstitial would return zero of course as no CYP3A4 is there. PK-Sim assumes that the user is knowing where the metabolism takes place and does chose the right sub-compartment ;-)
Hope that might help a bit.
Best, Tobias

title: MoBi 11.2: Cannot create simulation with Pregnant Woman spatial structure for rifampicin
body_text:
Hi!
I'm trying to build a pregnancy model for rifampin.
Environment
PK-Sim 11.2, MoBi 11.2. (had to downgrade from the latest version, since it was not possible to upload the spatial structure of the pregnant woman in MoBi)
Drug: rifampicin (AADAC metabolism, OATP1B1 uptake, P-gp efflux, GFR).
What I have done so far:
Steps (per Dallmann et al. 2018)
PK-Sim: built Non-Pregnant Woman prototype (based on the European(P-gp modified, CYP3A4 36 h)), exported sim “Non-Pregnant Woman” to MoBi.
MoBi: imported SpatialStructure_PregnantWoman and PassiveTransport_PregnantWoman (matched pair).
Re-linked passive clearance: in PregnantWoman passive transports, added rifampicin to GFR Include and set fraction = 1.
Created PregnancyMSV (Molecule Start Values) → Merge into MSV: Non-Pregnant Woman with Keep existing.
In MSV:Non-Pregnant Woman, set Is present = false in all pregnancy-only organs (placenta maternal/fetal, fetus, myometrium, endometrium, breasts, amniotic fluid, umbilical-cord blood pools) for all proteins present: AADAC, OATP1B1, P-gp. (have tried also to include CYP3A4, UGT1A4, ATP1A2, GABRG2)
Wizard: Spatial structure = PregnantWoman; Passive transports = PregnantWoman; Molecules/Reactions/Observers/Events = S1; MSV = S1; PSV = S1 (also tried a fresh PSV tied to PregnantWoman). → ### Modal error; Notification view empty.
Does anyone know why this happens or could guide me on how to fix it so that I can create the simulation for the pregnant individual? Am I missing a required step after importing the PregnantWoman structure and passive transports?
Is there any additional passive clearance or block that must be re-linked beyond GFR? Any known issue where Notifications don’t populate?
Thanks in advance for any guidance!

title: intestinal intrinsic clearance
body_text:
Hello,
Teams.
I would like to add an intestinal CYP3A biotransformation.
Firstly, I add the concentration of binding/metabolizing protein;
Secondly, I make the steps shown in the figure.

As for the blue parts, that is a liver content, but I need to make an intestinal content.
Should I need to use Mobi?
Can you help me to solve this problem?
Thank you very much!
Yours,
Carrie

comments.body_text:
First, please be aware that for metabolism the only parameter that is used in PKSim is the 'specific clearance' for first order processes and 'kcat' for Michaelis-Menten processes. The calculation parameters do not matter. The calculation parameters are just rough guesses or conversions for arriving at 'specific clearance' from an intrinsic clearance value.
So what you could then is instead use the 'in vitro clearance - first order' formula rather than the 'intrinsic clearance - first order' formula for your gut metabolism because in this sense you define specific clearance directly and cleanly. Provide some justification for how you convert from intrinsic clearance to specific clearance if you would like to use an intrinsic clearance value from enterocytes but this is generally not well-scalable and requires optimization. Specific clearance is the actual first order rate of metabolism inside cells.
When doing gut metabolism you'll want to make sure that the gut metabolizing enzyme is expressed intracellularly in mucosal cells and you may or may not want it in the normal intestinal cells (i.e. do you want it contributing to systemic metabolism or just first pass?)
The most ideal way of doing this would be by adding a CYP3A4 enzyme (liver, gut, whole body) with relative expression and being able to modulate the degree of intestinal metabolism with a scaling factor as I have suggested before. [https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim/issues/1897]
Other ways that gut metabolism has been implemented is by Sebastian Frechen by modulating the basolateral intestinal permeability (see midazolam, finerenone, alfentanil) to increase residence time in the mucosa, but this does not scale well to special populations or for certain DDIs. In SimCyp what is done is increasing the fraction unbound in gut mucosa.

title: Bupropion PBPK Model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have been working on a PBPK model of the CYP2B6 victim drug bupropion and its three metabolites hydroxybupropion, erythrohydrobupropion and threohydrobupropion. The model is able to predict and describe drug-drug(-gene) interactions (DD(G)I) with rifampicin (CYP2B6, CYP2C19 and UGT2B7 perpetrator drug), fluvoxamine (CYP2C19 perpetrator drug) and voriconazole (CYP2B6 and CYP2C19 perpetrator drug).
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Pharmaceutics and the bupropion model as well as PK-Sim projects presenting the predicted DD(G)I studies can be found here.
Related publication:
Marok FZ, Fuhr LM, Hanke N, Selzer D, Lehr T. Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling of Bupropion and Its Metabolites in a CYP2B6 Drug-Drug-Gene Interaction Network. Pharmaceutics. 2021; 13(3),331
All the best,
Fatima

title: Need help with my P-gp BBB research project
body_text:
Hello,
Basically, this is what is happening:

Trying to simulate sertraline's efflux by p-gp, and then showing how increased p-gp activity effluxes more of the drug out of the brain [I am using brain interstitial].
It is not showing a reduction when using the base PK-sim settings, and I had to resort to trying to use the digoxin p-gp snapshot, which started to show a reduction in Cmax by 2mg/mL (not very significant)
I've read somewhere that PK-sim might not be calculating sertraline's permeability very well, and it's not permeability-limited in the BBB.
I've tried doubling the genetic expression of p-gp in the model, and it's not giving me a shift.

How would I demonstrate that p-gp effluxes more of sertraline out of the brain? Or should I use a different drug with a lower log P? Should I try to play around with my transporter settings by increasing vmax?
Really need help because I'm very confused about what is going wrong, because it seems like the BBB doesn't exist, and the drug just passes between the compartments too well.
And the digoxin model has a P-gp-like transporter mechanism, whereas it doesn't in the base model, which didn't shift my Cmax at all when it was set to efflux.
I really think the base model doesn't, and it didn't shift my Cmax at all.
I really need help with my small research project.
BW
Pat

title: Suggestions on working with transporters mediated DDI: Example of dagibatran and P-gp inhibitors
body_text:
Hello everyone:
I am currently working on a drug–drug interaction (DDI) simulation in PK-Sim involving dabigatran etexilate and several P-gp inhibitors. However, after multiple attempts, I have not observed any differences between the simulation of dabigatran alone and dabigatran co-administered with P-gp inhibitors.
To investigate this issue, I tested several different approaches:

Adjusted Vmax and Km/Kmax-related parameters for MDR1/ABCB1 in the dabigatran compound model.
Implemented inhibition parameters for the P-gp inhibitors and modified the Ki values to reflect strong inhibition.
Verified that P-gp transport processes were enabled in the relevant tissues (intestinal, hepatic, renal where applicable).
Confirmed that transporter-mediated processes were active in the simulation.

Despite these adjustments, the plasma concentration–time profiles and exposure metrics (AUC, Cmax) remained essentially unchanged between the dabigatran-only simulation and the co-administration scenario.
At this point, I am wondering if there might be something I am overlooking in the model configuration.
Some questions I have:

Are there additional steps required in PK-Sim to activate transporter-mediated DDIs beyond specifying Vmax/Km and Ki parameters?
Could the lack of effect be related to dabigatran etexilate absorption or conversion processes (e.g., ester hydrolysis) masking transporter effects?
Is it necessary to explicitly define intestinal efflux processes linked to MDR1 for the parent compound or prodrug?
Are there recommended workflows or examples for simulating P-gp–mediated DDIs in PK-Sim/MoBi?

Any suggestions or guidance would be greatly appreciated.
Thank you in advance for your help.

title: Question: Modelling compression effects on small and large molecules in MoBi
body_text:
Hi all,
I would appreciate any insight into modelling the effects of external compression on the skin using PK-Sim/MoBi. I’m modelling small molecules (eg paclitaxel, doxorubicin, docetaxel and cyclopshophamide). I want to model these drugs and mimic applying 'external compression' to a skin compartment. I think compression would cause the following effects on blood vessels but not sure how to model them:
•	Reduced perfusion
•	Starling Effect
•	Capillary crushing
•	Capillary pore size
Modelling a change in blood flow from compression was pretty straight-forward. I created a formula for Skin Specific Blood Flow rate (Flow per weight organ) and reduced that from 10.856 ml/min/100g organ to 50% etc to represent compression.
Also, compression is expected to increase interstitial hydrostatic pressure, thereby reducing the net driving force for drug extravasation from the blood vessels into the surrounding tissue. I’m thinking altering endothelial permeability somehow would capture this, but cannot find a formula/ parameter to change for this. Any guidance on how best to implement this would be greatly appreciated.
I am also interested in modelling capillary compression in order to represent microvascular changes under external pressure. At sufficiently high external pressures, capillaries are expected to narrow or collapse (typically above ~40 mmHg). I propose to represent this in MoBi by reducing the effective endothelial surface area; however, I have not been able to identify a suitable parameter/formula to describe this effect. Would anyone be able to suggest an appropriate parameter to change or modelling approach?
Lastly, capillary pore size is a concept I’m trying to model. Compression would reduce the size of the openings in the capillary wall (e.g., endothelial fenestrations or gaps between cells). I am seeking guidance on how to model pore size reduction in MoBi, ideally as a function of applied pressure.
Many thanks in advance for any suggestions! 😊

comments.body_text:
The answer below was provided by AI (Claude Code) and has not been verified for accuracy.
Use it as a source of inspiration and information, not as a step-by-step guide.
Click to expand
Comprehensive Answer to GitHub Discussion #2131: Modeling External Compression Effects on Skin in PK-Sim/MoBi
Overview
This document provides detailed guidance on modeling the effects of external compression on the skin using PK-Sim® and MoBi® from the Open Systems Pharmacology Suite. The analysis addresses modeling small molecule chemotherapy agents (paclitaxel, doxorubicin, docetaxel, and cyclophosphamide) under conditions of external compression affecting skin perfusion, capillary dynamics, and drug extravasation.

Table of Contents

Background on OSP Suite Capabilities
Modeling Reduced Perfusion
Modeling Starling Forces and Interstitial Pressure
Modeling Capillary Crushing and Surface Area
Modeling Capillary Pore Size Reduction
Drug-Specific Considerations
Implementation Workflow in MoBi
References and Resources


1. Background on OSP Suite Capabilities
1.1 PK-Sim and MoBi Architecture
The Open Systems Pharmacology Suite provides a comprehensive PBPK modeling framework:

PK-Sim®: Whole-body PBPK modeling with predefined physiological parameters for humans and laboratory animals
MoBi®: Advanced modeling platform enabling detailed customization of tissue-level processes, including vascular dynamics

1.2 Skin Permeation Model
OSP Suite includes a validated implementation of the Dancik et al. (2013) skin permeation model, which simulates:

Multi-layer skin structure (stratum corneum, viable epidermis, dermis, sub-dermis)
Diffusion and partitioning through anatomical layers
Metabolism (Michaelis-Menten kinetics)
Connection to whole-body PBPK models

Repository: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Skin-permeation-model
1.3 Validated Drug Models
OSP provides validated PBPK models for several chemotherapy agents:


Paclitaxel: Validated for CYP2C8-mediated clearance in adults and pediatrics

Repository: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Paclitaxel-Model
References: Multiple clinical studies [Huizing et al. 1993; Ibrahim et al. 2002]



Doxorubicin: Multiple PBPK models available

Validated for breast milk transfer predictions [Damoiseaux et al. 2023, CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol]
Tissue disposition and toxicity assessment models [Chao et al. 2023, Biomed Pharmacother]




2. Modeling Reduced Perfusion
2.1 Current Approach (Already Implemented)
You correctly identified the parameter for blood flow modification:
Parameter: Specific Blood Flow Rate (Flow per weight of organ)
Location in PK-Sim/MoBi: Organ properties → Skin → Blood flow parameters
Units: mL/min/100g tissue
Standard value: ~10.856 mL/min/100g
Compressed value: Reduce by desired percentage (e.g., 50% = 5.428 mL/min/100g)
2.2 Physiological Basis
External compression increases tissue pressure, which:

Compresses venules and arterioles, increasing vascular resistance
Reduces blood flow according to pressure-flow relationships
At ~40 mmHg external pressure, capillary collapse begins

2.3 Implementation Strategy
Create formula in MoBi:
Skin_Blood_Flow = Baseline_Flow × Compression_Factor

Where Compression_Factor varies with:
- Mild compression (10-20 mmHg): 0.7-0.9
- Moderate compression (20-40 mmHg): 0.4-0.7
- Severe compression (>40 mmHg): 0.1-0.4

Recommendation: Use a time-dependent formula if compression is applied dynamically, or create Events in MoBi to change blood flow at specific time points.

3. Modeling Starling Forces and Interstitial Pressure
3.1 Physiological Context
The Starling equation describes fluid movement across capillary walls:
Jv = Lp × S × [(Pc - Pi) - σ(πc - πi)]

Where:

Jv = Net fluid filtration
Lp = Hydraulic conductivity (capillary permeability to water)
S = Capillary surface area
Pc = Capillary hydrostatic pressure
Pi = Interstitial hydrostatic pressure (increased by compression)
πc = Capillary oncotic pressure
πi = Interstitial oncotic pressure
σ = Reflection coefficient (0-1)

Effect of External Compression: Increases Pi, which:

Reduces net filtration (Pc - Pi becomes smaller)
Reduces driving force for drug extravasation from blood to interstitium
Effectively reduces drug delivery to tissue

3.2 Modeling in MoBi
Since MoBi doesn't explicitly model Starling forces by default, you can approximate this effect through endothelial permeability modifications:
Approach 1: Modify Permeability-Surface Area Product (PS)
Parameter Location:

Navigate to: Spatial Structure → Skin → Endothelial layer
Parameter: Permeability × Surface Area (PS parameter)

Mathematical relationship:
Drug_Flux = PS × (C_plasma - C_interstitial)

Under compression with increased Pi:
PS_compressed = PS_baseline × (1 - Compression_Effect)

Where Compression_Effect relates to increased Pi:
Compression_Effect = k × ΔPi / (Pc - Pi_baseline)

Typical values:
- k = 0.5 to 1.0 (empirical fitting factor)
- Baseline Pi ≈ 0-2 mmHg
- Compressed Pi ≈ 10-50 mmHg (depending on compression)
- Pc ≈ 25-35 mmHg (capillary pressure)

Approach 2: Modify Endothelial Permeability Directly
For small molecules (including your drugs of interest):
Parameter: Permeability coefficient for the compound in skin endothelium
Location: Compound properties → Permeability parameters → Skin
Implementation:
Create a formula for permeability:
P_eff = P_baseline × f(Pi)

Where f(Pi) = 1 / (1 + α × (Pi - Pi_baseline))

Suggested values:
- α = 0.02-0.05 per mmHg (calibrate with literature data)
- Pi_baseline = 0-2 mmHg
- Pi_compressed = external pressure - tissue compliance effects

3.3 Parameter Access in MoBi
To implement these changes:

Export PK-Sim model to MoBi (if starting from PK-Sim)
Navigate to: Building Blocks → Spatial Structures → Skin
Modify container parameters for vascular/interstitial exchange
Create custom formulas for pressure-dependent permeability
Use Parameter Values building block to set compression-dependent values

Key parameters to locate:

Endothelial permeability (may be compound-specific)
P (vascular → interstitial) - passive transport permeability
Surface area - can be reduced to model capillary collapse


4. Modeling Capillary Crushing and Surface Area
4.1 Physiological Mechanism
At external pressures > ~40 mmHg:

Capillaries narrow or collapse
Effective perfused capillary density decreases
Exchange surface area is reduced

4.2 Implementation in MoBi
Parameter: Endothelial Surface Area
Location: Spatial Structure → Skin → Endothelium
Parameter name: Surface area or Endothelial surface area
Units: dm² (square decimeters) or cm²
Baseline values (from literature):

Capillary surface area density: 30-50 cm²/cm³ tissue
Total skin surface area (for exchange): Organ-volume dependent

Compression effect:
S_effective = S_baseline × Perfusion_Fraction

Where Perfusion_Fraction depends on external pressure:
- < 20 mmHg: Perfusion_Fraction ≈ 1.0 (minimal effect)
- 20-40 mmHg: Perfusion_Fraction ≈ 0.7-1.0 (proportional reduction)
- 40-60 mmHg: Perfusion_Fraction ≈ 0.3-0.7 (significant collapse)
- > 60 mmHg: Perfusion_Fraction ≈ 0.1-0.3 (severe collapse)

Implementation Steps


Create pressure-dependent formula:
In MoBi → Formulas:
Create new formula: "Compressed_Surface_Area"

S_compressed = S_baseline × (1 - Collapse_Factor)

Collapse_Factor = max(0, min(0.9, (P_external - 40) / 60))



Apply to skin endothelium:

Set Surface area parameter to use this formula
Link to external pressure parameter



Couple with blood flow reduction:

Both surface area and blood flow should decrease together
Maintain physiological consistency



4.3 Alternative: Modify Exchange Rate Constant
If direct surface area modification is difficult, modify the mass transfer coefficient:
Parameter: P × S product (permeability-surface area product)
Effect: Directly controls exchange rate between vascular and interstitial compartments
(P × S)_compressed = (P × S)_baseline × Compression_Factor

This is mathematically equivalent to reducing S while keeping P constant


5. Modeling Capillary Pore Size Reduction
5.1 Physiological Background
Capillary pore theory distinguishes:

Small pores (~6-12 nm): Paracellular junctions, main pathway for small molecules
Large pores (~50-70 nm): Vesicular transport and fenestrations
Reflection coefficient (σ): Depends on molecular size relative to pore size

External compression may:

Mechanically narrow inter-endothelial junctions
Reduce pore density
Increase tortuosity of paracellular pathway

5.2 Pore Size and Molecular Permeability
For small molecules (your compounds of interest):



Compound
MW (Da)
Size
Expected Permeability




Paclitaxel
854
~2-3 nm
High (lipophilic)


Doxorubicin
544
~1-2 nm
Moderate


Docetaxel
808
~2-3 nm
High (lipophilic)


Cyclophosphamide
261
~1 nm
High (small, hydrophilic)



These molecules are significantly smaller than capillary pores, so pore size changes have limited direct effect on their permeability. However:

Lipophilic molecules (paclitaxel, docetaxel, doxorubicin) cross via both transcellular and paracellular routes
Compression-induced changes in membrane tension may affect transcellular permeability

5.3 Implementation Strategy
Option 1: Modify Reflection Coefficient (for large molecules)
Parameter: Reflection coefficient (σ)
Location: Compound properties → Protein binding / Tissue partitioning
Range: 0 (freely permeable) to 1 (impermeable)
For small molecules, σ is typically close to 0, so this has limited applicability.
Option 2: Modify Compound-Specific Permeability
More relevant for your small molecules:
Parameter: Permeability coefficient for each compound
Compression effect (empirical):
P_compressed = P_baseline × [1 - β × (P_external / P_critical)]

Where:
- β = 0.3-0.5 (empirical factor, fit to data)
- P_critical = 40-50 mmHg (pressure at significant pore narrowing)

Option 3: Two-Pore Model Modification
For more sophisticated modeling (especially if extending to larger molecules):
In MoBi, implement:
Total_Permeability = P_small_pores + P_large_pores

Under compression:
P_small_pores_compressed = P_small × (1 - 0.3 × Compression_Factor)
P_large_pores_compressed = P_large × (1 - 0.6 × Compression_Factor)

(Large pores are more susceptible to compression)

5.4 Practical Recommendation
For your specific case (small molecules):

Primary mechanism: Modify overall permeability coefficient (combines all effects)
Secondary mechanism: Reduce surface area (models capillary collapse)
Tertiary mechanism: Reduce blood flow (models reduced perfusion)

Combined effect on drug delivery:
Drug_Delivery = Blood_Flow × [(C_plasma - C_tissue) / (1/PS_eff)]

Where all three parameters (Flow, PS, Surface Area) decrease with compression


6. Drug-Specific Considerations
6.1 Paclitaxel
Characteristics:

Highly lipophilic (logP ≈ 3.0)
Extensive tissue binding
CYP2C8/CYP3A4 metabolism
High volume of distribution

Compression effects:

Reduced perfusion → Decreased delivery to skin
Reduced permeability → Limited effect (high lipophilicity allows transcellular passage)
Net effect: Primarily flow-limited distribution

Modeling priority:

Blood flow reduction (primary)
Surface area reduction (secondary)
Permeability (minor effect)

Available model: OSP Paclitaxel model validated for IV administration
6.2 Doxorubicin
Characteristics:

Amphipathic (logP ≈ 1.3)
DNA intercalation
Extensive tissue binding
Cardiotoxicity concerns

Compression effects:

Moderate lipophilicity → Both transcellular and paracellular transport
Net effect: Mixed flow- and permeability-limited

Modeling priority:

Blood flow reduction (primary)
Permeability reduction (co-primary)
Surface area reduction (secondary)

Available models: Multiple PBPK models for doxorubicin in literature using OSP platform
6.3 Docetaxel
Characteristics:

Highly lipophilic (similar to paclitaxel)
CYP3A4 metabolism
Similar PK to paclitaxel

Compression effects: Similar to paclitaxel
6.4 Cyclophosphamide
Characteristics:

Small molecule (MW 261)
Prodrug (requires metabolic activation)
Hydrophilic (logP ≈ 0.6)
High bioavailability

Compression effects:

Hydrophilic → Primarily paracellular transport
Small size → Less affected by pore size changes
Net effect: Primarily flow-limited

Modeling priority:

Blood flow reduction (primary)
Metabolism in tissue (important for prodrug activation)
Permeability (minor effect)

Available models: PBPK models available in literature

7. Implementation Workflow in MoBi
7.1 Step-by-Step Procedure
Step 1: Start with Base Model


Option A: Use existing PK-Sim model

Load validated compound model (e.g., Paclitaxel)
Create simulation with IV administration
Export to MoBi for customization



Option B: Build in MoBi from scratch

Import skin permeation model template
Add compound and metabolism



Step 2: Identify Parameters to Modify
Navigate to Building Blocks in MoBi:


Spatial Structures → Skin → Parameters:

Specific blood flow rate
Endothelial surface area
Volume (if modeling tissue edema)



Passive Transports → Blood to Interstitium:

Permeability coefficient
Create pressure-dependent formulas



Parameter Values:

Set baseline values
Create parameters for External_Pressure



Step 3: Create Formulas for Compression Effects
In Building Blocks → Formulas:
Formula 1: Compressed_Blood_Flow
= Baseline_Blood_Flow × (1 - 0.015 × max(0, External_Pressure - 10))

Formula 2: Compressed_Permeability
= Baseline_Permeability × (1 / (1 + 0.03 × max(0, External_Pressure - 10)))

Formula 3: Compressed_Surface_Area
= Baseline_Surface_Area × (1 - max(0, min(0.8, (External_Pressure - 40) / 50)))

Step 4: Link Formulas to Parameters

Right-click on parameter → Create formula
Select created formula
Set formula dependencies (link to External_Pressure parameter)

Step 5: Create Event for Compression Application
In Building Blocks → Events:
Event: Apply_Compression
Condition: Time >= Compression_Start_Time
Actions:
  - Set External_Pressure = Desired_Pressure_mmHg
  - (Formulas automatically recalculate dependent parameters)

Step 6: Run Simulations


Create Simulation from building blocks


Define scenarios:

Baseline (no compression)
Mild compression (20 mmHg)
Moderate compression (40 mmHg)
Severe compression (60 mmHg)



Compare drug concentrations in:

Plasma
Skin (interstitial)
Systemic tissues



Step 7: Validate and Calibrate

Compare with literature data (if available)
Adjust empirical factors (α, β, k) for best fit
Perform sensitivity analysis

7.2 Advanced: Time-Varying Compression
For dynamic compression (e.g., intermittent pressure):
Create table parameter or formula:
External_Pressure(t) = Amplitude × (1 if (t mod Period) < Duration else 0)

Or use Observer pattern for more complex temporal profiles

7.3 Coupling with Whole-Body PBPK
To analyze systemic effects:

Start with full-body PK-Sim model
Export to MoBi
Modify only skin compartment parameters
Maintain all other organs at default values
Assess impact on:

Total body exposure (AUC)
Peak plasma concentration (Cmax)
Tissue distribution




8. References and Resources
8.1 OSP Suite Documentation
Primary Documentation:

OSP Suite: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/
PK-Sim Documentation: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation
MoBi Documentation: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-mobi/mobi-documentation

Model Repositories:

Skin Permeation Model: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Skin-permeation-model

User Guide PDF included with detailed parameter descriptions


Paclitaxel Model: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Paclitaxel-Model

8.2 Key Scientific Publications
OSP-Based Studies on Chemotherapy Agents:


Damoiseaux D, et al. (2023). Physiologically-based pharmacokinetic model to predict doxorubicin and paclitaxel exposure in infants through breast milk. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol. doi: 10.1002/psp4.13043


Chao FC, et al. (2023). A physiologically-based pharmacokinetic model for predicting doxorubicin disposition in multiple tissue levels and quantitative toxicity assessment. Biomed Pharmacother. 168:115636. doi: 10.1016/j.biopha.2023.115636


Hanke N, et al. (2018). A physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) parent-metabolite model of the chemotherapeutic zoptarelin doxorubicin. Cancer Chemother Pharmacol. 81(2):291-304. doi: 10.1007/s00280-017-3495-2


Kullenberg F, et al. (2021). In Vitro Cell Toxicity and Intracellular Uptake of Doxorubicin. Cells 10(7):1717. doi: 10.3390/cells10071717


Verheijen M, et al. (2018). Bringing in vitro analysis closer to in vivo: Studying doxorubicin toxicity and associated mechanisms in 3D human microtissues with PBPK-based dose modelling. Toxicol Lett. 294:184-192. doi: 10.1016/j.toxlet.2018.05.029


Docetaxel/Cyclophosphamide/Epirubicin PBPK study (2024). Application of PBPK modeling for drug-drug interaction assessment. J Pharmacokinet Pharmacodyn. doi: 10.1007/s10928-024-09912-z


Dermal Absorption and Skin Models:

Dancik Y, et al. (2013). Design and Performance of a Spreadsheet-Based Model for Estimating Bioavailability of Chemicals from Dermal Exposure. Adv Drug Deliv Rev. 65(2):221-236. doi: 10.1016/j.addr.2012.01.006

PBPK Theory and Vascular Permeability:


Niederalt C, et al. (2017). A generic whole body physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for therapeutic proteins in PK-Sim. J Pharmacokinet Pharmacodyn. doi: 10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4

Describes PS parameters and two-pore model implementation



Willmann S, et al. (2007). Development of a physiology-based whole-body population model for assessing the influence of individual variability on the pharmacokinetics of drugs. J Pharmacokinet Pharmacodyn. doi: 10.1007/s10928-007-9053-5

Includes paclitaxel as example compound



Starling Forces and Capillary Exchange:

General physiology references on Starling equation and microvascular exchange

Classic equations for fluid filtration and solute transport
Pressure-flow relationships in microcirculation



8.3 Additional Resources
OSP Community:

Forum: https://forum.open-systems-pharmacology.org/
GitHub Organization: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology
Publications Repository: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSP-based-publications-and-content

Tutorials and Workflows:

OSP Conference presentations (available on website)
YouTube tutorials for PK-Sim and MoBi
Example workflows in documentation

8.4 Parameter Value Sources
Physiological Parameters:

PK-Sim built-in database (human physiology)
ICRP Publication 89 (anatomical/physiological data)
Tissue composition databases

Vascular Parameters for Skin:

Capillary surface area density: 30-50 cm²/cm³
Baseline blood flow: 10-15 mL/min/100g (highly variable with temperature)
Capillary pressure: 25-35 mmHg
Interstitial pressure: 0-2 mmHg (baseline), up to external pressure under compression


9. Summary and Recommendations
9.1 Answers to Your Specific Questions
Q1: How to model interstitial hydrostatic pressure changes and their effect on drug extravasation?
A: Increase interstitial pressure reduces extravasation by decreasing the driving force in the Starling equation. Model this by:

Modifying the permeability-surface area product (PS) or permeability coefficient for drug transport from blood to interstitium
Use pressure-dependent formula: P_effective = P_baseline × (1 - k × ΔPi)
Parameters are found in: Spatial Structures → Skin → Passive transport parameters

Q2: How to model capillary compression (narrowing/collapse) by reducing endothelial surface area?
A:

Parameter: Surface area or Endothelial surface area in the Skin spatial structure
Implementation: Create pressure-dependent formula that reduces surface area above critical pressure (~40 mmHg)
Formula: S_effective = S_baseline × (1 - Collapse_Factor) where Collapse_Factor increases with external pressure

Q3: How to model capillary pore size reduction?
A: For small molecules (your compounds), pore size has minimal direct effect since molecules are much smaller than pores. Instead:

Primary approach: Modify overall permeability coefficient to capture combined effects of pore narrowing and membrane tension changes
Secondary approach: For large molecules, adjust reflection coefficient (σ)
Both parameters are compound-specific and found in compound properties or passive transport definitions

9.2 Recommended Modeling Strategy
Priority 1 (Essential):

✓ Blood flow reduction (already implemented)
Endothelial surface area reduction (for pressures > 40 mmHg)

Priority 2 (Important):
3. Permeability coefficient reduction (pressure-dependent)
4. Couple all three mechanisms with consistent pressure relationships
Priority 3 (Optional/Advanced):
5. Two-pore model for mechanistic insight
6. Time-varying compression patterns
7. Coupling with drug metabolism changes
9.3 Validation Approach
Due to limited literature data on compression effects:

Start conservative: Use mild reduction factors (30-50% at moderate pressures)
Sensitivity analysis: Test parameter ranges systematically
Cross-check: Ensure combined effects are physiologically reasonable
Literature comparison: Compare with perfusion imaging studies if available
Expert consultation: Discuss with OSP community forum

9.4 Software Version Recommendations

Use latest OSP Suite (Version 12.x or higher)
Skin permeation model updated for recent MoBi versions
Ensure compatibility between PK-Sim and MoBi versions


10. Practical Example: Implementation Checklist
For implementing compression effects on paclitaxel in skin:

 Load paclitaxel model in PK-Sim or create compound definition
 Export to MoBi for customization
 Create parameter: External_Pressure (0-100 mmHg)
 Modify Skin_Blood_Flow with formula (50-90% of baseline under compression)
 Modify Endothelial_Surface_Area with threshold formula (pressure > 40 mmHg)
 Modify Permeability_Coefficient with pressure-dependent reduction (20-40%)
 Create Events for compression application/removal timing
 Run baseline scenario (no compression)
 Run compression scenarios (20, 40, 60 mmHg)
 Compare drug concentrations in skin interstitium and plasma
 Perform sensitivity analysis on reduction factors
 Document parameter sources and assumptions


Contact and Support
For technical implementation questions:

OSP Community Forum: https://forum.open-systems-pharmacology.org/
GitHub Issues: Report issues in respective model repositories
Documentation: Detailed MoBi tutorials at https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/

For scientific questions on compression physiology:

Consult microcirculation and wound healing literature
Consider collaboration with experts in dermatology/pressure effects


Acknowledgments
This answer synthesizes information from:

Open Systems Pharmacology Suite documentation
Skin permeation model user guide (Dancik et al. 2013 implementation)
OSP-based publications database
PBPK modeling literature on chemotherapy agents
Physiological literature on Starling forces and microvascular dynamics


Document Version
Version: 1.0
Date: 2026-03-13
Prepared for: GitHub Discussion #2131 - Open Systems Pharmacology Forum
Author: Claude Code Analysis based on OSP documentation and scientific literature

Note: This document provides guidance based on current OSP capabilities and physiological principles. Actual parameter values should be validated against experimental data when available. The OSP community welcomes contributions of validated models for compression effects.

title: Can't find the 'Merge' function in Mobi12
body_text:
Hi all, I’m currently working through the ESQlabs tutorials and have encountered an issue when trying to add an external spatial structure to the base spatial structure. The tutorial mentions using the “Merge” function, but I’m unable to locate this option in MoBi12.

My MoBi interface looks like this:

Could anyone please advise on where to find it or whether this function has been renamed or relocated in the current version?
Many thanks in advance.

answer.body_text:
Yes, the merge button has been removed.
Also, this training module has not been updated for v12  yet.

comments.body_text:
Can you tell me which training module you're working on?
I believed all the training modules were up to date for V12.
The V11 training modules are also still present, but you have to use V11 release to follow along with those.

title: Problem with installing the global sensitivity analysis package
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I am a student who wanted to try the global sensitivity analysis for my own project. So, I found the ospsuite.globalsensitivity package and tried to use it. But I immediately ran into the problem that I can not install the package with the next error message:
Warning messages:
1: packages ‘SobolSequence’, ‘ospsuite’ are not available for this version of R
Versions of these packages for your version of R might be available elsewhere,
see the ideas at
https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-patched/R-admin.html#Installing-packages
I also switched my R into version 4.3.2, 4.4.3 and 4.5.2, as the warning message mentioned the version of R, but it did not work. So, does someone have a solution for this problem?
Thanks in advance.

answer.body_text:

title: Traceability via History Functionality
body_text: @Yuri05 , Hi Juri and hi OSP team, The history functionality in PK-Sim/MoBi can be used for traceability purposes. However, the availability of the “Clear history” option compromises full traceability. Do you have any thoughts on this? Why is the Clear history option not disabled?

comments.body_text:
Thanks a lot for raising this, Ghazal — it’s an important point, and we agree that the History can be a very useful element for traceability.
That said, we intentionally keep the “Clear history” option available for a few practical reasons:


Histories can grow very large over time. As work progresses, the project history may become extensive and can noticeably increase the overall project size (and sometimes also impact handling/sharing of the file). Being able to clear it helps keep projects manageable.


Histories may contain information that should not be shared. The history can sometimes still include (sensitive) details about entities that were later deleted (e.g., simulations, observed data, intermediate assumptions, etc.). If a project is going to become public—or is simply shared with external collaborators—those details may need to be removed.


You can export the history before removing it. If you want to keep a record, the history can be exported (e.g., to Excel) and archived externally before clearing it.


So in practice: if you need the history as complete evidence of all actions, the safest approach is simply not to delete it (or to export it first and then clear it if required for sharing).
Finally, for documentation and traceability in a broader sense, a Working Journal is often a better alternative/complement than relying solely on the internal history. It supports richer documentation (attachments, Word-style narrative, diagrams of project development with labels/milestones, etc.). Full documentation is here:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/shared-tools-and-example-workflows/working-journal
Thank you so much Juro for your detailed explanations.

title: Combining literature inhalatory model with an already developed full body PBPK
body_text:
Hello,
I am a new user of MoBi and PK-Sim and I am trying to model inhalation as a route of administration. I started from an inhalation model describing lung and bronchial disposition (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0939641120302836) and merged it with an existing full-body PBPK model that does not include inhalation (https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Midazolam-Model/tree/master).
After merging the two models, I simulated a single inhaled dose of 5 mg, but the simulated plasma concentrations strongly underpredict the observed data. When performing parameter identification, I noticed that the observed data can only be fitted when using an effective dose of approximately 80 mg, which suggests that something is wrong in the model structure or assumptions rather than parameter values.
I am not sure whether there is an issue in the model definition or I made something wrong when I merged the inhalation model with the full body PBPK. I attached the MoBi project to this post, many thanks in advice for any feedback/suggestions.
model_merged.zip

title: IV furosemide PBPK model: Cmax underprediction and transporter (OAT1/OAT3/MRP4) parameterization in PK-Sim
body_text:
Hello everyone!
I am a beginner in PBPK modeling. I started working with PK-Sim only recently and I am currently developing a PBPK model for IV furosemide in healthy volunteers.
At the moment, I am parameterizing renal elimination using in vitro transporter data for OAT1, OAT3, and MRP4, based on values reported in the literature (Km, Vmax). I would really appreciate some guidance from more experienced modelers regarding a few points in my model. I am attaching a figure comparing simulated vs observed plasma concentration–time profiles (IV administration):

My simulated Cmax is lower than most observed data points. Although the values remain within a 2-fold observed/simulated ratio, for many individual data points the difference is still noticeable. This suggests that the model is systematically underpredicting the initial peak concentration.
I also observe a pattern where the simulation is slightly lower than observed in the early phase (suggesting faster initial elimination or distribution), but becomes higher than observed in the later phase (suggesting slower terminal elimination). This creates a crossing pattern: lower than data initially, higher than data at later times, which suggests an imbalance between distribution parameters and elimination processes. However, I am not sure how to correct this. I have already tried changing both the tissue partition coefficient calculation methods and the cellular permeability/partitioning methods. At the moment, I am using the ‘PK-Sim standard’ option for both, which has provided the best overall performance so far.
For transporter implementation, I selected “specific active transport – Michaelis–Menten.” In many in vitro studies, the authors report Km and Vmax, but do not report transporter concentration (for example: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25981193/).
In these cases, how is this typically handled in PBPK models? Is it acceptable to estimate transporter concentration? Or is it standard practice to keep the default value (e.g., 1.00) provided by the software and rely on Vmax/Km alone? Do modelers usually lump scaling into Vmax rather than explicitly modeling transporter abundance?
I also noticed that in a published PBPK model of furosemide (Britz et al., 2021: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33237382/
), the authors implemented transport using Km values, and then calibrated kcat via parameter identification, rather than explicitly defining transporter concentration. This approach seems reasonable, but I am unsure whether I should follow a similar strategy or try to refine my model by explicitly handling transporter abundance.
Any advice, references, or modeling strategies would be extremely helpful.
Thank you very much for your time and support!

comments.body_text: Did you check https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Furosemide-Model ?

title: Vss and Vd changed after oral administration
body_text:
When switching from IV to PO administration, both clearance (CL) and volume of distribution (Vd) change unexpectedly.
Is this route-dependent variation in CL and Vd expected behavior in PK-Sim?
If not, what model settings or parameters might cause this artifact？
Thanks!
Best regards,

comments.body_text:
Hi, here are my two pence quickly: Yes that is expected behaviour. In pharmacokinetics, the true clearance (CL) and true volume of distribution (Vd) do not actually change when switching from IV to PO administration. These are intrinsic biological properties of the patient and the drug. But this is not what you see in the model. What changes are the apparent (simulated) values, typically denoted as CL/F and Vd/F. Why the values "appear" to change: Bioavailability (F): Intravenous (IV) administration has 100% bioavailability ((F=1)). Oral (PO) drugs often have much lower bioavailability ((F<1)) due to incomplete absorption or the first-pass effect in the liver and intestinal metabolism. So depending on the structure and parameters of you underlaying model a change in these parameters can be expected.
Hope that might help you!
Best, Tobias

title: Observed vs. predicted or predicted vs. observed?
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I would like to discuss the option of switching the axes in the observed vs predicted plot.
There are several reasons to do so, most of which are explained in this article.
In short, the dependent variable goes typically on the y-axis, which should be the observed.
In PK-Sim, the observed is currently placed on the x-axis. This is also not clearly indicated, which enhances the potential problems with this orientation. As an illustration, a hypothetical but not unlikely scenario is shown for a situation where data is available at only one time point for multiple individuals, and they are distributed around the predicted geometric mean value at that time point. The right-hand plot shows the currently implemented axis orientation in PK-Sim, where one could think there is a model misspecification because of an observed trend: A trendline would show a horizontal line instead of the desired diagonal around the line of identity. The left-hand plot shows a good distribution around the predicted value, and a trendline is uninformative for this dataset.
Let me know what your thoughts are on this.
Best,
Wilbert

title: Publication: "Integrating Population Approaches With Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Models: A Novel Framework for Parameter Estimation"
body_text:
📣 New Publication Alert! 📣
I'm excited to share the publication of our latest work in CPT: Pharmacometrics & Systems Pharmacology! 🎉
https://ascpt.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/psp4.70186
📝 Publication Details:
Title: "Integrating Population Approaches With Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Models: A Novel Framework for Parameter Estimation"
Authors: Donato Teutonico, David Marchionni, Marc Lavielle, Laurent Nguyen
💡 What's it about?
In this tutorial, we illustrate and explain the methodology connecting PBPK models developed with the OSP Suite to the SAEM algorithm to perform estimation of individual parameter values on mechanistic parameters. The methodology also opens the possibility to integrate covariates external to the PBPK model to explain the observed variability.

🙏 Acknowledgments:
A huge thank you to all the co-authors and everyone involved in making this work possible!

title: Environment Incompatibility for ospsuite R on Cluster for Parameter Optimization and Sensitivity Analysis
body_text:
Dear Open Systems Pharmacology Team,
I am reaching out for assistance with a technical challenge. Our project relies heavily on ospsuite R for parameter optimization and sensitivity analyses on models built in PK-Sim/MoBi. However, our local workstations lack the computational power to run these resource-intensive tasks efficiently—jobs are frequently interrupted and take excessively long to complete.
We aimed to deploy these on our Linux HPC cluster (via SLURM), but encountered environment incompatibilities, resulting in failed installations of ospsuite and its core dependencies (rSharp, ospsuite.utils, tlf, globalsensitivity).
Key requirements:


Continue building/optimizing models with PK-Sim/MoBi.


Run parameter estimation workflows and GSA (Morris/Sobol/EFAST) on resulting PKML models.


Scale to cluster for multiple simulations.


Could you kindly advise on:


A Singularity/Docker-compatible distribution or build instructions for ospsuite R on Linux HPC?


Any conda/mamba environment or minimal dependencies workaround?


Alternative export workflows from PKML to native R optimizers while preserving OSP functionality?


Direct support channels or community experts for cluster deployments?


Your guidance would greatly accelerate our research. Thank you for your outstanding tools and support!
Best regards,
Bach

answer.body_text:
The easiest option would probably be to set up an Ubuntu Docker container and install all the required OSP R packages.
We created Docker containers previously, but stopped doing so a while ago.
The latest available Docker container (Ubuntu 18 / ospsuite-R 11.0.123 / R 4.1) can be found here.
As an alternative, you could compile all the necessary R packages and their underlying C++ libraries manually. Each package provides a build script tailored for Ubuntu, but this can easily be modified for your target distribution.

title: Question about specific clearance calculation
body_text:
Hi all,
When we define renal plasma clearance, we define it as per body weight. I wonder if we need to input the body weight as well for the simulated subject because by default it's 73 kg. But if this is the case we need to update also the other parameters (e.g. kidney volume, blood flow...) for this specific individual because all of them contribute to specific clearance calculations? answer.body_text:

Kind regards
Salma

comments.body_text:
Just showing this example, if someone can help me with this.
I am simulating a drug with a renal clearance of 7.15 ml/min/kg in a population with a mean weight of 57. The specific clearance would be different based on the weight used for its calculation and accordingly getting different simulated profiled.




So the question is what is the right way to do this? do I need to add different clearance profiles for the same drug based on the simulated population's body weight?
The specific clearance unit is 1/time, so is it supposed to be calculated as a standard measurement for drug clearance for a patient weight 73 kg, age 30 and not to be played with?
Many thanks
Salma

Dear Salma,
sorry for the slow reply and I am not sure if my answer is still relevant, but the specific clearance is calculated from your input as plasma clearance according to this formula:

So basically the plasma clearance is back-calculated to an intrinsic kidney CL and normalized to the kidney volume.
PK-Sim uses the specific clearance for calculation in populations. So If you would exchange your Individual with a virtual population PK-sim will use e.g. 2.22 1/min and multiply it with the kidney volume in each virtual individual. That way from the same specific CL each Individual will get a different plasma CL according to the individual body weight and Kidney volume.
Based on that, if you think your plasma CL is based totally on Kidney clearance, you might think of refining your specific kidney CL to your measured data and use the refined value for extrapolation to populations.
Hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias

Thanks a lot, Tobias for the clarification. It makes sense now!
Kind regards
Salma

I'm very sorry, but I may not have fully understood your response. For the specific clearance used in the simulation in the screenshot, was it calculated based on the 73.00kg input caculation parameters Body weights, or based on the 57kg in the individuals module?

Looking forward to your reply!

title: GPL licensing with respect to physiological parameters
body_text: Does the GPL license permit basic physiological parameter values (e.g., tissue volumes and perfusions) contained in PK-Sim in to be used in non-GPL projects?  In our case, the values would be delivered with the application and be contained in CSV files.  OSP/PK-Sim would be referenced as the source of the values.  Our application would not use, or interface with, any PK-Sim software or source code.  The model code itself would be open-source and delivered with the tool.  Only the user interface would be closed-source.

comments.body_text:
I don't think that should be a problem – but I'm not a lawyer, so I can't guarantee the accuracy of this statement.
AI (GPT 5.2) response
I’m not a lawyer, but I can give a practical GPLv2-based analysis.
Does the PK‑Sim GPLv2 license force your non‑GPL app to become GPL if you only use the parameter values?
In general: no. If you are not using, linking to, bundling, or interfacing with PK‑Sim code, and you are only taking physiological parameter values (facts/data) and shipping them as CSV files with your application, that typically does not trigger GPLv2’s copyleft.
Why GPL copyleft usually doesn’t apply here
GPLv2’s copyleft is triggered when you distribute a work based on the Program (derivative of the copyrighted software/code), or when you distribute something that contains GPL code, links to it, or is otherwise combined with it.
The “Clarifying Addendum” in your LICENSE text says the copyleft effect applies if you distribute software:

containing any GPL code, or
linked to GPL software, or
otherwise combined with GPL software,
and then you must license that program under GPLv2.

In your scenario, you explicitly say:

you do not use PK‑Sim software or source code,
you do not interface with it,
you only reuse numeric physiological values in CSV.

That typically falls outside (i)-(iii), and outside “work based on the Program”.
Data vs. code
Physiological parameters like tissue volumes and perfusions are typically facts. Facts themselves are usually not copyrightable; what can be protected is:

the creative selection/arrangement of a dataset, or
database rights in some jurisdictions (not covered by GPL text, and depends on jurisdiction), or
trademark / attribution considerations.

So, reusing individual parameter values or even many of them often isn’t creating a derivative of the software.
Practical cautions / things to verify
Even though GPLv2 likely doesn’t “infect” your app here, watch for these common gotchas:


Don’t ship any PK‑Sim content that is actually code or GPL-covered assets

Example: exporting and embedding PK‑Sim model files/scripts/templates that contain copyrighted expression from the project (not just raw numbers).



Check whether the dataset has its own licensing terms

Sometimes projects include separate licensing or attribution requirements for data/content distinct from the software license. (GPL applies to the “Program”; projects sometimes add separate notices for datasets.)



Avoid implying endorsement / trademark use

The addendum explicitly says GPLv2 is not intended to grant trademark/trade name rights. So referencing PK‑Sim as a source is usually fine (nominative reference), but don’t brand your product in a way that suggests official endorsement.



Jurisdiction-specific database rights

In the EU, “sui generis database rights” can complicate purely “facts aren’t copyright” reasoning, depending on how the dataset was created and extracted. That’s not a GPL issue per se, but it can still matter legally.



What you can do safely (typical best practice)

Ship the CSV values with clear source attribution (as you planned).
Include citations (publication references) if PK‑Sim derived them from literature.
Document that your software does not include or link to PK‑Sim code.
Don’t copy any PK‑Sim text/structures beyond what’s necessary (e.g., avoid copying descriptive prose, comments, UI strings, etc., unless permitted).

If you want, paste (or point me to) where in PK‑Sim/PK‑Sim data these physiological parameters live (file names/paths), and I can help you assess whether it looks like “raw factual data” vs. something more likely to be protected/individually licensed.

title: To ask for the explain of the changes of inhibitor exposure in the DDI simulation
body_text: Dear all, I posted this in case since I have been stucked with this issue for days and looking for a solution. Recently, we are doing a DDI PBPK model of a victim drug and a inhibitor via BCRP in the intestine. We want to simulate the DDI under different situations. The inhibitor drug is only metabolized via CYP3A4 but not a substrate of BCRP in the model. But when we simulated the DDI, we discovered that the changes in the Kcat of BCRP or other paraneters of victim drug lead to the changes of the inhibitor also, the Cmax ranges from 840 to 870 or 880 ng/ml. Even if I turn off the BCRP inhibition and change the Kcat of BCRP to a half, the Cmax and AUC of the inhibitor changed too. We read about the previous discussion which mentioned to check the resolution but in our case, the resolution kept unchanged. This change in the inhibitor concentration is more obvious in population simulation than the individual simulation. In addition, when we tighten the absolute tolerance from 10^-10 to 10^-13 and relative tolerance from 10^-5 to 10^-8. The difference seem to be reduce significantly to around 15 ng/ml, Could anyone please explain me why the exposure of inhibitor change even when there is no changes in the model of inhibitor or give me any suggestion? Thank you in advance
