title: OSP Suite Version 12 Update 1 released
body_text:
You can download the version here.
Release Notes for the Open Systems Pharmacology Software Suite 12 Update 1
New features

MoBi: Trace Source of Parameters and Molecule Initial Values in Simulations
Previously, determining why a parameter (or an initial molecule amount) had a certain value in a simulation could be difficult because definitions might come from several sources (Spatial Structure, Parameter Values, Initial Conditions, Individuals, Expression Profiles, etc.).
With MoBi 12.1, you can now trace the origin of these values in the simulation:

View the originating module and building block
Identify the type quickly via an icon in the “Building Block” column
Distinguish between competing definitions and understand which one was applied

Scope and applicability:

Available for simulations newly created (or rebuilt) in MoBi 12.1.
Existing simulations from earlier versions must be recreated to expose traceability details.
Applies to parameters and initial values of molecule amounts.

Benefits:

Faster troubleshooting of unexpected values
More transparent scenario refinement
Reduced risk of misconfiguration when reusing modules


MoBi: Spatial Structure with Individual selection for preview

Adds a way to select a specific individual for preview in the spatial structure view. This feature enables users to visualize individual-specific settings directly within the spatial structure, thereby improving model personalization and analysis.
In this preview mode, individual parameters are shown as read-only within the spatial structure, but the user can quickly navigate to the parameter definition within the individual for editing.

PK-Sim and MoBi: Concurrent Execution of Simulations
Previously, starting a simulation locked the entire user interface until the run completed. This is no longer the case. Now, only simulations that are currently running are locked against modification; all other simulations and project elements remain fully editable.
While one or more simulations are running, you can, for example:

Start additional simulation runs in parallel
Create, edit, or view charts
Create new simulations
Adjust settings or parameters of simulations that are not currently running

Stopping Simulations


To stop all currently running simulations at once, click the Stop All Simulations button:



To stop a single simulation, use the context menu of that simulation in the Simulations Explorer:



Progress Indicator
When more than one simulation is running, the progress bar displays the number of active simulations:

PK-Sim and MoBi: Batch Edit Mode for Charts
Editing charts with many data points or numerous curves could previously feel slow because every single modification (style change, axis setting, visibility toggle, etc.) triggered an immediate re-render. The new Batch Edit Mode lets you pause automatic chart updates, perform multiple edits, and then apply them all at once—greatly improving responsiveness for large datasets.
Key Capabilities

Temporarily disables live chart re-drawing while you adjust multiple curves or settings.
Clear visual indication that the chart is out of date (so you don’t lose track).
One-click (re)activation applies all accumulated changes in a single re-render.

Typical Workflow


Disable auto-update in the Chart Editor.



Make one or more changes (e.g., add/remove curves, adjust appearance, change axes). After the first modification, the chart is marked as inactive/out-of-date with a hint overlay.



Re-enable auto-update. All pending modifications are applied at once and the chart refreshes.



Exports chart to png

A highly requested feature, this adds the ability to export any chart directly to a PNG file for use in presentations or publications. The implementation supports custom formatting and watermark settings, similar to the "copy to clipboard" function.

MoBi: Create Subfolders possible for modules

Enables users to organize modules by creating subfolders.
This feature is especially useful when working with multiple structural models, helping to keep the workspace tidy and navigable.

MoBi: Save/load neighborhood

Users can now save a neighborhood configuration to a PKML file and load it from PKML. This supports efficient reuse and sharing of neighborhood setups, streamlining workflows for complex modeling scenarios.
PK-Sim and MoBi: Calculation of sensitivity in PI should be interruptable

Sensitivity calculations in parameter identification (PI) can now be interrupted by the user, similar to regular simulation runs. This saves time and resources, allowing users to halt long or unneeded calculations without waiting for them to finish.

OSP Platform qualification library and PBPK Models Library
PBPK Models library extended
New PBPK models added:

Propofol

Model building process and model quality of every new PBPK model is documented in the corresponding model evaluation report.
New releases of OSP Platform qualification library and PBPK Models Library
As with every new OSP Suite release, ALL platform qualification reports and model evaluation reports have been recreated with the new version of the OSP Suite:

OSP Qualification Reports library

See the Release Notes for a full list of changes.


OSP-PBPK-Model-Library

See the Release Notes for a full list of changes.

title: To ask for reason why exposure of inhibitor changed.
body_text: Dear all, I posted this in case since I have been stucked with this issue for days and looking for a solution. Recently, we are doing a DDI PBPK model of a victim drug and a inhibitor via BCRP in the intestine. We want to simulate the DDI under different situations. The inhibitor drug is only metabolized via CYP3A4 but not a substrate of BCRP in the model. But when we simulated the DDI, we discovered that the changes in the Kcat of BCRP or other paraneters of victim drug lead to the changes of the inhibitor also, the Cmax ranges from 840 to 870 or 880 ng/ml. Even if I turn off the BCRP inhibition and change the Kcat of BCRP to a half, the Cmax and AUC of the inhibitor changed too. We read about the previous discussion which mentioned to check the resolution but in our case, the resolution kept unchanged. This change in the inhibitor concentration is more obvious in population simulation than the individual simulation. In addition, when we tighten the absolute tolerance from 10^-10 to 10^-13 and relative tolerance from 10^-5 to 10^-8. The difference seem to be reduce significantly to around 15 ng/ml, Could anyone please explain me why the exposure of inhibitor change even when there is no changes in the model of inhibitor or give me any suggestion? Thank you in advance

title: How to set up the excretion process to achieve the target fecal excretion ratio of 36%？
body_text:
Hello! I am trying to build a model that describes the elimination pathways of a compound . The compound is a substrate for efflux transporters p-gp.This drug is metabolized by three CYP enzymes.The drugs also undergo renal clearance and biliary clearance.
I would like to know if this approach is correct?：
Through the material balance experiment, the excretion rates and proportions of the parent drug in urine and feces were obtained. Based on this, the renal clearance, biliary clearance, and hepatic clearance rates and proportions of the drug were calculated. This serves as the initial input value.The result was that the Lumen-Feces-Fraction excreted to feces curve was almost zero.

In order to achieve  excretion of feces,I tried the following two methods.I made adjustments according to the ideas discussed. #1688 (comment):
1、Firstly, I  reduced the Specific Intestinal Permeability, the ratio was appropriate and the curve fitting was poor.Predicted Cmax is lower .

Then,I reduced B/P in order to optimize the absorption part of the curve.I'm not quite sure what caused this elimination phase.

2、I added a transporter (MDR1) to the apical side of the liver, without altering any expression levels. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) of the transporter was assumed as 10 μmol/L. And I want to determine  and maximum rate of reaction (Vmax) of the transporter  by fitting to the fraction of fecal excretion and excreted as unchanged drug in the urine. But I failed.

Could everyone please offer me some assistance? I'm looking forward to any responses from you all.

title: Help with changing x-axis in population simulation PKsim
body_text:
Hi,
I did some individual and population simulations to simulate a multiple dosing study. for this, I want to modify the x-axis to show a specific part of the whole concentration-time profile. In the individual simulation this is possible via the chart editor but in the population simulations I do not have this option in the chart options. Is it possible to modify the x-axis in a population simulation at all and if so I would value any input on how to do it.
Many thanks in advance!

answer.body_text:
Hi,
unfortunately this is not possible in PK-sim directly. You may propose this as feature wish. The clumsy workaround so far ist that you can import your desired x and y axis boundaries as observed data points in to PK-Sim. Than drag and drop them in the population simulation chart and change their color to white so that they are not to be seen. However the Graph Editor will smooth the zoom to these made up boundaries now.
Best, Tobias

title: Questions about the proportion of metabolic contribution of different enzymes
body_text:
Hello everyone.
If multiple metabolizing enzymes are involved in the metabolism of a drug, but the enzymatic kinetic information is incomplete and only the metabolic ratios of the various enzymes are known. I want to optimize it directly by Monte Carlo simulation, how do I fix the metabolic ratio of each enzyme to optimize the parameters?
If there is a prototype drug excreted directly from the kidney, can I fix the ratio of hepatic to renal clearance separately?
An example is shown in the following figure:

(Yeung CHT, Beers JL, Jackson KD, Edginton AN. Verifying in vitro-determined enzyme contributions to cannabidiol clearance for exposure predictions in human through physiologically-based pharmacokinetic modeling. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol. 2022 Dec 20. doi: 10.1002/psp4.12908. Epub ahead of print. PMID: 36540909.)

answer.body_text:
For each enzyme: create and Import an observed data set with just one point: {simulation_end_time; fraction metabolized}
Then you can optimize the corresponding Total fraction metabolized of the enzyme vs. this data point

title: Question about CYP abundance
body_text:
I want to simulate a PBPK model in patients with hepatic or renal impairment. The target drug is primarily metabolized by CYP enzymes. I referred to relevant literature and found that the CYP abundance (pmol/mg protein) can be adjusted. I think this should be modified in the 'In vitro metabolic rate in the presence of liver microsomes - First Order' section. As the disease severity increases, the CYP abundance decreases, but the specific clearance increases. Under the condition of a constant reference concentration, the simulated AUC values demonstrate a decreasing trend, which contradicts the observed clinical AUC values. I reviewed several similar studies where CYP abundance was similarly modified. In these studies, the simulated AUC trend was consistent with the actual AUC, showing an increase. Why is my simulated trend the opposite, and where could the possible error lie?
Relevant literature：DOI: 10.1177/ 20420986231220222；https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-023-43258-9

comments.body_text:
Hi,
the error is that you modify the Cyp abundance in the wrong place. The abundance is defined in the Expression profile like here for the CYP3A4  Expression profile European (P-gp modified, CYP3A4 36 h). Note that the 4.32 µM corresponds to the 108 pmol/mg CYP abundance in liver as it was used for Mild liver impairment patients in the reference you cited (Table S2). I you want to reduce that CYP3A4 Abundance to e.g. 56  pmol/mg for Moderate liver impairment paitents you best clone the healthy expression profile to a "moderate HI" Expression profile and reduce the Abundance to 56/108*4.32 µM and use that expression profile in your Moderate Liver impaired individual.

 Hope that might help you
Best, Tobias

title: How to add different containers in sum formula
body_text:
I noticed that in MoBi v12, the reactions for FcRn binding use a sum formula, instead of listing out all of the amounts in every single organ. For example this is the calculation for total amount of drug in interstitial:

If I wanted to add a new compartment to this sum formula, but it is not named as interstitial, what would be the best way to do it? If the operator for the parameter criteria allowed for both AND and OR, it would make it very simple, but this doesn't seem to be the case. Is it possible to add a new path to the amount of drug in my new compartment and add it to the sum formula below (the M_#i)? I'm not too familiar with how these sum formulas are defined and if it is even possible to add another value  after the M_#i (for example, M_#i + amount in new compartment).

answer.body_text:
You could add a new tag called Interstitial to your compartment, then the drug and complex will be automatically captured by the sum formula. This is the easiest solution I can think of.
If adding a tag leads to unwanted side effects: you could modify the equation of the binding reaction by including the drug and complex in your compartment explicitly.

Modifying the sum formula directly is in principle possible, but pretty ugly and error prone.
You are right: combining AND and OR operators is not possible.
So if you would like to include the drug in your "non-interstitial" compartment, you could extend the sum formula like this:
M_#i + YourDrug/N
Where YourDrug is the drug amount in your compartment and N is the number of summands which will be created by the sum formula in the simulation (= number of interstitial containers which satisfy the criteria).

This will be expanded in the simulation as:

title: LLOQ unit for observed data in json
body_text:
Dear OSP expert, I picked Midazolam-Model.pksim5 from osp library and there for observed data called 'Lutz 2018 - Cohort 1, Control - Midazolam - PO - 2 mg - Plasma - agg. (n=20)' as you see below in pksim the LLOQ is 1E-4 mg/l.

Then I export the model to json and below you see the json part corresponding to this observed data:

the unit in json is ng/ml and lloQ is 1E-10. Actually 1E-4 mg/l (as given in pksim) is equal to 0.1 ng/ml and not 1E-10. Can you explain this? we have no specific unit for LLOQ in json, we have only one unit and that is for observed data of each dataset. So, if I assumed the same unit for lloq, it does not fit.

comments.body_text:
Seems that LLOQ is stored in the internal unit, which in this case is [kg/l].
You can verify this by reimporting the project from snapshot

@Yuri05 thank you so much Juri, so it means unlike pksim, observed data and lloq do not have necessarily the same unit. When observed data has unit of concentration (molar) then both lloq and observed data have unit of umol/L but in case of observed data with unit of concentration (mass), then irrespective of unit given in json for observed data, lloq has base unit of kg/L. So, while observed data can be set to everything but lloq unit is always the base unit. Is that correct?

title: Using different events for administration protocols in MoBi v12.1
body_text:
I used to make my models using MoBi v11 before and in v11, it was very easy to just switch out the event building block when using different administration protocols. In v12, it seems like there are "Modules" then within them, "building blocks". The issue I'm facing here is I cannot add another event building block within the same Module. Because of this, I need to create a new module and add an event building block there. This is fine but the problem is when I try to run a simulation and select a specific event for my administration protocol, it doesn't allow me to unselect the event within a specific module.
Here is an example:

I have a rat simulation with all the necessary building blocks including events which has an IV bolus administration protocol.
However, I also want to use the same model but run a simulation to give an ICM administration. If I add as seen above, it will administer both the IV and ICM protocol that I have added. The only way it seems to me currently is to remove the IV 10mg/kg from the Rat simulation completely and make it a separate module with just event inside. Then I can select which event I want to use. But I feel like there has to be an easier/better way to do this? Or is that how v12 is when using different administration protocols?

comments.body_text:
In your "ICM" module, you can either set the "Dose" parameter of the IV administration to 0, or "overwrite" the event from "IV 10mg/kg" by creating an empty node with the same name.
I would change the "Dose" param, then you have the flexibility to trigger both administration routes from the same simulation.

title: Add User Defined PK Parameters for Population Simulation Result in OSPsuite R
body_text:
Hi all,
I need to calculate my own AUC for my population simulations. After defining a custom parameter myAUC (based on the standard parameter AUC_tEnd with my own start and end times), the simulation returns empty results for each individual. However, when I define another parameter such as C_max, it works and provides values.
My questions are:
1.	Is it possible to use a custom AUC parameter (AUC_tEND or AUC_tEND_norm) in population simulation results?
2.	When performing PK analysis for population simulation results, how can I extract the mean value of the results?
3.	Is it possible to calculate total clearance in PK-Sim/OSPSuite, when the simulation uses advanced administration protocols(e.g. two different infusion regimen)?
Any advice would be greatly appreciated.
Many thanks in advance!
Kind regards,
Zhilan

answer.body_text:
I need to calculate my own AUC for my population simulations. After defining a custom parameter myAUC (based on the standard parameter AUC_tEnd with my own start and end times), the simulation returns empty results for each individual. However, when I define another parameter such as C_max, it works and provides values.

Yes, it is possible to define your own AUC for a population simulation. Please take into account that e.g. start/end time of such a parameter are always defined in the internal units (which is [min] for time).
myParam = addUserDefinedPKParameter(name="myAUC", standardPKParameter = StandardPKParameter$AUC_tEnd)
myParam$startTime = 10 # 10 minutes
myParam$endTime = 20  # 20 minutes
...
pkAnalysis <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = results)
...

title: How to Apply GFR Formula?
body_text:
Dear to all,
The GFR value in PK-Sim  is actually calculated according to the following formula. I wonder if the applicable age of this GFR is 30-100 years old? Do the FPMA、Vmax、TA50、Hill cofficient in the formula need to be changed at different age stages? Or between 30 and 100 years old, the FPMA is 26.6 ml/min/100g,Vmax is 0.9 ml/min/100g,TA50 was 59 years for
females and 54 years for males. The Hill coefficient was parameterized at 1.5, these values are fixed?
Sincerely!

answer.body_text:
I am attaching the full equation for specific GFR in PK-Sim. Specific GFR (mL/min/100g kidney) is then multiplied out by the individual's kidney volume to get the individual GFR (mL/min). Be aware that the GFR would then need to be standardized by the individual BSA if comparing to clinical measurements, which are reported in units of mL/min/1.73m2.
The hill function for aging does not apply when age is less than 30. There is a piecewise limiter.
In virtual populations, there is inter-individual variability on individual kidney volume, GFRmat, TM50 (maturation), and the Hill coefficient for maturation (H). These values can be viewed for each individual by exporting a virtual population to .csv format.
In non-preterm populations, the Post-menstrual age is equal to the Age in weeks + 40 weeks.
The Hill coefficient for aging is fixed at 1.5. The aging half-times (TA50) are fixed at 54 and 59 years for males and females, respectively. The maximal decreasing factor is fixed at 0.9 mL/min/100g kidney. The volume of the standard kidney (Vstd) is 440 mL. These values are the same for all individuals in the population.

comments.body_text: @JanSchlender

I'm sorry I didn' t understand what's your meaning,Can you explain the details?

I was only tagging another OSP community member (Jan Schlender) to prompt him to answer to this question as he is quite the expert on renal clearance in PK-Sim ;-)

I am attaching the full equation for specific GFR in PK-Sim. Specific GFR (mL/min/100g kidney) is then multiplied out by the individual's kidney volume to get the individual GFR (mL/min). Be aware that the GFR would then need to be standardized by the individual BSA if comparing to clinical measurements, which are reported in units of mL/min/1.73m2.
The hill function for aging does not apply when age is less than 30. There is a piecewise limiter.
In virtual populations, there is inter-individual variability on individual kidney volume, GFRmat, TM50 (maturation), and the Hill coefficient for maturation (H). These values can be viewed for each individual by exporting a virtual population to .csv format.
In non-preterm populations, the Post-menstrual age is equal to the Age in weeks + 40 weeks.
The Hill coefficient for aging is fixed at 1.5. The aging half-times (TA50) are fixed at 54 and 59 years for males and females, respectively. The maximal decreasing factor is fixed at 0.9 mL/min/100g kidney. The volume of the standard kidney (Vstd) is 440 mL. These values are the same for all individuals in the population.
No. The GFR in mL/min in PKSim would be divided by the individual's BSA (Mosteller formula usually), and then multiplied by 1.73m2.

@prvmalik
I think I understand very well. Thank you for your detailed answers！
Yours，
Sincerely！
Hello, may I ask how I found the creatinine clearance and how to fill this value into PKSIM?

Hi, I would like to know how to get from the GFRspec (ml/min/100g kidney) to the GFR of a simulated individual (ml/min).
The simulated organ volumes are in liters and not in grams. I would like to compare simulated GFRs with real world data of specific populations, but I don't understand how to calculate the absolute GFR with the PK Sim values.
Thank you!

Hi,
PK-Sim assumes a density of 1 g/mL for all organs, meaning that 100 g of kidney tissue is equivalent to 100 mL in volume. I hope this information helps!
Best, Tobias

title: PK-Sim Error: only the data from partial virtual subjects are successfully obtained during population simulation
body_text:
Dear everyone,
I developed a whole-body PBPK/PD model in MoBi to predict the pharmacokinetic (PK) behavior of an ADC drug and the dynamic changes in tumor size under the drug's effect. It could well simulate the PK profiles and tumor growth inhibition profiles over a two-year period in MoBi. This model was then transferred to PK-Sim for population simulation with a sample size of 100; however, only approximately 30% of the virtual subjects yielded successful simulation outcomes. A similar issue also occurred when reducing the simulation period, such as shortening it to 6 weeks. Moreover, the simulation still failed to yield successful results when either certain parameters were allowed to take negative values or solver parameters (e.g., tolerance) were reduced. Despite multiple attempts, these efforts proved unsuccessful. I suspect this discrepancy might arise because tumor size is a time-dependent dynamic parameter, and other physiological variables associated with tumor size could be adjusted to unreasonable values during the simulation. Currently, I lack clear solutions to address this phenomenon. Can someone help me to solve the problems? Thanks in advance.
Kind regards!
Fan

comments.body_text:
Find an individual from your population that is not running.
Extract this individual as a new building block (s. Extracting Individuals)

This is only possible if you created your population in PK-Sim and did not, for example, load it from a CSV file.


Save the extracted individual as a PKML file.
Switch to MoBi and load the saved individual

This is possible with MoBi versions 12.0 and higher.


Recreate your simulation in MoBi using the loaded individual.
Find the approximate maximal simulation time at which the simulation is still running.
Check for "suspicious" behavior of any kind (e.g., organ sizes that become zero, negative, or tend to infinity). This may provide insight into what is going wrong with the "problematic" individuals.

title: Accelerating Simulation Running
body_text:
Dear all,
I would like to inquire if there are any methods to accelerate the running of simulations (individual/population)? If the model becomes particularly complex after customization, this can cause simulations to run extremely slowly or even fail to produce results.

comments.body_text:
Some options below.
Use reasonable solver tolerances
In MoBi: Calculate and apply the Scale Divisor in a simulation

After this: Relax tolerances (if scientifically acceptable)

Default relative (RelTol) and absolute (AbsTol) tolerances are often conservative.
Try:

Increase RelTol from e.g. 1e-5 → 1e-4
Increase AbsTol from e.g. 1e-10 → 1e-8 or 1e-7 (or even more)


Compare key outputs (e.g. plasma concentration, AUC, Cmax) between original vs relaxed settings.
If there is negligible difference, keep the looser tolerances.

Simplify the model structure where possible


Reduce number of state variables


Remove compartments or species that are not needed for the simulation objective.
For example, if you have an irreversible metabolization reaction where the reaction rate does not depend on metabolite concentration, and you are not interested in the amount, concentration, or fraction of your metabolite:
replace the Drug ➡ Metabolite reaction with a Drug ➡ ∅ reaction (meaning the reaction has no products), and then completely eliminate the metabolite from your system.
(of course also assuming that the metabolite is not used elsewhere in your system, e.g. as an educt of further reactions etc.)


Replace some dynamic states with algebraic relationships if they are quasi-steady or can be approximated.




Simplify reaction network

Combine sequential steps into a single effective process when mechanistic detail is not needed for the question at hand.
Replace extremely fast reversible reactions with an equilibrium expression



Review piecewise and non-smooth functions

if–else, max, min, and discontinuous functions cause step size reduction.
Where possible:

Replace sharp switches with smooth transitions (e.g. sigmoid functions).
Minimize nested piecewise expressions.





Reorganize complicated formulas

Check the WIKI Writing efficient formulas for details.



3. Optimize events and discontinuities
Events are costly because the solver must constantly check for event conditions.


Reduce number of events

Combine multiple events that trigger at the same time into a single event if possible.
Avoid events that fire very frequently (e.g. at every small time interval). Consider replacing with continuous control variables.



Simplify event conditions

Use simple comparisons on slowly varying variables for event triggers.
Avoid complicated boolean expressions or conditions based on rapidly oscillating signals.



Avoid unnecessary event-generated algebra

If an event only adjusts a parameter by a small amount repeatedly, check if this can be modeled as a continuous change instead.



(Not yet possible): Compile a model as C++ code and evaluate it from R.
This will probably be available in version 14 of the OSP Suite.

title: Batch-Updating Clearance Parameters and Running PK Simulations in OSPSuite R
body_text:
Hello everyone,
I performed parameter identification in PK-Sim followed by a Monte-Carlo simulation and obtained 30 sets of clearance parameters, all with similar total error values.
Each parameter set contains the target enzyme intrinsic clearance CLspecific,enz and the remaining total hepatic clearance CLspecific,liv. Their differences are small, but I need to insert each parameter set into the model to simulate AUC and Cmax in order to compare which set is more appropriate.
My question is:
Is it possible in OSPSuite R to automatically loop through these 30 parameter sets, update the model parameters (CLspecific,enz and CLspecific,liv), run the simulations, and calculate the PK metrics (AUC, Cmax) in one batch? (I tried but couldn't find the correct path)
Thank you in advance!
Regards,
Jane

answer.body_text:
Thank you very much for your detailed explanation — it was extremely helpful.
The small details you mentioned were exactly what I was missing. I finally managed to get the method working!
Really appreciate your help!

comments.body_text:
It is possible in several ways. One option:

Save your simulation of interest as pkml file
Create a csv file with 3 columns like below and 1 row per clearance values (Example: PopCL.csv)

Column 1: IndividualId with values 0, 1, 2, 3, ... N-1 (where N is the number of your clearance values)
Column 2: Path of the enzymatic specific clearance and all values in the internal unit! (1/min for the specific clearance).
Column 3: Path of the Total hepatic specific clearance and all values in the internal units! (1/min for the specific clearance).






IndividualId
MyCompound-CYP3A4-xyz|Specific clearance
MyCompound-Total Hepatic   Clearance-Tot|Specific clearance




0
0.10
0.07


1
0.11
0.07


2
0.08
0.05


3
0.12
0.11


...
...
...



If you are not sure about the paths: load the simulation in R and look for all Specific clearance parameters:
sim <- loadSimulation("mySimulation.pkml")
allParams <- getAllParametersMatching("**|*Specific clearance*", sim)
print(allParams)


Now you can evaluate all clearance combinations in parallel via running a population simulation

library(ospsuite)

# Adjust with your data
simulationFile <- "MySimulation.pkml"
populationFile <- "PopCL.csv"
outputPath <- "Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|MyCompound|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"

# Load simulation
sim <- loadSimulation(simulationFile)

# Load CL data as "population"
popCL <- loadPopulation(populationFile)

# Set up parallel execution
simRunOptions <- SimulationRunOptions$new()
simRunOptions$numberOfCores <- parallel::detectCores() -1  # Use multiple cores
simRunOptions$showProgress <- TRUE

# Add output of interest (if not yet added)
clearOutputs(sim)
addOutputs(getQuantity(outputPath, sim), sim)

# Run population simulation in parallel
simulationResults <- runSimulations(
  simulations = sim,
  population = popCL,
  simulationRunOptions = simRunOptions
)[[1]]

# Calculate PK parameters for all individuals
pkAnalysis <- calculatePKAnalyses(results = simulationResults)

# Get pk parameters of interest
AUC = pkAnalysis$pKParameterFor(quantityPath = outputPath, pkParameter = "AUC_tEnd")
Cmax = pkAnalysis$pKParameterFor(quantityPath = outputPath, pkParameter = "C_max")

# Create results dataframe
results <- data.frame(
  AUC = AUC$values,
  AUC_unit = AUC$unit,
  Cmax = Cmax$values,
  Cmax_unit = Cmax$unit
)

print(results)

🔽 🔽 🔽 🔽 🔽
> print(results)
        AUC   AUC_unit     Cmax Cmax_unit
1  337.8278 µmol*min/l 6.036497    µmol/l
2  332.3074 µmol*min/l 6.036314    µmol/l
3  352.5096 µmol*min/l 6.036951    µmol/l
4  322.3159 µmol*min/l 6.035961    µmol/l
5  358.9559 µmol*min/l 6.037135    µmol/l
...

Additional Resources

Loading a simulation and accessing entities: https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/load-get.html
PK Analysis: https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/pk-analysis.html
Population Simulations: https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/create-run-population.html
Efficient Calculations: https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/efficient-calculations.html
runSimulations Reference: https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/reference/runSimulations.html

Thank you very much for your detailed explanation — it was extremely helpful.
The small details you mentioned were exactly what I was missing. I finally managed to get the method working!
Really appreciate your help!

title: Adjusting Tissue Water Fractions in Preterm Neonates (Rodgers & Rowland) — Which Fractions Should Be Reduced?
body_text:
Hello OSP community,
I am currently customizing tissue-specific water fractions for preterm neonates in MoBi using the Rodgers & Rowland distribution model.
Specifically, I am increasing Vf (extracellular water) and Vf (intracellular water) in several highly perfused organs (brain, heart, kidney, liver, lung, muscle).
unfortunately, the literature reveals only the Total Body Water percentage in preterm neonates but does not inform about the amount of fraction of other tissue compositions.
Since the total fraction of all components within an organ must not exceed 1, I assume that any increase in water fractions must be compensated by reducing another fraction. However, it is unclear which component(s) should be decreased.
The fractions for acidic phospholipids, neutral lipid, and neutral phospholipid are extremely small in neonates, so adjusting them does not meaningfully offset the water increase.
Is it correct that the additional water should be subtracted from another larger tissue composition, and if so, which one? Fat? Protein? Something else?
At the same time, the R&R distribution model does not include an explicit protein fraction in MoBi, which makes it difficult to determine whether protein should be reduced, and if so, by how much.
I would appreciate any guidance, references, or best practices from others who have modeled neonatal tissues.
Thank you!

title: Low plasma concentration after intramuscular administration in MoBi
body_text:
Good evening,
I have a PBPK model of a drug that was developed and validated for the intravenous route in dogs. I now need to extend this model to the intramuscular route. To do this, I exported the model to MoBi, saved the “muscle” compartment in PKML format, and reloaded it as a new container, assigning it a considerably smaller volume. The goal was to represent a specific muscle where the intramuscular injection occurs.
However, after performing the intramuscular administration, the simulated plasma concentration was much lower than the values observed in clinical data in dogs. I would like to know what might be causing this discrepancy and how I can correct this issue in MoBi.

title: How to build the intramuscular route
body_text:
Hello, I am currently trying to simulate the intramuscular (IM) route in dogs using PK-Sim, but I have encountered some difficulties in constructing the model. To address this, I created an advanced administration protocol, setting the target organ as muscle and the target compartment as interstitial. However, the resulting plasma concentrations were much lower than clinical data, even when I defined extremely short release times to mimic fast-absorbing formulations. It is worth noting that the model fits intravenous data very well.
Upon analyzing the model behavior, I believe this issue occurs because PK-Sim considers the muscle as the entire muscular tissue of the body, rather than a specific injection site (e.g., quadriceps or gluteus). Consequently, the administered dose seems to be distributed across a very large compartment, which dilutes the local concentration and reduces the absorption gradient into the bloodstream, leading to underestimated plasma concentrations.
Given this, I would like to know if there is a more appropriate way to represent intramuscular administration in PK-Sim. Is there any official method or recommended practice to apply the dose into a local depot (i.e., a specific muscle) instead of distributing it throughout the entire muscle organ? Would it be possible to create a custom compartment representing the volume of a single muscle, adjust the target-organ fraction to simulate a localized injection, or perhaps use any feature related to regional administration such as local application or regional depot?
Additionally, I would like to know if there are any example projects, publications, or tutorials that demonstrate the construction of a realistic intramuscular route in PK-Sim — even for humans — that could serve as references and be adapted for canine models.
I am part of a research team working on the development of PBPK models for veterinary medicine, where the intramuscular route is highly relevant and widely used across nearly all animal species. Therefore, understanding the correct way to represent this route in PK-Sim is crucial for us to continue advancing toward physiologically realistic models for dogs and other species.
I would greatly appreciate any guidance or example projects that could help represent the intramuscular route as a local depot in PK-Sim.

title: Mobi error: Error soving ODE at time t=0.033333333: Either convergence test failures ocurred too many times during one internal time step, or wth |h|= hmin (CV_CON_FAILURE).
body_text: I developed a PBPK model of monoclonal antibodies for pregnant women. I can access the simulation interface without any errors, but when I click “Run,” the error shown in the image appears. I’d like to understand what’s causing this.

comments.body_text:
Do you start with the resolution 30 pts/min? If so, the simulation cannot reach the very first output point, which with high probability means:
Either one of the initial conditions is not finite (either +- Infinity or NaN) or one of the transport or reaction rates is not finite already at t=0.
To verify this, you can increase the resolution by factor 10 (300 pts/min).
If you get the same error message, just for t=0.00(3) - then indeed the initial model at t=0 is not valid.

Hi @Hikari1118, please note that the pregnancy model and building blocks distributed via the pregnancy-repository has not been built/validated for large molecules, as Yuri said. Hence, all parameters relevant to large molecules would need to be adjusted to reflect potential changes during pregnancy. The following publication might be helpful for developing PBPK models for large molecules: https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt.3031

title: QSPC2026 early bird registration open!
body_text:
Dear OSP Community,
We are more than excited to announce that Early-Bird Registration has opened for the upcoming Quantitative Systems Pharmacology conference (QSPC2026) to be organized in Leiden, The Netherlands between April 8 to April 10, 2026.
QSPC2026 is the premier quadrennial conference on mechanism-based PK/PD and QSP, which was last organized in 2022, and builds on a decades long history of mechanism-based pharmacology meetings known as the “Noordwijkerhout meeting”.
A preliminary plenary program is now published on our website. This program has been designed with input from our extensive scientific advisory board composed of leading experts in the field of QSP.
In addition to an exciting plenary program, QSPC will offer ample opportunities for scientific exchange and networking, through poster sessions, plenary flash talks from trainees, social activities, tutorials, point-counterpoint debates, software demonstrations by exhibitors, and satellite workshops.

title: How to Model Guinea Pig Ototoxicity using PK-Sim?
body_text:
Hello PK-Sim Team and Experts,
I am planning to develop a PBPK model to simulate drug-induced ototoxicity in guinea pigs. My goal is to model the pharmacokinetics and eventual exposure of a compound (e.g., an aminoglycoside antibiotic or a chemotherapeutic agent) specifically in the inner ear (e.g., cochlea).
I have reviewed the available species in PK-Sim and see that while rat and mouse are well-defined, the guinea pig (Cavia porcellus) is not a built-in option.
Could you please provide guidance on the best strategy to approach this?
Thank you for your time and support.

answer.body_text:
Interesting application, I can see two paths:

Use a rat model and use a higher weight, then add an organ structure in MoBi that would capture inner ear physiology (I would estimate a 3 month timeline for this)
Create an actual guinea pig species model (a bit more effort) and then add an organ structure in MoBi that would capture inner ear physiology (I would estimate a 6-9 month timeline for this)

title: How to seed the same baseline population for Healthy vs. CKD population in PK-Sim
body_text:
Dear QSP team,
I am currently using PK-Sim for a population simulation and would appreciate any advice you can give me.
I generated a population of 100 virtual individuals in PK-Sim, population parameter ranges, with (sex, age, weight, height, BMI), and I want to keep the exact same normal individuals population parameter ranges when simulating different CKD stages. In other words, I want to keep everything the same for (sex, age, weight, height, BMI) but change the CKD status [mild, moderate, severe kidney damage]. So, I will have 400 virtual individuals with the same sex, age, weight, height, and BMI but with different CKD statuses.
The goal is to “clone” the same baseline population across healthy, mild, moderate, and severe CKD, so I can compare results (e.g., Cmax) for each individual directly across healthy, mild, moderate, and severe CKD, without introducing randomness from regenerating new populations.
Is there a way to fix the random seed or reuse the same population file and then apply CKD-specific parameter changes while keeping demographics constant? If so, what is the recommended workflow to do this in PK-Sim or R, please?
Thanks in advance for your help!

comments.body_text: Not possible through the "default" app. @Yuri05 , any workarounds?

This is not directly possible in PK-Sim because you do not have access to the seed(s) of the random numbers' generator.
One workaround is to go via editing of PK-Sim snapshots (which are files in json format).
Create the first CKD population (for any CKD stage) and export the project to PK-Sim snapshot.
In the snapshot, you will find the Populations node with the previously created population.
Duplicate the population block N times and insert after the first population
(don't forget the commas between population blocks😉. Ideally, use a dedicated json editor like e.g. VSCode to avoid syntax errors).
For each copied population, change the population name (must be unique within the project) and modify the eGFR parameter range.
Then load the modified snapshot back in PK-Sim. This will create N populations with the same basis demography and different CKD ranges.

Please note: every parameter added under User defined variability has its own random seed!
Thus if you want to add such a parameter after importing a project from snapshot, you would need to add it to the first population, export the project to snapshot again, remove all populations except the one with the new distributed parameter and then replicate the population N times again.

title: PBPK Model Problem:  Extremely Fast Elimination
body_text:
Hello,
I am currently developing a PBPK model, and have run into a issue with the elimination phase.
As you can see from the attached plot, I have successfully modeled the absorption phase. The simulated Tmax and Cmax are in good agreement with observed clinical data from two different studies.
The Problem: The model significantly overpredicts the clearance, leading to an extremely short terminal half-life. The simulated concentration drops to near-zero by ~60 hours, while the clinical data clearly shows the drug persists in plasma for well over 250 hours.
I have optimized several important parameters such as intrinsic clearance (CLint), LogP, pKa, and solubility, but I am still unable to solve this problem. How should I approach this, and what are the subsequent steps for optimizing the model?

title: Why are there no building blocks in mobi?
body_text:
Dear everyone:
Hi! I am a beginner with PK SIM. I tried to recreate the PBPK-PD model of ciprofloxacin based on the published hands-on tutorial (paper title: "Applied Concepts in PBPK Modeling: How to Build a PBPK/PD Model"). But due to the version differences of MoBi, I can not find the parts described in the tutorial, such as "To also foresee the consideration of in vivo case and the ultimate PBPK/PD coupling, expand the spatial structure of ‘1000 mg p.o. OD’ in the ‘possible reference objects’ window. Select the concentration of
ciproflocacin (mass) in the lung plasma and rename the alias name of this variable by ‘C’. Note that this concentration is not
used for the calculation of N in the in vitro situation. Click ‘OK’(Figure 28)." I do not find the "possible reference objects" and the variable "C”.  And the "pksim5" files can not be opened by the current version of MoBi (12.1), which I am using. There are also no building blocks in the MoBi too.
Can someone help me to solve the problems?
Kind regards!
Johnny Zhou

answer.body_text:
Possible Referenced Objects
In your screenshot you would need to expand the right-most pane with the arrow highlighted

You will then see a context specific view showing properties of the selected parameter. So depending what parameter you select, you should see

Since this shows a parameter with an explicit formula, the 'possible referenced objects' is shown as highlighted
Building Blocks
Since MoBi V12, building blocks are shown within modules. in your screenshot click the highlighted button to show the modules, where you will find building blocks nested within.

pksim5 file
You can't open a pksim5 file in MoBi. How data is exchanged is through exporting simulations to a format that MoBi can import. I usually export to a file, but you can use either of the highlighted context menu items

Depending on the age of the pksim5 file, it may not be possible to directly upgrade to the newest format. If that's the case you'll need to do an intermediate upgrade as outlined here. For user in that thread it was simply not possible because the file was too old.
Hope this helps

When I go to the Observers tab and click Add Observers, the Create Observer Set window appears. However, there are no available templates to select.If I click the dropdown button or right-click on the empty row, nothing happens.

title: Integrating in vitro dissolution model into PBPM when using lognormal distribution
body_text:
Hi all, the workflow described here https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Oral-PBBM-Workflow/tree/main/In-Vitro-Dissolution-Model refers to step 3 of the old IVIVC workflow (https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/IVIVC-with-particle-dissolution-module-in-OSP/tree/master) to implement the fitted P-PSD into a formulation and administration block in pksim. However, what if you used the approach where r_mean and r_gsd, potentially with disintegration alpha and lambda, were fitted in the in vitro dissolution model? Can these estimated parameters be used somewhere in the last step of the IVIVC-PBBM workflow or should I revert to the 'bins' approach?
Thank you very much!

answer.body_text:
Dear Robin,
After optimizing the geometric mean (r_mean) and geometric standard deviation (r_gsd) of the particle radius, these values can be used to define a formulation in PK-Sim directly. This requires converting the r_gsd to a Coefficient of Variation (CV).

Disintegration parameters (alpha and lambda) are currently not implemented in PK-Sim. If the in vitro dissolution data is not adequately described by r_mean and r_gsd alone, it could be beneficial to revert to the 'bins' approach to better represent the data.
Best regards,
Paul

title: OSP Suite Version 12 released
body_text:
You can download the version here.
Release Notes for the Open Systems Pharmacology Software Suite 12
New features

New modularization concept in MoBi
Building blocks can be grouped into modules, which allows the definition of sub-models and ultimately provides a much more flexible way of modeling. See the documentation for a detailed description of the new concept.

There are 2 types of modules: PK-Sim Modules and Extension Modules.

When exporting a simulation from PK-Sim to MoBi, a PK-Sim module is created.
If this exported model should be extended/modified in MoBi, there are 2 options:


Modify an existing PK-Sim module. This will automatically convert it to an extension module.


Much better: Create new extension module(s) and place all extensions/modifications there. This has several advantages:

All modifications to the basic PK-Sim model are clearly visible and can be easily documented, etc.
All modifications can be applied to other models exported from PK-Sim ▶ no need for duplication, reduced risk of errors



See the documentation for a detailed explanation of how to combine modules into simulations.

Create Individuals and Expression Profiles directly in MoBi (or import from PK-Sim)


Individuals and expression profiles can be created in MoBi in the same way as in PK-Sim.



Alternatively, Individuals and Expression Profiles can be exported as PKML in PK-Sim and imported in MoBi.




Individuals and Expression Profiles can be combined with modules in simulations and provide even more modeling flexibility.

Example use case: Adding a tumor "organ" in MoBi 12


Export a simulation from PK-Sim to MoBi. This will create a PK-Sim module.



Open the spatial structure of the PK-Sim module, select a tissue organ (e.g. muscle) and export it to pkml, e.g. as "Muscle.pkml".



Optionally select individual and expression profile(s) to include in the exported organ.



Create a new "Tumor" extension module with the "Spatial Structure" and "Initial Conditions" building blocks.
 


In the "Tumor" extension module: open the spatial structure and load the top container from the previously saved file Muscle.pkml. Note that the neighborhoods between muscle and arterial/venous blood are also loaded.
  


Rename the "Muscle" container to "Tumor". Be sure to check the "Rename Related Entities" checkbox in the next dialog!.




Note that after renaming, the neighborhoods have been renamed accordingly.



Open the Initial Conditions building block of the Tumor module and click on "Extend".



Select molecules to be incorporated into the tumor.




Create a new simulation and select both modules (make sure that the PK-Sim module is on top!).



In the next step, optionally select Individual and Expression Profile(s).



The "Tumor" organ will now appear in the simulation.



Tumor drug concentrations, etc. can now be plotted in the simulation.




 
New "Changes" tab in MoBi simulations shows all values modified in the simulation

[Experimental!] Creating individuals and populations with HI (Hepatic Impairment)
The HI algorithm is based on the work of Edginton et al. 2008 and Johnson et al. 2010.
This feature has not yet been fully qualified and is therefore marked as experimental in the software.

Extension of formula references concepts
More flexible (generic) referencing of quantities (parameters, molecule amounts) with new path keywords.

Referencing quantities in neighborhoods:

Referencing quantities in the next/previous lumen segment (in "natural" order: stomach to rectum):


Referencing the lumen segment from "its" mucosa:

New keywords for the sum formulas:


In parent (Open-Systems-Pharmacology/MoBi#837)


In children (Open-Systems-Pharmacology/MoBi#1374)






New releases of OSP Platform qualification library and PBPK Models Library
As with every new OSP Suite release, ALL platform qualification reports and model evaluation reports have been recreated with the new version of the OSP Suite:

OSP Qualification Reports library
OSP-PBPK-Model-Library

New release of the ospsuite R package

Installation and new features: s. here
Documentation: s. here

New release of the tlf R package

Installation and new features: s. here
Documentation: s. here

Fixed issues and Improvements
PK-Sim

Clone of "Compare Results" Charts
Expression profile inheritance does not work
Organ volumes not distributed as per default in a pregnancy population

MoBi

Clone simulation
Copy-paste formulas
Event Assignment - allow entering changed path
Multiselect context menu for Parameter Identifications
Exporting PKML of a container exports also the neighborhoods of the container and all sub-containers
Create Neighborhood without the diagram
Comparison of passive transports: difference in "Create process rate" flag is not shown
Create process option when configuring a simulation
Remove parameter type Property
Project-wide chart settings
Support distinct copy and move operations when dropping nodes
Check formula assignments in events for circular references
Copy path of a parameter/container in the simulation
Do not automatically create the "Volume" parameter for physical containers

PK-Sim and MoBi

Exporting observed data to Excel/CSV ignores LLOQ
It is possible to create formulas with the same name as existing
Crash in cloned Parameter Identification after project save/load
Data importer: error when "Reload under the same settings"
Simulations that error while running under sensitivity analysis do not show any error message

title: Reduction in blood flow causing different trends in different species
body_text:
Hi,
I was using the BAY974620 model (from the model library), and looking at the profile in arbitrarily the skin at normal blood flow levels and at 10% levels. Then I created a healthy human model (45 year old European female) and repeated the two simulations on her.
The administration protocol in all cases was 1.25 mg/kg IV bolus.
I'm confused aas to why (a) the magnitude of concentration levels are so different despite the dosing being mg/kg; and more so (b) why the trend in the mouse is reduced ADC at lower perfusion but this is the opposite for the human.
See attached plots from MATLAB using the PK-Sim data.
Thanks and kind regards, Barry

answer.body_text:
Hi Barry,
I cannot reproduce your observation a). With a dose of 1.25 mg/kg, I get a maximum tissue concentration of about 1 mg/L in mouse which is the same order of magnitude as the concentration in humans.
Are you sure you are using the correct units for dosing and plotting the concentrations (the factor ~1000 might hint to a unit error…)?
Regarding the different trends after blood flow reduction:
Note that, if you alter the blood flow, you indirectly also change the lymph flow rate and the fluid recirculation flow rate.
In the model, the lymph flow rate is calculated as a certain fraction of the blood flow rate and the recirculation flow rate is calculated as a certain fraction of lymph flow rate (additionally, there is an empirical allometric scaling for the recirculation flow).
The net effect from both parameter changes together (lymph and recirculation flow rates) on interstitial concentrations is not easy to predict.
Reducing recirculation flow rates reduces interstitial concentrations.
The lymph low influences the extravasation rate but also the draining of drug from the interstitial space to the venous blood.
From these two mechanisms, decreasing just lymph flow can have opposing effects on the interstitial concentration: A) Reduced lymph flow -> reduced extravasation -> reduced interstitial concentrations but also B) reduced lymph flow -> reduced drainage by lymph -> increased interstitial concentrations. (cf. DOI 10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4 for a model description) It turns out that a 10-fold reduction of just lymph flow in skin leads in mouse to a net effect dominated by A), while  in humans (with scaled vascular surface areas and volumes) a 10-fold reduction in lymph flow leads to a net effect dominated by B). It seems that your observations are dominated by the change in lymph flow.
If you reduce the skin blood flow to model a certain pathological condition, make sure you also reflect possible changes in relative  lymph flow and recirculation flow (flow fractions might be different from the usual fractions) and possible changes in vascular properties.
Hope that helps.

title: Receptor occupancy for competitive binding
body_text:
Hi All,
With the current implementation in PK-Sim (v11.0), the calculated receptor occupancy in the default receptor occupancy observer does not take multiple compounds binding to the same receptor into account. The applied formula is "Protein + Complex > 0 ? Complex/(Protein + Complex) : 0", where Protein is the free receptor and Complex the complex with the molecule for which the occupancy is calculated. With competitive binding, this should be Complex/Total, where Total = Protein + Complex + complexes to competitive binders. I guess this is difficult to implement in such a generic way that it detects the total target even in MoBi where free naming and reaction modification occurs, but maybe it's more straightforward in PK-Sim? Otherwise a flag in case of competitive binding or renaming of the observer might be helpful.

comments.body_text:
Hi all,
I’d like to revive this discussion and provide a more detailed explanation.
The calculation of the occupancy is not accurate in the case of competitive binding. The default observer accounts only for one drug-target complex, while we have, e.g., two complexes affecting the target occupancy.  In other words, the default observer calculates occupancy using the formula:

while the occupancy should be calculated as the bound receptor divided by the total receptor amount:

where the denominator is the total receptor, including all the receptor states.
The default calculation of the target occupancy in the case with several competing ligands misses the contribution of receptors bound by other ligands (e.g., bound_by_ligandB). As a result, it only considers ligandA and the free receptor, leading to an incomplete representation of total receptor occupancy.

@pavel Balazki ***@***.***> yes something like that would work
I guess, Vanessa also suggested that. Alternatively, for the protein
binding processes, the complexes have automatically generated names, so
that would allow to also adapt the formula automatically in PK-Sim. If we
want it to be also appropriate for any new binding reaction that is added
in MoBi, its probably nicer to have the tag approach I guess? We would only
need to keep track of the complexes btw for the occupancy formula, not the
ligands.

Op do 11 sep 2025 om 11:26 schreef Pavel Balazki ***@***.***>:
…
 @wilbertdew <https://github.com/wilbertdew> @Tan-Zas
 <https://github.com/Tan-Zas> If you work out a concept how this could be
 implemented, we can do it.

 Spontaneous thoughts - use tags and sum formulas. Each complex of protein
 A could get a tag A-Complex, each ligand could get a tag A-Ligand. The
 same could apply to products of reactions, e.g. to calculate total fraction
 metabolized.

 Then use sum formula to calculate Total amount A-Lingads etc.

title: Enteric coated formulation
body_text:
Dear all,
I hope you are having a good time.
I would like to ask you if you have a workaround for an administration of an enteric coated formulation using a mechanistic approach.
The solubility at low pH is much higher than at pH 6, so the if the drug is released in the intestine, the amount dissolved will be much lower (~10% of the IR formulation).
I have attempted this without success

Tried with an advanced protocol setting the administration in the Small intestine mucosa, but it only accepts Table, Zero or First order and Weibull, not Particle Dissolution.
Tried using a plain oral administration and setting the stomach solubility at 0 but is dissolving there anyway.
Tried to set the stomach transit time at the lowest, but it seems that 1 min is enough to dissolve the formulation.

Have you got any suggestions to do this in PKSim or MoBi? I mean using the Particle dissolution method in the Duodenum.
Many thanks,
Ricardo

answer.body_text:
Dear Ricardo,
unfortunately, there is no simple solution for this, but the following workaround in MoBi actives particle dissolution once the tablet has entered the duodenum:


In your simulation, add a second oral administration that occurs at the same time than the enteric coated tablet. Set the dose of this administration to 0 mg and the water volume ingested with the dose to 0 mg/kg:

As formulation for this dummy administration you can use e.g. the table input with arbitrary values, but not Dissolved - you need a solid oral dosage form as a dummy to track the location of your administered formulations in the GI tract.


Send your simulation from PK-Sim to MoBi, navigate to the administration of the particle formulation in the Events building block and set the Number of particles fraction Stomach to 0 and the Number of particles fraction Duodenum to 1:



Now you need to change the start event triggering the dissolution of the particle formulation under ...|Application_StartEvent for each particle size bin. The default setting here triggers dissolution with the start time of the simulation, but you need to add a new formula that uses the location of the dummy tablet (i.e. the entering of the dummy tablet in the duodenum) as start event for the dissolution of the particle formulation:



You may optionally make some parameters plottable, e.g. to track the location of the dummy tablet and to visualize the number of particles fraction in the GI segments:



Update your simulation using the changed Events building block; you may also want to send back your simulation to PK-Sim now (optionally).


When simulating now, the Number of particles fraction Stomach should be constantly 0, while the Number of particles fraction Duodenum is 1 as long as the dissolution of the particle formulation is inactive. As soon as the dummy tablet enters the duodenum (i.e. the parameter Tablet_Location increases to 3), the dissolution of the particle formulation starts, as indicated by the change in the  Number of particles fraction {Small_Intestinal_Segments}:

A MoBi file where the suggested workflow is implemented is attached here:
EntericCoatedTablet.zip
Note however, that particle dissolution in this workaround is triggered by tablet location and not by the pH value in the lumen.
I hope this helps!
André

title: Prevent modification of "History" entries
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I was wondering if there is an option to prevent the "History" from being altered in any way? On a first look it seems as if disabling the "Clear History" button would be enough to achieve this? Am I correct or is there another option to remove/alter entries in the History? If I am correct, is there a way to disable the "Clear History" button by a configuration file or a start-up parameter?
Thanks in advance for your help,
Kai

comments.body_text:
There's no built-in way to alter the history other than to annotate it using comments and labels or clear it.
There isn't a configuration option to disable the button.
Even with such a feature, altering the history through the project file, outside OSPSuite, would still be possible.

title: Create a population simulation in PK-Sim using MoBi-modified building blocks
body_text:
Hello everyone,
I’m building a pediatric PBPK model in PK-Sim. I exported a population simulation to MoBi to edit ontogeny functions (e.g., GFR and hepatic clearance). Because edits in MoBi are applied per individual, I modified these functions for a single individual and then transferred the simulation back to PK-Sim.
Now I’d like to run a population simulation that uses these MoBi-modified building blocks. However, I can’t figure out how to separate the building blocks from the imported simulation and create a population with my altered individual and/or compound. Since I implemented time-varying functions for clearance parameters, simply changing fixed values in PK-Sim won’t reproduce the MoBi updates.
Question: How can I create a population simulation in PK-Sim that uses the building blocks I altered in MoBi? I changed tissue composition and the renal ontogeny function, so I can’t rely on PK-Sim’s default individuals.
Thanks in advance for your advice!
Best,
Habib

comments.body_text:
Save the PKML file containing the “individual” simulation from MOBI, then import it into PKSIM via “Import → Population Simulation”.
Built-in parameters can be directly set for variation; custom parameters require enabling “Can be varied in population” in MOBI.
Not sure if I've understood your question correctly, but I hope this helps!

title: Pint - Peff
body_text:
Dear all,
I have a question regarding Specific intestinal permeability order of magnitude.
After a small literature review of PK-sim published models, the order of magnitude of Pint is usually between 10-7 and 10-5 cm/s (broad range), which is 2-3 orders of magnitude different from human intestinal effective permeability (Peff).
I'm wondering if anyone knows from which parameter/scaling factor this could come from ?
I have identified the following ones as potential clue but not certain:

In the effective surface area definition in Mobi there is this "Effective surface area enhancement factor"


Is it something related to microvili amplification factor as in Eq (1) from the following publication or something else ? (https://doi.org/10.1002/jps.22726)

In the drug absorption rate definition from lumen to mucosa there are two factors "Pint_trans_lum_cell_factor" and "Pint_trans_cell_lum_factor", do you know for what purpose ?


I know there have been some discussions on this topic (such as the one below) but this is still not completely clear to me.
Extremely low Specific intestinal permeability values calculated with PK-Sim · Issue #23 · Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Forum
Thank you for your help.
Best regards,
Florian

title: P-gp is not affecting the bioavailability of the drug
body_text:
Hello,
I have developed a PBPK model for a weak basic drug in PKsim. The drug is a substrate for P-gp. When I add Pgp kinetics to the model. It dosen't affect the bioavailability after inclusion of P-gp. I tried different values of intestinal permeability but the bioavailability dosen't change at all. P-gp increases the drug excretion only. I implemented P-gp using the expression file for MDR1 and sat up the direction as efflux. Do you have any thoughts about this??? Thanks in advance

comments.body_text: Have you implemented the interaction of the drug with PgP? E.g. implemented a drug transport rate per unit of transporter? See e.g. https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#transport-and-excretion

title: Error when using relative expression in formula in Mobi
body_text:
Somehow I just cannot get the corrrect path for the local parameter 'relative expression'. Other local parameter (eg: initial concentration) with path start with '..|' worked, global parameters (eg: ontogeny factor) with no '..|' in their path worked.

Wondering how to get it fixed.

answer.body_text:
The Relative expression parameter is located in the Intracellular space only - check the "Container Criteria" tab of the parameter.
So in your path, you need ..|..|Intracellular|MOLECULE|Relative expression

comments.body_text:

title: Workshop at ACoP Colorado: Building Scalable PBPK-QSP Models - Modularization in MoBi for OSP Suite V12
body_text:
Don't miss the ESQlabs' hands-on workshop at ACoP in Colorado this year!
This session is a must-attend if you're looking to strengthen your skills using version 12 of the OSP Suite and explore the power of the new modularization concept.
𝐖𝐡𝐚𝐭 𝐲𝐨𝐮’𝐥𝐥 𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐧:
• Build a PBPK model for both large and small molecule payloads
• Modularization 1: Add a Target-Mediated Drug Disposition (TMDD) reaction network as a reaction extension module
• Modularization 2: Incorporate a tumor organ as a physiological extension module
• Modularization 3: Implement a pharmacodynamic effect module to simulate tumor growth inhibition
• Leverage modular workflows to streamline model management, reuse, and extension
Led by our colleagues Dr. Stephan Schaller, Pavel Balazki, and Dr. Wilbert de Witte, this interactive workshop will equip you with the practical skills to master modular modeling in MoBi.
Friday, 17 October

08.00 - 17.00
Red Rock 6 - Gaylord Rockies. Aurora, Colorado.

👉Register here and choose our workshop: https://www.edgereg.net/er/Registration/StepRegInfo.jsp?ActivityID=43485&StepNumber=1&v=YmIyNTU3NDQtNTRlNS00OTdjLWE2ZDgtOWJlYTlkZjEzNTU4

title: How to run PBPK model to simulate drug concentration of a real patient (using CLI)

answer.body_text:
You should use the ospsuite-r for this.

title: How is permeability obtained
body_text: I want to see how the permeability between cells and the intercellular matrix as well as that between the intercellular matrix and plasma is calculated, but I can't find specific formulas. Moreover, the permeability value between the interstitium and plasma in pksim is too high, at 100cm/min. I would like to take a look at how it was specifically obtained

answer.body_text:
You can see the equations if you hover over the respective parameter in PK-Sim (see screenshot below for the permeability cells/interstitial space). For long equations, it can be more convenient to export a simulation to a text file (right hand side-click at the simulation -> Export Simulation Structure to File) or to MoBi.

Regarding the 100 cm/min used for plasma/interstitial space for most organs in the model for small molecules: This is just a high value assuming fast exchange.  This is a good approximation for many standard drugs.  If the barrier between plasma/interstitial space is relevant in your case, you can use the “Model for proteins and large molecules” also for small molecules (combine the property “Is small molecule” at the basic physico-chemical properties of the molecule with the selection of the “Model for proteins and large molecules”). If you do this, a permeability of P/2 is assumed for the transcellular exchange (P=cellular permeability, crossing two cell membrane layers) plus the exchange via pores from the two-pore theory (see manual).

title: Sanity check for individual sizes in virtual populations
body_text:
Hi OSP community,
IIRC, there are some sanity checks in place when creating virtual individuals when building a population to avoid unrealistic values. However, when creating a virtual population (n=1000) to see the impact of OAT3 variation throughout an otherwise healthy population (based on the reported geomSD in Hanke et al., 2021), I saw a strange profile (the outlying one in the graph below) which prompted me to look at the individual responsible. This individual (# 306 in the attached population csv) has an incredible low kidney volume (0.0232144069295564 L compared to the average of 0.42 L) which of course leads to higher exposure for this renally excreted compound. However, I don't think it is realistic to have a healthy adult individual with a kidney volume of 23 mL. I assume this is a statistical artifact arising from simulating 1000 individuals with an assumed SD around the average kidney volume, but perhaps there should eb a built-in sanity check here as well?
OAT3 Population_forforum.csv

comments.body_text:
HI @RobinM92, thanks for sharing this is interesting! I agree that a kidney volume of 23 mL (equivalent to approx. 5 cherries) is unrealistic.
I recall a discussion a couple of years ago to define limits for organ weights and actually the PK-Sim database has columns for allowed min and max values, although they are often empty (or min_allowed is set to a very low value slightly above 0 to avoid having 0 as divisor). Maybe @Yuri05 can comment...

title: How to Customize the B:P in the Model?
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I’m running a pharmacokinetic model and running into trouble with the blood‑to‑plasma (B:P) ratios. The lipophilicity (Log P) and fraction unbound (fᵤ) values I pulled from the literature don’t reproduce the published B:P ratio when I plug them into the model’s built‑in prediction formula.
Where to override B:P?
I can’t find any field in the PK‑Sim GUI to enter a user‑defined B:P. Is the override only exposed in a specific module—like the “Specific Clearance” panel—or do I need to switch over to MoBi or use an R script to hack it in?
Impact on Rodgers & Rowland partitioning
Since the Rodgers & Rowland method also uses the B:P value when calculating tissue partition coefficients, will a manual override apply there as well, or does it only affect clearance computations?
Parameter bounds issue
If I try to force the model to match the literature B:P by tweaking Log P or fᵤ, I end up having to decrease one of them by an order of magnitude—well outside any plausible range reported experimentally.
Direct clearance‐formula edit or alternatives?
Is it possible (or advisable) to edit the B:P term directly? If not, are there other parameters—pKa, hematocrit, membrane binding coefficients, etc.—that I could adjust more subtly to bring modelled B:P into line without violating credible literature bounds?
Or maybe I don’t need to get hung up on this small issue.
Thank you in advance.

comments.body_text:
Hi Jane,
You can override B/P in this window.
I think the one that you are looking for is the one at the bottom.

Not completely sure that this will update the partition coeffiecients and clearance.
Let's wait for someone else with a deeper knowledge answers.

title: PBPK model for an endogenous biomarker
body_text:
Hi Folks,
I am trying to build a PBPK model for an endogenous metabolite used as biomarker. Since the molecule is endogenously synthesised I have its baseline levels from human subjects. What type of formulation and administration protocol I should use in order to simulate the PK profile. For your reference I am providing below the observed data profile.

Here I want to predict the plasma concentration at 0 h and since it is an endogenous metabolite it is not zero concentration. I tried using IV infusion but I am getting a profile indicated in red solid line.
I would be greatful if anyone can help me with suggestions.
Regards,
Gautam

comments.body_text:
Dear Gautam,
Just out of curiosity, is the endogenous biomarker you are modeling CP-1? If so, please be aware that we will be publishing an OSP PBPK model for it soon, and it will also be made available on GitHub. If not, please bear with us as we will be describing the model development in the publication.
Best,
Tobias

Dear Gautam,
For different endogenous biomarkers, model files are available in OSP model repositories, for example Creatinine, NMN and Coproporphyrin-I.
Endogenous synthesis has been modeled differently, either as specific process in MoBi (Creatinine, NMN) or as long-term zero order administration into the relevant synthesis organ (Coproporphyrin-I model). Please note that in the latter case the observed data have been shifted by several hours to allow the simulated plasma concentrations to achieve steady-state.
Best regards,
Denise

title: Is It Justifiable to Adjust Fu to Match B/P Ratios in PBPK Parameter Identification?
body_text:
Hello, everyone!
In my model, in order to match the B/P ratio with values reported in the literature, I had to reduce the fraction unbound (Fu) during parameter identification. However, this led to Fu values that are significantly lower than those reported in the literature, sometimes even below the physiologically reasonable lower limit.
Is it reasonable to sacrifice the accuracy of Fu in order to maintain consistency with the B/P ratio? Alternatively, would it be acceptable to ignore the influence of B/P in the calculation and instead directly optimize for specific clearance, especially if B/P has a minimal impact on the final PK predictions?

title: PK-Sim under Parallels
body_text:
I installed the latest ver 12 using the ARM version of Win11 on a Mac running Parallels Desktop.
The installation finished without an error but PK-Sim exits with an error during start-up. It can't find the OSPSuiteFuncParserNative.dll library even though the file is present in the PK-Sim folder.
Does anyone know how to solve this?

answer.body_text:
Suite is only supported on Intel based 64 bit Windows.
There are some native assemblies that are compiled for ARM but that is for R interfaces. You won't be able to start the apps that way.

title: Add deviation lines

My mistake here - in predicted vs. observed the x-axis is not time, but the value from the observation data repository. The error message probably mistakenly reports it as time because this is the only chart where x-axis is not time.
Do you have observations where this value 0.127000.... occurs in the value column more than once?

comments.body_text:
I am just using some sample projects, but I have no problem plotting deviation lines in predicted vs observed.
Can you find this time point that is duplicated in the observed data and see if the data was imported with 2 measurements for that time point. Right now we only handle observed data with single measurements per time point.
Usually you will get an error if you try to import data with that characteristic. If you find it, can you actually preview the observed data?
