title: Question regarding Gene Expression Database in version 12.0
body_text:
Hi,
My question is regarding the use of the gene expression database in version 12.0. I noticed that additional gene databases have been released with this version, but the validation appears to be incomplete. I was wondering whether we can still use these animal (e.g., rat) gene expression data for our modelling, or if we should continue to use the human gene expression database for all species until validation is complete.
Thank you in advance for your answers.
Kind regards,
Sajid

answer.body_text:
Hi @sash0155 ,
there might be a missunderstanding, the human expression data should never be used for species modeling, not the old and not the knew one. The data in the new expression database if perfectly fine. Its base is the bgee db with currate healthy expression data derived from primary tissue samples. In the previous expression data it is fine to relay on the PCR data, just make sure you filter for the appropriate setting you want to model (exlude cell line or cancer data).

As reply to:

I using human expression data for any other species then human is, as far as I know, not an accepted default in the field . If would not use the old and not the new expression DB across species. In additon to expression level changes, sequence differnce may also cause a different drug-enzyme kinetic.
It depends on the setting you are modeling. If you know for a fact that expression in particular tissue are altered in the disese state, you can apply those changes to the expression values in your model and evaluate the results. The previous expression db holds information for  Liver tumor and GI tumor form a particular subset of studies and tumor. I'm not sure if those resemple the setting you are interested in. I would rather do a literature review on the particular setting you are interested in and scale the expression data accordingly.

Best,
Henrik

comments.body_text:
Hi @sash0155,
thank you for your post.
The latest gene expression databases for human and the newly provided expression information for other species can be found here:
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Gene-Expression-Databases/releases'
The technical validation of the expression databases can be found here:
Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Gene-Expression-Databases#3
Further we are working on an update of the already published PBPK models and DDI libraries with the new expression databases:
#1929
Please consider that there are significant differences across species with respect to the gene variant (sequence differneces and sometimes no corresponding homolog) and the whole body expression profile.
I would strongly recommend avoiding the use of human expression information for other species.
Furthermore one should consider the unserlaying source of the expression information, the legacy database contains a conglomeration of expression sources (primary tissue, diseased tissue, cancer cells, cell lines) that needs to be filtered for the conditions that your particular modeling case is representing.
The above mentioned lastest expression data however only contains healthy primary tissue samples as source regardsless of the specie.
Hoping this helps,
Henrik

title: How to express metabolizing enzymes in PK-SIM for Vmax, Km data by HLM
body_text:
Hi,
I have to include saturation kinetics in my model rather than linear kinetics. So I got Vmax, and Km data by Human Liver Microsomes in vitro and do not have recombinant CYP-based data. Now, how should I express the HLM enzymes under 'Metabolizing enzymes' for metabolism?
Does mentioning HLM in the metabolizing enzyme work I tried but no enzymes were expressed in the individual and cannot see anything for the metabolism process during the simulation.

I know it is easier to express them if we have individual recombinant CYP data but no idea related to HLM. Moreover, how to express all CYP enzymes in the individual? Please explain how to do this. Thanks in advance

comments.body_text:
Hi,
you have to add the metabolizing enzymes to your individual first. Than you can connect them to the metabolizing processes of the compound. Please check the documentation and here could be a good start: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-expression-profile#the-workflow.
Best, Tobias

Hi and Sorry for slow answering. Now I see your point and you cannot define microsomal CL for pooled enzymes here. this place is only for single enzymes. If you can calculate an in vitro intrinsic clearance from your microsomal experiment, scale it to an hepatic intrinsic clearance. Than you can transform the  hepatic intrinsic clearance to PK-Sim specific clearance and input it directly as total hepatic clearance process e.g. here:

N.B. this is an example for rat. You might have to adapt the species. Hope this might help you.
Best, Tobias

Hi, Are you really shure you need a saturable process here? Normally fisrt order works in most cases. But if you want to input Km an Vmax that would be the way:
include some enzyme in your individual BB

call it at the compound BB
and choose a saturable process e.g.

In principle you should inform the process now by your enzyme concentration. But normally you don't have this info and especially for pooled enzymes. So just leave the PK-sim default values and fit Kcat to your data. That should give a reasonable description including the info for Vmax and enzyme expresse. The thinking behind this is described here:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22293118/
Keep in mind that this is now, if you do so, not a pure bottom up approach anymore, as you lack knowledge about expression of you pooled enzyme. But you can overcome this lack of knowledge by fitting to plasma date in a middle out fit like fashion.
Hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias
I also encountered this problem and would like to know your solution. Thank you.

title: Saturable plasma protein binding model
body_text:
Hello everyone,
We are working in a PBPK model for a drug which has saturable plasma protein binding kinetic. As strategy, we tried to model free plasma concentrations using unbound fraction (fu) equal to 1 (no real value) and free plasma clearance, and then calculate the total plasma concentrations. However, we have this question: Is fu used for something else besides estimate free plasma concentrations? We read it is used to estimate partition distributions. If the answer is "yes", what strategy do you recommend to model  a saturable plasma protein binding process?
Thank you,
Best regards,
Alejandra

answer.body_text:
Hi Alejandra,
I had a similar a similar case with a drug that had saturable plasma protein binding kinetics.
An accurate value of fraction unbound at each time point is very important as it significantly affects clearance and volume of distribution. In PKSim/Mobi suite you can do that very efficiently. The idea is to implement in PKsim/Mobi an equation that relates the fu as a dependent variable to the total concentration of your drug that will be the independent variable .
So in order to implement in your model the change of fraction unbound in relation to your drug's concentration you will have to :

Find in the literature the fraction unbound baseline (Fubase)  , i.e. the lowest fraction unbound noted for your compound and use it in your compound -> physicochemical parameters block
Identify in the literature or develop through regression an equation linking the fraction unbound (FU) to the total drug concentration.
Perform the regression and/or modifications to the equation from step 2 to retrieve an equation linking the FU/Fubase to the total drug concentration
Export your simulation in Mobi
In Mobi: Go to the building block ‘Spatial Structures’  -> Tree  -> MoleculeProperties and choose the folder parameters.
There add the total venous concentration  of your drug as a new parameter in the following way:
a)	Click ‘add parameter’ of type: Global, Dimension: Concentration (mass or molar depending on your equation) , Group: Mobi, Formula type: Formula (an explicit formula) and name it eg. Ctotal
b)	Then right click in the white space prepared for adding equation parameters and ‘add reference’.
c)	There include the total venous blood concentration using the path: Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|MOLECULE|Concentration
d)	If your equation was developed with the  total drug concentration in molar units you can continue to the next steps, if it was developed with the  total drug concentration in mass units then add also the include the molecular weight.
e)	Set the dimensions of this parameter in the table carefully to much the dimensions of your fu~Ctotal equation.

You might end up having something like this:


Add a new parameter of type: Global, Dimension: Dimensionless , Group: Mobi, Formula type: Formula (an explicit formula) and name it eg. Dynamic . Then add you total concentration as a parameter of the equation for the quantification of this parameter and then write the equation you obtained in step 3 above.
You might end up having something like this:

Please feel free to contact me if you need additional information.



Go to ‘Spatial Structures’  -> Tree  -> MoleculeProperties -> Parameters -> Fraction unbound (plasma) and multiply the parameter you have created on step 7 with the equation  already set there for the estimation of the Fraction unbound
Like this:



Update your simulation with the new version of your spatial structure and then run your simulation.


Hope that it helps,
Eleni
Please find an example of the 'spatial structure' after doing the process above here:
scalar_fu.zip

comments.body_text:
As strategy, we tried to model free plasma concentrations using unbound fraction (fu) equal to 1 (no real value) and free plasma clearance, and then calculate the total plasma concentrations

I am not sure I can follow what you have done, but to model concentration-dependent fu, you would need to go to MoBi and change the value of fu to a formula.
Fu is considered for the calculation of partition coefficients but this can remain as only the unbound fraction is subject to distribution.
In the past, we have modeled concentration-dependent fu (for a compound primarily bound to albumin) by explicitly modeling albumin as a compound and implementing an albumin binding process in MoBi.
hope this helps

I had a similar a similar case with a drug that had saturable plasma protein binding kinetics.

So in order to implement in your model the change of fraction unbound in relation to your drug's concentration you will have to :

Perform the regression and/or modifications to the equation from step 2 to retrieve an equation linking the FU/Fubase to the total drug concentration
Export your simulation in Mobi
In Mobi: Go to the building block ‘Spatial Structures’  -> Tree  -> MoleculeProperties and choose the folder parameters.
There add the total venous concentration  of your drug as a new parameter in the following way:
a)	Click ‘add parameter’ of type: Global, Dimension: Concentration (mass or molar depending on your equation) , Group: Mobi, Formula type: Formula (an explicit formula) and name it eg. Ctotal
b)	Then right click in the white space prepared for adding equation parameters and ‘add reference’.
c)	There include the total venous blood concentration using the path: Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|MOLECULE|Concentration
d)	If your equation was developed with the  total drug concentration in molar units you can continue to the next steps, if it was developed with the  total drug concentration in mass units then add also the include the molecular weight.
e)	Set the dimensions of this parameter in the table carefully to much the dimensions of your fu~Ctotal equation.

You might end up having something like this:


Add a new parameter of type: Global, Dimension: Dimensionless , Group: Mobi, Formula type: Formula (an explicit formula) and name it eg. Dynamic . Then add you total concentration as a parameter of the equation for the quantification of this parameter and then write the equation you obtained in step 3 above.
You might end up having something like this:

Please feel free to contact me if you need additional information.



Go to ‘Spatial Structures’  -> Tree  -> MoleculeProperties -> Parameters -> Fraction unbound (plasma) and multiply the parameter you have created on step 7 with the equation  already set there for the estimation of the Fraction unbound
Like this:



Update your simulation with the new version of your spatial structure and then run your simulation.


Hope that it helps,
Eleni
Please find an example of the 'spatial structure' after doing the process above here:
scalar_fu.zip

Hi Eleni,
Thank you very much for your step by step guide, it will useful to us since we could not change the fu as a dependent variable before that. We will read it and implement this strategy in PK-Sim/MoBi.

Hi Nina,

Thank you for reaching out.

Please find the answers below :

I noticed you use a function for Fu/Fubase. I was wondering why you would not do the regression immediately for Fu related to total concentration? Is there any reason why you also need to include the Fubase?

It is mandatory to set a reference fraction unbound value in the compound block that will be used when the concentration is 0 as a starting point.

The equation that is developed need to have as dependent variable the Fu corrected with the baseline (Fu/Fubaseline) as this equation is going to be used as a scalar to multiply with the baseline fraction unbound value within the block 'spatial structures' .

So in the spatial structure before including your scalar you have this pre-specified equation for fu :
1 / (1 + (BindingPartner = Albumin ? AlbuminOntgenyFactor : (BindingPartner = Agp ? AgpOntogenyFactor : 1) ) * (1 - fu_ref) / fu_ref) * PlasmaProteinScaleFactor

 which for normal adults equalts to fu reference value, i.e. the baseline value set in the compound block in pksim (it was expressed this way to allow the flexibility to scale in children or to pathological states) .

If you  develop your scalar equation as fu then it will turn out that the resulting fu, used in the simulation will be
Fu reference * Fu (the fu value yielded for your equation) , on the contrary if you develop and you scalar equation for fu/fu reference then when you implent it you will have Fu reference* Fu/Fu reference =Fu (the value of fraction unbound that you want to use for each concentration).


Could you please explain why you need the Ctotal?

You need to define as a new parameter the Concentration of your drug in the venous blood and then use this parameter as part of your equation that estimates the fu as a function of concentration.


What does this max(Ctotal; 0) function do?

It prevents the ODE solver to yield negative values for Ctotal (can happen for very low concentrations) that will not allow your simulations to run. It is a protection that says that Ctotal is allowed to range from 0 and above.



Is Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|MOLECULE|Concentration not already the total plasma concentration?

Yes it is but adding it as a parameter allows you to set the dimensions that you want, i..e mg/L or mM or umol/L etc and allows you to introduce protection as described before .

I hope that it helps.

Best regards,
Eleni

title: Considering Weibull release function when creating a user-defined administration
body_text:
Hi all,
We looked into the user-defined administration functionality and figured out, that only a limited number of formulation types can be considered (dissolved, table, zero or first order). Is there any reason why a Weibull function cannot be selected?

answer.body_text:
Is there any reason why a Weibull function cannot be selected?

Yes. It was not implemented :)
Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#1169
As a workaround: create your Weibull formulation and export it to Excel, then create a table formulation and import it from the exported Weibull formulation. Then you can combine it with the userdef administration.

title: OSPSuite R DataCombined objects with different units
body_text:
Hi all,
I'm trying to implement more of the OSPsuiteR functions into my PBPK post-processing workflows, and one issue that I encountered is when a simulation contains multiple units (e.g. plasma concentration in molar and fraction excreted to urine unitless) none of the DataCombined-based functions can really handle the object resulting from mydata$addSimulationResults(sim) and mydata$addDataSets(obs), even when using convertUnits (as the latter tries to convert all units to the same unit, which is not the desirec behavior in this case). Any pointers on what workflow to use to go from this combined simulation to some GoF plots?

comments.body_text: Right now, the plotting functions implemented in ospstuite do not support data sets with different dimensions within the same plot (which would require a second axis in most cases). This could change in the future, but right now you would have to write manual code to create such figures. As a workaround, you could specify different DataCombined for different dimensions (outputs) and e.g. create multi-pannel plots.

title: New containers not saving
body_text:
Hi this issue has happened me each time I've tried to create a new container. I created a 'mini skin' from the 'skin' container in Spatial Structures.

When I did this I Control S'd multiple times throughout the process, connected my container up to the others, linked the 'mini skin' to some formulas, set parameters etc, however when I looked at it this morning the 'mini skin' I created had disappeared. I saved my file before finishing up so I don't know why that happened, also the steps I created are in visible in History but all that work is lost and I'll have to redo it.

I'm wondering why the container didn't save and if there are extra steps to take to ensure it saves next time I do it?

comments.body_text:
Can you see the skin in the spatial structure tree? Check there too just in case there's a problem drawing it in the diagram even though it is still present.
To be sure it's saving, you could open another MoBi instance and open the same project file. You'll get a warning saying that it's read-only but you can view the saved project.
Can you give me the steps you used to create the container? Don't worry about steps for the editing, just the basics how the container was added. Was it in the diagram, or in the tree. Did you clone it, or create a new one etc. Be as specific as you can.

The skin is present in the spatial structure tree, however the Mini Skin is not.
These are the initial steps I followed to create the container:
Spatial Structure – Embed Mini Skin
Export Building Block/Spatial Structures/ European IBP/ Tree/ Organism/ Skin as skin.pkml.
Then go to Building Block/Spatial Structures/ European IBP / Tree/ Organism and right click – “Load Container” – load the skin.pkml file.
Select the container “Skin” and click okay (the other subcontainers will be also loaded as substructures) – change name to “Mini Skin”.
All the neighbourhoods also need to update to “Mini Skin” instead of “Skin”.
All the subcompartment tags (BloodCells, Endosome, Interstitial, Intracellular, Plasma) – delete “Skin” tag in Properties, replace with “Mini Skin”
*Endosome wasn’t present for small molecule
Note: Mini Skin will be modified to act as either scalp, hand, or foot skin as required. However, all these are still effectively skin.
Spatial Structure – Embed Mini Skin
Open Building Block/Spatial Structures/ European IBP / Parameters/ Diagram – move the new “Mini Skin” to bottom left. Expand and move the subcompartments apart until space and connections seen.
Spatial Structure – Connect Mini Skin
Create the following neighbourhoods to other organs:

Confirm all neighbourhoods (internal – created automatically, and external – just created above) are present in Building Block/Spatial Structures/ European IBP/ Parameters/ Tree:
Can you confirm what version are you using?
I am using version 11 update 3

ok, I was not able to reproduce, so I looked more closely at the commands in the history. This last one

Means that you have overwritten the changes in the template building block (the project building block) from the simulation building block.
The commit pushes the simulation building block to the project, the update is probably what you wanted so that you get the new container in the simulation.

title: Tissue transporters
body_text:
In PBPK models, the transport of substances from plasma to interstitial space is often treated as passive diffusion. However, we know that endothelial cells can express membrane transporters (e.g., SLCs, ABCs), especially in specialized barriers like the BBB or renal capillaries.
That said, most references and expression data focus on transporters in parenchymal cell membranes, not the endothelium. Is there a reason why transporter-mediated processes at the endothelial interface are often overlooked or simplified in models? Maybe there is a lack of information.
I’m wondering if I’m missing a source of information — or if transporter expression and function at the endothelial level are just under-characterized in general.
Thank you!

answer.body_text:
Dear @lauraolivo11 ,
Thank you for posting your question reagarding usage of active transporters and expression information.
In general, the distribution of a compound into organs is driven by passive processes based on the compounds properties and its interaction with tissue components (binding). For some compounds, in addition to passive processes, active transporters or carriers mediated processes influence the transition between two compartments. (for example blood plasma -> intracellular liver or intracellular liver -> bile)
Please be aware that the OSP suite does not explicitly consider membranes or endothelium as separete compartments:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-expression-profile
Thus, transport processes are always represented as transition between neighboring compartments (e.g. plasma / liver, plasma / kidnes etc)
Further, most expression information is derived from tissue homogenates rather than single cell or cell type specifc samples.
Comprehansive PBPK models usually do consider active transport processes between compartments. However, such mechanistic information regarding the explicit transporter and transporter variant, and in addition the corresponding compound-transporter specific kinetics (Vmax, Km) need to be known and are difficult to obtain. Furthermore, this detailed mechanistic information is usually available for human variants only.
You can find the latest gene expression information integrated into OSP suite for human and other pre-clinical species here: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Gene-Expression-Databases/releases/tag/v3.0.1
Hoping this helps.
Best,
Henrik

title: Approach to Using Arithmetic vs Geometric Mean in Observed Data for PBPK Model Validation
body_text:
I am currently developing a model using clinical data from a Single Ascending Dose (SAD) study, which includes individual subject data as well as arithmetic and geometric means. I would like guidance on which type of data should be used as the reference in the Observed Data block to validate the model against this particular scenario.
My objective is to use the model to predict food effect, and regulatory guidelines recommend using the geometric mean ratio for such assessments. Therefore, my initial thought was to use the geometric mean (GM) from the clinical study as the reference dataset. However, I encountered a limitation: at early absorption and late elimination phases, some subjects had zero concentrations, making it impossible to calculate the GM at those time points.
Could you please advise on the appropriate approach for selecting and using arithmetic versus geometric mean in this context?

title: How to input particle size distribution measurements measurements in PK-sim?
body_text:
Dear all,
I got these kind of particle measurements (laser difraction measurement with mass size calculation:  Mass percent (Volume distribution, density 1) from different compound milling processes:

However in PK-sim I can only input mean and SD (assuming normal distribution) or a geomean particle radius with a coefficient of variation (assuming log normal particle size distribution).
Any Idea how can I used the measured mean, 10 and 90 % measurements in PK-Sim?
Thanks for your Input!
Best, Tobias

answer.body_text:
Hi Tobias,
a few weeks ago I published a mechanistic IVIVC workflow using the particle dissolution model in OSP; you can find it here.
In this repository you'll also find dummy .json files for the formulation and administration building block in PK-Sim; in the formulation json, you can specify the particle size for each bin and the in the administration json the drug amount in each of these bins is defined. The R script helps you to generate json files tailored to your specific data.
I guess that will help you
Best,
André

comments.body_text:
As far as I understand you have 3 predefined particle sizes. From your distribution of particles and the total dose it should be possible to calculate the dose for each particle size (however I don't quite understand how to interprete your inputs, so I cannot say how)
Then you could proceed like below:

Create 3 monodisperse particles formulations, one for each radius
Create user defined administration protocol with 3 single administrations (calculate


3) Map the 3 formulations in the simulation

Hi Tobias,
a few weeks ago I published a mechanistic IVIVC workflow using the particle dissolution model in OSP; you can find it here.
In this repository you'll also find dummy .json files for the formulation and administration building block in PK-Sim; in the formulation json, you can specify the particle size for each bin and the in the administration json the drug amount in each of these bins is defined. The R script helps you to generate json files tailored to your specific data.
I guess that will help you
Best,
André

Dear both,
thank you for the quick reply. @juri I don't think it will work that way as I have particle distribution measurements, which I interpret as
mean X 10 = 10 % of the number of all particles are smaller or equal to a size of  0.56 µm
mean X 50 = 50 %/median of the number of all particles are smaller or equal to a size of  2.01 µm
mean X 90 = 90 %/median of the number of all particles are smaller or equal to a size of  5.66 µm
So I don't think I can translate that into 3 bins of particles.
I was wondering if that can be translated to a mean and SD of the distribution (assuming normal distribution). But it seems that the shape is not symmetrically enough to allow that and it could be an oversimplification.
@André, looking at your input data (particle_distribution_raw_data.csv, cumulative distribution for 21 particle sizes) you used in your example it seems to be also not directly comparable to the measured output I have. My feeling now is that I have to get back to the CMC-guys who did the measurements and ask them if they can create some usable output from their measurements. It does not seem to be easy to get an standard way how to use particle size measurements. However, your workflow is a great step forward.
Thanks you both very much!
Best, Tobias

@tobiasK2001 Well, you need to make somehow an assumption about your particles radius distribution. For example:

10% of the particles have the radius 0.56 / 2 µm
40 % (=50-10) of particles have the radius (0.56 + 2.01) / 2 µm
40 % (=90-50) of the particles have the radius (2.01 + 5.66) / 2 µm
10% of the particles have the radius 5.66 µm

Based on this you can define 4 monodisperse formulations, calculate the dose for each particle radius and proceed as suggested above.
Or you can refine the binning above by linear interpolation (thus introducing N bins for each Interval [0 .. 0.56], [0.56 .. 2.01], ... and setting their radius via linear interpolation between min and max)
Of course if you have a better knowledge about the particles radius distribution, you can incorporate it into your binning.

Dear both,
Thanks a lot for the explanation! I will check it out!
Best, Tobias

Dear both,
now I run the workflow and it works like a charm :-). Very cool stuff indeed. Thanks a lot for this.

Amit

Dear Amit (@amitvtaneja),
this is currently not available for the PSD formulation out-of-the-box in PK-Sim. Also fitting the particle sizes as described by @AndreDlm above to such a strongly delayed dissolution time will likely fail. You likely already know, but this can currently only be defined for the other formulations such as lint80 or Weibull. With some tools you can also easily characterize / fit the Weibull distribution based on a biorelevant dissolution test.
There might be options to delay the release of a PSD through adaptations of the PSD release model in MoBi.
Best,
Stephan

this is currently not available for the PSD formulation out-of-the-box in PK-Sim. Also fitting the particle sizes as described by @AndreDlm above to such a strongly delayed dissolution time will likely fail. You likely already know, but this can currently only be defined for the other formulations such as lint80 or Weibull. best regards
Amit

title: Adding user-defined variability on fraction vascular
body_text:
Hi
I'd like to add user-defined variability to fraction vascular. I've gone to Mobi and checked "can be varied in a population" for the fraction vascular in each organ and then transferred the simulation back to PK-Sim for a population simulation.
But now I can't remember what to do next so that the fraction vascular parameters actually show up to enable user-defined variability. Thanks in advance for the help.

comments.body_text:
The problem is that Fraction vascular is initially marked as read-only in PK-Sim.
When exporting to MoBi, this information is kept internally (but not accessible in the UI).
When sending back to PK-Sim, parameter becomes read-only again and cannot be distributed in population simulation.
As a workaround: delete the parameter Fraction vascular in MoBi, then recreate it and check Can be varied in population.
Now when sending the simulation to PK-Sim, parameter can be distributed in a pop simulation

title: Clarification on Enzyme Activity Variability Implementation in PK-Sim
body_text:
Hello!
I'm trying to better understand how inter-individual variability in enzyme activity and expression is implemented in PK-Sim. I've come across some publications, including the one from Hanke et al. 2018 (https://ascpt.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1002/psp4.12343), that report a "Geometric standard deviation of reference concentration" for the protein/enzyme "Mean reference concentration". The authors state:

In addition to the variability in drug PK that results from these physiological differences within the virtual populations, the expression levels of the implemented drug metabolizing enzymes, transporters, and protein binding partners were varied around their reference values. If available, default population variabilities for enzyme expression in PK-Sim were used.  Otherwise, a log-normal distribution of the protein expression level in the population was assumed, and variabilities were implemented as geometric SDs derived from literature reports. If no valid source could be found, log-normal distributions with a moderate geometric SD of 1.4 (~35% coefficient of variation) were assumed.

Taking CYP3A4 as an example, the publication reports a reference concentration of 4.32 μmol protein/L, with the GeoSD of 1.18 for the liver and 1.46 for the intestine. This reference concentration matches the gene expression database from PK Sim. And the GeoSD corresponds to the geometric SD implemented in the activity of the Ontogeny equation from the PK Sim Ontogeny Database, which the authors reference as their source for these numbers.
This leads to a few questions I hope someone can help clarify:


Are the authors simply restating the default variability already present in PK-Sim (the GeoSD values from the ontogeny database)? That is, they are not explicitly adding additional variability. They are just relying on what is already built into PK-Sim?
As far as I understand, it is not possible to add individual variabilities for the reference concentration per organ; this is only possible for the ontogeny function, which in this case is available on PK-Sim for the liver and the intestines. Is that correct? If so, then the reported GeoSDs are implemented as part of the ontogeny-based activity calculation, and there is no separate inter-individual variability directly on the reference concentration in the simulation.


When generating a virtual population in PK-Sim, does the GeoSD from the ontogeny function automatically introduce variability in enzyme activity across individuals? In other words, even if the reference concentration is the same for all individuals, does the combination of ontogeny and its associated variability already produce a range of enzyme activities?


Assuming 2 is yes, would manually adding variability to the reference concentration, for example, using the GeoSD from the ontogeny database, lead to a duplication of variability?


Is there a way to inspect how the ontogeny function and variability are applied to each individual in MoBi? I have tried to find it, but I'm not used to MoBi, and I don't know exactly where to look.  I can view the ontogeny factor distribution when generating a population, but I’d like to trace how this impacts the final enzyme activity.


Finally, this brings me to my final question: Would it make sense to add additional variability to the reference concentration, especially considering enzymes affected by genotype differences, such as CYP2C19? I know the strategy of changing the reference concentration to reflect each phenotype, but would it be beneficial to add variability to allow for differences in the metabolic capacities within individuals of the same phenotype?


Any guidance on how to approach this, especially to ensure I’m modeling variability correctly and not redundantly, would be greatly appreciated.
Thank you,
Victoria

title: Hi, everyone, how to adjust the time interval of PK-SIM simulation results?
body_text: Hi, everyone, how to adjust the time interval of PK-SIM simulation results?

answer.body_text:
In the parameters section of a simulation you'll see 'Settings'

In there you can change the output schema to your liking. This one has only one frequency for the whole duration: 60 points per hour for 4 hours.

You can modify that, or if you have a need for different evaluation frequencies for different phases, add as many timespans as you need and adjust. More frequent evaluation means longer simulation time generally, so use high frequencies sparingly.

comments.body_text:

title: PK-Simbegineer
body_text:
Dear all,
I am a beginner in using PK-Sim and have encountered some difficulties. As shown in the figure below, the model does not provide a satisfactory fit to the observed data. Dose optimization was conducted using a 1 mg/kg dose, and the other doses were used for external validation. Unfortunately, after parameter optimization, the predicted Cmax was higher than the observed value, placing me in a difficult position once again. Therefore, I would like to ask whether you are aware of any commonly adjusted parameters during optimization, or if you have any suggestions for my next steps. The optimized parameter values are also provided below.
Here are the specific issues I am facing:
1.The plasma protein binding rate of this compound ranges from 79% to 90%. Is it reasonable to use the average value as the initial input for model development?
2. The optimized LogP value of eravacycline was 1.53, whereas values reported in the literature are −3.5 or 0.24. How should I interpret and justify a significant deviation in physicochemical parameters that arises from parameter identification? For instance, if the optimized value contradicts known chemical properties, is it acceptable to retain such a value for the sake of systemic predictive performance? Does this suggest that the model fitting process prioritized systemic accuracy over physicochemical plausibility, and how should such discrepancies be handled during model validation?
The details of the drug are as follows:
(1)Molecular weight: 558.563g/mol;
(2) Compound type/pKa: Acid/2.96, Base/9;
(3) Log P: -0.24 or -3.5;
(4) Fraction unbound: 10%-21%
(5) Metabolism：Eravacycline is metabolized primarily by CYP3A4- and FMO-mediated oxidation
(6)Route of elimination：Following a single intravenous dose of radiolabeled eravacycline 60 mg, approximately 34% of the dose is excreted in urine and 47% in feces as unchanged eravacycline (20% in urine and 17% in feces) and metabolites.
(7)17.82 L/min
Thank you for your attention
Looking forward to your reply.
PKSIM beginner - figure.docx

comments.body_text:
Hi,
looking quickly over your fit and qualification plots I think the look quite good. In case of IV administration the Cmax might not be the best PK-parameter to judge as it is super steep in early timepoint's after infusion and differences in reported sampling time and actual sampling time can lead to maximal errors. AUC is much more robust here. Overall the variation (SD) in data would be good to know for better judgment.
Regarding your other questions
1.)  average is a good starting point. How is your simulated fraction excreted dose in urine? does it mach to the 20 % reported?
2.) I think it is OK, check out the discussion here
#1949 (reply in thread)
Hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias

title: Subcutaneous administration in MoBi and export workflow to PK-Sim
body_text:
I'm currently working on a subcutaneous administration model in MoBi and would like to share my approach as well as ask a couple of questions regarding workflow and model reuse.
Based on the pregnancy model shared by Pavel Balazki here on GitHub, I created a new compartment in MoBi and included formulas for plasma volume and other physiological parameters relevant to subcutaneous administration.
At this point, I have two main questions:
Is it possible to export this newly created subcutaneous compartment from MoBi to PK-Sim? Or is it more appropriate to continue working directly in MoBi after making these structural modifications?
If I continue working in MoBi, will I need to recreate the subcutaneous compartment manually for each new simulation? Or is there a way to reuse or automate this setup across multiple MoBi projects?
Any suggestions, experiences, or resources would be greatly appreciated!
Best regards,

comments.body_text:
Is it possible to export this newly created subcutaneous compartment from MoBi to PK-Sim? Or is it more appropriate to continue working directly in MoBi after making these structural modifications?

You cannot re-export any structural changes from MoBi to PK-Sim, so you have to stay in MoBi.

If I continue working in MoBi, will I need to recreate the subcutaneous compartment manually for each new simulation? Or is there a way to reuse or automate this setup across multiple MoBi projects?

I am not sure I fully understand the question. Once integrated into the structure, the sc compartment and the administration will be available for all simulations created from these building blocks. Or, you could implement the sc compartment and the relevant reactions as an extension module and apply it to any model exported from PK-Sim or created in MoBi. If you want to learn more about the modularization concept introduced in version 12 of the OSP software, you can find some courses at the ESQlabs learning platfrom or visit one of the upcoming hands-on workshops (the next one will be at PAGE).

title: Parameter identification: how justify the values?
body_text:
I’m new to PBPK modeling and I’ve been exploring the compound database in PK-Sim. While reviewing some entries, I found that the vancomycin molecule has a reported logP value of approximately 2.2, with "parameter identification" listed as the source. However, vancomycin is known to be a highly hydrophilic compound, with literature logP values around -3.
My question is: How should I interpret and justify such a significant deviation in physicochemical parameters when they result from parameter identification? Specifically, in cases like this, how can I accept a fitted value that seems to contradict known chemical properties of the compound? Is this discrepancy a sign that model fitting prioritized systemic performance over physicochemical accuracy, and how should we handle that in model validation?

answer.body_text:
@lauraolivo11 , the current PBPK model is based on the published Vancomycin PBPK model as referenced in the evaluation report:
Radke 2017 Radke C, Horn D, Lanckohr C, Ellger B, Meyer M, Eissing T, Hempel G. Development of a Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modelling Approach to Predict the Pharmacokinetics of Vancomycin in Critically Ill Septic Patients. Clin Pharmacokinet. 2017 Jul;56(7):759-779. doi: 10.1007/s40262-016-0475-3.
Quoting from there:
During model development, the estimation of the lipophilicity input parameter logP, which substantially contributes to the rate and extent of passive drug distribution in the model, proved to be difficult. In the literature, vancomycin is described as a mainly hydrophilic substance. LogP values of −4.4 up to 1.1 could be found, supporting the hydrophilic to semi-polar characteristics of vancomycin (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40262-016-0475-3). However, the validity is questionable because these values are calculated based on chemical structure and experimental measurements are virtually not available. Moreover, logP is only a surrogate for membrane affinity and cannot reflect a physiological membrane in its whole complexity. To optimize PBPK model predictions, parameterisation of logP was conducted, resulting in a more lipophilic value of 2.45. This can be explained by readily back-to-back dimer formation and even multimer complex binding of vancomycin in solution (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40262-016-0475-3), resulting in a reduction of accessible hydrogen-binding partners and consequently a decreased hydrophilicity.
Hope this helps,

comments.body_text: @Incei Can you comment on this?

title: IV infusion stop event
body_text:
Hello!
I am trying to make a model which have a 30 minutes IV infusion. For this, I create the application in PK-Sim and then I import it to MoBi. When in events, I adjust the deposition site to central compartment , but I don't know why, my curve looks like a 15 minutes infusion:

answer.body_text:

title: esqlabsR sensitivity analysis
body_text:
Hi all.
I'm having problems with sensitivity analysis using esqlabsR, I can't figure out how to change the code to customize my legend to my own name, because the original legend name is the “path” of the parameter, which is too long!
The following is an example of plotting code:
library(esqlabsR)
library(ggplot2)
Load Simulation
.......
Outputpath target
OutputPaths <- Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|Mol|Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"
parameter
parameter <- data.frame(
path = "Mol|Lipophilicity",
name = "Lipophilicity",
stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)
Run
analysis <- sensitivityCalculation(sim,
OutputPaths,
parameterPaths = parameter$path,
pkParameters = c("C_max", "AUC_tEnd"),
variationRange = c(seq(0.5, 1, by = 0.125), seq(1.25, 2, by = 0.25)))
plot
myPlotConfiguration <- createEsqlabsPlotConfiguration()
p <- sensitivitySpiderPlot(analysis,
defaultPlotConfiguration = myPlotConfiguration)
How do I change the “Mol|Lipophilicity” legend to “Lipophilicity”, I've tried a number of options including using ggplot2 externally but it seems that the environment is locked and cannot be changed.

answer.body_text:
Hi @PavelBal,
Yes, I have solved this problem.
I found that when analyzing multiple “pkParameters”, for example adding “Cmax/AUC_tEnd/Tmax”.
The multiple subplots generated by SensitivitySpiderPlot() are a patchwork object and have a layout with multiple rows and columns, and the legend defaults to displaying the parameter that comes first in alphabetical order (usually AUC). If we want to modify the legend, we need to modify all the ggplot layers in each patchwork object, and then remove the duplicate displays, such as in the following code:
myPlotConfiguration <- createEsqlabsPlotConfiguration()
p <- sensitivitySpiderPlot(analysis,
defaultPlotConfiguration = myPlotConfiguration)
p_modified <- lapply(p, function(pw) {
plots <- patchwork:::as.list.patchwork(pw)
wrap_plots(
lapply(plots, (g) g +
scale_color_manual(
values = Color,
labels = parameter_info$name,
breaks = parameter_info$path
) +
guides(color = guide_legend(ncol = 9)) +
theme(),
ncol = 2
) +
plot_layout(guides = "collect") &
theme(legend.position = "bottom")
})
But I think this method is really inconvenient. It would be great if we could directly create a property in createEsqlabsPlotConfiguration(). For example, by adding a command like: "$legendLabels"？

comments.body_text:
Hi @SCZ1174, happy to hear that you are using esqlabsR! Were you able to solve your problem? If so, could you explain your solution here so other user could also benefit from it?
@rengelke I think we should pick up this user feedback and offer a way to adjust legend entries. Would you create an issue in the esqlabsR repo for this, please?

@SCZ1174 Thank you again for your input, we have implemented this feature (esqLABS/esqlabsR#811) and just released a new version of the package with it! https://github.com/esqLABS/esqlabsR/tree/v5.4.0

There are probably better ways to do this- but how I approach it:

title: Interstitial unbound concentrations and endothelial membrane
body_text:
Hi all,
I’m currently developing a PBPK model in PK-Sim for a class of compounds that includes both passively diffusing molecules and others with transporter-mediated distribution.
I have a question regarding the transcapillary transport (plasma ↔ interstitial space) and how it is represented in PK-Sim:
👉 How can I make reliable predictions of tissue concentrations if I only include passive endothelial permeability? Is this sufficient to capture the real physiological distribution of some compounds?
Interestingly, when I simulate unbound intracellular concentrations, the results match microdialysis data quite well — much better than the plasma ↔ interstitial predictions. This suggests that the plasma–interstitial barrier might be oversimplified, or that the interstitial-to-intracellular transport is somehow more physiologically accurate in the model.
So my questions are:
What is the mechanistic basis of the plasma ↔ interstitial transport in PK-Sim? Is it possible to assign different permeability values for the two directions (plasma → interstitium vs. interstitium → plasma), or to represent asymmetric fluxes?
Why might intracellular predictions be more consistent with in vivo data than the interstitial concentrations?
Would you recommend any best practices or workarounds to improve tissue distribution predictions in such cases?
Thanks in advance for your insights — I appreciate the help!
Best regards, Laura

comments.body_text:
Dear Laura,
yes that is an interesting question. With the rise of available microdialysis data it is interesting to investigate the representation of the plasma/interstitial/intracellular barriers deeper.
In general tissue concentrations are are governed by tissue distribution coefficients depending on your chosen distribution model

and passive cellular permeability.

Both are major impacted by lipophilicity.
You can assign different permeability values for the two directions (plasma → interstitium vs. interstitium → plasma) here:

and here

for each organ separately or for all at once. Be sure to select advanced mode at the bottom!
Additionally you can influence tissue distribution globale by selecting Calculation methods for Partition coefficient oand or celleular permeability And you can fit the Specific organ permeability in the compound BB:

So there are alot of possibilities to adjust...
Hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias

Thank you for the suggestions, Tobias!
I’m familiar with these options. However, what I’m still trying to understand more deeply is:
How can I estimate or calculate a physiologically reasonable value for endothelial permeability (P), or PxSA (permeability × surface area), in a data-driven way? Especially for tissues theres no interstitial concetrations data available
In other words, beyond using default or fitted values, what are the best practices to:
Estimate realistic passive permeability values (P) across the endothelium based on physico-chemical properties (e.g., MW, logP, charge)?
Account for tissue-specific endothelial heterogeneity, such as continuous vs. fenestrated vs. discontinuous capillaries?
Also, I’d like to clarify that when I previously referred to asymmetric transport, I meant directional differences across the endothelial membrane, i.e., the possibility that permeability or flux could differ between plasma → interstitium and interstitium → plasma, due to either transporter orientation or barrier characteristics.
In addition, I’m still a bit confused about how intracellular concentrations are calculated in PK-Sim. In my case, the simulated intracellular profiles match in vivo microdialysis data surprisingly well — even though the plasma ↔ interstitial predictions are not as convincing. I would appreciate more clarification on how the model handles interstitial ↔ intracellular transport, and why this part might behave more realistically than the transendothelial layer.
Any input, references, or suggestions on how to approach this would be greatly appreciated!
Regards, Laura

Hi Laura,
which model do you use, the small molecule model or the model for large molecules?
In the model for small molecules, there is effectively no barrier between plasma and interstitial space, i.e. the endothelial permeabilities are just very large for all organs except brain.
But if you are interested in the endothelial barrier, you could try the model for large molecules also for a "small" one (just keep the checkbox “is small molecule” at the compound properties checked). With that you have the endothelial permeability for diffusion across endothelial cells estimated from the general permeability across cell membranes (it is P/2 because you cross 2 membranes) and additionally the extravasation across pores from the two pore model.

Hi Laura, I am not sure I fully understand the questions but I am also curious of what cases this endothelial permeability might impact small molecules kinetics and how well PK-Sim assumption of an endothelial permeability of 100 is accurate. While it should represent very high permeability, since only unbound chemical permeate, for a chemical with Fu of 0.001, then the effective permeability =100 *0.001 is already much reduced and, in some cases, can become limiting. Of course, for tissues with discontinuous endothelium, this will possibly underpredict the Cmax in the tissue since, in fact, even the bound molecules can cross the endothelium.  This is especially relevant for the liver also because the permeability can actually limit the hepatic clearance. This is all theoretical, and we need systematic evaluation, but the kind of in vivo data needed to do so is not so easy to come by. So, I would be interested in what the outcome is for your molecules.

Hi Susana. I'm working with a large dataset of human microdialysis studies. The challenge I'm facing is that I'm not able to make good predictions of free drug concentrations in muscle and subcutaneous tissues.
I've applied the two-pore model again, and it worked really well for hydrophilic compounds that are mostly ionized at physiological pH — especially when optimizing the small pore permeability. However, for other compounds, although I can predict the peak tissue concentration (Cmax), I struggle to reproduce the rate at which the drug leaves the tissue over time and the curve shape. (Already tried all models of KP and cellular permeability)

Because of that, I started thinking the issue might be related to how endothelial permeability is represented in the vascular compartment. And just to clarify, this all refers to drugs that do not rely on membrane transporters.
I also ran sensitivity analyses in both PK-Sim and MoBi. Interestingly, the parameters that improve tissue predictions tend to disrupt the plasma concentrations.
This is my first project using PBPK modeling, so I'm still learning and exploring the software's features. If anyone has ideas or suggestions on what I could try, I'd really appreciate it!
Thanks in advance!

title: Workshop: PBPK modeling for First-in-human & DDI with OSP suite version 12 on June 2-3
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We are delighted to offer this 2-day workshop in connection to PAGE, complementing the ones offered by Esqlabs:
PBPK modeling for First-in-human & DDI with OSP suite version 12 on June 2-3.
This workshop will teach you how to use and leverage the Open Systems Pharmacology (OSP) suite version 12, for translational modeling to inform First-in-Human and Drug-Drug interactions (DDI) in the context of the ICH M12 and the recent ICH M15 guidelines.
Register via this link
The workshop covers:

Fundamentals of PK-Sim® and MoBi® version 12
Setting up PBPK models in different species (e.g., Rat, Dog, Monkey)
Using WB-PBPK models to establish in vitro-in vivo relationships
Running simulations to inform First-in-Human design
Retrieving reference compound files (victims & perpetrators) and enzyme/transporter framework
Handling non-standard DDI cases (e.g., multiple enzymes, transporters, endogenous DDI biomarkers) with MoBi®
Model verification, qualification, and applicability
Running simulations to inform DDI study design
Assessing model risk and providing recommendations for DDI labeling

We are so looking forward to meeting you and dedicating two full days to understand the potential of the OSP suite.
Workshop leaders are Tobias Kanacher & Johanna Eriksson and workshop host is Marylore Chenel.
The early bird offer is valid until April 7!
Read more about the workshop here.
Best,
Tobias Kanacher

title: PAGE Satellite Workshops
body_text:
Dear OSP Community,
If you are interested in learning more about PBPK modeling and the new features of Version 12 of OSPSuite, join ESQlabs for our Satellite Workshops at PAGE 2025.
Please check below:
Workshop Day 1: PBPK Modeling in OSP Suite V12 – Foundations and Special Populations
Physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling is a relevant approach to streamline drug development, providing a mechanistic framework to predict drug behaviour across different populations. With the latest developments in OSP Suite V12, PBPK modeling has become even more intuitive, efficient, and scalable. This hands-on workshop will guide participants through the basis of PBPK modeling using PK-Sim, with a special focus on paediatric, geriatric, and organ-impairment applications.
Know more: https://esqlabs.shop/products/workshop-page-2025?variant=51349749891403
Workshop Day 2: Building Scalable PBPK-QSP Models – Modularization in MoBi for OSP Suite V12
The latest OSP Suite V12 introduces a revolutionary modularization approach that significantly enhances the flexibility, scalability, and efficiency of quantitative systems pharmacology (QSP) modeling and integrations with physiologically-based PK (PBPK-QSP). This full-day hands-on workshop will equip participants with the skills to leverage modular workflows in MoBi to build, extend, and refine PBPK-QSP models like never before. Led by ESQlabs scientists, attendees will start with a simple two-compartment model to grasp the fundamentals of modularization before diving into a real-world case study: modeling an ADC modality for tumor treatment.
Know more: https://esqlabs.shop/products/workshop-day-2-page-2025
Workshop Day 3: Modular PBPK Modeling and Quality-Controlled Simulation Workflows in R with OSP Suite V12 – Use Case: Pregnancy PBPK
This full-day workshop offers a powerful combination of hands-on modular customized PBPK(-QSP) modeling and quality-controlled simulation workflows in R, leveraging the latest advancements in OSP Suite V12. Participants will explore the new modularization framework in PK-Sim and MoBi, learning how to extend a PBPK model with a pregnancy module to simulate fetal drug exposure. The second half of the workshop focuses on streamlining PBPK-QSP modeling workflows in R using {esqlabsR}, a package designed to enhance reproducibility, transparency, and automation.
Know more: https://esqlabs.shop/products/workshop-day-3-page-2025
Register until April 7 for Early Bird discounts!
Thank you, and Best wishes,
Monique

title: parameters from the ex vivo cotyledon perfusion model
body_text:
Hi,
I have completed the ex vivo cotyledon perfusion model of the drug, and I do not know how to obtain the two parameters Dpl and Kf,m from the results. Whether it's a model output or a calculation.
Thank you.
Best regards,
wanxiang

comments.body_text:
Hello @wanxiang227,
following the example published by Mian et al., Dpl and Kf,m are unknown model input parameters. Because they are unknown, they are fitted to observed data in the ex vivo cotyledon assay using the ex vivo model in MoBi.
After fitting the values of these parameters were used as input for a whole-body PBPK model. Specifically, the following parameters in the PBPK model were changed to the fitted values:
Neighborhoods|PlacentaFetal_int_PlacentaFetal_cell|Paracetamol|Partition coefficient (intracellular/plasma) --> 4.313041
Neighborhoods|PlacentaMaternal_pls_PlacentaFetal_cell|Paracetamol|Partition coefficient (intracellular/plasma) --> 4.313041
Organism|Permeability factor cell-F_pls-M --> 0.050733
Organism|Permeability factor pls-M_cell-F --> 0.059578
These parameters of the PBPK model might not be visible in PK-Sim, so you need to change them in MoBi.
If you do not amend the differential equation of placental transfer in the PBPK model as suggested by Mian et al., the latter two parameters (Permeability factors) won't exist in the PBPK model. In that case, the default organ permeability (applied to all organs, under path: MOLECULE|Permeability) will also be used for the placenta.
To use a different (user-defined) permeability, you need to change the ordinary differential equation for placental transfer in the passive transports building block:

To do so, you first need to add placental permeability as a new parameter to you model, e.g., in the compound building block, and then use this placental permeability in the differential equation shown above, e.g., something like:
dN/dt = P_placenta*SA*C_pls*fu - P_placenta* SA* C_cell/K_cell_pls
with P_placenta referring to the newly added parameter (make sure to adjust the path accordingly!).
As you can see in the screenshot above, SA refers to the surface area between PlacentaMaternal_pls and PlacentaFetal_cell which is the apical membrane of the trophoblasts and should be around 11800 cm² at term. If you only fit the permeability in your ex vivo model, you do not need to upscale it to the whole placenta, because - as shown in the equation above - it will be multiplied by SA.
However, if you fit the product of P*SA in your ex vivo MoBi model, you would need to upscale that to the whole placenta (e.g., as suggested by de Sousa Mendes et al.) and adjust the equation and parameterization in your PBPK model accordingly.
I hope this clarifies things a bit.
Best,
André

title: placental transfer
body_text:
Hi,
I know three different methods for informing the transplacental passive diffusion clearance (Dpl) and partition coefficient between the fetal and maternal compartment (Kf,m) of placental transfer, but I don't get the method to estimate the Dpl and Kf,m from the ex vivo cotyledon perfusion experiment. Can you give me some help calculating these two parameters?
Thanks for the help in advance.
Kind regards,
Zhihai

comments.body_text:
Dear @caozhihai,
to estimate these parameters you need to conduct a parameter identification using a compartmental model and observed data from the ex vivo cotyledon perfusion experiment.
Several models have been published in the literature (e.g., Mian et al.) and a MoBi model has been shared here. When using this model, you need to adjust the compound-specific parameter values (e.g., molecular weight, lipophilicity, pKa, fraction unbound etc.) and potentially also few system-specific parameters (e.g., volumes of the maternal and fetal reservoirs, flow rates used in the cotyledon perfusion experiment). Next, load in your observed data from the cotyledon perfusion experiment, set up a parameter identification and fit Dpl and Kf,m.
Be careful with non-identifiability issues/parameter correlations when fitting these two parameters together - you can evaluate that by checking the option Calculate Sensitivity in the Configuration tab of the parameter identification.
When integrating the fitted values in a PBPK model make sure to upscale Dpl from one cotyledon to the whole placenta, e.g., via the volume. Further information can be found in the article by Mian et al..
Best regards
André

title: I changed small intestinal transit time in Mobi. When I send sim to PK-sim for Popuplation simulation, it doesn't work.
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I changed small intestinal transit time in Mobi. When I send sim to PK-sim for Popuplation simulation, it doesn't work. I have confirmed that the value of small intestinal transit time in mobi or pk-sim has been changed as following pictures.


Could you please clarify where the problems occurred and suggest potential remedies? Thanks!

answer.body_text:
This happens because in the population simulation, the values for "Small intestinal transit time" are sampled from a distribution defined in PK-Sim data base, the sampled values can be viewed in the tab "Distribution" of the population:

PK-Sim does not allow to change the distribution (or set to a fixed value) of these "distributed" parameters @Yuri05  though I think it should be allowed, for such cases?
As a workaround, you can create a population in R (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/create-run-population.html), the set the value of the parameter Small Intestinal Transit Time in R using the method Population$setParameterValues (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/reference/Population.html#method-Population-setParameterValues), export the population to csv (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/reference/exportPopulationToCSV.html) and load this population in PK-Sim:

comments.body_text:
This happens because in the population simulation, the values for "Small intestinal transit time" are sampled from a distribution defined in PK-Sim data base, the sampled values can be viewed in the tab "Distribution" of the population:

PK-Sim does not allow to change the distribution (or set to a fixed value) of these "distributed" parameters @Yuri05  though I think it should be allowed, for such cases?
As a workaround, you can create a population in R (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/create-run-population.html), the set the value of the parameter Small Intestinal Transit Time in R using the method Population$setParameterValues (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/reference/Population.html#method-Population-setParameterValues), export the population to csv (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/reference/exportPopulationToCSV.html) and load this population in PK-Sim:

title: Evaluation of new gene expression database using OSP library models
body_text:
Dear OSP-Community,
The latest animal & human gene expression database based on Cordes et al. is currently not part of the regular releases of OSP-Suite. This database has been compiled for various species, which would e.g., allow scaling of active processes across different species.
We are working on a functional evaluation of this database focusing on the OSP model library. We aim to replace the expression data currently used in the models with expression data from the new database. We're interested in understanding what impact this will have on model performance, which will guide our next steps. In the long term, we're working toward enabling the selection of these new expression data in the OSP Suite.
We welcome any thoughts, ideas, or feedback you might have on this topic.
Best regards,
Denise

title: Interspecies scaling rat oral bioavailability and other problems
body_text:
I am working with an OTC product that has no in vivo IV PK. Therefore, I am using interspecies scaling to convert from rat IV to rat oral and then to human oral. There is a good in vivo rat PK study (IV and oral) of the parent compound.
Metabolism of the parent compound is primarily by hydrolysis followed by multiple other phase 1 and or phase 2 routes. Approximately 1/3 of the metabolism is in the small intestines and up to 2/3 in the large intestines. Hydrolysis and some other phase 1 metabolisms are by bacterial enzymes. I am only tracking the disappearance of the parent compound with a PBPK model for now. F is 0.48% for the rat oral.
I have developed an IV rat PBPK model that is a fair fit for the in vivo PK results. Vss and MRT are a little high. T1/2 and Tmax are good fits. However, I have been unable to fit the rat oral. I assume that a large first-pass effect is responsible for the low bioavailability. Adding an unidentified metabolic enzyme in the Compound (expression in Individual) as given in OSP GitHub forum discussion #1433 resulted in an error “Using hepatic plasma clearance in conjunction with metabolism process might lead to more clearance than” (the error stopped there) in the Process portion of the Simulation. I ran the simulation anyway and got the following error when hovering over some of the PK-Analysis parameters: “Absorption seems to be incomplete, or absorption process is not finished.” The error then went on to say to compare Vd, Vss, MRT, etc. to the IV. These values were significantly different from the IV. The bioavailability was decreased but not enough and increasing the specific clearance for the unidentified metabolic enzyme did not result in a change in bioavailability. The curve fit was bad. Another warning noted that I should simulate as long as the GI process, but my observed data was only obtained for six hours, and I read that I am only supposed to simulate as long as the observed data was collected. I also added biliary clearance which was no help. Using the default specific intestinal permeability resulted in F=0 so I used an in-silico value of Peff.
Any help would be greatly appreciated!
Thanks!

title: solubility gain per charge
body_text: Hello everyone, I would like to ask a question, that is, what does this mean in the title, and don't we have to consider this parameter when building the model?thanks

answer.body_text:
Dear @jiameng04,
The solubility gain per charge is a factor that determines by how much the solubility of a non-neutral compound changes when the pH of the medium is changed.
You can display the solubility-pH profile by clicking on Show Graph next to the solubility gain per charge:

Decreasing the solubility gain per charge generally reduces the effect of altered pH on the drug's solubility (e.g., if solubility gain per charge is 1, there is no effect of pH on the drug's solubility and its solubility is constant across the entire pH range), and increasing the solubility gain per charge enhances the effect.
If you have (aqueous) solubility data measured at different pH, you could use these data to fit the solubility gain per charge.
@Paul-Vrnkn is currently developing a tool for this that will be shared in a few weeks at the latest.

title: Non-linear protein binding in MoBi (follow up on #1838 discussion)
body_text:
Hi,
I am working on a project where I have to implement non-linear protein binding. I followed this discussion #1838 and tried to use the equation in a similar way but still there have been some issues.
So, I am not using 'if else' statement but rather using a formula from a literature where they defined the saturated protein binding with this formula: free fraction=f1C/(f2+C). I am using the same values of f1 and f2 from the literature (f2 being converted to uM as per MoBi concentration unit). While using the same formula, MoBi returns with an error but then I used 0.0001 + f1C/(f2+C) to avoid fu being 0 which worked and I got a nice curve as well. But when I plotted the fraction unbound parameter I noticed that at C = 0, the whole equation becomes 0.0001 and then fu increases over time as total plasma concentration increases which was ok but the final value of fu after implementing this formula is still 0.0001 at the end of simulation which in reality should be a higher number (not at Ct=0) .
I am not sure if this a right way to implement since any value added before f1 results in a same fu value at the end.
Any insights on how to correctly implement would really help and appreciated :)
Kind regards,
Sajid

First is all the proteins, whether enzymes or transporters, the relative expression is set to 0 at the default, it means the model doesn't use the protein even if we set the model as the substrate of enzyme and transporter. I think I am wrong at this point but I can't explain at this point.
Secondly, my interested protein is ABCG2, I want to increase the expression of this protein 2 folds in the kidney, duodenum and jejunum for example,  however since the relative expression is set to 0 at default, how can I change the expression of this protein? Can you suggest me this step?

comments.body_text:
Hi @dat994,
the OSP-Suite specific concept of using gene expression data is nicely outlined in the help:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-expression-profile
and in primary literature:
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22293118/
https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1002/psp4.12904
The concept builds up on two distanced information:

The reference concentration (how much protein in the compartment associated to the highest expression value e.g. 100%)
The expression pattern (relative amount compared the compartment with the highest expression)

Best,
Henrik

title: Run failed when constructing a pregnancy model
body_text: hello，The required parameters have been filled in, and the steps follow the tutorial. If run failed appears, what should I do

comments.body_text: @D7abc , which tutorial are you referring to?

Assuming that you have the default output resolution of 20 pts/h, this error most likely tells you that the numerical integrations fails already in the first step (i.e. at the simulation time of 3 min). To check this you could further increase the initial resolution, e.g. to 1000 pts/minute.
If you get the same error at t=1/1000 min, something is wrongly implemented in the model and I suspect that some variable or parameter, e.g. molecule concentration or amount, is <0 at t=0 min.
Without knowing the model it is quite difficult to pin down the error. To identify the erroneous parameter, you could try inactivating all new transport processes (i.e., transfer across the placenta and subsequent distribution to the fetal compartments) in the passive transports building block like this:

You need to untick the box for all new processes (PassiveDiffusionPlacentaBarrier, MassTransferPlacentaPl2VeinCordBlood, MassTransferPlacentaRBC2VeinCordBlood etc).
Then, tick again the box consecutively for the different processes (starting with placental transfer, thereafter the distribution from placenta to Venous Umbilical Cord Blood plasma/RBC, then the distribution to the Fetus, and finally to Arterial Cord Blood) and simulate each time you have activated a new transport process. This should help you to identify the process that throws this error.
Once you have identified it, you could have a look at all variables used in this process (e.g., fetal hematocrit, compartment volume etc) and check which of these parameters leads to a negative output (=simulated amount) of the process.

title: Binding of a small molecule to albumin AND alpha1-acid glycoprotein simultaneously
body_text:
Dear all,
How can I add a protein binding of a compound to albumin and alpha1-acid glycoprotein simultaneously in PK-sim?
Is it better to choose "unknown"?
I have KD values for both bindings:
to Albumin: Kd=45650nM
to AAG: Kd= 83nM

Thank you in advance for your help

comments.body_text:
You can add both proteins explicitly and define a binding reaction for each:

Add expression profile for Albumin
i. Set the reference concentration of albumin (in plasma) (if known)
ii. Set the relative expression in plasma = 1
iii. Optionally import albumin ontogeny (if known)



Repeat for AGP
Add both proteins to your individual or simulation



In your compound:
i. Set fraction unbound = 1 and binding partner = "Unknown"
ii. Add both proteins as binding partners and enter your Kd values




Make sure to map both proteins when creating a simulation

title: PK-Sim Error: TOO_MUCH_WORK
body_text:
Hi!
I received an error. Description of error as well as PBPK model is as follows:
Error=Error solving at ODE at time t=195. The solver took 100000 steps but could not reach the output time (TOO_MUCH_WORK).
PBPK model= It is a mouse model with default properties but changed EHC values in the physiology to account for EHC of the drug.
The drug has only two metabolites. Events of gallbladder emptying and meal(customized for mice) have been added. Administration is 5 mg/kg.
Please advise what is it that I am doing wrong. I am a beginner and am not sure how to exactly incorporate enterohepatic circulation for a drug in mice while also accounting for gallbladder emptying and a mouse customized meal.
IMPORTANT: I just re-ran the simulation after deleting the meal event and it completed which means that the problem is with meal. I am using roughly estimated meal values for a mouse and have attached a snapshot of my values. What should be done in this case?

Thanking in advance!

comments.body_text: The meal volume is 0.5 L - seems to be a bit too high for a mouse 😉
Hello, I've encountered the same error too. I'd like to ask if you have solved this problem now?

If you see this TOO_MUCH_WORK error it indicates that the system of ODE's created from the model are not solvable by the CVODES solver within the amount of steps defined by MXStep.

100000 steps is a lot of steps, so just check that it's not accidentally set to a small number. If you have the default, then it could help to adjust the tolerances.

title: Model evaluation criterion
body_text:
Dear community,
I have questions about the criterion based on which PK-sim evaluates a model when performing Parameter Optimization.
Actually I am trying to fit simultaneously concentrations for the primary molecule and its two metabolites.
However, it appears that PK-Sim only fitted observations for the parent molecule only. It did not work for my metabolites, for which the concentrations are very low.
I wonder how is the Total Error displayed in the Result Section calculated ? Is it absolute value, or relative compared to the observation ? Is it a non-weighted sum from all observation types ?
What should I do to improve my model fitting ?
Thank you so much for your help !

comments.body_text:
I wonder how is the Total Error displayed in the Result Section calculated ? Is it absolute value, or relative compared to the observation ? Is it a non-weighted sum from all observation types ?

Total error is the square root of squared weighted residuals, where each residual is either simulated-observed or log10(simulated)-log10(observed) (depending on the scaling settings - s. Mapping Simulation Outputs to Observed Data‌).
Weights (which are all = 1 as per default) can be set per observed data set and per observed data point as described in Mapping Simulation Outputs to Observed Data‌
For the small values below LLOQ residual calculation is done depending on the LLOQ handling settings as described in Handling of LLOQ values‌

What should I do to improve my model fitting ?


Try to modify (increase) the optimization weights for your metabolites. You can also modify the optimization weights within every output.




You could try fitting the parent compound first, then fix those parameters and fit the metabolite parameters separately. This can help if the parameters influencing the parent and metabolites are highly intertwined.


Check Model Structure: Ensure your model structure is appropriate for the system. Are the formation and elimination pathways of the metabolites correctly represented? Are there any missing pathways or compartments?


Try different initial values for your identification parameters via Multiple optimization


Experiment with different optimization algorithms and their settings
