title: Hello community, I would like to ask how to modify the volume of distribution of the human body？
body_text:
Just like the one in this article Vd(L),Physiologically‑based pharmacokinetic/ pharmacodynamic modeling of meropenem in critically ill
patients.(DOI:https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-64223-0)

comments.body_text:
Hi,
I am just a user of PK-Sim, but this is what I understand:
The volume of distribution is being calculated based on the selected method for calculating the partition coefficients (e.g. PK-Sim Standard, Poulin and Theil, etc.), see the following page for explanation of each method and significantly by the lipophilicity.
In order to optimize the Vd (i.e. select the best fitting method) you can go into your parameter identification run, select the configuration tab, from the Option menu select 'Calculation methods variation', select checkbox for 'All the same', select checkbox for 'Partition coefficients', and select the five methods. As a parameter, you can add the Lipophilicity to optimize.
After clicking on run, the model will try your parameters based on every calculation method and the lowest error in the results will be the best method with the optimized Vd.

title: Understanding transporter ODE and parameter correlation (Vmax/Km)
body_text:
I've been exploring a transport process in PK-Sim and this led me to a few questions about how transporters are modelled (specifically: Influx transporter with "Intrinsic active transport - Michaelis-Menten"):


Correlation Between Vmax and Km: After performing a parameter identification in which I wanted to estimate both Vmax and Km, the correlation matrix indicated a very high correlation (0.95) between those two parameters. I would have expected this rather to be close to -1, with the understanding that a correlation of 1 implies both parameters change the output in the same direction. Assuming we are in the linear range of Michaelis-Menten kinetics, we approximately have the rate Vmax/Km, so these two parameters should impact the output in opposite directions. Is there something I'm missing about this interpretation?


Transporter Concentration in Differential Equations: The ODE in MoBi for the transport reads:
TM * kcat * K_water_src * C_src / (Km + K_water_src * C_src)
(TM: transporter amount, C_src: molecule concentration)
Here, kcat and the transporter amount are multiplied. However, kcat is defined as Vmax/TransporterConcentration. This seems to suggest that the transporter concentration and amount effectively cancel each other out, leaving only a volume factor. However, this cannot be the case since the transporter reference concentration (and local concentration) still affect the result. Could someone help clarify this?


Looking forward to any insights or explanations from the community!

title: Why do some intracellular compartments accumulate more concentration than others? (for example brain vs kidney)
body_text:
Dear all,
I am working on mice PBPK model and I have noticed that some of the intracellular compartments (brain, skin, and fat) have accumulated concentration than the rest of the compartments (figure 1). If I simulate for a longer time period than I can see the concentration goes down but in case of brain, I simulated for a very long time which only result in a slight change in the concentration. I checked this behavior with other PK-Sim models as well.
I am not quite sure what is the work around here, the volume of brain interstitial concentration is smaller than intracellular concentration (7.77E-7 L vs 1.86E-4 L) which is because of the f_int and f_cell (0.004 vs 0.96). When I checked this equation
K_water_int * PA_int_cell * C_int - K_water_cell * PA_cell_int * C_cell; PA is the permeability surface area product and permeability is same for both compartments and the surface area is 1.19 cm2 (int <-> cell) although the SA is very small compared to all other organs but still if we see this equation, the PA for both compartments will be the same. Now, K_water_int and K_water_cell values are different (0.48 vs 0.04), I am thinking the change of the concentration between interstitial and intracellular compartments is because of this partition coefficient (figure 2) (though I am still in search of finding where these values comes from). Is this the right way of explaining this?
Since, I am working with the brain homogenate measurements where I need both interstitial and intracellular (assuming tissue); which parameters should I change here because the concentration declines in the measurements after some time. I tried to optimize the permeabilities and SA, which resulted in a sufficiently good profile although the interstitial permeability did not change significantly but SA and intracellular permeability values were high. The compound is small molecule and has a good lipophilicity, and enters the brain freely by passive diffusion.
Thank you for your answers.
Kind regards,
Sajid

title: Which Value Should Be Used for Lipophilicity: LogP or LogD, If LogMA is unavailable?
body_text:
Dear Researchers,
Most of the time, we find that the experimental logD7.4 value was reported and predicted to be a clog. Logically, the lipophilicity of a molecule could be represented by LogD7.4 if logMA is unavailable (as mentioned, the best descriptor for lipophilicity is the partition coefficient between lipid membranes and water, as determined at physiological pH). However, I am getting confused reading the tutorial given in the OSP documentation as it uses the terminology logP/ cLogP to be used if logMA is unavailable and states "A common observation is that membrane affinity is much less pH-dependent than e.g. logD".
I'd like to ask experts suggestions on Which Value Should Be Used for Lipophilicity: LogP or LogD, If LogMA is unavailable for a basic molecule with pKa around 8 and with no significant pH-dependent aqueous solubility.

Thank you.

title: Error in rclr..some variables became negative... Solver Tolerance ospsuite R
body_text:
I am consistently getting this error and having to reduce the solver tolerances. Sometimes it works, sometimes I still get the error, sometimes it works for one dosage administration and then I get the error when I run the simulation for a different dose.
Is there a more full proof way around this? I have looked through the discussions and haven't seen anything.
Thanks,
DS

answer.body_text:
What you could try:

load your pkml simulation in MoBi
open the MSV (Molecule Start Values) building block
search for the molecule paths which are reported in the error message in R
Check the "Neg. Values Allowed" checkbox

Update the MSV in your simulation from the MSV-building block

Save modified simulation to pkml

You should also plot all quantities where you allowed negative values - to check, if the negative values are tolerable (can be considered as numerical noise) or not.

title: Relative expression differences between v10 and older causing problems in MoBi
body_text:
Dear community,
I am trying to model a compound that binds to EGFR and undergoes TMDD. After including EGFR, based on the gene expression database, into PKSim (expressed interstitially, as there's no real tissue-specific apical cell membrane if I am not mistaken?) and adding binding parameters derived from in vitro, I exported the simulation to MoBi to create EGFR synthesis, degradation and internalisation reactions as is done for example in the ADC-tumor tutorial. However, to include the relative expression one should use the (relatively assigned) parameter 'Relative Expression Out.' (e.g. ..||EGFR|Relative expression out.) but this does not seem to be present. The only expression related parameter on that location is 'Relative expression', but this leads to problems with the paths and creates errors such as:

Looking closer, there seem to be several differences between the way protein expression is handled between v10 and v9 (as also stated in the release notes), so I was wondering if this could have caused this problem and what the 'new' way of handling relative expression in MoBi then would be?

title: Poor fit of absorption of a sustained-release preparation
body_text:
Dear community,
I am developing a PBPK model for a sustained-release formulation of a BCS-1 drug. In this model , I import the dissolution data of the formulation into the Formulation building block and validate this model using PK data from the same literature. However, the simulation results are not ideal (figure below).

I then tried to optimize the parameters for dissolution shape and dissolution time (50% dissolved), but with no success. It seems that parameter optimization is difficult to fit both peak concentration and time to peak concentration. So I would like to know how to adjust the model to get a good fit of absorption or are there other extended release tutorials I can refer to?
Many thanks.

comments.body_text: Depending on the drug you are modeling and the information you have from, e.g., in vitro assays, you might consider fitting the intestinal permeability as well. Be cautious to avoid non-identifiability issues. If you have in vitro dissolution data of your formulation in biorelevant media, you could use an in vitro model and fit a dissolution function to these data first, before fixing them to the fitted values in your PBPK model

title: How to predict values of ocular drug concentration after oral administration by PK-sim?
body_text:
Hi all
I’m learning to use PK-SIM. And I have a couple of questions.
I’d like to predict values of ocular drug concentration after oral administration by PK-sim. But I found that the default 'Organ' seems to have no eyes. Is there a place where eyes can be added? Alternatively, PK sim is currently unable to predict eye concentration after oral administration.
Looking forward to your reply very much. Thanks in advance.

Kind regards,
Lillian

comments.body_text:
Dear Lillian,
Currently, there is no compartment for the eyes in PK-Sim. To simulate ocular drug concentrations, there are two workarounds:

In PK-Sim, you could replace an existing organ that is irrelevant for your simulation (e.g., gonads) with the eye. Make sure to adjust all anatomical and physiological parameters accordingly so that they reflect the eye
You could export your model to MoBi and add a new organ for the eye. The advantage of doing this in MoBi would be more flexibility. For example, you are not limited to the 4 compartmental substructure of typical organs (blood cells, plasma, interstitial and intracellular) and you could define any number of compartments that are relevant for the eye. You can find further information here

I hope this helps.
Best,
André

title: Understanding and thinking about the differences in expression of PassiveDiffusionPl2Int and PassiveDiffusionInt2Cell formulas
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I found PassiveDiffusionPl2Int (Pl2Int) and PassiveDiffusionInt2Cell (Int2Cell) formulas expressed differently in mobi, and I've done a deep dive on them and generated some questions and ideas, I'm not sure if I'm on the right track.
PassiveDiffusionPl2Int = fu * P_endothelial * SA * (C_pls - C_int / K_int_pls)

PassiveDiffusionInt2Cell = K_water_int * PA_int_cell * C_int - K_water_cell * PA_cell_int * C_cell

Question 1: In Int2Cell, the reason for the separate representation of PA_int_cell and PA_cell_int takes into account the possible presence of transporter proteins in the cell membrane? But in Pl2Int, why is it the same P_endothelial * SA representation in both directions? Is there no transport proteins involved in the transvascular endothelium process from plasma to interstitial (although it is thought that most of  the transport proteins are on the cell membrane)? P_endothelial = 100 cm/min, implying instantaneous equilibration between plasma and interstitial space.
Question 2: Can PassiveDiffusionPl2Int be written in the following form? This way everyone can understand the difference between the two formulas.
PassiveDiffusionPl2Int = K_water_int * PA_endothelial * C_pls - K_water_int * PA_endothelial * C_int
The derivation process is as follows:

PassiveDiffusionPl2Int = fu * P_endothelial * SA * (C_pls - C_int / K_int_pls)
PassiveDiffusionPl2Int = fu / K_int_pls * P_endothelial * SA * C_pls - fu / K_int_pls * P_endothelial * SA * C_int
K_water_int = fu / K_int_pls
PA_endothelial = P_endothelial * SA


Question 3: Can you help me decipher what this formula means?
K_int_pls = (f_water_int+f_proteins_int_to_pls * (1/fu – f_water_pls)) * fu

comments.body_text:
Hi @SCZ1174, indeed maybe the passive diffusion equations could be written more homogenously but they are both describing the same
permeability x surface area x ( Unbound concentration Ext-Unbound concentration In).
The permeability in and out in this equation are the same because this  equation is describing passive permeability and not active transport. Active transport , if existent, it is described in a separate one for active transport, this is an example of how the equation look like for active transport from plasma to interstitial ( transporter in the endothelial layer


A  good reason why the active transport enters in a separate equation is because active transport rates are not usually multiplied by surface area and do not depend on the gradient of concentrations.  I am not entirely sure  why the permeability through endothelial layer is so high but for many organs ( e.g. liver) this endothelial layer is discontinuous or fenestrated so it should not be a large barrier for unbound molecules . This an exemption for some organs such as the brain [ref DOI:10.3390/ijms23031477]
. I am not sure how this differences in fenestration are accounted in the different tissues, maybe someone else knows..

About the last equation, this is to scale the fraction unbound in the interstitial base don the different of proteins between plasma and interstital fluid . The derivation of this equation is; We first want to know the partition just to interstitial:
Pint=f_water_int+f_protein_int * kprotein
We do not know kprotein directly but I can get it from the plasma fu that we input.
Pplasma=f_water_plasma+f_protein_plasma* kprotein
Pplasma=1/fu
1/fu=f_water_plasma+f_protein_plasma* kprotein <=>
<=>1/fu- f_water_plasma= f_protein_plasma* kprotein<=>
<=> (1/fu- f_water_plasma)/ f_protein_plasma=kprotein
Then if I replace kprotein the first equation:
Pint=f_water_int+f_protein_int * (1/fu- f_water_plasma)/ f_protein_plasma
We aggregate the fraction of protein in plasma and interstitial in the ration of protein in interstitial and plasma, f_protein_int_to_pls= f_protein_int/ f_protein_plasma
Then, Pint=f_water_int+ f_protein_int_to_pls * (1/fu- f_water_plasma)
Since we want to know Pint/Pplasma, then we get
Pint/Pplasma=f_water_int+ f_protein_int_to_pls * (1/fu- f_water_plasma)/Pplasma
Pint/Pplasma=f_water_int+ f_protein_int_to_pls * (1/fu- f_water_plasma)* fu
I did not confirm but I guess the f_protein_int_to_pls depends on if you are setting albumin or other protein binding partner

title: Observers in MoBi and PK-Sim
body_text:
Hi,
I have created a new compartment as an observer in MoBi since the default compartments in PK-Sim are plasma, interstitial, intracellular and tissue but I needed a total tissue and unbound tissue. So assuming that tissue compartment represents the total (interstitial and intracellular), I created the unbound tissue concentration compartment by just multiplying the unbound tissue fraction with the tissue concentration in MoBi. However, when I saved the observer and import it back in PK-Sim and configure it, I couldn't see the unbound tissue compartment in the "define settings and run". Is this normal or did I miss something here?
The other option which I tried is to save the simulation from MoBi with the new compartment and import it in PK-Sim and I found a new compartment in the "define settings and run" but with this I cannot configure it since it doesn't save the building blocks.
Thanks for the help in advance.
Kind regards,
Sajid

answer.body_text:
That's true - if you introduce new entities in MoBi (parameters, containers, whatsoever) - they are not exported with the observer.
At the moment, there is no possibility to pass such a parameter to PK-Sim.
You only could decouple your observer from the new parameter and use the parameter value (or formula) directly in the observer formula.

comments.body_text:
Did you specify where the Unbound Tissue observer should be created (e.g. compare the Tissue observer below)?
If yes: create a new simulation first and check if the new observer is created where expected. Once it looks good in MoBi, import the observer again in PK-Sim.

title: Partition coefficient (water/container) cannot be found
body_text:

title: Calculating blood flow rate.
body_text:
Hello everyone.
I have a question about calculating blood flow rate.
When I select "Species; human" , "Gender; Male", "Age; 30 years", "Weight; 61.87 kg" , "Height=168.99 cm" and "BMI; 21.67" in "Create individual", the blood flow rate at Liver is shown as 0.39 l/hr.
In general, the hepatic blood flow rate in Japanese people is approximately 1.6 L/min. Why is this difference?
Could someone please give me an answer?

comments.body_text: Blood flow to the liver is implemented as two separate routes - flow from the arterial blood, and the flow from the portal vein. The "Blood flow rate" parameter of the liver organ only reflects the arterial blood entering the liver. The total blood flow rate is the sum of this parameter and the blood flow rate of the portal vein (in your case, 1.1 l/min).

title: Fraction unbound (plasma, reference value) vs Fraction unbound (experiment)
body_text:
Dear community,
If I only consider the free fraction of the drug in the plasma, I wonder whether the values of fraction unbound (plasma, reference value) and fraction unbound (experiment) have to be the same？

Looking for your reply.
Thank you!!

comments.body_text: The Fraction unbound (experiment) is used for calculation of the specific renal clearance rate constant from renal clearance input value. So to correctly apply the measured or predicte renal clearance value to the model, the two Fraction unbound parameters should be equal. You should also consider this during parameter identification, if you are optimizing the fraction unbound.

title: nonlinear absorption model
body_text:
Hi everyone
I have a drug that has non-linear absorption, and the AUC increase is lower than the linear ratio when the dose is greater than 100mg. How can this be achieved in PK-Sim? If anyone can provide assistance, I would be extremely grateful!
Thanks！

answer.body_text: Hi Shixing-Ju , the non-linear absorption can be due to many reasons. Is the chemical in the solution or a specific formulation? Are the higher doses getting closer to the water solubility limit? If we exclude the solubility/dissolution, the remaining option is that you have a membrane transporter. The most pragmatic way is to add a transporter (lumen->enterocytes) and fit the Vmax and Km. The saturation of such a transporter could be what causes the non-linearity. You need to be careful to see if the transporter influx into the cells is not causing an abnormal accumulation in the enterocytes.. maybe compare the accumulation of chemical in enterocytes in the simple passive model and model with active transporter..

comments.body_text: Hi Shixing-Ju , the non-linear absorption can be due to many reasons. Is the chemical in the solution or a specific formulation? Are the higher doses getting closer to the water solubility limit? If we exclude the solubility/dissolution, the remaining option is that you have a membrane transporter. The most pragmatic way is to add a transporter (lumen->enterocytes) and fit the Vmax and Km. The saturation of such a transporter could be what causes the non-linearity. You need to be careful to see if the transporter influx into the cells is not causing an abnormal accumulation in the enterocytes.. maybe compare the accumulation of chemical in enterocytes in the simple passive model and model with active transporter..

title: Unable to understand the different PK profiles between published paper (Britz et al 2020) and my model while replicating
body_text:
Dear OSP community,
I am trying to use the example studies from published paper of Probenecid from Britz et al 2020 (https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11095-020-02964-z). I have used the same parameters as mentioned in the PK-Sim model of probenecid (same parameters after parameter identification in the model) but I got a different PK profile. I have checked all the tabs/fields to make sure I am not missing anything but still unable to find out the problem. I did my own parameter identification to fit the model but then as expected, my final parameter values are different especially kcat for UGT1A9 and OAT3. I have attached only one study from the paper (I have used other studies as well i.e, Selen 1982_1000mg_p.o._s.d._29 and Vree 1992_1000mg_p.o._s.d._36+37 to develop a model and to see if I was missing any details but the issue was persistent). I would really appreciate if I can get some help to understand the issue in case I am missing anything in the model.
Thanks in advance,
Sajid
The following example is from Emanuelsson 1987 500 mg po study in the probenecid model (1st and 3rd pictures are from original paper and 2nd and 4th are from a replicated model).
P.S. in the 4th picture, I don't know why Resolution didn't show up but I have used the same resolution (20 pts/h).

answer.body_text:
For your questions:

if you open a V9 in a V10+ Version, you will see a difference in the simulation only if (one of) the proteins is expressed in interstitial space.
2.The kcats from the model V9 are also fitted. So now you merely re-fit them to adjust for the change of how PK.Sim allocates expressed protein to the tissue subcompartments post V9 (so this change was only from V9 to V10, after that all remains the same). So you merely requalify the model for a new software version. Knowing the "true" kcat depends on if yu really know the accurate expression of the transporter.

comments.body_text:
Dear Sajid,
The model from Britz et al was developed in PK-sim Version 9.
You are likely working in PK-Sim Version 11. Please not that between Version 9 and 11 there has been an substantial change in modeling of Proteins expressions. See details here:
#945
To correct the change for Probenecid you might want to change the Kcat for OAT3 from 1966.57 to eg 1118.93 1/min. With that change you should be able to reproduce the profiles from Britz et al. The kacat for UGT1A9 cis not affected and can be left as it is.
Please let me know if it is working for you.
Best, Tobias

title: Intestinal permeability (transcellular) giving different PK profiles depending on input type
body_text:
Hello,
According to the help summary for the parameter "Intestinal permeability (transcellular)", the formula is 10^(m*log10(Specific intestinal permeability (transcellular))+b). I would like this value to be two orders of magnitude larger than my specific intestinal permeability, so initially I have been manually overriding the value in the corresponding field for my simulations. However, to perform pop simulations, I have decided to use the equation and began adjusting b to 2 to account for a similar higher intestinal permeability. I can visually confirm that the calculated value is correct, however my PK profiles are largely different, with the latter method resulting in significantly reduced absorption. I have looked over the other permeability values (eg. P (BC>PLS), etc.) and they do not seem to be affected by the manipulation. Can someone please shed light on this discrepancy? It seems that manually overriding the value directly is affecting more parameters than using the built-in equation, but it is unclear what these parameters are.

title: Predicting Kps
body_text: Hello, is there any information available on the differences between the PK-Sim standard vs. Rodgers Rowland calculations for partition coefficients?

comments.body_text:
This paper will give the best overview https://dmd.aspetjournals.org/content/48/10/903.abstract
General method to determine best fit between partition coefficient algorithms is to compare model performance for curve shape when lipophilicity is fitted against an IV profile.
From experience, try R-R first if dealing with a weak acid or weak base compound in humans.
If neutral compound or tightly plasma-protein bound, I may suggest that it will be more likely that PK-Sim standard will be a better fit for curve shape.

title: Unit conversions of Transporter`s Vmax
body_text:
Dear all.
I am a Japanese university student and a PK-Sim beginner.
I have obtained information on transporter Km and Vmax from the literature.
In the literature, the unit of Vmax was stated as XX nmol/min/10^6cells. The cells used are HEK and MEL.
The Vmax units for PK-Sim were XX mol/min/kg tissue, XX mol/l/min and XX nmol/min/pmol transporter.
How should I convert XX nmol/min/10^6cells?
We would be grateful for your answer.
Yours sincerely

answer.body_text:
Dear @StephanSchaller .
Thank you for your reply.
I have also read previous question. #1295
To find the number of cell/mL, the publication was presented.
It stated that 1 cm^3 = 10^9 cell number.
Does it then mean that the amount of transporters per volume should be known?
I am looking into this, but I have not found any publication yet.
I would like to know if you know of any references.
Yours sincerely.

comments.body_text: Hi @Rumi0708, A PBPK software doesn't have the info on numbers/counts or concentration of transport enzymes per transfected or 10^6 cells in specific HEK/MEL assays. So you would have to find this reference data (pmol transporter/10^6cells) in the publication (or find a general number from the literature) to translate your data.

title: optimized permeability are not getting committed to compound property
body_text:
The optimized permeability are not getting committed to compound property, as on each new simulation it needs manual feeding. However on cloning it's working. How to commit optimised/fitted permeability across organs to compound block that can be saved in templet compound for further use in plugging with another drug model for DDI evaluation?
Thank you in advance for your support.
Regards,
Bhim

answer.body_text:
OK, I see. This indeed is not possible at the moment.
The problem is, that organ permeabilities are defined on the simulation level only (because they depend both on compound and individual properties) and thus cannot be transferred back e.g. into the compound.
There is an ongoing discussion/feature request on this topic (Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#2035) and this extension will be probably implemented in PK-Sim 13.

comments.body_text:
In your compound, create a new alternative for permeability



Make sure to use this permeability alternative when creating/updating a simulation:



Update your optimized permeability value in used simulation(s) from the parameter identification via "Transfer to simulation" button


Use the "Commit to Building block" functionality to transfer the optimized permeability value back into the compound

Dear Yuri, Thank you for your quick guide. As you suggested, it works well to optimize specific intestinal permeability. However, my query is for the permeability optimized specifically for particular organs as shown in the picture.


After committing to building blocks, on adding new simulations, the permeability changes for the brain and fat from parameter identification doesn't update, It reflects again the same previous values.

The optimized values for permeability to the organ (here brain and fat) are not committed to building blocks, so in the new simulation, again, I need to add fitted values manually.
Need ideas to commit to this permeability value.
Thank you.

title: Parameter identification fixing multiple parameters as same value
body_text:
As I was reading the details for how PK-Sim has done its parameter identification, I came across this:
"To improve identifiability flymph,org for liver and spleen and fJiso,org for small and large intestine were assumed to have the same value."
By that, I assume that they were able to fix the fJiso,org for small and large intestine as the same value when doing the parameter identification. How would one do this, as I have read the document on the open systems pharmacology website (https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/shared-tools-and-example-workflows/parameter-identification) and still was not able to find exactly how to do so.

answer.body_text:
Hi, you can fix or identify the same parameter for different organs like this:
In the PI select the Parameter of interest in the organs you want (here it is solubility in the small intestinal compartments for example):

than press the upper Add button:

click the Fixed button:

you can now modify the parameter in all 6 sub compartment at the same time:
with the lower Add button you can also add organs to the Parameter

e.g. here for caecum intestinal solubility

hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias

comments.body_text:
Hi, you can fix or identify the same parameter for different organs like this:
In the PI select the Parameter of interest in the organs you want (here it is solubility in the small intestinal compartments for example):

than press the upper Add button:

click the Fixed button:

you can now modify the parameter in all 6 sub compartment at the same time:
with the lower Add button you can also add organs to the Parameter

e.g. here for caecum intestinal solubility

hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias
Please also see the documentation for details: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/shared-tools-and-example-workflows/parameter-identification#defining-identification-parameters

title: Does Evaluation of CYP1A2 Inhibitor & Inducer mediated DDI Simultaneously
body_text:
If an investigational drug is both an inducer and inhibitor of CYP1A2, for the combined effect, should we take the same substrate, such as Caffeine, for CYP1A2? As I see in the DDI network evaluation for CYP1A2, Caffeine is the substrate, and the model is validated with fluvoxamine (inhibitor). Separately for induction, the model is evaluated by taking Theophylline as substrate and Rifampin as inducer. In such a case, we can evaluate inhibition with caffeine and induction with Theophylline. However, how should we proceed if we have to evaluate both inhibition and induction? Can we take caffeine as the substrate for both inhibition and induction DDI?
Thank you in advance for resolving my query.

comments.body_text:
Hi,
I think you can take theophylline and caffeine as victim substrates for both effects: inhibition and induction. Please make sure to include both processes in your Simulation.
Best, Tobias

title: Microdialysis concentrations
body_text:
Hello!
I'm currently working to construct a model with literature human data and I found a study that has quantification of interstitial unbound concentrations in muscle and subcutaneous tissue in healthy volunteers using the microdialysis technique.
They found an AUC tissue unbound/AUC plasma unbound ratio of 0.5 after a single dose for both tissues and 0.66 and 0.75 after 3 doses (q12h) for muscle and subcutaneous tissue respectively (the half-life of the drug is 2.3 h, and it is administered through a 1-hour infusion as a prodrug that is rapidly converted in plasma to the drug).
In the concentration vs time profile, the initial concentrations are lower in the tissues than those in the plasma unbound, and then at the time of 4 hours, the concentrations in plasma and tissue become equal.
I have a question about trying to simulate those microdialysis concentrations in PK-Sim. Have anyone done?
Because I considered as interstitial unbound, but in PK-Sim these simulated concentrations are equal to that on plasma unbound, right?
Also, I thought about inserting some efflux transporter to explain those finds, but I started to wonder where these transporters are located in PK-Sim? They influence also the concentrations in the interstitial fluid or only in the tissue (meaning, only when entering the tissue cells that has an effect)?
Additionally, I thought about changing the partition coefficient, but, I was confused about the options that we have in PK-Sim: interstitial : plasma or intracellular : plasma, which one I could choose to alter in this case?

comments.body_text:
Dear Vichelfer,
I am not sure what you mean by this:

Because I considered as interstitial unbound, but in PK-Sim these simulated concentrations are equal to that on plasma unbound, right?
Is it for you compound the case? Obviously this is compound dependent. E.g. for Midazolam it can be different


But the difference is not big. Overall I am not sure how well the PK-Sim observer describes microdialysis data, or how comparable this data is at all. I used this data only once in one project and there it was highly dependent on experienta set up (e.g. the exact position of the needle, buffer flow) and we saw high interindividual variability (e.g. fat tissue content in subcutaneous).
Probably other may have more experience with this.
Best, Tobias

Dear @Vichelfer ,
as mentioned by @tobiasK2001 , this is compound-dependent.
What size is your molecule?
What you describe and observe depends on the plasma:interstitial exchange which depends on partitioning and permeability.
As you observe a lower difference in plasma:interstitial at a later point in time, partitioning is likely not much smaller than 1. But the time it takes to equilibrate indicates slow extravasation of the drug.
Thus you will need to reduce endothelial permeability:

This is reasonable for large molecules or molecules with low lipophilicity.
Get the values down to 1e-3 cm/min and you should certainly see some delay in interstitial PK.
Hope this helps,
Stephan

Thank you so much for giving your time to respond to me!
Dear @tobiasK2001, I'm sorry for not being clear! What I tried to say was that the PK-Sim consider the passive diffusion from plasma unbound to interstitial space, and then the transport to the intracellular space from the interstitial (that, in this case, can suffer the influence from the transporters). Like in this image from the article of Willmann et al (2003):

But now is much more clear for me, it is highly dependent on the molecule! Thank you! I'm still studying to understand more and more every day!
Thank you for sharing your experience! I agree that this type of data commonly presents high variability, but I think that the results can be reliable in this case (I'm hoping)!
Dear @StephanSchaller, thank you for this suggestion!
I forgot to mention that the molecule that I'm working on is a small one. I don't know what can be considered low lipophilicity?

I don't know what can be considered low lipophilicity?

Negative values or maybe values < 1 in the LogP.
What do you consider a "small" molecule? What is the molecular weight?
And what is the level of protein binding (what is the fraction unbound)?

Negative values or maybe values < 1 in the LogP.

I don't have experimental data of LogP but after optimizing I ended up having a value of 0.46. So I guess it could be considered low lipophilicity. Thank you!

What do you consider a "small" molecule? What is the molecular weight?

I consider as not being a protein! The molecular weight is 604.7 g/mol.

And what is the level of protein binding (what is the fraction unbound)?

The fraction unbound is 80%.

Hello,
I want to reopen the discussion regarding the prediction of unbound interstitial concentrations using microdialysis in human tissue. I am now working on a PBPK model to predict the unbound fraction in the interstitial compartment. The reference I am using is an article by Romain Garreau from 2022 (https://doi.org/10.1007/s40262-022-01168-5), in which they optimize the endothelial permeability for the tissue they have data available. However, I have some concerns about doing that based on optimization, and I am having trouble finding experimental data for diffusion speed in tissue. Based on the value of experimental Kp (for humans or rodents), can I input any formula for predicting endothelial permeability more reliably? Or can I just optimize the Kp for this?
Sorry for reopening this discussion after some years but we still have limited studies on this field.
Laura

title: Error with PK-Analysis using PKsim
body_text:
Hi all,
I am encountering an issue whenever I try to show the PK-analysis for a population simulation using PK-sim. The error message I receive is as follows:
Application:
PK-Sim® 11.2.142
Value cannot be null.
Parameter name: source
Stack trace:
at System.Linq.OrderedEnumerable`2..ctor(IEnumerable`1 source, Func`2 keySelector, IComparer`1 comparer, Boolean descending)
   at System.Linq.Enumerable.OrderBy[TSource,TKey](IEnumerable`1 source, Func`2 keySelector)
   at PKSim.Core.Mappers.ProtocolToSchemaItemsMapper.MapFrom(Protocol protocol)
   at PKSim.Presentation.Presenters.Simulations.GlobalPKAnalysisPresenter.<CanCalculateGlobalPK>b__33_0(Compound compound)

   at PKSim.Presentation.Presenters.Simulations.PopulationPKAnalysisPresenter.CalculatePKAnalyses(IPopulationDataCollector populationDataCollector, ChartData`2 timeProfileChartData, PopulationStatisticalAnalysis populationAnalysis)
   at PKSim.Presentation.Presenters.Charts.EditTimeProfileAnalysisChartPresenter.calculatePKAnalysis()
   at PKSim.Presentation.Presenters.Charts.EditTimeProfileAnalysisChartPresenter.RefreshData()
   at PKSim.Presentation.Presenters.Charts.EditPopulationAnalysisChartPresenter`3.UpdateAnalysisBasedOn(IPopulationDataCollector populationDataCollector)
   at PKSim.Presentation.Presenters.Charts.EditPopulationAnalysisChartPresenter`3.editPopulationAnalysis()
   at PKSim.Presentation.Presenters.Charts.EditPopulationAnalysisChartPresenter`3.<.ctor>b__9_0(Object o, EventArgs e)

Can someone guide me, why I get such an error and how to fix it?
Also I exported the PK-analysis to a CSV file and worked out but all values in molar, is there way to get it in mg/L?
Thanks in advance,
Aya

Can you please check if the error still occurs with PK-Sim 11.3?

title: Creating 'Observers' in MoBi
body_text:
Hi!
Sorry I am not at all versed with MoBi functionality. I tried to go through the on-line documentation of MoBi. However I am not able to practically implement that, as I feel a hands-on training would be a better way; else any on-line resource/Webinars/Video tutorials on creating 'Observers' in MoBi would also do.
I see many of the expert team members recommending the participants to create an observer in MoBi after exporting the project from PK-Sim. However there is no clarity with real-time examples/case studies, which involve very frequently asked questions from users for parameters, like amounts in container, combined fractions metabolized by multiple CYPs, and many more PK parameters.
Appreciate if someone could help.
It would be further helpful to have a user-friendly interface in PK-Sim or MoBi to create customized observers with some clicks, drag-and-drop, and with easy to create formulae. That would be a great help for users like me, who are not much into the kind of programming-like activity required for such tasks at present. If I am not wrong there would be many, who would be looking for such types of user-friendly tools, which would aid in more 'application driven' platforms.
While writing this I understand and appreciate the relentless efforts, which are going-on in maintaining and improvising the PK-Sim and MoBi platforms!!!
Sincerely.
Anant

comments.body_text:

Yes, I am building a simulation from scratch using version 11.3 Build 208.  I am new to PK-Sim and am trying to run a simulation using test data to make sure I understand how to use the software. It is interesting that you can't see my screenshots.  I sent those by replying to the email notifying me that I had a message in github.
Here's the first message (that was on a screenshot I sent) when I clicked on the down arrow in Observer:  "Unexpected exception has occurred in your application.  If you click continue, the application will ignore this error and attempt to continue..."  When I clicked continue, nothing happened.  There was a place to click on details. This gave me a long list of things.  I think the bottom line of the details was something about just-in-time (JIT) debugging, but I don't know how to work with that.  I cannot click "OK" and simply bypass Observer as OK is not activated.
How does one share a model file?
Thanks very much for your continued help!

I figured it out!  I looked at an earlier post and also one of the old videos and realized I wasn't seeing the "OK" button for formulation and for Observers. Fortunately, it came to me why I couldn't see them.  My eyesight isn't good and I had set the "scale" on my computer to higher than the recommended (125%) value.  The "OK" was off-screen and even though I have a touch-screen, I couldn't get it to come on screen (I tried that earlier to see if there was something I was missing).  Changing back to 125% brought "OK" back on screen, and then my formulation could be saved and I could put in "none" for observer and click ok.  The simulation worked.
A simple (dumb?) problem that I should have thought of earlier, but hopefully my blunder will help someone else in the future.
THANKS VERY MUCH for helping me!  It was a comfort having someone helping me.  Sorry for taking your time, but it did mean a lot to me.

Thanks for sharing this and happy to hear it's resolved!

title: How to input HLM CLint in PK-sim
body_text:
I want to add the HLM CLint (unit: uL/min/pmol) in my PK-sim.
In PK-sim when I click 'Total hepatic Clearance -> In vitro liver microsomes' I can't find any blank to put the HLM CLint value.
Can anyone help me for this? Or is there any other way to put this information in PKsim?

comments.body_text:
Hi,
if have a 1/2 from microsomal incubations you can use the input you show above.
If you have a already physiologically scaled Clint you have to go via the liver plasma CL like this: i.e in put as specific Clearance via dividing by bodyweight and liver volume ( the liver mass assuming density is 1). Here is an example calculation for a literature value of e.g. midazolam:

Hope this might help.
Best, Tobias

Hi,
The units of your clearance  uL/min/pmol seem to be form a recombinant enzyme assay since it has pmol.
If it is recombinant enzyme you need the option :

Usually microsomal clearance is in uL/min/mg protein.  Does your source report somehow what is the pmol referring to?

title: A request for assistance with the Mobi model simulation of the interactions between macromolecular drugs and small molecule drugs
body_text:
I used PK-Sim to simulate a physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) model for the interaction between a macromolecule and a small molecule. Then, I exported it to MoBi software to create a PBPK model for pregnant women. After adding the organs specific to pregnant women, imitating the organ compartments of a normal person, I planned to run the model simulation. However, it resulted in an error, and the simulation failed. Here is the table of error logs that I exported. If anyone has experience with this issue and could assist in resolving it, I would greatly appreciate it.
errors.csv

comments.body_text:
It is a lot of errors, so sth. went clearly wrong.
Did you follow an instruction to add the additional organs?
You can check the pregnancy training on our platform or the one on "Tumor treatment" (which showcases the adding one ONE organ in the large molecule model): learn.esqlabs.com

@Hikari1118 This seems to be a common mistake with adding new compartments: by default all the molecules are set to "is present" in the MSV building block, but especially all the molecules related to FcRn binding need to be unchecked in all new organs. Examples can be found in the trainings (especially "Antibody Therapy in Oncology") Stephan referred to.

title: Non-linear clearance
body_text:
Hello all,
I am building a model for anticancer drug which has a non-linear clearance with time as it's clearance increase after multiple dosing.
This non-linearity is of physiological origin (related more to the metastasis happens in the patients) and is not related to enzyme induction.
Anyone know how can I add that change to the clearance of the drug after several doses.
Note: I thought of adding a new drug to be introduced at a time point after the initial dosing with the original drug but don't know how to make this or if that possible.
Thanks,
O.Zaher.

comments.body_text: I have a similar problem.  How do I enter concentration dependant clearance for an IV dose?

title: PK-Sim AUC_tEnd calculating method
body_text: I wanted to compare AUCs for some of my models that I am building in MoBi vs. the base PK-Sim model. In order to do so as accurately as possible, I was wondering what method PK-Sim uses in its PK-Analysis to calculate the AUC_tEnd value for the model simulation curve, so that I can apply the same method to my simulation results in MoBi to determine the AUC, since MoBi does not have a built-in PK-Analysis (which I think would be a great addition, unless it already exists and I just happened to miss it completely).

comments.body_text:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-simulations#pk-analysis-view
An alternative for your problem is: save the mobi simulation as .pkml file, import it in PK-Sim, do the PK-Analysis there

title: How to find historical versions of the model?
body_text:
How to find historical versions of the model?
I just made a mistake during the operation and deleted a very important and complex structure of the model, and then accidentally pressed the save button. What can I do now to retrieve the lost data?

comments.body_text: You can try to "undo" your recent actions from the history. See this section of the documentation about the rollback function: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/shared-tools-and-example-workflows/history-manager-history-reporting

title: Is an observer needed?
body_text:
I'm just starting to learn PK-Sim.  I think I have the basics, but don't understand what an "observer" is.  It's not in the tutorial video which is clearly for an earlier version.  Can I simply eliminate the observer?  I can't get the simulation to run without an observer.  I am doing a simple single oral dose.
Thanks!

title: Question about the Intestinal permeability parameters & their role to intestinal metabolism
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I have a question regarding the intestinal permeability parameters. I understand that the specific intestinal permeability (transcellular) is calculated based on the lipophilicity and MWeff of the compound. However, there are two additional parameters in the advanced settings: mucosa P(intracellular -> interstitial) and mucosa P(interstitial -> intracellular).
Could someone explain the difference between these parameters? Specifically, how do these parameters relate to accounting for intestinal metabolism in the model?
Thanks in advance,
Aya

comments.body_text:
Dear Aya,
The specific intestinal permeability (transcellular) describes compound permeability from the gut lumen into the mucosa cells (apical). The parameters P(interstitial -> intracellular) und P(intracellular -> interstitial) describe the permeability of the compound between mucosa cells and interstitial space (basolateral). Both are calculated from organ permeability multiplied by a scale factor that takes into account the intestinal permeability (transcellular) applied in the model (e.g., manually entered) divided by the "default" intestinal permeability (transcellular) calculated from lipophilicity and MWeff. To my knowledge, the permeability parameters do not take intestinal metabolism directly into account.
You can find detailed information about the calculation of different permeabilites in the documentation: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#absorption
Best regards,
Denise

Dear Denise,
Thanks for your detailed explanation and the helpful documentation link.
From the documentation, I understood that if the specific intestinal permeability (transcellular) is modified in the simulation, the permeability between the intracellular and interstitial space within the mucosa (P (intracellular -> interstitial)) will also be automatically scaled by the same factor. This adjustment prevents a disproportion between the permeability of the apical and basolateral sides of the enterocytes, avoiding drug accumulation in the enterocytes.
To clarify my understanding: If I aim to increase the contact time or residence time between the drug and the intestinal enzymes (such as CYP3A4), which are localized intracellularly (in the apical part of the intestine), the approach should be:

Increase the intestinal permeability (transcellular) to facilitate the faster entry of the drug into the enterocytes.
And/OR decrease the mucosa P(intracellular -> interstitial) to slow down the absorption of the drug into blood stream.

Does this interpretation correctly align with the mechanism for optimising contact time with intestinal enzymes?
Thank you again for your assistance.
Kind regards,
Aya

Dear Aya,
For me this approach sounds plausible. Maybe someone else can comment who has experience in changing gut permeability with the aim to modify intestinal metabolism.
Best regards,
Denise

Dear Aya,
that is also my understanding. If parameters P(interstitial -> intracellular) and P(intracellular -> interstitial) are decreased drug absorption is slowed down and at the same time more drug is metabolized by the intestinal enzymes (e.g. CYP3A4) as the residual tim inside the cell and thus the availability for metabolism is increased. That leads to a higher fraction metabolised intestinal and a lower F.
Hope this might help you.
Best, Tobias

Dear @DeniTue  & @tobiasK2001,
Thanks a lot for the explanation and yes it is more clear now :)
Kind regards,
Aya

title: How to set up emulsion injection or injection suspension in PKSIM?
body_text: I found that the dosing regimen settings for special injections are not possible in PKSIM. i.e. in relation to injectable solution agents should we consider the process of their "release" in the blood? Where should this question be set? Thanks for the reply!

title: What is the difference between Molecule Parameters "Intestinal Permeability (transcellular)" and "Specific Intestinal Permeability (transcellular)"?
body_text:
I'm new to PBPK modelling but I asked a more experienced colleague who uses another software and he did not know the answer either.  I could not find the answer in the documentation.
I have both in an oral PBPK model but it's not clear how each is contributing to the model and they don't have the same value after parameter identification.

answer.body_text: I am not entirely sure but I have the ideia  that Specific Intestinal permeability is the one you input the value yourself and the Intestinal Permeability (transcellular) is the one the suite uses for calculation. If, when you make your simulation, you select to use your input value, it takes the Specific Intestinal permeability; if you do not select it, it takes the Calculated Specific intestinal clearance from the internal QSAR that considers MWeff and lipophilicity.

comments.body_text: I am not entirely sure but I have the ideia  that Specific Intestinal permeability is the one you input the value yourself and the Intestinal Permeability (transcellular) is the one the suite uses for calculation. If, when you make your simulation, you select to use your input value, it takes the Specific Intestinal permeability; if you do not select it, it takes the Calculated Specific intestinal clearance from the internal QSAR that considers MWeff and lipophilicity.

title: Calculation of aggregated data from individual data
body_text:
Hi all,
it would be great if PK-Sim and MoBi would be able to calculate aggregated data (mean, median, 5th and 95th quartiles, SD, etc.,,,) from individual data. It would also be great if these data can be exported.
Thanks!
Luis

title: Export Confidence Intervals from PK-Sim/MoBi Confidence Interval plots to Excel
body_text:
Hi all,
after a parameter identification with Sensitivity Calculation, both PK-Sim and MoBi can produce Confidence interval, Visual Predictive Check interval and Prediction Interval plots. These plots include the different simulated intervals. However, when exporting these plots to Excel, the intervals are not exported. It would be great if PK-Sim and MoBi could also export the intervals when exporting the plots to Excel.
This was previously reported by @RobinM92 in Oct. 2022
Thanks!
Luis

title: Subcutaneous dosing for large  molecule
body_text:
Hi,
I am working on subcutaneous PK data of a large molecule and am trying to do PBPK modeling. My question is how to do subcutaneous dosing in PK-Sim and Mobi, is the same to the small molecule? I would really appreciate the help.
Thanks.

title: Define a validation process for PK-Sim multiple specie gene expression databases
body_text:
Dear Community,
the latest animal & human gene expression databases are currently not part of the regular releases of PK-Sim.
As discussed previously #1623 a validation process should be in place that ensures the containing information (expression values & meta information) is correctly represented as in the source.
In addition, there might be PK-Sim updates/new features needed better enable the usage of the gene expression values:

Animal expression databases can be queried for human homologs (e.g. CYP3A4, OATP1B1) the databases also contain the % sequence homology which could be used as indicator for an appropriate homolog
Genes/protein annotation can also contain the subcellular location (intracellular, membrane, extracellular) which currently needs to be entered manually after the expression values are transferred from the databases
Amounts/concentrations of the gene/proteins are currently not retrieved from the expression databases
The expression databases contain information from multiple sources, thus the expression per organ is rather a distribution than a single value. Currently the expression distribution from the databases cannot be used for population/uncertainty simulations.

We would appreciate your community input to this topic.
Best
Henrik

title: Modify parameter to be variable parameter for sensitivity analysis with the ospsuite R package
body_text: I have noticed that some parameter paths are not available for sensitivity analysis in R. I can look at the list of variable parameters using the function potentialVariableParameterPathsFor(), but how do I add parameters of interest to this list? I have tried going into the pkml and modifying the flag for the parameters I want so that ReadOnly = FALSE and CanBeVaried == TRUE, i.e. flag %% 8 == 2. However, this only works for some of the parameters that I want. What exactly are the criteria for a parameter to be valid for sensitivity analysis in R?

answer.body_text:
The flag is a bit-field where each bit represents a specific state of the parameter in PK-Sim/MoBi.
If the n-th bit is set to 1: the flag is enabled, otherwise disabled.
The states (bits) are defined as following
CanBeVaried = 2,
ReadOnly = 4,
Visible = 8,
MinIsAllowed = 16,
MaxIsAllowed = 32,
CanBeVariedInPopulation = 64,
IsChangedByCreateIndividual = 128,

Example:
<Parameter id="xxx" ... >
    <Info flag="52" ... />
</Parameter>

Here: 52 = 32+16+4 meaning that the parameter is defined as:
CanBeVaried = FALSE
ReadOnly = TRUE
Visible = FALSE
MinIsAllowed = TRUE
MaxIsAllowed = TRUE
CanBeVariedInPopulation = FALSE
IsChangedByCreateIndividual = FALSE

The visible flag is used by PK-Sim only, but if one day you send a simulation from MoBi back to PK-Sim parameter will disappear in the UI, so we should set it as visible as well.
So we need to make 3 changes
a)	Switch the CanBeVaried state from FALSE to TRUE ➡️  Add 2
b)	Switch the ReadOnly state from TRUE to False ➡️  Substract 4
c)	Switch the Visible state from FALSE to TRUE ➡️ Add 8
So in total: +2 -4 +8 ➡️ Add 6 to the original flag to make you parameter appear everywhere (in the PI etc.)
So the modified parameter chunk in the pkml file will be:
<Parameter id="xxx" ... >
    <Info flag="58" ... />
</Parameter>

If after that the parameter still does not appear in the sensitivity analysis, it might have other reasons, e.g.

parameter value = 0
parameter is defined by a table
etc.

comments.body_text:

title: parameter estimation method: Monte Carlo in Mobi
body_text:
Hi all,
I was taught that Levenberg-Marquardt is prone to local minima, However I'm not sure how is Monte Carlo algorithm work?
In the user manual, reference for Monte Carlo is quite brief, and here's what ChatGPT tells me about Monte Carlo in PKSIM:
PKSIM, a software tool for pharmacokinetic (PK) and pharmacodynamic (PD) modeling and simulation, likely employs Monte Carlo Parameter Estimation (MCPE) to estimate the parameters of PK/PD models using Monte Carlo methods. Here's how it generally works within the context of PKSIM:
PK/PD Model Specification: First, you define a mathematical model that describes the pharmacokinetics (how the body absorbs, distributes, metabolizes, and excretes drugs) and/or pharmacodynamics (how drugs affect the body) of a particular drug or compound.
Parameter Estimation Setup: You specify the parameters of the PK/PD model that need to be estimated. These parameters could include variables such as clearance rates, volume of distribution, absorption rates, etc.
Data Collection: You input observed data into the software. This data typically includes information about drug concentrations in biological samples (e.g., blood plasma) collected over time after drug administration. This data is crucial for estimating the parameters of the model.
Monte Carlo Sampling: The MCPE algorithm in PKSIM utilizes Monte Carlo sampling techniques to explore the parameter space of the PK/PD model. It generates a large number of parameter sets by randomly sampling from the parameter space according to some probability distribution.
Model Simulation: For each set of sampled parameters, PKSIM simulates the behavior of the PK/PD model using those parameters. This involves solving the differential equations or other mathematical expressions that define the model to predict drug concentrations or other relevant outcomes over time.
Comparison with Observed Data: The simulated results are compared with the observed data collected in step 3. This allows the software to evaluate how well the model with the sampled parameters fits the observed data.
Parameter Estimation: The MCPE algorithm iteratively adjusts the sampled parameters based on the comparison between simulated and observed data. It uses statistical techniques to update the parameter values in a way that improves the fit between the model predictions and the observed data.
Convergence and Final Parameter Estimates: The MCPE algorithm continues iterating until it converges to a set of parameter values that provide a satisfactory fit to the observed data. These converged parameter values are then considered as the estimated parameters of the PK/PD model.
Overall, Monte Carlo Parameter Estimation in PKSIM leverages the power of Monte Carlo methods to efficiently explore the parameter space of PK/PD models and find the best-fitting parameter values based on observed data.


While it does sounds very sensible, I still wanna confirm with people from the forum since I can't find the source of the information...
Also, since it will generate samples of parameters, is the result of optimization impacted by prior values of parameters?
when the manual says multiple times of Levenberg-Marquardt are preferred, how many times are we talking about?
I previously thought we were using Markov Chain Monte Carlo.... what's the difference between our current method and MCMC method? Since that seems to be able to provide distributions of parameters.

comments.body_text:
From the documentation:


Monte - CarloIn each iteration, every free parameter value is separately varied one step upwards and one step downwards from its current value and the objective function is calculated.If the objective function improves (residual sum of squares becomes smaller), the variation is accepted.Here, the variation order is a random permutation. The variation steps are taken at random within in the given parameter intervals and the probability for a step near to the current parameter value is higher than for a step far from the current value, corresponding to the projection grade parameter (alpha).When a parameters variation shows improvement, its projection grade is decreased, otherwise it is increased.This iteration is performed until the break condition holds.Algorithm parameters
Break condition for relative error improvement | Termination occurs when the relative improvement of the error evaluation is less than the break condition.
-- | --
Scale of projection degree (alpha) | Start value for projection degree. Termination occurs when the minimal alpha is larger than 10 times alpha.
Maximum number of iterations | The maximum number of iterations performed.



Unfortunately, I could not find the source for the algorithm description. Maybe @Yuri05 can help?

Dear hainiwen,
Sorry for jumping in so late, but here are my two pence for some of your points:
1.) regarding what CharGPT found: yes it sounds sensible, but as the AI fails to connect PK-Sim With PBPK modelling and uses PK/PD model instead. The answer is likely not entirely right. Good Idea from you to double check here.
3.) How many times using Levenberg-Marquardt: The default value 10 x with randomized start values is a good starting point.
4.) regarding using Markov Chain Monte Carlo: That is a bit of a different story and takes a lot more computing time than runiing an MC alone. See here for more details https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4230716/
Hope this might help you. I guess @Yuri05 can jump in for more details ;-)
Best, Tobias

title: Questions for the default length of small intestinal in PKsim
body_text:
Hello, PKsim users
I'm Wonho Kang working as a postdoc in the UF.
I have some questions about the default values of small intestinal length in PKSim.


There isn't any data origin provided in the table.
Additionally, those values are not close to real lengths. Generally, the length of the jejunum and ileum are approximately 2.5 and 3.0 meters, respectively.

If somebody knows why the default values are set like that, please let me know.
Thank you in advance.

comments.body_text:
The values are based on the
ICRP, 2002. Basic Anatomical and Physiological Data for Use in Radiological Protection Reference Values. ICRP Publication 89. Ann. ICRP 32 (3-4)
You can find the reference values for the default length of the small intestine and its segments for mean individual on page 114:


Additionally, those values are not close to real lengths. Generally, the length of the jejunum and ileum are approximately 2.5 and 3.0 meters, respectively.

You will find the explanation in the same report:
(232) The anatomical length of the intestine represents length as measured at
autopsy or from material removed during surgery, while the physiological length
represents length as measured in a living person. Anatomical lengths are often given in
standard textbooks of anatomy. These values are usually higher than the physiological
lengths, unless they were measured shortly after death when shortening of the intestines
may be observed. Soon after death, the tissue loses its tonus, resulting in elongation.
(233) Reference values for the length of the small intestine given here refer to the
physiological length. Reference values for newborns and adults are based on central
estimates of data summarised in Table 58 of Publication 23 (ICRP, 1975). For both
newborns and adults, the central estimates of the length of the small intestine are
approximately 1.6 body height. This relation is assumed to hold for ages 1–15 years
and for adult females.

title: Parameter controlling intestinal permeability
body_text:
Dear all,
I was looking at one of the semaglutide papers, and I was wondering if the stomach absorption (caused by the permeability enhancer in their formulation) could be simulated in PKSim/MoBi.

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30429357/
I was checking if there was a way that stomach permeability could be modified but I think that it is not part of the model, as until recently, absorption in stomach was not considered.
Then I was trying to find how to shut down absorption in different regions of the GI tract to see if I could think in a workaround.
I think I was disabling absorption to the mucosa and from the mucosa to blood, but although there is nothing in circulation, the compound is absorbed intracellularly in the intestine (I just checked duodenum, upper and lower jejunum).
I was using one molecule from the templates. I set all the Permeability parameters to zero or to 1E-08  cm/min (just to avoid any problem if there is a division by zero).
Transcellular was left with its original value

Mucosas were modified to this

Permeabilitites to this

I was expecting no absorption at all in any segment of the intestine, however, there is intracellular concentration in duodenum and fraction absorbed into mucosa up to ~20%.
This was either with zero or with 1E-8 cm/min.

Please find attached the PKSim file if of any help, it was generated in PKSim v11.
So my questions are:

Is there a way to manipulate gastric absorption in PKSim, or does it require to change many advance parameters in MoBi?
Is there a way to stop absorption to the cells of the intestine? Or is it only controlled by the main transcellular Permeabily value? Or the way I tried to shut down permeability is not the correct?

Many thanks,
Ricardo
Permeability.zip

answer.body_text:
I was checking if there was a way that stomach permeability could be modified but I think that it is not part of the model, as until recently, absorption in stomach was not considered.

Correct.

Then I was trying to find how to shut down absorption in different regions of the GI tract to see if I could think in a workaround.

You can do it in several ways.


If you want to disable absorption in ALL GI segments (not sure though, what's then the rationale for an oral administration...) - you could e.g. set the Specific intestinal permeability to zero for your compound.



if you want to disable absorption in a particular GI segment for all compounds - you could e.g. set the  Effective surface area enhancement factor for those segments to zero



if you want to disable absorption in a particular GI segment for a specific compound - you could e.g. set the Intestinal solubility to zero




I think I was disabling absorption to the mucosa and from the mucosa to blood, but although there is nothing in circulation, the compound is absorbed intracellularly in the intestine (I just checked duodenum, upper and lower jejunum).

In fact, in the first part you were disabling the flow within the mucosa, but not the absorption into the mucosa.

if you want to disable absorption in a particular GI segment for a specific compound - you could e.g. set the Intestinal solubility to zero

In fact, in the first part you were disabling the flow within the mucosa, but not the absorption into the mucosa.

Hi Juri,
Thanks for your reply.
I was thinking in changing the characteristics of duodenum to make it look like a stomach, then disable all the other GI tract to simulate that setup of the picture with the stomach flow closed.
I think that changing the "Effective surface area enhancement fact" would work better .
Thanks,
Ricardo

title: For each enzyme: create and Import an observed data set with just one point: `{simulation_end_time; fraction metabolized}`

title: PK-Sim FcRn recycling fraction to interstitial space
body_text:
As I was working on building a large molecule brain model in MoBi, I noticed that PK-Sim has the Fraction recycled to plasma (or commonly referred to as FR in literature) set to 1, which suggests that effectively no recycling of drug–FcRn or endogenous IgG–FcRn from the endosome to interstitial space occurs. Additionally, it also has Fraction of endosomal uptake from plasma set to 1, which does not allow for any transport from interstitial space back to the endosome since the passive transport formula includes (1-Fraction of endosomal uptake).
The explanation given in Niederalt (2017) says "In order to prevent the net extravasation via the endosomal space and to restrict extravasation to the two-pore mechanism, f up vas and f rec vas were both set to 1 for all simulations in all species, i.e., no uptake from interstitial space to the endosomal space and no recycling to interstitial space was taken into account."
However, I feel like this does not really justify neglecting FcRn-mediated transcytosis into the interstitial space, as literature does suggest bidirectional transport via FcRn-mediated transcytosis from apical to basal and basal to apical transport (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4451002/). I know that the value of FR = 0.715 that is commonly used in some of the large molecule PBPK models was derived from fitting the parameter to observed data and not a physiological value obtained from experiments, but especially in organs such as the brain, where the two-pore model (convective transport and diffusion) is almost negligible due to the blood-brain barrier (maybe a bit more relevant for BCSFB), I think transcytosis can play a significant role in determining tissue concentrations.
Is there another specific reason other than the one stated above for PK-Sim to not include transcytosis via FcRn? Why does PK-Sim focus on restricting extravasation only to the two-pore mechanism?

comments.body_text:
Hi @akim2000,
The main reason indeed seems to be the separation of clearance and distribution mechanisms and the benefit this brings for parameter identifiability. For antibodies or antibody fragments, the difference between the two parameterizations seems to be small as well for most tissues. Exceptions would be the gut, the placenta, and maybe the brain, so it would make sense to explore different versions of these fractions in my opinion.

title: how to investigate precipitation for a BCS class 2 drug
body_text:
Dear all,
best wishes for 2024!
I am interested in performing a simulation to investigate precipitation for a particular formulation of a BCS class 2 compound for which i have a PBPK model developed for a solution (no formulation effect). For this, as i understand the dissolution input needs to be as PSD. The PSD  is available in um as  deciles (d10-x1 um,d20-x2 um,d30-x3 um...d90). I am using PKsim v 11.1
My question are

as the PSD is available as volume density, is a conversion to numerical density required?
Can the method described by Yuri05 in the thread below be applied? Thus creation of 10 monodisperse formulations each with dose fraction corresponding to the respective number  fraction?
#639
In order to run the simulation assuming precipitation, on inputting PSD as aforesaid, under Compounds, can i simply choose Treated precipitated drug as Insoluble from the drop down menu? Or does the simulation need to be exported to MOBI for this step as mentioned in the post below?
#435


thanks in advance
Amit Taneja

answer.body_text:
Dear Amit,
Thanks for your question.
Can you elaborate on the purpose for modeling the precipitation?
If you "only" want to account for the solubility threshold, precipitation is accounted for in a static (immediate) relationship.
Otherwise, the PSD model can be used, including options for supersaturation for a slower precipitation process.
The Info in the link on Point 3 is only for characterization / IVIVE from in-vitro.
Point 2 on what conversion is intended is unclear to me. Can you explain?
Best,
Stephan

title: The Model of scaling  across species
body_text:
Hi everyone
Recently, I leran the model of scaling  across species followed esQlabs tutorials, I still wonder how the scaling realized in the model. It  seems to be taking CL from animals and putting it directly into humans. Would anyone explain the mechanisam for me? I will fell every appreciated!
Thankyou!

comments.body_text:
Search the term allometric scale  in Google.
Energy consumption is of extreme importance in understanding interspecies variation in pharmacokinetics, because it influences in one or
another way the amount of energy to be directed towards elimination of foreign chemicals including drugs. An increase in body mass is generally associated with a progressive reduction in the metabolic rate per unit mass, in turn influencing the turnover rate at the cellular level

Personal opinion. In the tutorial, species scaling is implemented using "specific clearance". It is a "well-stirred model" (unsure) where you can derive a "specific clearance" based on realistic parameters, and then the system scales according to the individuals or populations you build. The only parameter used in the simulation is the CL_spec that is independent of the original individual. For example you can scale between ages, between species.
I don't know if I'm making sense, so I hope this helps.
Also, recommend watching the previous review #1442 #818

title: Fraction unbound (experiment) vs. Fraction unbound (plasma)
body_text:
Hello!
I am referring to the reply to a discussion on the difference between the fraction unbound plasma vs experiment:
The Fraction unbound (experiment) is used for calculation of the specific renal clearance rate constant from renal clearance input value. So to correctly apply the measured or predicted renal clearance value to the model, the two Fraction unbound parameters should be equal. You should also consider this during parameter identification, if you are optimizing the fraction unbound.
Originally posted by @PavelBal in #1452 (comment)
My question is: if the two fraction unbound values must be equal and I want to do parameter identification, I must select both of them as Identification Parameters. However, the optimized values may not be identical. Is there a way to force the two values to be identical?
Looking forward to your reply.
Thanks

comments.body_text:
If you want that some parameters are always identical during the parameter identification process, you must link them to the same identification parameter
For this, select all parameters of interest and then click the upper "Add" button
(by clicking the lower "Add" button you can link new simulation parameters to a previously defined identification parameter)

S. the documentation for details:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/shared-tools-and-example-workflows/parameter-identification#defining-identification-parameters

title: Metabolic exposure in PK-sim
body_text:
I'm now building a PBPK model using parent drug and its metabolites.
The simulation results shows too low metabolic exposure.
How can I make my metabolic ratio go higher in PK-sim?

answer.body_text:
To increase the metabolic ratio in PK-Sim, you can consider the following approaches:


Adjust Enzyme Parameters:

Modify the enzyme parameters such as Vmax and Km for the metabolizing enzymes involved in the biotransformation of the parent drug to its metabolites. Increasing Vmax or decreasing Km can lead to higher metabolic rates.



Incorporate Inducers/Inhibitors:

Introduce inducers or inhibitors of specific enzymes involved in the metabolism of the parent drug. This can be achieved by adjusting the relevant parameters or adding specific drug-drug interaction models.



Consider Physiological Changes:

Incorporate any physiological changes or disease states that may affect the activity of metabolic enzymes. For instance, changes in liver blood flow, enzyme expression levels, or organ volumes can impact metabolic ratios.



Explore Model Assumptions:

Review the assumptions made in the model regarding the metabolic pathways and their kinetics. Adjusting these assumptions may lead to higher metabolic ratios.



Parameter Sensitivity Analysis:

Conduct a sensitivity analysis on the model parameters related to metabolism to identify which parameters have the most significant impact on the metabolic ratio. This can help in pinpointing the key determinants of metabolic exposure.



Model Validation:

Validate the model by comparing the simulated metabolic ratios with available in vivo or in vitro data. Adjust the model parameters to better match the observed metabolic ratios.

title: Solubility at Ref-pH intrinsic to drug?
body_text:
I was working with the Itraconazole simulation found on GIT and noticed the value for this parameter (the parameter being Itracazonle's Solubility at Ref-pH) changed based on whether the patient was fasted or fed.
Why isn't this parameter intrinsic to drug - I assumed it would remain the same regardless of whether or not the patient has been fed?

answer.body_text: I would argue this is not only compound dependent but may also be dependent on the media composition (in addition to pH) in which the compound dissolves. Formulation excipients can have similar effects: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7284856/

title: OSP Pararameter Identification R package release
body_text:
✨ We are happy to announce the release of the parameter identification R package for the OSP ecosystem! You can download the latest release, explore the user documentation, and request for new features on the package repository. ✨
The {ospsuite.parameteridentification} R package provides the functionality of performing parameter identification (i.e., fitting the model to observed data) with simulations created in the Open Systems Pharmacology Software tools PKSim and MoBi. The package provides a user-friendly workflow for setting up such tasks based on existing *.pkml models, mapping model outputs to observed data, and estimating parameters.

title: Retrieving "Organism|R*T" parameter using ospsuite R package
body_text:

answer.body_text:
@moPellowe
You can't escape this. * Is a special character that we use as a wildcard. We did not think about the fact that * would be part of the name of parameters. In general, * should not be allowed as a parameter name. @Yuri05. Do you agree?

title: Second order drug - protein reaction
body_text: How do you include a second order reaction (describing drug -  plasma protein interaction)  in a PK Sim model?

comments.body_text: Hi @zt1947, any protein binding partner that you create in PK-Sim will use a second order reaction. However, if you want to do that for a circulating protein like Albumin, you can either do this via a protein expression in vasculature (but you wont have distribution to tissues of that protein) or you can make Albumin a molecule similar to a drug molecule and implement the binding reactions manually in MoBi.

title: New version of CYP3A4-DDI qualification report released
