For the DDI simulation, I used the models in the OSP-PBPK-Model-Library. However, during the DDI simulation, I found that some models are modeled with different enzyme expression databases, some are RT-PCR, and some are Array.
How should I perform the DDI simulation when I face the models of perpetrator and victim that use different enzyme expression databases?

comments.body_text:
Dear @noxavier, for the same enzymes, all QUALIFIED models from the model library use the same enzyme database (e.g. CYP3A4 is always from RT-PCR).
If different Enzymes/Transporters are from different databases, this is not an issue for combining model files.

title: Question about choosing simulation model and where to find pKa values
body_text:
Hi! I am looking for examples of PBPK models of peptide drug, and I found this inulin model in https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5845054/
Inulin was modeled as a small molecule in Large Molecule Model referring to the material provided on this website. Inulin (with no Fc part) was assumed not metabolized and all excreted in urine. Yet in PK-SIM, the reaction diagram is like

I am confused:

Is this a mistake and what can I do to change the reaction diagram to comply with the true process in body?
I am trying to build a model of a peptide drug, which is metabolized by peptidases and excreted with form of metabolites. The molecule weight is similar to inulin. If I model it as a small molecule in small molecule model, is pKa a necessary parameter? I searched a lot of websites( drugbank, pubchem, etc.)  but cannot find pKa of this drug. Can you give me some advice on how to find this value?
Looking forward for your help!

comments.body_text:
Hi!

This is no mistake. The reaction is always present structurally if you use the large molecules model. For the inulin model, the compound type is set to "small molecule" so that it is not getting to the endosome. So, the FcRn binding becomes irrelevant although the reaction is present. So, nothing to modify for inulin.
The pKa value is used for a) calculating solubility in the different segments of the gastrointestinal tract, b) for calculation of partition coefficients only if you use the methods by Rodgers/Rowland or Schmitt and c) for calculation the permeability across cell membranes (interface interstitial - cell) in case of the charge-dependent methods of Schmitt only.
a) should be irrelevant in your case.
Regarding b) and c): I wouldn't trust the calculation methods for this type of compounds too much anyway (& probably also your lipophilicity is not extremely reliable?). So, I would say the pKa value is not so relevant here and you could go without it.
I would consider in your case to define your peptide as "small molecule" (molecule can diffuse into cells to some extend) but to use the "large molecule" model, so that you have the barrier between the plasma and interstitial space from the "large molecule" model (as it was done for inulin). Then you have no clearance in the endosome but the clearance from your peptidases & the renal clearance should be much larger anyway.

title: Problems in PBPK model of oral absorption
body_text:
Hello everyone,
I am developing a PBPK model for a drug with a narrow window of absorption (absorbed mainly in the upper part of the small intestine). After having a model for iv administration that adjusted very well to the experimental values, I went on to make the model for the oral administration of the same drug.
In the following image I show you what the simulation of the model for oral administration looks like without making any adjustments to the parameters involved in the absorption:

As shown in the image, the shape of the urinary drug excretion curve appears strange as it appears that the drug continues to be eliminated indefinitely.
When adjusting the transcellular intestinal permeability value, it is not possible to optimize the shape of the urinary excretion curve.

I was looking at various exchanges in the forum related to drug absorption and one that was very helpful to me was #942 where it is recommended to optimize specific intestinal permeability with experimental data on the administration of oral solutions of the drug. I did the same but the shape of the curve still follows the same trend.
I would like to know if there is any way to delimit the section where the absorption of the drug occurs in order to improve the simulation?
My drug has a pKa of 3.5 and it is set that the pH of each section of the GIT is taken into account in its absorption (in any case, by checking and unchecking this item in the simulations I have not seen changes in the resulting simulations).
Thank you in advance
Regards

comments.body_text:
Dear @MaLuisaEti, to understand the behavior of urinary excretion, you have to look at how it is defined as a kinetic (i.e. the rate equation) and what drives it (what are its dependencies/input parameters).
Renal excretion is a linear function by default and it is depending on plasma concentrations. Thus, if you still have systemic drug concentrations, renal CL will continue.
A flattening. i.e., effectively nonexistent CL, despite significant systemic drug concentration levels, could be caused by tubular reabsorption, i.e., active transport in the kidney, which can push excreted drug from the tubuli back into circulation. It might be high affinity-low capacity as excretion still occurs at higher systemic drug concentrations.
A very prominent example of such a kinetic is glucose. Here the reabsorption with SGLT1/2 is extremely effective.
Best,
Stephan

title: Can't a large molecular drug be simulated with metabolites?
body_text:
hi,
I want to simulate the process of metabolizing a large molecule drug and transforming it into a metabolite at the same time.
I tried it, but the concentration of the metabolite is zero.
If you set the molecule of the parent drug to be small, even a little metabolite will come out, but if it is a large molecule, the concentration is zero.
Or should this be built with Mobi?
prodrug.zip

comments.body_text:
Hi,
if you  define your drug to be a "large molecule", passive diffusion across cell membranes is set to zero.
You need to add a mechanism by which the drug enters the cell, e.g. target mediated uptake into the cell or an active transport.
Best,
Christoph
or - unlike in your attached file - you could define a metabolization process in the plasma or interstitial space.

title: stoichiometric reaction and competitive binding
body_text:
Hi,
I would like to create particular binding/complex formation reactions in MoBi and have several questions about stoichiometriy coefficients and how it is taken into account into the equations.
I have an antibody Ab with 2 binding sites (a and b) on one arm and each site can bind to receptor R.
2kon                                     kon
Ab + R <====> AbRa or AbRb  + R <========>AbRaRb
koff                                          2koff
For the first part:
Does it make sense to create 1 reaction as follow : Ab + R => AbRa + AbRb ?
Stoichiometry coefficients of 2 for Ab and R and 1 for AbRa and AbRb
And do we write 2*kon in the equation formula, or is it already considered by the stoichiometry coefficients to consider the competitive binding?
Or does it make more sense to create 2 reactions as follow : Ab + R => AbRa and Ab + R => AbRb ?
Stoichiometry coefficients of 1 for Ab, R, AbRa and also 1 for Ab, R, AbRb
And do we write 2*kon in the equation formula to consider the competitive binding?
For the second part:
We have 2 reactions: AbRa + R => AbRaRb and AbRb + R => AbRaRb
Stoichiometry coefficients of 1 for Ab, R, AbRa and also 1 for Ab, R, AbRb
Stoichiometry coefficients of 1 for Ab, R, AbRa and also 1 for Ab, R, AbRb
And do we write 2*koff in the equation formula to consider the competitive dissociation?
Hope it is clear.
Thanks for your help.

answer.body_text:
Hope it is clear.

no :)
So are you trying to create 2 binding reactions like this?
$$2Ab + R \underset{\text{koff}}{\overset{\text{kon}}{\rightleftharpoons}} AbRa$$
$$2Ab + R \underset{\text{koff}}{\overset{\text{kon}}{\rightleftharpoons}} 2AbRb$$
If so, then define 2 separate reactions:

Reaction 1 with stoich coeffs 2,1,1 and reaction rate kon*[Ab]*[R] - koff*[AbRa]
Reaction 2 with stoich coeffs 2,1,2 and reaction rate kon*[Ab]*[R] - koff*[AbRb]

comments.body_text:
Hope it is clear.

no :)
So are you trying to create 2 binding reactions like this?
$$2Ab + R \underset{\text{koff}}{\overset{\text{kon}}{\rightleftharpoons}} AbRa$$
$$2Ab + R \underset{\text{koff}}{\overset{\text{kon}}{\rightleftharpoons}} 2AbRb$$
If so, then define 2 separate reactions:

Reaction 1 with stoich coeffs 2,1,1 and reaction rate kon*[Ab]*[R] - koff*[AbRa]
Reaction 2 with stoich coeffs 2,1,2 and reaction rate kon*[Ab]*[R] - koff*[AbRb]

title: Drug with MW between 4 and 5kDa is considered as small or large molecule in PK-SIM?

Hello! I would like to know which model should be used when dealing with a peptide drug with molecule weight between 4kDa and 5kDa. Is it considered a small molecule or large molecule in PK-SIM?
I read the tutorials on the learn-esqlabs website. It says " Molecules are considered small up to a molecular weight of 5 kDa.". So I am a little confused.
Looking forward for your help!

comments.body_text:
Hi @Gre-ux, unfortunately, there is no general "true" value for the right threshold for when to switch to the large molecule model.
Starting from 0.5 kD to 5 kD it could already make sense. Just try it out and compare the outcomes.
Make sure to keep the property "is small molecule" checked/active so have passive diffusion into cells.
Best,
Stephan

Dear OSPSuite experts, I downloaded the files available in:
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Evaluation-plan-template/tree/master
and executed it with no error. Then, I simplified the 'evaluation_plan.json' file by setting: "GOFMergedPlots": [] and removed the corresponding part in the 'Sections'. This way in the report only 'Concentration-Time Profiles' are plotted (no GoF plots). Executing the reportingEngine worked well. Then, in PK-Sim, I loaded the 'Compound.json' and removed the observed data in the 'Sim 1 - intravenous' simulation and export the updated file as 'Compound.json' and executed the createQualificationReport. However, this time I got this error:
! Warning	argument 1 is not a vector
Configuration Plan Information:
Project: Compound
Simulation: Sim 1 - intravenous
SectionReference: ct-profiles

PlotNumber: 0 (json numbering starting at 0);  1 (actual plot index)

Error Trace

↪ workflow$runWorkflow()
↪ self[[plotTask]]$runTask(self$configurationPlan)
↪ self$getTaskResults(configurationPlan, self$settings)
↪ ifNotNull(timeProfilePlan$Plot$Type, plotQualificationPopulationTimeProfile(simulationAnalysis = timeProfilePlan$Plot$Analysis,
↪ plotQualificationMeanTimeProfile(configurationPlanCurves = timeProfilePlan$Plot$Curves,
↪ updateQualificationTimeProfilePlotConfiguration(simulatedMetaData = simMetaData,
↪ order(observedMetaData$id)
Error: argument 1 is not a vector
Configuration Plan Information:
Project: Compound
Simulation: Sim 1 - intravenous
SectionReference: ct-profiles
Plot: list(...)
PlotNumber: 0 (json numbering starting at 0);  1 (actual plot index)

here is the zip file:
[Files.zip](https://github.com/GhazalMontaseri/Test2)
Two points:

When I create a single datapoint observation at time=0 (or any time) and concentration = 0 mmol/l and add it to the simulation, it works again.
population simulations without observation work well, only with individual population I had the issue.

I would appreciate if you can help me with this issue?

comments.body_text: seems to be a bug. created an issue for this: Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSPSuite.ReportingEngine#1082

Dear @GhazalMontaseri
The bug has been fixed in the development version of the reporting engine.
Please find the link to the corresponding bundle here: ospsuite.reportingengine_2.2.283.zip

While reproducing the issue from your files, I noticed you were loading the namespace of many other packages.
Since the reporting engine package is often used within the ecosystem of {tlf}, {ospsuite} and {rClr} packages,
loading {ospsuite.reportingengine} into the namespace directly loads the 3 previous libraries into the namespace (as illustrated below).
Thus, you don't need to call for these libraries in your script.
library(ospsuite.reportingengine)
#> Loading required package: ospsuite.reportingengine
#> Loading required package: ospsuite
#> Loading required package: rClr
#> Loading the dynamic library for Microsoft .NET runtime...
#> Loaded Common Language Runtime version 4.0.30319.42000
#> Loading required package: tlf

title: Predictive ability (statistical analysis) of PBPK Models
body_text: I am wondering what methods are used today to evaluate the predictive ability of a PBPK model. I have seen the use of fold error (commonly 2-fold, but maybe even 1.5 and 1.33-fold) for evaluating PK parameters, but is the same used/enough for predicted concentration of drugs in the body? Are there any newer, more desirable methods of evaluating a PBPK model? And if a 2-fold error method is still used these days, then would that in itself be enough statistical analysis in a paper to publish your results, or are there other analyses that are also commonly performed on top of that?

comments.body_text:
It depends on the concrete use case. E.g. for DDI other criteria are better than 2-fold: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/21036951/
S. also e.g.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6856026/
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31652029/
https://www.oecd.org/chemicalsafety/risk-assessment/guidance-document-on-the-characterisation-validation-and-reporting-of-physiologically-based-kinetic-models-for-regulatory-purposes.pdf
https://www.ema.europa.eu/en/documents/scientific-guideline/guideline-reporting-physiologically-based-pharmacokinetic-pbpk-modelling-simulation_en.pdf

title: Variation in partition coefficient methods based on dose - which to choose?
body_text:
Hi,
I come across a situation while performing Parameter identification (PI) in PK-Sim using the 5 partition methods. Now, at a 400 mg dose, the drug PK profile is well-predicted with the 'PK-SIM standard' method as the partition coefficient method whereas it is poorly predicted for a supra-therapeutic dose of 800 mg. However, Poulin and Theil's method predicted the PK profile of 800 mg very well as shown below. I tried to do PI together with 400 mg and 800 mg PK profiles and got the PK-Sim method as best but not really explain the distribution of 800 mg. Here the drug shows a non-linear PK behavior across the doses 200, 400, and 800 mg.
800 mg PI simulation:

Red dots-observed data
Green line-Poulin and Theil PC method simulation
Blue line-PK-Sim method simulation
Please advise the best method or option to get a generalized model. For a generalized model, I tried to use binding partners like lysosomes and protein partners to fix the distribution phase. However, the Tmax got shifted to half of its original like 0.75 hr from the actual 1.5 to 2  hr. I have no idea whether it is the right way to do it or not. Moreover, I observed when using the binding partners, and optimizing the tissue partitioning values by fixing them based on preclinical data, I felt the 5 PC methods optimization is not working anymore and the model lost its integrity as there is a manual optimization into play. Do I have to try anything more here?
Thanks in advance.

comments.body_text: Certainly, if you fix partitioning values, the PC method optimization wont work any more and you either fit individual uncertain PC values directly together with the processes you added and thenusual fitting parameters (clearance, LogP, fu...).

Hi Stephan,
Thank you.
Apart from the fixed partitioning values, I think the protein binding partners could also alter the normal control over PC method optimization in PKSIM, is it right?
I am curious to know the scientific solution for a generalized mechanistic PBPK model. So, could you suggest anything to make the model work at both 400 and 800 mg? Do I have to introduce any non-linearity parameter in the distribution phase for this, as elimination is approximately close between the PKSIM and Poulin and Theil methods for 800 mg? I read the literature where logP(oil/water) is introduced in Poulin and Theil and Berezhkovsky methods which is missing in the PK-Sim PC method. Please advise.

Hi,
sorry for jumping in late but it looks like you are speaking about 400 and 800 mg p.o. doses, right? So how does it look like if you overlay your dose normalized observed data? Could there be some difference on the absorption side between 400 and 800 mg. Decisions about the distribution models can be done best based on i.v. data.
Best, Tobias

Hi Folks,
I guess the lysosomal trapping consideration in PK-Sim through a protein binding partner is like linear kinetics following a first-order process with kd and koff kinetics. However, there is a research paper (https://dmd.aspetjournals.org/content/dmd/47/1/49.full.pdf) stating that lysosomal trapping might be non-linear and dose-dependent with saturation kinetics. In my case, I can see 200 and 400 mg PK data is well explained by PK-Sim standard PC method without any need for lysosomal trapping. However, for higher doses like 800 mg and above, lysosomal trapping is profoundly having an effect, and incorporation of it showed the best simulation during model development. Now my question is - is it possible to consider lysosomal trapping in Michaelis-Menten kinetic fashion (non-linear kinetics) the same as a CYP metabolism reaction in PK-Sim (assuming lysosomal trapping as a metabolizing enzyme rather than a protein binding partner)?  Please advise, correct me if I am wrong here, and thanks in advance.

title: How to set the gastric emptying time in Populations in PK-sim v11.2?
body_text: I have assumed gastric emptying to be 45min in Individuals and then set Populations based on Individuals, resulting in a large difference in PK between the two scenario modes. The gastric emptying time is shown in the figure, and I want to modify the gastric emptying in Populations to be concentrated at 45min or normally distributed centered at 45min. If I can't modify it, then how can I get the 95% confidence interval for the results simulated with Individuals?

comments.body_text:
The gastric emptying time has a lognormal distribution by default.
You cannot change this directly in PK-Sim. If you want a different type of distribution, you can export your population to a CSV file, adjust the GET values to suit your needs and then re-import the population from the CSV file.

title: FcRn binding competition
body_text:
Hello,
When modeling a concomitant administration of two large molecules with different affinity for FcRn receptor in endosomes, I was expecting to see an impact on the PK of the less potent one due to saturation or blocking of FcRn receptors by the potent compound administered at very high dose (to serve as a blocking agent).
However, simulations in PK-sim show no impact at all on the second compound whatever the simulated amounts and dosing intervals are.
Is there another way to model FcRn binding competition or saturation in PK-sim?
Thank you.

Raphael

answer.body_text:
Hi @RaphBil , we explored the use of high-dose Igg as a rescue treatment for high-affinity FcRn binders in the past, and this worked. Doses above 1 mg are needed, though, before FcRn is saturated sufficiently.
Happy to chat to discuss details.
Best,
Stephan

title: IgG perturbations
body_text:
Dear community,
Hope you are doing well! I was wondering what underlying processes are already put in place for IgG in the therapeutic protein model. More specifically, do the current models already allow to predict the perturbation of IgG concentrations in plasma after dosing it (IgG therapy) or depleting it (e.g. to reduce antibody response to certain therapies)? When I try to export a random model from PKsim to Mobi and look at the predicted IgG concentrations (which is called EndoLigand, correct?) they are shown as a stable line, but when I e.g. look at the Idarucizumab, the IgG pool (or at least the EndoLigand pool) seems to deplete. Any insights are very welcome!
Thanks,
Robin

comments.body_text:
Hi Robin,
There is a submodel for endogenous IgG (indeed called LigandEndo in the implementation) and FcRn lumping the whole organism within a single PK-Sim organ as described in https://doi.org/10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4.
So - given the model of an “antibody A” - if you add an application for LigandEndo into the plasma compartment of the “EndogenousIgG organ” in MoBi at a certain timepoint, you can see the expected behavior (increase of endogenous IgG concentration at that time point and slow return to the steady state of the endogenous IgG and increase in clearance for the “antibody A” starting at the timepoint of the application of the endogenous IgG. I have however never tested how well it works quantitatively with the current parameterization.
(What will not work, is to change the steady state start concentration of endogenous IgG (if it is e.g. constantly depleted) because the parameters for the steady state start concentrations for both endogenous IgG and FcRn refer to the free concentrations. One could then directly adapt the steady state start concentrations of free FcRn.)
I’m wondering why you mention Idarucizumab in this context. Isn’t it a Fab fragment, i.e. it should not compete with the endogenous IgG for FcRn?

Hi Christoph,

Thank you for your answer! Good to know that I at least got the IgG name
right :)
I don't completely follow with the second part; why does the fact that
IgG and FcRn are free concentrations limits one from including an
elimination process of IgG (e.g. depletion in a disease state)?

The Idarucizumab model was just one of the ready to play models I found
in the repository which I used to explore the behaviour of IgG, I had no
interest in the compound itself (although it is a neat model :) )

Kind regards,
Robin

On 08-02-22 11:19 AM, Christoph Niederalt wrote:

 Hi Robin,

 There is a submodel for endogenous IgG (indeed called LigandEndo in
 the implementation) and FcRn lumping the whole organism within a
 single PK-Sim organ as described in
 https://doi.org/10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4.
 So - given the model of an “antibody A” - if you add an application
 for LigandEndo into the plasma compartment of the “EndogenousIgG
 organ” in MoBi at a certain timepoint, you can see the expected
 behavior (increase of endogenous IgG concentration at that time point
 and slow return to the steady state of the endogenous IgG and increase
 in clearance for the “antibody A” starting at the timepoint of the
 application of the endogenous IgG. I have however never tested how
 well it works quantitatively with the current parameterization.
 (What will not work, is to change the steady state start concentration
 of endogenous IgG (if it is e.g. constantly depleted) because the
 parameters for the steady state start concentrations for both
 endogenous IgG and FcRn refer to the free concentrations. One could
 then directly adapt the steady state start concentrations of free FcRn.)

 I’m wondering why you mention Idarucizumab in this context. Isn’t it a
 Fab fragment, i.e. it should not compete with the endogenous IgG for FcRn?

I was just wondering if you might have tried to just change the steady state start concentration of free endogenous IgG and were thinking why nothing is happening to the clearance of the drug antibody (The reason – if not clear from above – is that for the clearance the available free FcRn is relevant which is not changing if not adjusted. I have developed a PBPK model and modified it in MoBi, making one of the parameters (specific blood flow rate in Kidneys) drug´s concentration-dependent. I would like to know if there is something I should take into account when exporting the simulation to PKSim that I am currently overlooking.
To export the simulation from MoBi to PKSim, I tried the following two options: 1) I used the tool in MoBi that appears when right-clicking and selecting "Send to PKSim for Population Simulation." 2) I saved the MoBi simulation in PKML format and then opened it in PKSim. Ma Luisa

answer.body_text:
@MaLuisaEti This is the right way of doing this
However, your population also has values for the kidney blood flow (as this is an individual parameter for which a distribution is defined in the database). That means that it is CRUCIAL that you remove this parameter from your population before simulating
HOW:

Export to csv,
remove the column,
create a new population-based on the modified csv
enjoy life

title: How to get results after using the function runSimulationBatches
body_text:
Dear all,
I would like to use the runSimulationBatches() function in R. I think I got how to use the function, but I don't find how to get the plasma concentrations simulated by the batch simulation. I only get a sort of "code" as reported in the examples in the manual of the package.
Could you please help me on how to get the plasma concentrations generated by the simulation?
Below I reported the code I have so far.
mySim <- loadSimulation(mySim.pkml)
mySimBatch <- createSimulationBatch(simulation = mySim , parametersOrPaths = myParamPath)
mySimBatch$addRunValues(parameterValues = XXXX)
simResults <- runSimulationBatches(mySimBatch)
What I would need is how to go from here to the PK concentrations :-)
Since what I got after the run is something like this:
[1] "14809397-c10f-4947-9f1b-64409124129b"
Thank you very much in advance!
Donato

answer.body_text:
Hi Donato,
simResults is a named list, the names are composed from the id of the simulation batch and the id of the parameter values sets. The reason is that you can execute multiple SimulationBatch objects with multiple run values in parallel, and by knowing the ID you can then access the right results.
In your case, there is only one batch with one values set, so simResults is a list with only one entry. Just access the fist element of this list, which is a SimulationResults object. You can then access the outputs as described here https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/dev/articles/run-simulation.html#running-individual-simulation-and-retrieving-the-results

title: Number of small and large pores on Mobi ?
body_text:
Hi all 😃

comments.body_text:
HI Salih,
I believe this is not an explicit parameter, but would be calculated by Por Surface Area divided by pore size

Hi Salih,
as Stephan wrote the number of pores is not an explicit parameter. Also, the total pore surface area is not an explicit parameter, but it occurs conceptionally to estimate the permeabilities for small and large pores. Direct parameters used in the model related to this are the pore radii of small & large pores, the hydraulic conductivity of the endothelium, the fraction of flow vial large and small pores (and the viscosity). You can look at equations 10 and 13 in https://doi.org/10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4 for more details.

title: how to check the implementation of particle dissolution method of PKSIM
body_text:
Hi there!
I want to use the particle dissolution function of PKSIM, however, I wonder how PKSIM implements it.
I am aware of the publication is
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20554023/
But I still don't get how PKSIM compute it.
For example, does the number of bins equal to k that was talked about in the paper? meaning, how many groups of particles were assigned into?
Also, in the paper, it seems to be partial differential equations where both location and time are involved in, what are algorithms like in PKSIM? Should it be approximated to ODE to implement?
How and where can I find these equations so that I understand what's going on better?
Lots of thanks!!
Haini

answer.body_text:
Number of particle bins is user input. Particles radius within each bin is estimated from the properties of the radius probability distribution, [min..max] radius range and the number of bins.
E.g. if you have 3 bins, normal distribution, it would look like below
 
If you have 3 bins, lognormal distribution, it would look like below:
 
Number of particles in the i-th particle bin ($N_i$ ) is assumed to be proportional to the probability density function for the bin radius ($f(r_i)$ ):
$$N_i=alpha*f(r_i)$$
Knowing the total administered dose you can estimate the proportionality factor $alpha$ from the relationship
$$Dose = \sum_{i=1}^{Number\ of\ bins} 4/3*\pi*r_i^3*\rho*N_i$$
where $\rho$ is the density of drug particles.
From this you can estimate the number of particles in each bin as
$$N_i = \frac{Dose}{\rho}*\left[ \frac{f(r_i)}{4/3*\pi* \sum \left( r_i^3*f(r_i) \right)} \right]$$
The expression in the square brackets above is defined as Number_Of_Particles_Factor and is calculated only in PK-Sim (because probability density functions are not supported by MoBi).
So finally the number of particles in each bin is defined as
$$N_i = \frac{Dose}{\rho}*Number\ Of\ Particles\ Factor = \frac{DrugMass * MolWeight}{\rho}*Number\ Of\ Particles\ Factor$$

comments.body_text:
In particular, I wonder how the method computes a Number Of Particles Factor to compute the number of particles based on the distribution of particles.
I read from several references that it should relate to pdf distribution of radius of particles, but I am also quite lost there since its quite a strange area to me...
maybe the formula is somewhere in PKSIM so that I understand how come can be computed.
Cheers,
Haini

Regarding the overall implementation: the 2010 paper is outdated, current implementation is different.

Particles in each bin are moving along the GI-tract. The movent is realized by calculation of the Number of particles fraction in each GI segment.
At t=0 Number of particles fraction stomach = 1 and Number of particles fraction other segments = 0
Then the particles start to distribute (Number of particles fraction stomach decreases, Number of particles fraction duodenum increases, etc.)

The Noyes-Whitney dissolution is then realized separately for each bin and each GI-segment.
In the events building block you can see a dissolution transport template for each GI segment (Solid<=>Dissolved):

When creating a simulation, each of those dissolution transports is created for each particles bin.

title: probenecid PBPK model and conversion reference concentration v9- v11 PKsim
body_text:
Dear colleague,
I have a question about the probenecid PBPK model (PMID: 33237382, this model is also open access available on github https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Probenecid-Model)
I wanted to convert the reference concentrations for both OAT3 and OATP1B1 from the probenecid PBPK model v9 in v11, but do not get the same concentrations in v9 and v11 after conversion. The concentrations are almost the same a lower time points in the concentration-time profile, but differences increases between v9 and v11 concentration with increasing time.
Is it possible that conversion of references concentrations for both OAT3 and OATP1B1 have already been made in the probenecid PBPK model which is upload on github?
Just to give some background on what I did:

Downloaded the probenecid PBPK model from github and load this V9 probenecid model in V11, after which I exported one simulation to Mobi
created identical probenecid simulation in V11, also exported this simulation to Mobi

-OAT3 V9 value in the kidney interstitial space is 0.04 umol.

OAT3 V11 value in the kidney interstitial space is 8.04E-3 umol
therefore the ratio is 0.8
within the expression profile block of OAT3 in V11 I have multiplied my reference concentration of 0.09 umol/L with 0.8 resulting in an " adapted"  reference concentration of 0.056 umol/L

For OATP1B1 the ratio is 0.667 in the periportal intersitital space in the liver. this value has been provided in the convertion tutorial op ESQlabs.

the ratio of 0.667 has been multiplied with the reference concentration 0f 0.07 umol/L in V11 PK sim, resulting in an "adapted"  reference concentration 0f 0.04669 umol/L

after comparing the PKsim simulation with the adapted reference concentration in V11 with those of V9 I got, for example, a concentration of 200.58 mg/mL in V11 and 201.19 mg/mL in V9 at t=1, while the difference is increased at, for example, t =12h (v11 79.81 mg/mL (V11) and 83.07 mg/mL (v9)

Does anyone have an idea about the differences who still exists between references concentrations in V9 and V11
Looking forward to the discussion
Paola

comments.body_text:
Dear Paola,
the model on Github you are referencing is still version 9. Currently we are working to provide an updated v11 model there.
But this is still work in the progress. Currently we are doing QC checks etc.
But good news: you can test our drfat-beta version! Please find the zip file for the snapshot attached
The only change to version 9  is that Kcat OAT3 is changed to this value:

using a correction factor 0.56897754 for OAT3
Probenecid-Model_v11-beta.zip
Please let me know if you still see differences with this version.
Best, Tobias

Dear Paola, there have been changes in how expressin data is loaded fromV9 to V10.
We have a short how-to, that @PavelBal or @sfrechen could upload (a PPT).
Best,
Stephan

title: Quinidine PBPK model for CYP2D6, CYP3A4 and P-gp drug-drug(-gene) interaction predictions
body_text:
Dear all,
We have been working on a parent-metabolite PBPK model of quinidine and 3-hydroxyquinidine, evaluated within a comprehensive drug-drug(-gene) interaction network as (i) CYP2D6 perpetrator and (ii) CYP3A4 and P-gp victim, including a total of 13 compounds.
The corresponding manuscript has been published in CPT: PSP and the model snapshots have been uploaded as OSP repositories to GitHub.
Related publication:
Feick D, Rüdesheim S, Marok FZ, Selzer D, Loer HLH, Teutonico D, Frechen S, van der Lee M, Moes DJAR, Swen JJ, Schwab M, Lehr T. Physiologically-based pharmacokinetic modeling of quinidine to establish a CYP3A4, P-gp, and CYP2D6 drug–drug–gene interaction network. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol 2023. DOI: 10.1002/psp4.12981.
All models can be found here.
Best regards,
Denise

title: About sensitivity analysis output
body_text:
Hi
I read the pk-sim manual about the meaning of the output of sensitivity analysis, but I am still confused about it. When we get a value of 0.5 for instance according to the manual, it means that upon increasing the parameter value by 10% the pk metric changes by 5%. My confusion is about the direction of change, the default values for variation range, and number of steps will allow a 10% increase and decrease of the parameter value, so why do we interpret the result as a 10% increase, not a decrease, in parameter value? we may actually get non-symmetric results for the change in the pk metric when we increase/decrease the parameter.
Can someone kindly clarify more about this?
Thanks

comments.body_text:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/shared-tools-and-example-workflows/sensitivity-analysis
--> Mathematical Background
This calculation is made for each step:

And then the average is calculated.
So if you decrease also your output will decrease if you have a positive sensitivity.
Best,
Stephan

title: use PKsim or Mobi to establish PBPK model, with the purpose of predicting brain tissue concentration through blood concentration of the two drugs to optimize the drug administration plan
body_text: Help! I have two small-molecule antibiotics, meropenem and sulbactam, that combine to treat Acinetobacter baumannii. I want to use PKsim or Mobi to establish PBPK model, with the purpose of predicting brain tissue concentration through blood concentration of the two drugs to optimize the drug administration plan. What should the modeling steps be like? Is there any modeling material I can refer to? Hope to get answers! Best wishes!

comments.body_text:
Hi,
it seems like you are on a starting point for an interesting journey.
Did you check out this paper: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5080648/
You may also want to check out our tutorials here:
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSP-based-publications-and-content/labels/Tutorial
And finally if you are looking for a F2F hands on Workshop you might want to check out this:
https://lnkd.in/gwA7rYPg
Good luck!
Tobias

Hi @pearlzhuzhu, in additiona to the links from @tobiasK2001, if you are from academia, you can find free well-guided up-to-date online training/tutorials here:
https://learn-esqlabs.talentlms.com/
For industry, this will require some sponsoring:
https://esqlabs.shop/  --> "courses (latest OSP Version)"

title: How dose Intrinsic clearance – First order method caculate the hepatic clearance?
body_text:
This is alfentanil PBPK model of adult published in Git hub, in which the metabolism way was setted by Intrinsic clearance – First order method. And CYP3A4 is the only one enzyme.
According to the instuction of PK-SIM, I try to caculate the hepatic clearance, the calculation steps are as follows. The results are completely different from the intravenous clearance calculated by the NCA.
I don't know how dose Intrinsic clearance – First order method caculate the hepatic clearance? There is very little description of this method in the manual.
Another question is that , in this method, when extrapolating the model from adults to children, will the ontogeny fraction of CYP3A4 enzymes in children be taken into account ? Because the intrinsic clearance rate seems to be a fixed number.

by the way, is there a more detailed instruction manual than this website"https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/"
Thank you very much for your answer!

title: MDCKII-MDR1 transporter Papp
body_text:
Hi,
I am new to OSP.
Great if somebody clarify me the following:
I have a measured MDCKII-MDR1 transporter Papp value and struggling to how to enter this in PK-Sim.
Tried "Transport Proteins" under "Transport and Excretion" tab but it requires Vamx and Km not the Permeability Coefficient or ER
values you would get in MDCK assays.
Thanks very much in advance.

comments.body_text:
Hi @RajFer,
If you know the cell surface area of your experiments and have the Papp at different substrate concentrations, you could in theory calculate Vmax and Km by fitting the data to a michaelis menten equation yourself:
CLint (µl/min) ~ Papp (cm/s) * SA (cm²) * 60 (s/min) * 10^(3) (µL/cm^3)
V (nmole/min) ~ CLint  (µl/min) * [S] (µM) * 1000 (nmole/µmole) for each substrate concentration
Plot V vs [S], fit V = Vmax*[S]/(Km + [S])
The problem is that you then still need your transporter concentration in order to be able to use this value.
I know work was being done to find default values for these transporter concentrations for the standard cell lines, but am not aware of any results there yet. Perhaps you have measured them?
See e.g. https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Forum/discussions/830
But I am sure the experts here can weigh in much more than I can :)

title: Recovering projects and avoiding software crashes with large amount of data
body_text:
Dear community,
I am facing difficulties while working with a large amount of observed data (approximately 100 C-t profiles) in MoBi's Parameter Identification function. The software crashes frequently, either during the run itself or when I attempt to add more observed data to the same PI step.
I tried increasing the value of GDIProcessHandleQuota, as suggested in one of the discussions, but MoBi still crashes. Moreover, when I tried to reopen the last saved workspace, I encountered the following error. Even after restarting both my PC and the project, I cannot locate any of the building blocks, simulations, or parameter identification steps that I had created.
Is there a way to recover this project and prevent future crashes?
Best regards,
Aya

title: what dose Time < T_lag ? 0 : k_a*A_ET mean (Expression in Mobi or PKSIM)
body_text:
Hi there!
What dose this expression ? 0 : means in Mobi?
I think it's something related to conditions, but I don't know what language is that
Can anybody explain a bit?

answer.body_text:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-mobi/mobi-documentation/model-building-components#working-with-formulas
It's a conditional expression, using the notation <condition> ? <formula string for true> : <formula string for false>
So Time < T_lag ? 0 : k_a*A_ET means:
IF Time < T_lag
  0
ELSE
  k_a*A_ET

Florian

title: Number of particles formula in particle dissolution model
body_text:
Can someone please explain the "Number of particles" formula within the Particle Dissolution model (within the ParticleBin event group)? I don't understand why MW is there, and the units don't seem to line up.
DrugMass [g] / DrugDensity [g/L] * MW [g/mol] * Number_Of_Particles_Factor [1/L]
Without MW, the formula makes more sense to me.
DrugVolume = DrugMass / DrugDensity
Number_Of_Particles_Factor : the number of particles for that molecule per volume unit
Number of particles = DrugVolume * Number_Of_Particles_Factor

answer.body_text:
DrugMass here is an amount => [µmol]
So in the internal units it is
DrugMass [µmol] / DrugDensity [kg/L] * MW [kg/µmol] * Number_Of_Particles_Factor [1/L] => Dimensionless

comments.body_text:
DrugMass here is an amount => [µmol]
So in the internal units it is
DrugMass [µmol] / DrugDensity [kg/L] * MW [kg/µmol] * Number_Of_Particles_Factor [1/L] => Dimensionless

title: Hepatic impairment population
body_text:
Hello everyone.
Based on the scaling factors in Edgiton et al 2008, I tried to create a population with cirrhosis, but the results were inconsistent with the OSPS tutorial. For example, in the tutorial file: Bone blood flow rate control: 2.75 mL/min/100g organ; Child-Pugh A: 4.42 mL/min/100g organ. This does not seem to match the 1.75 folds in the Edginton et al 2008.
I am wondering how should I modify the blood flow? Is it to modify the special blood flow ratio?
In addition, what parameters should Cardiac index, portal blood flow, and hepatic arterial correspond to? Is the portal blood flow ratio? (This seems to be automatically calculated in PK-Sim.) The liver specific blood flow ratio?
In addition, why is the special blood flow ratio of the small intestine, spleen and stomach lower? (The control group is 2.5 times the Child-Pugh A group?) Why not have a higher trend like other organs?

answer.body_text:
Hi @SCZ1174
The change in the portal blood flow rate refers to the blood flow rates of all portal vein supplying organs (large intestine, pancreas, small intestine, spleen, stomach) as the portal vein blood flow rate in PKSim is implemented as the sum of these organ blood flow rates. The change in hepatic arterial blood flow rate refers to the blood flow rate of the liver in PKSim. Note that the specific liver blood flow is calculate from two different factors. Firstly, it is changed according to the values from Edginton 2008.  Secondly, the liver volume is changed. As the total blood flow is calculated from the specific blood flow and the organ volume, the specific blood flow has to be scaled, so that the changed organ volume does not have an impact on the total blood flow (as the total blood flow was already adapted to liver cirrhosis).
As described by Edginton and Willman in the section “Physiological Scaling: Blood Flows” the changes in liver blood flow, portal venous blood flow and renal blood flow were informed from literature values. Additionally, the change in cardiac index is informed from literature. As there is no direct representation of the cardiac index in PKSim, its change was used to calculate the change in blood flow rates of “other organs”. The same was done in the tutorial. So instead of using the factor on “other organs” from Edginton and Willman we used the factor on cardiac index from the same publication to calculate the change in other organs as only the change in cardiac index is informed by literature and, therefore, more reliable.
The cardiac index (CI) equals the heart minute volume (HMV) per body surface area (BSA). The heart minute volume is the sum of all blood flows of the body circulation (excluding the pulmonary blood flow), with Qrest being the sum of the portal, hepatic arterial and renal blood flow and Qother being all other organs (bone, fat, gonads, heart, muscle, skin). As the brain blood flow is maintained stable in cirrhosis, it will not be included in Qother in the equations.
𝐶𝐼 = 𝐻𝑀𝑉/𝐵𝑆𝐴 = (𝑄𝑜𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑟 + 𝑄𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡 + Qbrain)/𝐵𝑆𝐴
In the disease state the CI and Qrest change to a known degree (DF_CI and DF_rest; “DF” stands for “disease factor”) and the body surface area stays the same.
𝐷𝐹_𝐶𝐼 = 𝐶𝐼𝑑𝑖𝑠𝑒𝑎𝑠𝑒/𝐶𝐼 = (𝑄𝑜𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑟 × 𝐷𝐹_𝑜𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑟 + 𝑄𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡 × 𝐷𝐹_𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡+ Qbrain)/(𝑄𝑜𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑟 + 𝑄𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡+ Qbrain)
The calculation using the number from the tutorial PK-Sim files (for Child-Pugh A):
DF_CI = 1.11
Qrest [L/min] = 1.21 + 0.29 + 1.33 = 2.83 (sum of healthy values of portal, hepatic arterial and renal blood flow in the individual that will be transformed to Child-Pugh A)
Qother [L/min] = 0.32 + 0.32 + 0.00325 + 0.26 + 1.12 +  0.33 = 2.35325 (sum of healthy values of “other organs” in the individual that will be transformed to Child-Pugh A)
Qbrain  [L/min] = 0.78
(𝑄𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡 × 𝐷𝐹_𝑟𝑒𝑠𝑡) [L/min] = 1.21 * 0.4 + 0.43 * 1.3 + 1.33 * 0.88 = 2.2134 (sum of Child-Pugh A adapted values of portal, hepatic arterial and renal blood flow)
Put all the numbers into the equation above:
1.11=  (2.35325 × 𝐷𝐹_𝑜𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑟 + 2.2134 + 0.78)/( 2.35325 + 2.83 + 0.78)
<-> 𝐷𝐹_𝑜𝑡ℎ𝑒𝑟  = (1.11*( 2.35325 + 2.83 + 0.78) -2.2134 - 0.78)/ 2.35325 = 1.54
So the factor applied to “other organs” is ~1.54 (might be a little different in the tutorial due to rounding etc.).
Hope that helps.

comments.body_text:
hm, probably a version issue. Can you please specify which tutorial file you refer to?
Best, Tobias

Ah Ok. In that case I am sure someone at ESQlab can sort that out. Isn't that so Stephan (@stephan.schaller@esqlabs.com)?
Best, Tobias

Dear @SCZ1174 , the exercise is an adoption of the work by Edginton et al 2008 as re-worked as a published exercise with the PBPK book from SA Peters.
Indeed, as stated in the exercise, modify the "Specific blood flow rate":

The other parameters you list are parameters in PK-Sim. Please elaborate on your corresponding questions as I am not sure I understand what answer you are looking for exactly.
Thanks,
Stephan

body_text:
Hi all,
I try to understand how distribution processes are implemented in MoBi and I was wondering where were hidden formulas which describe the binding to albumin in plasma ? Indeed, I could only find the albumin ontogeny factor which is just a parameter.
For example, I'm looking for the formula which talks about the stoichiometry between albumin and my compound ? Does this formula exist ?
Thank you very much for your help !

answer.body_text:
HI @Microbiotman , ALbumin binding is a static concept expressed within the parameter "fu" (fraction unbound in plasma). There is no actual binding process taking place.
You could implement this explicitly by defining a binding protein with expression according to the tissue distribution of Albumin, but this would neglect distribution kinetics / mass flow of Albumin-bound compound
To account for the latter, you have to implement Albumin as an actual PK.Sim compound in MoBi with synthesis (and degradation partially in the liver and through the endosomal system)

title: Half-life / clearance in ciprofloxacin tutorial
body_text:
Hi,
I am trying to learn how to work with PK Sim by the available tutorials. I have a question about the half life output. Ciprofloxacin has a half life of ~4h. But in the IV simulation it is calculated as 7h and for the oral simulation the half life is 11h.
I have tried to build my own simulations and have often the problem of a too long half life.
How does this work in PK Sim and is it possible to solve?

answer.body_text:
Hi,
Terminal half life time (calculated from the terminal 10% of data points).
(https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-simulations)
That is likely to be different to a half life calculated by e.g. NCA were a line is fitted through the last three data points.
Hope this might helps.
Best, Tobias

comments.body_text:
Hi,
Terminal half life time (calculated from the terminal 10% of data points).
(https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-simulations)
That is likely to be different to a half life calculated by e.g. NCA were a line is fitted through the last three data points.
Hope this might helps.
Best, Tobias

Dear Marjolein,
when comparing simulated vs observed PK parameters I would go for AUC (inf and tend), Cmax and maybe Tmax as "direct" parameters.
Other parameters like t1/2 or CL or more indirect in the sense as the contain implicit models (e.g. well stirred model) or assumptions (e.g. what is the terminal phase?). I would avoid these and use e.g. GOF plots showing plasma profiles.
Maybe others have more or different thoughts on that.
Best, Tobias

title: How to plug in the new 'tissue-specific ontogeny?
body_text:
Hello there,
I am a beginner in PK-Sim software.
I have a question about how to implement tissue-specific ontogeny, especially transporters.
According to the PK-Sim manual, I can import an ontogeny file for liver and intestine.
Can I import ontogeny data for the liver and kidney transporters separately?
For example, the preloaded enzyme CYP3A4 has two tissue-specific ontogenies like below:

However, I could not find ways to import tissue-specific ontogeny for my own transporters. I could import my sample Excel file as below, but I don't know how to specify the tissue or organ on the file.
I tried to specify the target organ in a column, but it seemed to be not working.

For the test data with meaningless values (not real expression data), I used this Excel file test.xlsx
.

The specific data for ontogeny, what I would like to use, is below:

Hepatic transporters: B D van Groen et al, Pharmacol Rev. 2021 https://doi.org/10.1124/pharmrev.120.000071 (might need to extract the value from the figures)
Renal transporters: Cheung et al., CPT 2019 https://doi.org/10.1002/cpt.1516 (There are specific values on the supplementary)

And FYI, B D van Groen et al wrote a discussion below:

Moreover, although this review is limited to ontogeny in the liver, DTs and/or DMEs are also abundant in other major organs, such as the kidney and gastrointestinal tract, and sanctuary sites, including the central nervous system (DeGorter et al., 2012). Developmental patterns of isoforms appear to be organ-dependent (Drozdzik et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019). However, for other organs, a quantitative approach as presented in this comprehensive review is not available but is highly needed. Once this is available, integration of ontogeny profiles in multiple tissues via a PBPK framework could provide a more holistic systems approach on the development of an entire organism (Smits et al., 2013).

Thank you for this open project!

answer.body_text: Consider separating the enzyme and its expression into two (or more). In other words add UGT1a9 (kidney only) and UGT1a9 (everything but kidney), for example.

title: How to add TMDD in the model of antibody?
body_text:
Hi,
I am a newbie here. I am working on PBPK model of an antibody. I have only find parameters related to FcRn to be adjusted. Could you please tell me how to add TMDD in the model? Thanks very much.

comments.body_text:
Hi,
For a typical TMDD model you need to extend the PBPK model in MoBi.
Typically, you can do the following steps to extend the model with the processes involved in TMDD:

Set up the base PBPK model in PK-Sim including the target protein as “Protein Binding Partner” (define the expression profile at Individual/Expression profile and the binding properties [dissociation & koff rate constant] at the drug molecule)
Export this model to MoBi and extend the model in Mobi with:
a) A target turnover reaction (incl. the turnover rate constant)
b) A reaction representing internalization/degradation of the drug-target complex (with the respective rate constant)

There is a tutorial where these steps (among others) are described in more detail:
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/ISOP-webinar---PK-Sim-MoBi-introduction
Unfortunately, the tutorial is a bit outdated, e.g., the synthesis part of the target turnover can now be implemented simpler since nowadays there is a parameter “Start amount” at the target molecule. The total turnover could nowadays look something like this:

Also, molecules to localize reactions (as e.g. “is interstitial”) are nowadays not needed anymore since you can define container criteria directly at the reactions you build.
Hope that helps …

title: what is first neighbour and second neighbour as implemented in Mobi?
body_text:
Hi,
So sometimes i meet with 'first neighbour' or 'second neighbour' in the path of parameters. Is it kind of default setting in Mobi? What are the general rules like? I am a bit lost when the model is big and I try to understand which parameter is now referring too...
Haini

answer.body_text: FIRST_NEIGHBOR and SECOND_NEIGHBOR refer to the "beginning" and "end" of a neighborhood. When you create a neighborhood in a spatial structure, you link from one compartment to another, i.e. from FIRST_NEIGHBOR to SECOND_NEIGHBOR.

title: Leveraging `opsuite` package to visualize body maps of PBPK models
body_text:
Sometimes it is nice to visualize and perform comparisons of PBPK models or model features using body maps (named anatograms).
For instance, comparing between populations (males vs females, adults vs infants, human vs murine, etc.) or between administrations (oral, dermal, IV, IP, etc.)
Since the PBPK models can be exported to R with {ospsuite} package, it can be integrated to the {gganatogram} package to create such body maps.
Then, the body map can be integrated to a website, shiny app or even video to make nice visuals of the models.
The main barrier being that your PK-Sim paths may need to be mapped to the anatogram body parts using a dictionary.
Here is a very simple example using a dummy model along with a screenshot below:

comments.body_text: Nice!

title: lipoprotein binding distribution
body_text:
Hi all,
I would like to add lipoprotein binding in PK-Sim. But in the compound building block --> Basic physico-chemistry, there are only 3 choices : Albumin, alpha-acid glycoprotein or unknown.
So I have 2 questions :

If we choose unknown but we know and inform the fraction unbound, what does the software understand ?
Is it possible to add lipoprotein binding in plasma with another way to proceed ?

Best,
Claire

answer.body_text:
If we choose unknown but we know and inform the fraction unbound, what does the software understand ?

THe selection of albumin or alpha-acid glycoprotein as a binding partner has only an impact on the ontogeny of the binding partner. By selecting "unknow", no maturation of binding partner will be considered, but you still can model the fraction unbound.

Is it possible to add lipoprotein binding in plasma with another way to proceed ?

You could add lipoprotein as a binding partner in the Expression-Building Block, set its expression to plasma, and implement binding of the compound to it. However, neither the free lipoprotein nor the drug-lipoprotein complex will be transported via blood flow. If you also want to consider blood flow transport of the complex, you would have to extend your model in MoBi.

However, neither the free lipoprotein nor the drug-lipoprotein complex will be transported via blood flow. If you also want to consider blood flow transport of the complex, you would have to extend your model in MoBi.

title: How to define new ethnic population?
body_text:
Hello there,
Since I was interested in the South Korean population, I tried to make a population on PK-Sim using their age, weight, and height distribution data which could be obtained from the national statistics in the country ["A Survey on the Dimension of Korean Persons" in Korean "한국인인체치수조사" by the Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy (Korean Government)].
Since the Koreans have different weight and height distribution over ages from European, East Asian, and Japanese in the population options of PK-Sim, I would like to make a user-defined population (Korean population).
However, I could not find the method to make a user-defined population. Even if I input the median height and weight for an individual (#965), the virtual population referencing the user-defined (height and weight) individual seems to generate one which has characteristics of the reference population in the PK-Sim (e.g., European, East Asian, and Japanese).
Here is a brief comparison of the PK-Sim incorporated populations and South Korean statistics (mean ± standard deviation) for an example. The statistics (mean and SD) for the South Korean population were averaged at 30-34 yr and 35-39 yr groups.



Weight (kg)
European (PK-Sim generated)

Can I define a user-defined population with new weight-age, height-age, and BMI-age correlation or functions on PK-Sim?
Attached below were for the detailed information.
Korean statistics

Body weights grouped by cities: Korean Body Weights.xlsx; Originally downloaded from https://kosis.kr/statHtml/statHtml.do?orgId=350&tblId=DT_35007_N132
Body weights grouped by sex and age: Korean Body Weight 3D measured.xlsx; Originally downloaded from https://kosis.kr/statHtml/statHtml.do?orgId=115&tblId=DT_115019_503&conn_path=I2
Height grouped by sex and age: Korean_Height 3D measured.xlsx; Originally downloaded from https://kosis.kr/statHtml/statHtml.do?orgId=115&tblId=DT_115019_432&conn_path=I2
Korean child's growth function: Korean_child_BW-Height.xlsx; Originally downloaded from https://knhanes.kdca.go.kr/knhanes/sub08/sub08_02.do
Dev. Korean population for Simcyp (EM/PM...): https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30921829/

answer.body_text:
I may be able to make a list of age, weight, and height using a random function of Excel, but it cannot make the other factors, including Ontogeny factor (albumin, alpha1-acid glycoprotein), organ volumes (fixed relative volume?, I don't know), blood flow, and so on.

Indeed, this is what I was referring to when I said, that this would require setting up a database for a Korean population similar to what now exists for Japanese and European, etc. populations in PK-Sim.
If you have all the data available, this should not be too much effort.

comments.body_text:
Dear @misol , thanks for this initiative. This would have to be solved on the database level.
Here is a few forum discussions on the topic:
#875
#87
Possibly @Yuri05 and @JanSchlender can support?

I may be able to make a list of age, weight, and height using a random function of Excel, but it cannot make the other factors, including Ontogeny factor (albumin, alpha1-acid glycoprotein), organ volumes (fixed relative volume?, I don't know), blood flow, and so on.

Indeed, this is what I was referring to when I said, that this would require setting up a database for a Korean population similar to what now exists for Japanese and European, etc. populations in PK-Sim.
If you have all the data available, this should not be too much effort.

Hello Misol,
As far as I can see, you only have body measures of the Korean population. These can be defined when generating a population in the PK-SIm Gui by defining a range matching your desired Korean population measures. Please do not do this in Excel, since the underlying anatomical and physiological information will only be scaled when a population is generated in PK-SIm. In case you change e.g. body weight in Excel afterwards, the sum of all organs would not match the new body weight.
You can also change ontogenies in the GUI within the individual under the respective ADME protein.
Things get a bit trickier once you want to change organ sizes, blood flows,... This can only be altered on a database level. Again, keep in mind the interdependencies of organ volumes (sum needs to add up to the body weight) and blood flows (sum needs to match Cardiac output). Details can be studied in the following references:
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40262-016-0422-3
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s40262-017-0539-z
https://link.springer.com/article/10.2165/00003088-200645100-00005
The easiest way to get this in PK-Sim is writing a script which modifies the exported population csv and re-import the file again. When conceptualising the script, I suggest to work with factors as deltas from the pre-selected population information. This way you are still making use of the population generation algorithm in PK-Sim.
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10928-007-9053-5

title: Accounting for polymorphism on a population level with clear group separation
body_text:
Hi all,
I was wondering about a smart incorporation of polymorphism on a population level. I would like to create equally sized groups for each genetic variant. While I see multiple ways of creating such a population, there are limitations for each approach.

When creating separate populations with different reference concentrations (as described here #128), I would like to add variability around this reference concentration. The tails of the each population will overlap with other genetic conditions and, thus, will encounter subjects in one condition which should belong to another condition. The ontogeny factor will even widen this distribution.
Creating a uniform distribution for a range, will not help to account for a clustering around the mean reference expression of a polymorphic condition.

Any good suggestion?

answer.body_text:
Hmm true.
Or create population in PK-Sim WITHOUT variability of reference concentration and/or ontogeny factor, sample "reference concentration" values in R and add to the population?

comments.body_text: How about 1., then export to csv, and post-process in R? Assign each individual to one of the populations based on the sampled reference concentration value.

Reference concentration * ontogeny factor and then assign to a group. But how to ensure equally sized groups? If you want a (log)normal distribution around each mean, you probably need to create multiple large populations for each polymorphic condition and sample from these populations within each reference concentration range

Hmm true.
Or create population in PK-Sim WITHOUT variability of reference concentration and/or ontogeny factor, sample "reference concentration" values in R and add to the population?

title: PK-Sim Biometric Tables
body_text:
Hello All,
This discussion concerns the biometric tables of PK-Sim to generate individuals and populations.
Part 1: How may I view the tables to ascertain the limits on individual characteristics? For example, it seems that it is not possible to create individuals with some combinations of biometric characteristics:

(a 30 yr old man 50 kg but 180 cm tall)
Part 2 (more complex): I would like to create or modify spatial structures in MoBi and then carry these changes into modified PK-Sim biometric tables for population simulations. For example, if I wanted to create a virtual population of athletes with higher proportion of muscle (larger muscle organ). Or, if I added a tumor compartment and wanted to simulate a distribution of tumor sizes/tissue compositions. Ideally, I could also modify the PK-Sim algorithm that references these tables so as to generate virtual populations with a customized algorithm.
Thank you very much!

comments.body_text:
Regarding you question 1: you can open the PK-Sim database with any SQLite viewer (e.g. you can use the online viewer https://sqliteviewer.app/ )

E.g. in the screenshot above you can see that the probabilty distribution for the 30yo male individual for the population NHANES White American is given by the lognormal distribution with Mean=80.35kg and GeoSD=1.196:

However the problem is not the distribution of weight (or height) itself, but the values in context of the Create Individual algorithm, which tries to create the "most probable individuum" for the given combination of {Weight, Height} based on the probability distributions of all organs.
If e.g. the weight is extremely low or extremely high, this might fail.
For those cases, better Create Individual algorithms are under discussion (@Aedginto maybe you can comment on this).
Regarding the 2nd question: you can create a population in PK-Sim first and export it to CSV. Then you could adjust the values in the table following your own algorithm (you can also add new columns to the table, e.g. if you have added a tumor tissue in MoBi, you can add columns for the tumor volume, blood flow etc.).
Then you can import this adjusted population back into PK-Sim and either use it to create new simulations in PK-Sim or combine it with imported MoBi models.

title: PBPK-PD model
body_text:
Hi!
I am working on a PBPK model and I´d like to evaluate my drug´s effect on urinary sodium excretion.
I have the following data: plasma concentrations of my drug over time, urinary excretion of my drug over time, and urinary sodium excretion over time.
What strategy would you recommend? May be, the easiest way is to find an equation that relates urinary sodium excretion to drug´s  plasma concentration or drug´s urinary excretion and add it as a parameter in Mobi. But I wonder if from a mechanistic point of view it is a better way.
If anyone can help me, I will appreciate it very much!
Thank you in advance
Ma Luisa

comments.body_text:
Hi @MaLuisaEti ,
It depends on how much detail you want to put into the model.
We have published a kidney model which could be adopted and extended with relevant PD mechanisms to model sodium excretion.: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6310870/
It could also be further extended with the mechanisms published by M Hallow et al.
But a simple solution as described by you might be a sufficient first step.
Best,
Stephan
Hi @MaLuisaEti , you can find the model that @StephanSchaller refers to here: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/SGLT2i-hyperfiltration-model

A week ago I had found that work but when I wanted to open the .mbp3 file of the supplementary material it gave me an error message (I assumed it was because of the MoBi version). But now I was able to access it without problems through the download link on GitHub.

title: When a drug has multiple metabolites via an enzyme, how should it be set up in PKsim?
body_text:
Suppose CYP3A4 metabolizes a drug to produce three metabolites M1, M2, and M3. I want to simulate the PK of M1-3 and have obtained the Km and Vmax of M1-3, but I have not done this before in PK-sim. Does anyone have a similar experience that can guide me?
Thanks!

comments.body_text:
A single enzyme can only create a single metabolite in PK-Sim so you will need two enzymes for two metabolites, and so on. If CYP3A4 happens to create two different metabolites, you will have to insert it twice, for instance.

This is not quite true: you can produce several metabolites for the same enzyme in PK-Sim.

Add enzyme in the individual (only once!)

Add new metabolization process for this enzyme and for every metabolite in compound (you will need to enter your Km/Vmax for each process)

When creating a simulation: select the compounds and all metabolites which time courses should be plotted

When it comes to the process mapping: PK-Sim suggests only 1 of created metabolization processes as per default.
Use the plus button to add a process for the same enzyme and other metabolites!




Will look like this at the end:

What you get in the simulation is then:


s. the example project:
M1M2M3.zip

title: How do I build the drug ADME process inside PK-sim when there are several drugs?
body_text: In PK-sim to build a drug metabolic network, precursor drugs are all converted into active forms, that the precursor only need to enter the absorption and distribution plate, the active form input distribution、elimination and  excretion of plate parameters is it?

answer.body_text:
If you want to follow plasma PK of both, prodrug and active form, you implement both, prodrug and active form as a compound in PK-Sim.
You then set up a metabolic process for the pro-drug to be converted into its active form.

comments.body_text:
If you want to follow plasma PK of both, prodrug and active form, you implement both, prodrug and active form as a compound in PK-Sim.
You then set up a metabolic process for the pro-drug to be converted into its active form.

title: AUC0-24h
body_text: Hello, I'm modeling with PK-SIM recently, and I need to use the parameter AUC0-24h for calculation. Do AUC_tD1-tD2 represent AUC0-24h in the pharmacokinetic parameters of population simulation?

comments.body_text:
AUC_tD1-tD2 is the AUC from the administration time of the first dose until the administration time of the second dose
(s. the documentation: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-simulations#pk-parameters)
So if your administration schema is "once daily" - it would give you AUC0-24h. Otherwise: no

title: CYP3A expression profile

comments.body_text:
Dear @SCZ1174,
I'm not sure what you are looking for when you mean CYP3A.
In humans, the most likely candidate when it comes to pharmacokinetics context you are looking for CYP3A4.  https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/gene/?term=CYP3A
Regarding the entries, I believe it should not matter which entry you use.
Did you check that both have the same expression profiles ?

Henrik

title: Questions about parameter identification process.
body_text:
Why there's no "Parameter Identification" option when the Simulation is based on "Population", though I can make the "Parameter Identification" when the simulation is built with "Individual".
Based on "Individual" simulation, I did the "Parameter Identification", but sometimes there was no comparison chart in the field of "Error history", "Predicted vs. Observed" and "Time Profile", I want to know the reason and the handling methods.
Thanks so much.

comments.body_text:
Hi,
the "standard workflow" in PK-sim is to optimize relevant processes in the simulation for an individual and than extrapolate it to a population. Other ways are currently not possible.
Regarding 2.) not sure why this sometimes happens. Is the PI is running to the end? like the notifacition in the screenshot shows up?

I can only reproduce it if I uncheck the "refresh option" on the top left on the window.
Or if I use non standard methods like the multiple optimizations with randomized start values

Hope this might help
Best, Tobias

Hi Tarya,
looks like your PI finishes very fast after only one second. That is probably to short for generating a history. Did you use Levenberg Marquard as algorithm? Try to change the start value for specific clearance to 1 and use Monte Carlo as algorithm. Lets see what you get than.
Best, Tobias

Hi, I guess you see the pictures with MC as it runs several cycles before getting the final result. The LM is faster and probably finishes already after the first round. So not seeing the history seems to be no error but be design and it is probably not so relevant. Important are the final results. They should be very similar with LM and MC.
Best, Tobias

title: Published: A generic framework for the physiologically-based pharmacokinetic platform qualification of PK-Sim and its application to predicting cytochrome P450 3A4-mediated drug-drug interactions
body_text:
We have published the OSP framework for platform qualification with a first showcase of predicting cytochrome P450 3A4-mediated drug-drug interactions. Thanks to all contributors!
Sebastian Frechen, Juri Solodenko, Thomas Wendl, André Dallmann, Ibrahim Ince, Thorsten Lehr  Jörg Lippert, Rolf Burghaus. A generic framework for the physiologically-based pharmacokinetic platform qualification of PK-Sim and its application to predicting cytochrome P450 3A4-mediated drug-drug interactions. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol
. 2021 Jun;10(6):633-644.
Abstract
The success of applications of physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling in drug development and drug labeling has triggered regulatory agencies to demand rigorous demonstration of the predictive capability of the specific PBPK platform for a particular intended application purpose. The effort needed to comply with such qualification requirements exceeds the costs for any individual PBPK application. Because changes or updates of a PBPK platform would require (re-)qualification, a reliable and efficient generic qualification framework is needed. We describe the development and implementation of an agile and sustainable technical framework for automatic PBPK platform (re-)qualification of PK-Sim® embedded in the open source and open science GitHub landscape of Open Systems Pharmacology. The qualification approach enables the efficient assessment of all aspects relevant to the qualification of a particular purpose and provides transparency and traceability for all stakeholders. As a showcase example for the power and versatility of the qualification framework, we present the qualification of PK-Sim® for the intended purpose of predicting cytochrome P450 3A4 (CYP3A4)-mediated drug-drug interactions (DDIs). Several perpetrator PBPK models featuring various degrees of CYP3A4 modulation and different types of mechanisms (competitive inhibition, mechanism-based inactivation, and induction) were coupled with a set of PBPK models of sensitive CYP3A4 victim drugs. Simulations were compared to a comprehensive data set of 135 observations from published clinical DDI studies. The platform's overall predictive performance showed reasonable accuracy and precision (geometric mean fold error of 1.4 for both area under the plasma concentration-time curve ratios and peak plasma concentration ratios with/without perpetrator) and suggests that PK-Sim® can be applied to quantitatively assess CYP3A4-mediated DDI in clinically untested scenarios.

title: Published: Predictive Performance of Physiology-Based Pharmacokinetic Dose Estimates for Pediatric Trials
body_text:
Predictive Performance of Physiology-Based Pharmacokinetic Dose Estimates for Pediatric Trials: Evaluation With 10 Bayer Small-Molecule Compounds in Children
Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSP-based-publications-and-content#310
Development and guidance of dosing schemes in children have been supported by physiology-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling for many years. PBPK models are built on a generic basis, where compound- and system-specific parameters are separated and can be exchanged, allowing the translation of these models from adults to children by accounting for physiological differences. Owing to these features, PBPK modeling is a valuable approach to support clinical decision making for dosing in children. In this analysis, we evaluate pediatric PBPK models for 10 small-molecule compounds that were applied to support clinical decision processes at Bayer for their predictive power in different age groups. Ratios of PBPK-predicted to observed PK parameters for the evaluated drugs in different pediatric age groups were estimated. Predictive performance was analyzed on the basis of a 2-fold error range and the bioequivalence range (ie, 0.8 ≤ predicted/observed ≤ 1.25). For all 10 compounds, all predicted-to-observed PK ratios were within a 2-fold error range (n = 27), with two-thirds of the ratios within the bioequivalence range (n = 18). The findings demonstrate that the pharmacokinetics of these compounds was successfully and adequately predicted in different pediatric age groups. This illustrates the applicability of PBPK for guiding dosing schemes in the pediatric population.
