title: Changing FcRn kinetics/ implementing turnover
body_text:
Hi All,
Can anybody give me some pointers to better underdstand FcRn kinetics in PK-Sim/Mobi?
Currently, I have the impression that no turnover of FcRn or degradation for the drug-FcRn complex is implemented. I do understand that FcRn binding is meant to minimize this degradation, but I would like to be able to implement it to describe the short half-life of drugs that bind to FcRn at neutral pH as well. In Mobi, I can implement degradation of the complex, but then I also want to implement degradation of FcRn itself (to avoid changing FcRn concentrations as a consequence of drug binding) for which I need to compensate with FcRn synthesis. I am now in doubt whether I need to use the endosomal or the membrane concentration of FcRn to calculate synthesis rate and if the latter is actually a parameter in the model. Also, I cannot find the reactions in mobi describing transfer of FcRn to endosome, or is this not defined as a reaction?
Thanks for your help and already a happy new year to everyone!
Wilbert

answer.body_text:
Hey everyone,
The discussion on this forum helped me a lot to properly look into it and work on a solution for the identified problem. The results are now documented and published, you can find a view-only link with model files here. We will make the model files available on GitHub as well. Feel free to comment, question or discuss this further. @msevestre Maybe this can be moved to a discussion also to make it more easy to find back?

comments.body_text:
Dear Wilbert,
please find the details how the FcRn mediated recycling for large molecules in PK-sim described here
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4
Hope this might help you.
Best, Tobias

Dear Wilbert,
yes, you are absolutely right that there is no turnover of FcRn implemented. While the publication that @tobiasK2001 mentions gives you an oversight, it will not help you understand the explicit implementation of what you are trying to do. Section 5 in the Supplementary Information might help you understand how the FcRn SteadyState is defined.
FcRn is located in the "Endogeneous_IgG" Organ. While the initial concentration in the endosome is defined (Parameter "..|Start concentration of free FcRn (endosome)" as a Parameter in the FcRn molecule in you Molecules BuildingBlock), the initial concentrations in Plasma and Interstitium are then calculated based in this value and the initial value of endogenous IgG.
I would thus suggest to create a turnover reaction for FcRn in the endosome of the Endogenous_IgG Organ using "..|Start concentration of free FcRn (endosome)" as the reference steadystate (use "endosome" as "match tag" container criteria.
Let me know if you need further instructions on how to do this.

but I would like to be able to implement it to describe the short half-life of drugs that bind to FcRn at neutral pH as well.

Just out of curiosity: what is the half-life of the drugs you are talking about?
And a happy new year to you, too!
Best,
Stephan

Dear Tobias and Stephan,
Thanks a lot for your help! I will try to get this working and will get back with the result :)
The main basis for this shorter half-life with increased binding at pH 7.4 comes from this paper  and their figure as inserted, where v3, 4, 5, 6 have increasing affinity at acidic and neutral pH up to  3 and 35 nM, respectively. Any alternative interpretation of these data is also welcome of course :)

That's indeed interesting.
But I am not sure if TMDD through FcRn could be the solution.
Is the soluble IL-6R also a FcRn binder (as it seems to have a long half-life by itself, but I could not find anything)?
If so: An alternative guess from my side would be that the high-affinity FcRn binders (300-fold increased affinity in comparison to IgG1) would lead to an FcRn saturation which will cause the half-life of all FcRn binders in circulation to drop significantly.

Hi Stephan, sIL-6R should not bind FcRn as in this study sIL-6R concentrations are decreased by the IgGs that bind sIL-6R extracellularly and release it in the endosomes. If it would bind FcRn, this would not be an effective strategy. It might indeed be an option that the increased binding at pH7 causes saturation in the endosomes as well and also shortens half-life of the IgG variant itself as well as its competitors, but at an antibody concentration of around 200 nM, I don't think this is an option given the current estimates of FcRn concentrations.

If it would bind FcRn, this would not be an effective strategy.

If FcRn is saturated, it would be effective.
But I agree, that FcRn saturation might be unlikely with the low dose of antibody.
But I also don't see that "normal" turnover of FcRn or Drug-FcRn could be a suitable explanation as other high-affinity FcRn binders (YTE antibodies) show a half-life of 70 days (and more): https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3837853/
However, it might be a compound-specific mechanism that triggerst Drug-Complex-FcRn degradation. Maybe it is the IL-6R.
I haven't checked the publication, but can you exclude binding and TMDD to/at other targets?

The principles of sweeping antibodies are explained here: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5627591/figure/f0001/
These antibodies are designed with pH-dependent binding to both their antigen and FcRn. The antibody collects the antigen outside cells and then the complex binds to FcRn on the cell membrane at pH 7-7.4, initiating cellular uptake and endosomal trafficking. As pH lowers in the endosome, the antigen is released from the antibody but the antibody remains bound to FcRn (ideally). The result is that the antigen is cleared but the antibody is recycled back to the extracellular fluid.
It is worth noting that most antibodies and proteins have negligible binding to FcRn at neutral pH. Their cellular uptake is largely by passive fluid-phase pinocytosis (non-specific). That is why most PBPK models for antibodies only consider FcRn binding in the intracellular endosomes.
The above PK profiles are observed because of increased active uptake into endothelial cells and leukocytes by FcRn binding on the cell membrane at neutral pH without enough recovery in low pH endosomes.
Modeling this with the whole-body PK-Sim framework is near impossible and the uncertainties cloud any potential conclusions. You will need to add pH-dependent antigen binding, pH-dependent FcRn binding, active drug uptake through binding to FcRn on cell membranes and finally endosomal trafficking. I would recommend going with a minimal model + FcRn in MATLAB.

without enough recovery in low pH endosomes.

Yes, the short half-life of e.g. PH-v6 (KD FcRn pH7.4 = 35 nM and KD FcRn pH6 = 3 nM) is what puzzles me.
Why would endosomal recovery be reduced?

You raise a great point. I made a poor assumption there. It is certainly plausible that recovery from the endosome is the same for each of these drug scenarios (with identical acidic FcRn affinity). You could even go so far as to approximate elimination with a first order rate constant in the endosome. As the neutral FcRn affinities become stronger, a greater proportion of total drug is sequestered in the endosome and thus the proportion of total drug eliminated over time increases. This would explain the sharper elimination slopes in the plasma PK profile. A sensitivity analysis with a minimal model might be illuminating.

The feature for FcRn binding in plasma doesn't appear to be set up for internalization yet

As a gross alternative, lumping the active (FcRn binding and internalization from the cell membrane) and passive (fluid phase pinocytosis) cellular uptake rates and optimizing the kup parameter while keeping the FcRn affinity at pH 6.0 constant gives you relevant results for the antibody. Modeling the antigen and complex interactions is another story. In this case, kup for PH-v3 is 0.77 1/min and kup for PH-v6 is 2.01 1/min. I used 5.34 nm for hydrodynamic radii. KD at pH 6.0 values extracted from the paper (15 nM and 3 nM respectively).

Of course this does not take into account the co-administration of IgG (as in the experiment) but it's a conceptual starting point.

Yes Wilbert, I did use 288 nM for the test but the feature does not work as intended. I created the screenshot afterward (thanks it is fixed now!). I haven't yet opened up Mobi to look at what the feature is doing but (based on the resulting PK profile) I imagine it is just binding and sequestering at the cell membrane rather than internalizing and trafficking to the intracellular endosomes.
The profiles I simulated are WITHOUT using this feature (i.e. KD FcRn in plasma/int is 999999 uM as default), only lumping the active and passive cellular uptake into the kup parameter.
FcRn degradation and turnover on the cell membrane would only be relevant to include in the model if binding of the antibody induced internalization. However what is more likely (as in the case of HER2 for instance) is that the mAb-FcRn receptor complex gets internalized according to the natural turnover rate of FcRn. Many membrane-bound receptors are in a constant flux from the membrane and back to the endosomes/cytoplasm as the cell membrane is quite dynamic in nature. Since every time an old FcRn receptor (or complex) is internalized it is simultaneously replaced with a new naked FcRn receptor, you can model kon/koff binding and a first order internalization rate for the complex without having to model the turnover explicitly (see the approximation example in Shah 2012 and in my 2017 trastuzumab paper).
From what I understand of what you have done, interstitial/cell membrane FcRn binding and subsequent degradation of the complex is another way of approximating the situation however then you cannot use the in vitro values for the pH 6 binding - the intracellular processes end up being lumped into the first order degradation of the complex. This would throw a reviewer for a loop because you still have passive uptake and intracellular FcRn binding going on that is not linked.

Here's what we're dealing with:

Mobi is challenging for this type of system because it only accepts passive transport of molecular species for rates; in other words to maintain a constant FcRn on the surface you will be replenishing it with [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x kint.

I haven't yet opened up Mobi to look at what the feature is doing but (based on the resulting PK profile)

@prvmalik How did you change kup in PK-Sim if you haven't opened MoBi, yet? This parameter should only be accessible in MoBi, no?

As a gross alternative, lumping the active (FcRn binding and internalization from the cell membrane) and passive (fluid phase pinocytosis) cellular uptake rates and optimizing the kup parameter while keeping the FcRn affinity at pH 6.0 constant gives you relevant results for the antibody.


The profiles I simulated are WITHOUT using this feature (i.e. KD FcRn in plasma/int is 999999 uM as default), only lumping the active and passive cellular uptake into the kup parameter.

@prvmalik can you elaborate a bit on how you are lumping these processes? Per default PK-Sim does not consider internalization of the drug-FcRn complex:

Are you thus reducing the recycling rate (NetMassTransfer_EndosomalToInt/Pls_FcRn_Complex) if you want to consider an increased drug-FcRn Internalization?
Also, in our discussion here, we might confuse internalization of FcRn and recycling vs FcRn degradation (actual molecular degradation and re-synthesis).

Thanks for your questions Dr. Schaller.
You can access the kup parameter in PK-Sim by viewing "advanced" parameters under the "vascular physiology" tab.
As for the lumping, process 1 (kon, koff, subsequent kint) is lumped with process 2 (kup) translating to net uptake of unbound mAb into the endosomal space. Of course, as I said before this is a gross approximation because what you end up with is only unbound mAb being taken up rather than unbound mAb (from kup) and FcRn-bound mAb (from kint). However if affinity is sufficiently strong it may be possible to assume that equilibrium is quickly reached at the new pH 6 affinity in the endosome.

The normal FcRn steady state kinetics at pH 6 inside the endosome remain unchanged. I only increased the kup parameter numerically to reflect that more drug is entering the endosome by binding to FcRn on the cell membrane and subsequent internalization

You can access the kup parameter in PK-Sim by viewing "advanced" parameters under the "vascular physiology" tab.

Thanks... must have missed this one.
As for MoBi, Drug-FcRn-Complex can be transported by passive transports (as it is not a "stationary" molecule).

Of course, as I said before this is a gross approximation because what you end up with is only unbound mAb being taken up rather than unbound mAb (from kup) and FcRn-bound mAb (from kint).

Yes, an approximation but a quick shortcut.
In MoBi you could substract the [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x kint term from the existing formula in the NetMassTransfer_EndosomalToInt/Pls_FcRn_Complex trannsports (be sure to use "TARGET|MOLECULE|Concentration" and just be aware you might need to reduce K_uptake ("..|Rate constant for endosomal uptake") accordingly).
@prvmalik you might have mixed up [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x kint and [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x krec in your drawing above.

Thanks @prvmalik for including these clarifying figures.
There are a few things I still don't get. The current implementation in PK-Sim as I understand it looks in a schematic way as depicted below

Compared to the scheme from the publication as included by Stephan, there are a few extra processes in my scheme:

there is internalisation of the drug-FcRn complex
there is recycling of the free FcRn

Are these processes not included in the PK-Sim model or just not in the published scheme? If they are not included in the model, is this just a simplification of the equations while keeping the same behaviour or are there some consequences for molecules binding at pH7?
You are saying "Mobi is challenging for this type of system because it only accepts passive transport of molecular species for rates; in other words to maintain a constant FcRn on the surface you will be replenishing it with [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x kint."
I do not understand this completely, in the reactions building block, you can implement any order of reactions I thought, can we not include synthesis as a reaction (rather than transport) in a way as depicted below?

The initial endosomal FcRn concentration should be equal to the total endosomal FcRn concentration as kdeg, kint and krec are equal for free and mab-bound FcRn. If this concentration is not available as parameter, we can just extract the value from a simulation without degradation in Mobi. It would make more sense physiologically to implement the synthesis to the membrane bound FcRn, maybe, but I don't think this will alter the behaviour of the model.
Sorry for the confusion on my side, please let me know if you think this discussion is running in circles and not progressing anymore :)

Are these processes not included in the PK-Sim model or just not in the published scheme? If they are not included in the model, is this just a simplification of the equations while keeping the same behaviour or are there some consequences for molecules binding at pH7?

Recycling of the free FcRn is included and indeed not correctly depicted in the figure i posted.
However, internalization of the Drug-FcRn complex is not. It seems this was omitted as the FcRn module was not forseen to be used for pH7-FcRn binders (they were also not part of the validation data set).
Assuming a significant fraction of FcRn on the membrane is bound to drug and the internalization rate is the same as for the unbound FcRn this could have consequences for molecules binding at pH7.
Thanks @StephanSchaller that helps a lot! And we probably cannot implement it ourselves as only passive transport is possible, as indicated by @prvmalik

@wilbertdew , I am not sure if maybe @prvmalik meant sth. else but within MoBi this could be implemented by simply substracting the missing term from the exocytosis transport reaction for the Drug-FcRn complex as I described above:

In MoBi you could substract the [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x kint term from the existing formula in the NetMassTransfer_EndosomalToInt/Pls_FcRn_Complex trannsports (be sure to use "TARGET|MOLECULE|Concentration" and just be aware you might need to reduce K_uptake ("..|Rate constant for endosomal uptake") accordingly).

Yes, I trust that it will be able to be done as Stephan has described. What I meant was that it's more complicated in Mobi because you have to use the 'Passive Transport' building blocks (this is their name, not necessarily meaning passive transport literally).

@prvmalik you might have mixed up [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x kint and [extracellular mAb-FcRn complex] x krec in your drawing above.

Yes it will take some finessing in Mobi to get mass balance correct

Hi @StephanSchaller,
I am still working on it when I have time, but it seems I can include bidirectional transport for each molecule by extending the selection of molecules of each monodirectional transport that is already present in the model. It is a bit of work to recalculate the parameter values from the original model and I am documenting all steps so hopefully I can share my solution in more detail soon.
Best,
Wilbert

Hi @wilbertdew , thanks. Good luck and looking forward to hearing how your solution will work.
Best,
Stephan

title: How to couple Emax PD model to PBPK model
body_text:
Hi Everyone,
I really hope someone can help me here. I have built PBPK model for a small lipophilic molecule in PK-Sim. Separately, in NONMEM, I have also built a simple Emax model relating plasma concentrations of that molecule to increase in cardiovascular effects like heart rate and blood pressure.
Now, what I want to do is to link PBPK model to PD model in Mobi and simulate different dosing scenarios where I can look into how plasma concentrations are affecting cardiac concentrations and consequently driving heart rate. I understand that I have to first define the Emax equation in Mobi and that's where I am having trouble right now. I know there is a tutorial but its not been helpful to me so far.
So there are 4 parameters in Emax equation:

Emax (which is dependent on plasma concentration)
Plasma concentration (which is time-variant)
EC50 (which is time-invariant)
Effect

Obviously, Effect = Emax * plasma concentration /(plasma concentration +EC50)
Now, I have defined EC50 which is a constant and time-invariant. That is easy. Here are the next steps I am stuck at:

How to define effect and Emax and its units which is beats per minute for heart rate?
How about plasma concentration? How to put that into the equation as time-variant?

I am also willing to set up a small zoom and skype meeting and I really hope that someone can come to my rescue.
Regards,
Sumeet

answer.body_text:
Hi Sumeet
I don't know if when you say that the tutorial hasn't been helpful, you refer to this paper.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5080648/
The supplemental information is a step by step procedure of how to set up a PD model in MoBi.
The example is an IC50 but the way you set the equations should be pretty much the same to your EC50.
I am not sure if MoBi has beats per minute as units but I think that you can use "Count" unitless and have it as beat per minute.
Regards,
Ricardo

title: Track internalization into intracellular when implementing TMDD
body_text:
Hi all,
I have implemented TMDD with a protein binding partner in interstitial space using the usual method (add synthesis/degradation and internalization of the complex). My question is, how can I set up a way to track the amount of complex that gets transported into the intracellular space via the complex internalization? If I right click and "insert molecule" using the reactions diagram in MoBi, I can connect to a reaction but get a warning that a reactant is not present.
My thought was to create a dummy molecule initialized to 0 to use to track this amount. In the end I would ask:

Is there a more simple way to track this value than a dummy molecule?
Is it possible to have this reaction move from the interstitial space into the intracellular (thus getting the intracellular concentration)
As a final side question, when Tissue or Whole Organ concentrations are calculated, do they include drug-receptor complexes, or only unbound drug? If the latter, I assume an observer would need to be defined to track the sum of all drug and complex values.

Any help or recommendations is very appreciated!

answer.body_text:
Is there a more simple way to track this value than a dummy molecule?

The easiest way which comes into my mind would be:
a) Activate "Create process rate" parameter for the reaction/transport of interest.
b) Create a new parameter with initial value 0 and define it as a state variable.
c) Set the right hand side (RHS) of this parameter either to a particular process rate parameter (if you want to track just one single reaction or transport) or to the sum of process rates for all processes of interest
d) Set this parameter to plottable
Check the example project: Reaction_Transfer.zip
Here, a reaction A2B is defined in 2 containers which (reversibly) transforms molecule A into molecule B.
In the organism, 2 parameters are defined: the first one tracks one particular reaction, the 2nd all reactions
Activate reaction process rate parameter


Tracking 1 reaction


Tracking N reactions



Plot transferred amounts



Is it possible to have this reaction move from the interstitial space into the intracellular (thus getting the intracellular concentration)

This can be done by a passive transport. Reactions always take place within the same container per design.

when Tissue or Whole Organ concentrations are calculated, do they include drug-receptor complexes, or only unbound drug? If the latter, I assume an observer would need to be defined to track the sum of all drug and complex values.

You assumption is correct: complexes are not included and you would need to define an extra observer for this.

title: How to solve the difference of PK curve obtained by setting different dosage simulation?
body_text: I am a beginner of PK-sim.The difference between the PK curve obtained when I administered the simulated drug at different doses (20-500mg) and the measured value is relatively large.The simulated value is much larger than the observed value when the dose of 20mg is set, and the simulated value is much smaller than the observed value when the dose of 500mg is set. What parameters are more critical when fitting PK curves of different doses?

comments.body_text:
Hi,
ab bit more details would help to answer your question. E.g. are you talking about IV or PO administration?
Best, Tobias

And, did you check out this tutorial?
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5080648/
Best, Tobias

title: Parameter identification Vmax and Km in PK-Sim 11
body_text: How can I select Vmax or Km for parameter identification in PK-Sim 11? The previous version had the tab "Reaction" on the drop down menu for organs in parameter identification section where you can locate enzyme parameters like Vmax or Km and select for parameter identification. But it seems that PK-Sim 11 does not have the "Reaction" on the menu list. How can I resolve this? N.B. If I try to open a file in version 11 that was saved under version 10, the "reaction" menu appears.

comments.body_text: Try using the "search" functionality in the parameter selection tab:

title: Ketoconazole PBPK Model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have been working on a PBPK model of the CYP3A4 and P-gp perpetrator drug ketoconazole including its two metabolites N-deacetylketoconazole and N-deacetyl-N-hydroxyketoconazole. The parent-metabolites model is able to predict and describe drug-drug interactions (DDIs) with the CYP3A4 victim drugs alfentanil, alprazolam, midazolam and triazolam, as well as with the P-gp victim drug digoxin. Here, both parent and metabolites are modeled as reversible inhibitors of CYP3A4 and P-gp.
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation has been published online in Pharmaceutics and the ketoconazole model as well as PK-Sim projects presenting the predicted DDI studies can be found here.
Related publication:
Marok, F.Z.; Wojtyniak, J.-G.; Fuhr, L.M.; Selzer, D.; Schwab, M.; Weiss, J.; Haefeli, W.E.; Lehr, T. A Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Model of Ketoconazole and Its Metabolites as Drug–Drug Interaction Perpetrators. Pharmaceutics 2023, 15, 679
All the best,
Fatima

title: Input of blood composition data
body_text: Using PK Sim & MoBi I am trying to develop a model for a compound that binds covalently to blood proteins and to blood cells. I understand that the covalent binding reactions should be developed in MoBi. For each patient I have data such as blood albumin, hematocrit, hemoglobin, and erythrocyte count.  I understand that the observed blood albumin and hemoglobin can be manually input into PK Sim. How do I put the observed values for hematocrit & erythrocyte count?

comments.body_text: moving the issue to the forum (@zt1947 please always ask the questions there)

@zt1947 , hematocrit is an existing parameter you can overwrite with your own value.
You could also create a dummy molecule for the RBCs within Plasma or RBC, depending on where your drug binds (inner / cytosolic structure or outer membrane). Initial value could be set by (1-HTC)*VolumeWholeBlood/meanRBCsize or sth. similar. Make sure to use the Avogadro constant to scale numbers to amounts.

title: Can't run model with Lipophilicity value > 10
body_text:
Hello,
I recently met an issue with the logP I have to set in compound parameters on PK-sim. I wanted to set this value to 11.2 but the software return this error "Value for lipophilicity should be less than or equal to 10 Log Units".
I would like to know why a value higher than 10 can't be handled by the software ?
Thank you in advance

answer.body_text:
@jonathanlsn, extending the comment ba @tobiasK2001,  the reason for PK-Sim to limit this range is related to "model confidence".
The partitioning methods contained in PK-Sim are developed for pharmaceutical NCEs where the "Rule of 5" referred to by @tobiasK2001 are mostly adhered to (LogP < 5 is one of these rules).
This means that the PK-Sim predicted partitioning based on a LogP>5 might be wrong, as the partition coefficient methods available in PK-Sim are not "qualified" for such compounds/LogP ranges.
We have worked with a couple of compounds (organic chemicals) with LogP > 5 (but < 10) and looked into other partitioning methods (e.g. Poulin & Haddad) to better characterize the ADME, but there are quite a few additional aspects that make things complicated for highly lipophilic chemicals.

comments.body_text:
Dear Jonathan,
why do you think your compound could hav a logP of 11.2? PK-Sim sets a limit here because a logP > 10 would be well outside the accepted possible ADMET space of NCEs. See here for more details
https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11259830/
best, Tobias

@jonathanlsn, extending the comment ba @tobiasK2001,  the reason for PK-Sim to limit this range is related to "model confidence".
The partitioning methods contained in PK-Sim are developed for pharmaceutical NCEs where the "Rule of 5" referred to by @tobiasK2001 are mostly adhered to (LogP < 5 is one of these rules).
This means that the PK-Sim predicted partitioning based on a LogP>5 might be wrong, as the partition coefficient methods available in PK-Sim are not "qualified" for such compounds/LogP ranges.
We have worked with a couple of compounds (organic chemicals) with LogP > 5 (but < 10) and looked into other partitioning methods (e.g. Poulin & Haddad) to better characterize the ADME, but there are quite a few additional aspects that make things complicated for highly lipophilic chemicals.

title: Blood/plasma concentration ratio has no impact on PK in PK-Sim standard model
body_text:
Dear all,
When using in a model the PK-Sim standard distribution model, I tried to modify the parameter "Blood/plasma concentration ratio", and simulating both the output "peripheral venous blood/whole blood" and "PVB/plasma". By changing the "Blood/plasma concentration ratio" there is no difference in the PK profiles generated, which I find very odd. Do you have any explanation? I could replicate this behavior also in the example model file for paracetamol provided with PK-Sim, so this is not limited to my model. (To replicate, I just changed the distribution model from R&R to PK-Sim standard in the example). Just as comparison, with the R&R distribution model the PK in plasma and blood was modified by changing the blood/plasma parameters for this model, so what I observed seems to be specific for the PK-Sim distribution model.
Does anyone has any explanation? And even more important, is this behavior correct?
Thanks in advance for your clarifications!
Best,
Donato

comments.body_text:
Well, the explanation is: not "Blood/plasma concentration ratio" (B2P) itself is used in the "PK-Sim standard" distribution model, but its formula.
This is how it looks like in PK-Sim standard model:
Parameter: S1|C1|Blood/Plasma concentration ratio
	Formula: (f_water_rbc + f_lipids_rbc * 10^LogP + f_proteins_rbc * KProt) * fu * HCT - HCT + 1
		fu: S1|C1|Fraction unbound (plasma)(0.11)
		LogP: S1|C1|Lipophilicity(3 [Log Units])
		KProt: S1|C1|Partition coefficient (water/protein)(22.2331460674157)
		HCT: S1|Organism|Hematocrit(0.47)
		f_lipids_rbc: S1|Organism|Vf (lipid, blood cells)(0.005)
		f_proteins_rbc: S1|Organism|Vf (protein,blood cells)(0.325)
		f_water_rbc: S1|Organism|Vf (water,blood cells)(0.625)

Parameter: S1|C1|Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma)
	Formula: (f_water_rbc + f_lipids_rbc * 10^LogMA + f_proteins_rbc * KProt)*fu
		fu: S1|C1|Fraction unbound (plasma)(0.11)
		LogMA: S1|C1|Lipophilicity(3 [Log Units])
		KProt: S1|C1|Partition coefficient (water/protein)(22.2331460674157)
		f_lipids_rbc: S1|Organism|Vf (lipid, blood cells)(0.005)
		f_proteins_rbc: S1|Organism|Vf (protein,blood cells)(0.325)
		f_water_rbc: S1|Organism|Vf (water,blood cells)(0.625)

As you can easily see, in fact it is Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma) = (HCT - 1 + B2P) / HCT
But due to the fact that B2P is not used directly, changing it does not have any effect.
Comparing it with e.g. Rogers and Rowland model:
Parameter: S2|C1|Blood/Plasma concentration ratio
	Formula: (f_water_rbc + f_lipids_rbc * 10^LogP + f_proteins_rbc * KProt) * fu * HCT - HCT + 1
		fu: S2|C1|Fraction unbound (plasma)(0.11)
		LogP: S2|C1|Lipophilicity(3 [Log Units])
		KProt: S2|C1|Partition coefficient (water/protein)(22.2331460674157)
		HCT: S2|Organism|Hematocrit(0.47)
		f_lipids_rbc: S2|Organism|Vf (lipid, blood cells)(0.005)
		f_proteins_rbc: S2|Organism|Vf (protein,blood cells)(0.325)
		f_water_rbc: S2|Organism|Vf (water,blood cells)(0.625)

Parameter: S2|C1|Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma unbound)
	Formula: (HCT - 1 + B2P) / fu / HCT
		B2P: S2|C1|Blood/Plasma concentration ratio(1.19438493679775)
		fu: S2|C1|Fraction unbound (plasma)(0.11)
		HCT: S2|Organism|Hematocrit(0.47)

Parameter: S2|C1|Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma)
	Formula: K_pu_bc*fu
		fu: S2|C1|Fraction unbound (plasma)(0.11)
		K_pu_bc: S2|C1|Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma unbound)(12.8507724719101)

As you can easily see from the last 2 equations
Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma) = (HCT - 1 + B2P) / HCT
Which is exactly the same as in the PK-Sim standard model. Just with the difference that B2P i used directly.
I guess this is due to historical reasons. "PK-Sim standard" was the first distribution model used in PK-Sim, R&R was added later.
"PK-Sim standrad" did not rely on B2P, which was introduced with R&R. "PK-Sim standard" was left AS IS.
As far as I remember, in PK-Sim 4 B2P was hidden in case "PK-Sim standard" distribution was selected - to avoid thid kind of confusion.
Don't remember why "PK-Sim standard" was kept AS IS - should be doable to rewrite the equations in the same way as in R&R.

Hi @Yuri05 ,
Thanks a lot for the explanation. I would say that if possible, it would be better to use directly the value provided, instead of the formula, in the PK-Sim dist model. Since it would avoid misunderstanding on one side, and most importantly, if someone has measured values for B/P partition (this is my case :-)) it would be possible to integrate this information in the distribution model, as it is already possible in the other distribution model.
Created an issue for this: Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#1455

Thanks a lot @Yuri05 !
Is there a way to change the blood to plasma ratio in a simulation so that is would be used instead of the formula? in PK-Sim (ideally) or in MoBi?

Is there a way to change the blood to plasma ratio in a simulation so that is would be used instead of the formula?

You mean right now?

If you need to do it in PK-Sim, you could just calculate of (HCT - 1 + B2P) / HCT and set it as the new value for Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma) (of course as soon as HCT or B2P changes it must be redone...). Better solution not possible.
In MoBi: In the Spatial Structure Building block, change the formula of Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma)



Change the formula type to "Explicit ormula"
Create new formula and set it to (HCT - 1 + B2P) / HCT (s. above)
OK, thanks a lot!

Sorry for being late into this discussion.
Is there a plan for doing this more convenient?
Lets say have an option for that if one manually input B:P ratio (at given HCT) in Compound BB, the "Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma)" is automatically calculated and used for all simulations?
Let's reopen, @Erik-Sjogren has a valid point.

title: Code to input simulation into MoBi
body_text:
Hello,
Does anyone know of a R code available to input a simulation into MoBi by specifying each building block? For example, I have simulations I need to run for 70+ different administrations (these are included in the events building block). I currently have all these event building blocks saved as a pkml file. It would be very convenient to have an R code that specifies what event pkml file to use and input each simulation into MoBi (instead of having to add each of these simulations by hand in MoBi). Is this possible?
For reference, I did download the ospsuite-R package. However, it seems this is for running a simulation in R, not inputting building block files into MoBi to run the simulation in MoBi.

comments.body_text:
Hi @rachudso,
I don't have a R code at hand that I could share, but this shouldn't be too difficult, depending of course on the administration types you have. What I would propose conceptually is:

Set up a simulation with a placeholder for your administration(s), e.g. i doses of zero drug amount at j times
Export the simulation to pkml and load it in R
Create an Excel list defining for each administration protocol, the number (i) and times (j) of the doses as well as the drug amount of each dose
In R, loop over the Excel list and with each iteration, access the Administration Protocol parameters (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/load-get.html) and set the parameter values according to the data in your Excel table (https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/articles/set-values.html)

Alternatively, and more efficiently than the loop, you could use the SimulationBatch functions described here
I hope this helps,
André

title: Calculation surface areas not comprehensible
body_text:
Hi,
To calculate flows from the interstitial to intracellular space (and vice versa) the permeability-surface area product is employed. The documentation for the prediction of the surface area (interstitial/intracellular) in MoBi states for e.g. fat tissue:
SA [cm2] = (V_fat * 1000 / 14.2)^0.75 * 5 * 1E2.
V_fat is the intersitial volume in fat tissue according to the reference, but the exponent 0.75 would not yield cm2 as a unit. Would you ellaborate on the provided equation and if possible point at where to find further information on the calculation of cellular surface areas in PK-Sim? I am aware that the information must be available somewhere in MoBi but could not find it yet.
Thank you very much in advance!
Best wishes,
David

answer.body_text: Sorry for my late answer (was on holidays). I also don't have any better reference than the supplemental material in 10.1002/psp4.12205 you mentioned. Since your question is on the interstitial/intracellular surface area, you won't find anything on that in the paper Jan mentioned. There only the plasma/interstitial surface area is given which is estimated by a completely different rationale (the proteins are not assumed to diffuse into cells).

comments.body_text: @davbus I'd suggest asking your question in the forum. I'll move this issue there on your behalf

Hi @davbus , this formula is set up not in an ideal way (hardcoded values) and thus may need some explanation. It is a fitted/informed allometric function and its constants can be interpreted the following way:

the 14.2 are a fitted reference value for fat volume (in "theoretical dimension" volume, e.g. cm^3 and the dimension with "v_fat" cancel out)
the 5*1e2 are the fitted reference value for fat surface area (in "theoretical dimension" surface, e.g., cm^2)

So in the end the formula adds up to the surface dimension, but the way it is set up technically (using the dimensionless constants), it  does not. So it is a technicality.
(This answer might need confirmation by @Yuri05 ;-))

Hi @StephanSchaller,
Many thanks for your reply! That makes perfect sense to me. I guess these values were determined as presented in the supplementary of this publication: 10.1002/psp4.12205. Are you aware if this is published for all organs represented in PK-Sim for further reading on the topic or is this Bayer internal work that was never shared (I mean beyond PK-Sim of course)?
Best wishes,
David
Actually, I don't know any publication from the top of my head. @Yuri05 might be able to help.

A recent publication on the large molecule model framework explains the concept as well (although not touching on the small molecule parametrization). Maybe this provides some clarity. Otherwise @Christoph27 might help here
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4

Sorry for my late answer (was on holidays). I also don't have any better reference than the supplemental material in 10.1002/psp4.12205 you mentioned. Since your question is on the interstitial/intracellular surface area, you won't find anything on that in the paper Jan mentioned. There only the plasma/interstitial surface area is given which is estimated by a completely different rationale (the proteins are not assumed to diffuse into cells).

title: Location of "Partition coefficient (interstitial/water)"
body_text: I am curious as to where the parameter "Partition coefficient (interstitial/water)" is defined. I can see it in the simulation, but I do not see it in the spatial structure. How and where is it defined?

answer.body_text:
as Pavel said :)
it is defined in "C:\ProgramData\Open Systems Pharmacology\MoBi\11.0\AllCalculationMethods.pkml"
The rationale behind it was: different calculation methods for partition coefficients, cellular permeabilities, etc. require different inetrmediate parameters for their calculation. To avoid "overloading" the spatial structure with all those parameters (which would be also completely irrelevant if a particular calculation method is not used by any molecule involved) this "dynamical" parameter creation procedure was chosen.
And like everything, it has advantages and disadvantages :)
Open-Systems-Pharmacology/MoBi#502

comments.body_text: This a "dynamic" parameter created during simulation creation. I think it is defined somewhere in the file AllCalculationMethods.pkml located in the MoBi installation folder.

it is defined in "C:\ProgramData\Open Systems Pharmacology\MoBi\11.0\AllCalculationMethods.pkml"

title: How to make the parent drug not inhibit its metabolite
body_text:
Dear team，
I'm simulating a parent-metabolite PBPK model. When I conduct single drug simulation, the fitting is good. As I know, it's a weak CYP3A and P-gp inhibitor. So I added inhibition to it.

But when I simulated it later, I found that the parent drug would inhibit its active metabolite.
The predicted concentration of its metabolites is too high and the fitting is not good.
Add these inhibitions, but is it possible not to let it inhibition its metabolites.
Its metabolite is cleared by “total hepatic clearance”
Yours，colin

answer.body_text:
Hi @balamo98 , certainly, an Inhibition is only active per each process, if you strictly activate it when creating a simulation.
In the tab "processes" at the very top are listed your inhibitions. Just remove the one for the metabolite process.
But if you only have "total hepatic CL" for the metabolite, you should not even be able to define a DDI for this process. DDIs can only be defined for processes associated with proteins (enzymes, Transporters, ..).

title: Endosomal clearance of ligand-FcRn complex in the large molecule model
body_text: I have a question about the passive transport EndosomalSpecificClearance_FirstOrder within the large molecule model. From what I can see, it looks like the drug, FcRn, LigandEndo, and LigandEndo-FcRn_Complex are all subject to this passive transport representing endosomal clearance. Shouldn't the LigandEndo_Complex also be excluded from this passive transport?

answer.body_text:
You are right, that the "EndosomalSpecificClearance_FirstOrder" transport should not transport "LigandEndo_Complex".
And the transport is indeed not transporting "LigandEndo_Complex" since "LigandEndo_Complex" is set to be not present in the compartment "EndosomalClearance" in the building block "Molecule Start Values".

title: Parameter values not getting shown in Simulation output
body_text:
Dear All,
After performing population simulation, I wanted to compare my predicted parameter values with that of the reported observed values. comments.body_text:
Dear Gautam,
Sorry for the late reply.
In my eyes there are several reasons for this and some of them are potential bugs or shortcomings of the PK analysis as discussed in more details here #1065.
But reading through the comprehensive discussion will not bring the values you need... So here a short workaround: Create a box Wisker Analysis for you population simulation.
Select the PK-Parameters of interest, e.g. all the missing and give some meaningful units:

select them as outputs

By mouseover you can see the Median (50%) and the 5 to 95 % percentile of you population.
By right click on your Box whisker analysis you can export the results to excel as well.
Hope this might help!
Best, Tobias

@Gautamvj Some adjustments in the calculation of population PK parameters were made in the upcoming version 11.1 of the OSP, which might solve your problems.
Check the (preliminary) release notes for more details: #1394

title: Change for PK profile after adding a new compartment
body_text:
I was able to run the simulation with added compartment. However the PK profile changed dramatically comparing to before adding the compartment. I attached the model here. Can you please help me to see where I missed？
adding breast.zip

answer.body_text:
I don't know exactly where this is coming from, but if you replace the formulas in the spatial structure --> neighborhoods --> Pericentral_pls_Pericentral_int for the Peclet numbers for large and small pores, respectively, by:
P_endothelial_l * SA_pls_int > 0 ? (J_iso+alpha_lQ_lymph)(1-sigma_l)/(P_endothelial_lSA_pls_int) : 0
P_endothelial_s * SA_pls_int > 0 ? (-J_iso+(1-alpha_l)Q_lymph)(1-sigma_s)/(P_endothelial_sSA_pls_int) : 0
these formulas are the default formulas, in v11, not sure if it was changed in your model or if the formulas in your model are coming from an older version.
I did this for both Pericentral and Periportal, but probably only Pericentral is enough. This worked for me and finally resulted in a strongly reduced clearance.

comments.body_text:
I can't look into your model at this time, but FYI here is a publication with PK-Sim (Verison 4.2, I believe) on breastfeeding:
https://ascpt.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1038/clpt.2009.151
An here a recent review: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0753332220312300

I had a short look and I did a few observations:

the neighborhood from Breast interstitial to VenousBlood Plasma was missing
the neighborhood from Breast endosome to EndosomalClearance was missing (causing accumulation in the endosome and saturation of FcRn --> more clearance)
the TwoPoresTransportLink in Passive Transports is not working anymore, I think because a condition was added for the source: "in container: AmnioticFluid". As a consequence, there is no interstitial concentration of Infliximab in any organ.

I could not fix this last part quickly for some reason and I saw there are many more changes between the two simulations so I had to stop here unfortunately. Hopefully this helps a bit.
Best,
Wilbert

title: Preterm Infant simulation not working
body_text:
Hello,
I am working with a project where I am scaling an adult PBPK model to a premature infant population (GA=24 weeks)
When I ran the adult simulation, everything went fine and got a decent simulation
But, when I changed the population to premature infant described above, the simulation resulted in no lines of model-predicted levels, even that PK-Sim did NOT indicate that the simulation failed to integrate and the simulation went to the end
Any suggestions on why did this happen?
In my model, I have three enzymes 3A4, 2D6, and 3A5 in addition to renal glomerular filtration
I tried two settings;
1- put the values of the expression levels in preterm infant as 100% of the adult values
2- Put the expression levels of CYP3A4, CYP3A5, CYP2D6 in the preterm infant as 3%, 3%, and 5%, respectively from the adult reference value
Both settings resulted in the same problem described above
Does PK-Sim scale the expression levels automatically or not (i.e. should I do the setting 1, or 2)?
Thank you for the help

answer.body_text:
Yes PKSim will scale the ontogeny of these three enzymes automatically (therefore do #1)
To accomplish this, the 'ontogeny/variability like' option must be designated for the appropriate CYP enzyme, as shown here.

Alternatively you can do the scaling manually (#2) but you must ensure that there is no link to the GeneDB and that the 'ontogeny/variability like' option is designated as 'Undefined.'

comments.body_text: Hi, the preterm population is not validated for oral simulations and will not work. Are you performing an oral simulation?

yes, I did an oral simulation
Is there a way to go around this problem?
No easy workaround. A parameterization would have to be developed and evaluated for the preterm infant gut. One has been proposed here: Abduljalil et al., 2020, doi: 10.1007/s40262-019-00825-6
many thanks
will this need dealing with MoBi or purely done via PK-Sim?

Also, assume if I want to do a preterm infant simulation after intravenous dosing
for setting the enzyme expression levels, which approach should I use?
1- put the values of the expression levels in preterm infant as 100% of the adult values
2- Put the expression levels of CYP3A4, CYP3A5, CYP2D6 in the preterm infant as 3%, 3%, and 5%, respectively from the adult reference value
i.e. does PK-Sim scale the expression levels automatically or not?

Yes PKSim will scale the ontogeny of these three enzymes automatically (therefore do #1)
To accomplish this, the 'ontogeny/variability like' option must be designated for the appropriate CYP enzyme, as shown here.

Alternatively you can do the scaling manually (#2) but you must ensure that there is no link to the GeneDB and that the 'ontogeny/variability like' option is designated as 'Undefined.'
I have not done this before. I think maybe others in the community have done it privately. I will let them respond as to the best course of action.

title: Why does PK not change after setting DDI？
body_text:
PK SIM simulates drug interactions only through transporters to affect drug-drug interaction. Why does PK not change after setting? What might go wrong?
Thank you in advance :)

answer.body_text:
Dear @noxavier,
Your observation can have different causes. First, please make sure that the Ki is selected when configuring the DDI simulation (under "Processes") and is linked to the correct transporter. If this is the case, it could be possible that the interaction is "too weak" because, e.g., the concentration of the perpetrator on the target is too low or the Ki value is too large. You can try to simulate the unbound concentration of your perpetrator in plasma or in transporter-relevant tissues. Also, the victim PK might not be sensitive enough to the transport process and therefore inhibition has only little effect.
Best,
Denise

title: mechanism-based inactivation
body_text:
Dear OSP community,
I’m developing a PBPK model to represent the [mechanism-based inactivation] of a macromolecule on voriconazole metabolism (by inhibiting metabolizing enzymes such as CYP2C19). However, the  the parameters ki and kincat are not avaliable. The following equations have been used in previous PBPK models.

Enzact,H-3A(t) is the amount of active CYP3A at any given time in the liver; E0,H-3A is the basal amount of CYP3A in the liver, (Enzact(t)=E0 at t=0). Emin is the minimum CYP enzyme activity (i.e. the maximum suppression) expressed as a fraction of vehicle control. EC50 is the concentration that supports half-Emin (i.e. half of the maximum suppressive effect); [I]t is the perpetrator (IL-6) concentration at time ‘t’.
My question is how to implement it in mobi?
I tried to add a inhibition process in pksim (mechanism-based inactivation), and enter the value of Emin and EC50 as Kincat and Ki. Then I exported the model to mobi. I deleted the MBI reaction and revised the enzyme turnover reaction as follows. “kdeg * M_0 *(1 +  (Emin - 1) * IL6/(EC50 + IL6)) - kdeg * M“ But it doesn't work very well. An obvious mistake is the output result of enzyme concentration.
Thanks in advance,
Cersei

title: Trimethoprim PBPK model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have been working on a PBPK model of the MATE1, OCT1, OCT2 and CYP2C8 perpetrator drug trimethoprim. The model is able to predict and describe drug-drug(-gene) interactions with metformin (MATE1, OCT1 and OCT2 victim drug), repaglinide (CYP2C8 victim drug), pioglitazone (CYP2C8 victim drug) and rifampicin (CYP3A4 and P-gp perpetrator drug).
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Pharmaceutics and the trimethoprim model as well as PK-Sim projects presenting the predicted DD(G)I studies can be found here
Related publication:
Türk D, Hanke N, Lehr T. A Physiologically-Based Pharmacokinetic Model of Trimethoprim for MATE1, OCT1, OCT2, and CYP2C8 Drug–Drug–Gene Interaction Predictions. Pharmaceutics 2020, 12(11), 1074.
Best regards,
Denise

title: Why hepatic clearance could surpass hepatic flow rate in PK-Sim?
body_text:
Hi all,
I used a dummy enzyme which only expressed in the liver and adjusted the in vitro specific clearance to see what happens. However, in an IV simulation I found the total body clearance will increase with the increase of in vitro  specific clearance without upper limitation.
It is very weird that the total clearance is much higher than the hepatic blood flow rate (~21 ml/min/h). How to explain this？

comments.body_text:
Hi there,
I get a similar result, though I do get an upper limit on plasma clearance.
Test compound:
MW 300g/mol, logP 1.00, fu 1.00, acid-base: neutral, KMET in vitro specific clearance
Test Individual:
European, male, age 30 years, default parameters (73kg), add KMET in liver periportal+pericentral
Liver blood flow rate is 0.43 L/min from the anatomy and physiology tab. We are interested in plasma flow (HCT = 0.47) so that gives us 0.20 L/min.
At very high specific clearances (e.g. > 5000 1/min), the PK clearance caps out at 16.28 mL/min/kg. For our individual, this is 1.18 L/min.
So plasma clearance is calculated as 6-fold higher than liver plasma flow...thinking on this.
Paul

Liver blood flow rate is 0.43 L/min from the anatomy and physiology tab. We are interested in plasma flow (HCT = 0.47) so that gives us 0.20 L/min.

Two points you might be overlooking:

The liver blood flow depicted in PK-Sim is ARTERIAL blood flow only. You will have to add portal vein blood flow.
As an upper limit, we are considering blood flow (not plasma only) as, in theory, also compound in blood cells can diffuse into liver tissue in a single pass-through.

Maybe this helps.
Regarding the issue from @jzq90, it might be of interest how the simulated output (the plotted curve) looks like.

@StephanSchaller  Great point about the portal flow. Oops!
I've observed that plasma clearances can blow out of proportion at high lipophilicity (>= 3). Will repost if I discover an explanation.

I've observed that plasma clearances can blow out of proportion at high lipophilicity (>= 3).

This may depend on the simulation time you are using. you might have distribution effects that add to apparent plasma clearance. After all, plasma clearance is calculated from the shape of the curve (if I am not mistaken).
So if you simulate for a very long time, this effect should be lower...

Another question is that the blood/plasma ratio increased too quickly with lipophilicity:
When lipophilicity=2.5, B/P is 2.5
When lipophilicity=3, B/P is 3
When lipophilicity=3.5, B/P is 18
This seems not reasonable because the B/P ratio of most drugs ranges from 0.5~2, however the situation when LogP exceeds 3 is not rare.
Maybe it is not a good idea to use LogP as a surrogate for lipophilicity in PK-sim.

Maybe it is not a good idea to use LogP as a surrogate for lipophilicity in PK-sim.

This depends on the calculation method you use.
"PKSim standard" and "Schmitt" e.g. require LogMA. Others (R&R) require LogD or P:
LogD is the descriptor for lipophilicity of ionizable compounds as it accounts for the pH dependence of a molecule in aqueous solution. LogP describes lipophilicity for neutral compounds only, and should be applied with caution, especially when working with ionizable compounds.

This depends on the calculation method you use.
"PKSim standard" and "Schmitt" e.g. require LogMA. Others (R&R) require LogD or P:

Thanks for the explanation.
I've compared the blood/plasma ratio of a dummy neutral compound with Log P=3.5 having low intrinsic clearance in the liver (high intrinsic clearance show a similar trend).

The simulations make me even more confused:

The blood/plasma ratio varies little for different methods, but all the cases show an unreasonable high blood/plasma ratio when LogP=3.5. For a compound having lipophilicity >3.5 or 3, most of the drug distributes into blood cells rather than tissues. Plasma PK will be meaningless.
Clearance varies dramatically for different methods. This makes no sense because clearance should be independent of the volume of distribution.

Dear jzq90,
yes this may be confusing on the first glance. And you rise an interesting point here:

Clearance varies dramatically for different methods. This makes no sense because clearance should be independent of the volume of distribution.
But if you think that this concept"clearance should be independence from Vss" comes from "top down" compartimental and non compartimental PK-analysis. This uses some inherent assumptions like:


you look on plasma only
drug binding in nonvascular regions (i.e. tissue binding) does not play a role.
the rest of the body consists 1, 2 or three well stirred compartments
These assumptions do not hold for PBPK modelling were you have a "bottom up approach". Here compound parameters like lipophilicity impact drug fate in the body, which is described by e.g. drug permeability from plasma to tissues with different velocities and tissue binding. PK-Sim calculates the PK parameters directly from the plasma curve were all the dynamics are reflected. If you realize that the different tissue distribution models use slightly different approaches to predict this behavior, it makes sense, that they lead to different results.

Hope this might help you.
Best, Tobias

Dear Tobias,
Thanks for your kind reply. But I have too many questions about this:

These assumptions do not hold for PBPK modelling were you have a "bottom up approach". Here compound parameters like lipophilicity impact drug fate in the body, which is described by e.g. drug permeability from plasma to tissues with different velocities and tissue binding. PK-Sim calculates the PK parameters directly from the plasma curve were all the dynamics are reflected. If you realize that the different tissue distribution models use slightly different approaches to predict this behavior, it makes sense, that they lead to different results.



Does the partition method affect the permeability？
Still, I can't understand why the partition to the tissue influence the clearance (e.g. AUC in the plasma). Elimination of the drug only occurs in the liver. If the drug partition more to the tissue, the free drug in plasma will decrease, then the elimination rate (CL×C) will decrease. All the drug partition into the tissue will flow back to plasma, which should keep the AUC unchanged.
The Vss varies only within around 5-fold range, while the clearance varies up to 400-fold for different partition method. Why?
The blood/plasma ratio is unreasonable high.

Best regards,
Aaron

Hi Aaron,

No
No, if the clearance is in liver , the concentration in plasma is not affecting clearance, but the concentration in liver intracellular, which may be changed with changes in partitioning (e.g the drug reaches the liver faster).
3 & 4: can you share the project file?

Hi StephanSchaller


No, if the clearance is in liver , the concentration in plasma is not affecting clearance, but the concentration in liver intracellular, which may be changed with changes in partitioning (e.g the drug reaches the liver faster).


If the partition method doesn't affect permeability, then they will reach the liver at the same speed (not faster). Isn't it?
I've uploaded the project as below:

If the partition method doesn't affect permeability, then they will reach the liver at the same speed (not faster). Isn't it?

No, partitioning also affects diffusion into cell. it changes the gradient of the diffusive flow, i.e. speed of diffusion.

No, partitioning also affects diffusion into cell. it changes the gradient of the diffusive flow, i.e. speed of diffusion.

I thought the partition only affects the steady-state, in which the Kp = Drug-in-tissue/Drug-in-blood keeps unchanged. But before it reach the steady-state, the permeation rate and gradient should be the same for different partition method.

The rate of diffusion from compartment 1 to compartment 2 is PSA x (C1 - C2/K21)
As K21 increases, the rate of diffusion (permeation rate) increases, and vice versa.
Thanks Paul. Forgot to follow-up, here

PSA x (C1 - C2/K21)

Hi, thanks. What's the meaning of K21 and the equation? I thought the drug flowing from compartment 1 to compartment 2 should be PSA x C1 with unit ng/s and the drug flowing from compartment 2 to compartment 1 should be PSA x C2 with unit ng/s.
Also, why K21 increases when change the partition method (not permeability method) ?

Getting off track but...
K21 is the partition coefficient between compartment 2 and compartment 1. In other words, it is the ratio of the drug concentration in compartment 2 vs. compartment 1 at a distributional steady state. Its determination is dependent on the partition coefficient algorithm that you select (e.g. Rodgers and Rowland, PK-Sim Standard and so on).
You are almost correct about the flow, but you must account for the partition coefficient. If you expand the differential equation for C2 you get:
d/dt(C2) = PSA x (C1 - C2/K21)
d/dt(C2) = PSA x C1 - PSA x C2/K21
And the opposite for C1:
d/dt(C1) = -PSA x (C1 - C2/K21)
d/dt(C1) = -PSA x C1 + PSA x C2/K21

at a distributional steady state
Yes, if you mean steady-state it makes sense, and definitely the partition method influences the tissue/plasma ratio. But like I mentioned, 'I thought the partition only affects the steady-state... But before it reaches the steady-state, the permeation rate and gradient should be the same for different partition method'

Also, K21= C2/C1, then  (C1 - C2/K21) will be 0.

Yes, change (diffusive flow) will be zero if the drug is in steady state.
And the calculated permeability is part of the "PSA" = "permeability x surface area factor".
So PSA defines the speed and K21 defines the ratio (and one-directional speed).

OK. Back to my original question:
Why does the partition method affect the clearance (both blood and plasma) to such a huge extent (400-fold), even larger than the change-fold of volume distribution?

@jzq90
I think this is a numerical aritifact caused by the unrealistic physchem properties of the dummy compound (highly lipophilic compounds tupically show substantial protein binding, wheres fu for the dummy was set to 1). In this case calculated volumes of disitrubution exceed 100L and half lifes are also extreme. If you would like to have accurate CL estimate based on NCA you will need to have a very long  observation period.

 PKSim.CLI.exe export
USAGE:
Export all simulations from a project to csv and xml using short notation:
  PKSim.CLI export -cx --logLevel Information --output <OutputFolder> --project <ProjectFile>.pksim5
Export all simulations from a project to csv and xml using long notation:
  PKSim.CLI export --csv --logLevel Information --output <OutputFolder> --project <ProjectFile>.pksim5 --xml
Run and export specified simulations from a project to csv using short notation:
  PKSim.CLI export -cr --logLevel Information --output <OutputFolder> --project <ProjectFile>.pksim5 --simulations "My 1st simulation" "My 2nd
  simulation" S3
Run and export specified simulations from a project to csv using long notation:
  PKSim.CLI export --csv --logLevel Information --output <OutputFolder> --project <ProjectFile>.pksim5 --run --simulations "My 1st simulation"
  "My 2nd simulation" S3

title: Getting The Tablet_Location Value From R
body_text:
When I try and get the location of the tablet ("...Application_1|Tablet_Location") by running my model in R I just get an array of zeros. Am I doing something wrong, or is it possible that the location is not accounted for in my model?
Thanks,
DS

answer.body_text:
For each tablet formulation different from "Particle dissolution", PK-Sim allows the option Use as suspension to be selected.
If Use as Suspension is activated (which is the default setting), in the simulation, the tablet will disintegrate in the stomach and the disintegrated particles will migrate along the gastrointestinal tract compartments. For Particle Dissolution it is the only option per construction.
In this case, Tablet_location is not used in the model and is indeed always zero. What is used are the Suspension fractions of different GI segments - which you should plot instead.
If Use as Suspension is deactivated, the tablet will be treated as non-disintegrating tablet with discrete transition in the different gastrointestinal tract compartments. In this case, Tablet_location is used and is not const zero.

comments.body_text:

If Use as Suspension is activated (which is the default setting), in the simulation, the tablet will disintegrate in the stomach and the disintegrated particles will migrate along the gastrointestinal tract compartments. For Particle Dissolution it is the only option per construction.
In this case, Tablet_location is not used in the model and is indeed always zero. What is used are the Suspension fractions of different GI segments - which you should plot instead.
If Use as Suspension is deactivated, the tablet will be treated as non-disintegrating tablet with discrete transition in the different gastrointestinal tract compartments. In this case, Tablet_location is used and is not const zero.

Thanks @Yuri05 , I think that makes sense.
So in the case where Use as Suspension is activated that means that the RHS of the lumen compartments (and maybe more?), for example the "...Lumen|LowerJejunum|..." will have at least one line that corresponds to pill dissolution but is simply zero since the "tablet is active" parameter is false?

title: Negative values of undefined metabolite in blood cell compartment of small intestine
body_text:
Hi All,
I am running into this strange simulation run failure where an undefined CYP1A2 metabolite is becoming negative in the blood cells within the lower jejunum. The amount of metabolite should theoretically be nearly 0, and allowing this molecular parameter to be negative solves the simulation run failure issue. However, the workflow of sending each and every simulation to MoBi to allow the parameter to be negative and sending back to PK-Sim before running development and validation simulations is quite cumbersome. Brian

answer.body_text: Hi Brian, this is likely due to numeric noise. Did you try adjusting the solver settings (tolerances)?

comments.body_text: Hi Brian, this is likely due to numeric noise. Did you try adjusting the solver settings (tolerances)?

@Bcicali Hi Brian,
general notes on tolerances. Try to keep the absolute and the relative tolerance values close together, e.g. 1e-9 and 1e-7. Best to reduce them together in one step. 1e-30 is an insane value, I am surprised it works at all :D From my experience, everything below 1e-15 for the absolute tolerance makes things even worse.

title: Dabigatran Reversal PBPK Model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have recently been working on a mechanistic PBPK/PD model to study dabigatran reversal, which includes the reversal of dabigatran by hemodialysis and its antidote idarucizumab.
Therefore, a PBPK model of idarucizumab has been carefully developed and evaluated. The model was then coupled with a recently published PBPK model of dabigatran to predict the interaction of both compounds. The dabigatran model was furthermore extended by a dialyzer compartment and a hemodialysis clearance as well as by a simple PD model.
Related publications:
Fuhr LM, Hanke N, Meibohm B, Lehr T
Effective Removal of Dabigatran by Idarucizumab or Hemodialysis: A Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling Analysis Clinical Pharmacokinetics 2020
All models can be found here.
Best regards,
Laura

title: Tissue partition coefficients: Reference for Rodgers/Rowland-specific parameters in muscle
body_text:
Dear all,
I looked into MoBi to understand how exactly the Rodgers/Rowland prediction method for tissue partition coefficients was implemented. More precisely, I wanted to know how the fractional volume content of interstitial/intercellular water was reconciled between the publications Rodgers et al. (2005), where interstitial/intercellular water fractions are listed without correction for residual blood, and Rodgers/Rowland (2006), where the total water fraction is listed with correction for residual blood.
The (very reasonable) result of that query was that the interstitial water fraction was taken from Rodgers et al. (2005), whereas the intercellular water fraction was calculated as the difference between total water fraction from Rodgers/Rowland (2006) and interstitial water fraction from Rodgers et al. (2005).
However, there is one exception: the values in muscle don't match that calculation. In fact, fractional volumes of all of the tissue constituents in muscle show discrepancies to the Rodgers/Rowland ones (i.e., neutral lipids, neutral phospholipid, interstitial water, intracellular water). Is there a reason why different values are used for muscle, and/or a reference for that?
See attached screenshot, which contains the MoBi fractional volumes formatted as a table and with the same naming/ordering as in the Rodgers/Rowland articles (iw=interstitial water, cw=intracellular water,nl=neutral lipids, np=neutral phospholipids)
Best,
Niklas

title: Question about the calculations of the fraction X, Y, Z for predicting the tissue plasma partition coefficient by Rodgers & Rowland method
body_text:
Hello team,
When I dig into all the parameters calculated in the PK-Sim by Mo-Bi and I found out that the fraction X, Y, Z used to calculate the partition coefficient by the Rodgers & Rowland method have a suspected mistake in implementing the physiological parameters.

The question I have is about the parameter pH_IW. Since in the physiological data we have all the intracellular pH for all organs, why the calculation for the fraction X is using a set value (7.0) for  pH_IW from the "Organism" for all organs, instead of using the pH (intracellular) from each organ individually? Is it for faster simulation? or I have some misunderstandings about the equation?
It will be great if you could give me some feedback.  Thank you very much.
Have a nice day.
Best,
Jesse

answer.body_text:
In the original publications (2005, 2006) with the associated evaluation, the Rodgers and Rowland method uses pH_IW 7.0 for solid organs and pH_IW 7.22 for blood cells. In PKSim, it has been employed as published and evaluated.
You could change the equation to use the pH_IW values from the 2008 toxicology paper, but the method may need to be re-evaluated with a suitable list of compounds, and any changes to the algorithm would have to be disclosed on academic or regulatory documents, if you are claiming that you are using the algorithm.

title: Combine simulations from different .pksim files created from MoBi?
body_text:
Hi All, I have two PK-Sim population simulations in separate .pksim files that were both created from MoBi using the "send to PK-Sim for population simulation" feature. I would like to combine both of these simulations into one .pksim file to use "Compare Results", etc.
It seems that PK-Sim simulations sent from MoBi in this way can't be saved as .pkml files and thus can't be imported as described: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/importing-exporting-project-data-models. Is there another way to combine these files?

answer.body_text:
Hi,
there is some easy way to Combine these Simulation in one PK-Sim file:
Just create or select an existing Working journal for the first created PK-Sim file. Than you attach the Population simulation by right clicking on it and select "Add to journal" from the sub-menu.

Than you open the WJ from the first file in second PK-Sim file. Go to the journal Page in the Journal view/related items and click the reload simulation button

next to the Attached Simulation.
This loads now your first PK-Sim Simulation in your second PK-Sim file were you can compare both.
N.B. a lot more can be interchanged, attached and reloaded between PK-Sim and MoBi files via the Working Journal. Check this out.
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/shared-tools-and-example-workflows/working-journal#project-documentation
Hope this might help you
Tobias

title: how to modify ordinate value in  population simulation?
body_text:
Dear to all,
I want to modify the maximum and minimum of the ordinate value in  population simulation, how to do it?

answer.body_text:
Hi,
unfortunately this is not possible by directly putting in numbers in PK-sim. You can just use the clumsy zoom in or reset zoom function. Please make a feature request for this.
Until this is solved you might try this humble workaround:
Insert fake observed data values to trim the ordinate like e.f 20000 ng/ml at time = 0 and last timepoint measured. Drag and drop the data in you plot and change colour to white, so that are not disturbing so much anymore (so want will not get invisible though). The PK-sim auto zoom function will try to adapt zoom around this data. You might try different values for optimal results.
Hope that helps.
Best, Tobias

title: How to install the R package ospsuite
body_text: I installed ospsuite-R according to the steps in the link（https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/OSPSuite-R/  ） ,but I encountered this problem when running ospsuite R. How can I solve it?

answer.body_text:
Hi @noxavier,
the ospsuite-R package has some prerequisite packages you need to install before loading it. It seems you are missing at least two of those. Some of these packages require manual installs, such as ospsuite.utils (not on CRAN).
The documentation on the github page contains the instructions here: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSPSuite-R#installation.
(Alternatively, if you have the remotes package installed, you should be able to install ospsuite.utils by running remotes::install_github("Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSPSuite.RUtils") in your R console.)
As for jsonlite, you can install the package from CRAN by running install.packages("jsonlite") in your R console.
Hope this helps.
Best regards, Simeon

title: PBPK Modeling of Buprenorphine in Adults, Children and Preterm Neonates
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have recently been working on PBPK modeling of buprenorphine in pediatrics. First, an adult PBPK model has been thoroughly developed, which is able to predict buprenorphine and norbuprenorphine venous and arterial blood plasma concentration-time profiles after intravenous administration. The model has been used for the assessment of buprenorphine drug-drug interactions with clarithromycin, itraconazole as well as rifampicin.
Subsequently, the adult PBPK model has been scaled to both a child and a preterm neonate population for predictions of individual plasma concentration-time profiles.
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Pharmaceutics. The PK-Sim model files presenting the PBPK models including simulations of clinical studies can be found here.
Related publication:
Kovar L, Schräpel C, Selzer D, Kohl Y, Bals R, Schwab M, Lehr T. Physiologically-Based Pharmacokinetic (PBPK) Modeling of Buprenorphine in Adults, Children and Preterm Neonates. Pharmaceutics. 2020; 12(6):578. https://www.mdpi.com/1999-4923/12/6/578
Best regards,
Lukas

title: the weight and height of cancer population
body_text: Hello everyone, I am trying to use pk-sim to establish a virtual population recently, and I am looking for relevant literature to determine the demographic parameters of cancer population, but I use the relationship between weight and height in the literature, and the weight calculated according to the height is very strange. When the height is measured in centimeters, the weight is too large and unreasonable, and when measured in meters, the weight is too small. Would you like to ask if you have encountered this situation? Looking forward to your reply!

comments.body_text: Cancer populations vary widely depending on the indication. I would advise using the age brackets defined by the epidemiological information in literature for the type of cancer you are interested in, and allowing height and weight to fall to default. You may look at decreasing weight (fat or muscle mass) manually thereafter if you discover that cancer patients of your indication are leaner than otherwise healthy subjects.

Hi Nilesh,

title: Morphine tutorial_Age dependent PBPK
body_text: Hello, everyone. I have a question for age dependent PBPK. In the tutorial, three pediatric groups were set as: 0-0.1 years (newborn), 0.1-2 years (toddlers), and 2-6 years (preschool).  I think this calculates postnatal age, but how do we take postmenstrual age into consideration? Postmenstrual age is important to age dependent maturation especially for younger children. Thank you.

answer.body_text:
Hi Xixi,
Regular populations in PK-Sim consider Neonates as Term-born (PMA=40weeks). In case PMA is important for your task, you should select the Preterm population and define the PMA as well as PNA. In addition you can consider the aging processes during the simulation in case of longer timespans by selecting "allow aging" in the first tab of the "create simulation" screen.
Best,
Jan

title: How to use in vitro dissolution models in PK-Sim?
body_text:
Hello all,
In PK-Sim, dissolution functions like lint 80 are suitable for fast dissolving drugs and the table option can be well utilized for plugging in the specific dissolution data. But, when I am giving a dissolution profile with a peculiar shape it needs to be fitted with the Weibull function but I see no option there to input my experimental dissolution profile to match with the Weibull function. Again if I used only the shape factor to define my profile it is tedious and also I think it cannot reproduce the same experimental profile.

But these functions are extremely helpful when we are looking for a target dissolution profile for modified release formulation. The model very well translated the predictions for modified release dosage forms. I am only concerned about the dosage forms with a complex release mechanism.
So, my ask here is, are there any other ways of plugging in the experimentally obtained profile in the Weibull function in the formulation tab?
Thank you in advance!

comments.body_text:
Hi Prajwala1921,
I'm not sure if this is what you are looking for, but there are ways to calculate Weibull parameters (dissolution shape and time) from experimental dissolution profiles, for example described in detail by Langenbucher. Note that the time interval here represents 63.2% of the dissolved dose, and thus does not match the t50 input value in PK-Sim, but it should at least give a good approximation. Hope this helps you.
Best regards,
Simeon

Hi @Prajwala1921,
in addition to Simeon's answer you could consider modeling the dissolution profile in the in vitro setting using MoBi or R and then feed the results into your whole-body PBPK model in PK-Sim.
For example, if you intend using the particle dissolution model, you may use the workflow described here where you first fit relevant parameters of the dissolution function to observed in vitro release kinetics and then push the parameterization of the dissolution function into the PBPK model in PK-Sim.
Some time ago, I have started implementing a 2-compartment dissolution model with the Weibull function in MoBi. I have attached the file including hypothetical in vitro dissolution data here:
InVitroDissolution_Weibull.zip
The model still has a few things that could be improved to make it more easy (e.g. the fraction dissolved can be >1, the implementation of the time lag and the dose is a bit cumbersome), but it's running. Feel free to use and improve it.
André

@Prajwala1921  Also you can use an app called DDsolver, it is free and you can model your formulation release there and plugin into PKSIM.
Here is the paper where you can get more information. doi: 10.1208/s12248-010-9185-1
Is there any way to change the Weibull equation implemented in the software (e.g., to use only the a, b parameters, or incorporate Fmax) to input dissolution data when creating a new formulation?
