title: Physiologically-Based Pharmacokinetic (PBPK) Modeling of Fentanyl in Adults and Pediatric Patients
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have recently been working on PBPK modeling of fentanyl in adults and pediatrics. First, an adult PBPK model has been thoroughly developed, which is able to predict fentanyl and norfentanyl venous and arterial blood plasma concentration-time profiles after intravenous administration. The adult PBPK model has been used for the assessment of the fentanyl drug-drug interaction with voriconazole.
Subsequently, the adult PBPK model has been scaled to several pediatric populations and was used to predict plasma concentration-time profiles as well as individual and mean clearances. Furthermore, the pediatric PBPK model was used to simulate differences in peak plasma concentrations after bolus injections and short infusions.
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Pharmaceutics. The PK-Sim model files presenting the PBPK models including simulations of clinical studies can be found here.
Related publication:
Kovar L., Weber A., Zemlin M., Kohl Y., Bals R., Meibohm B., Selzer D., Lehr T. Physiologically-Based Pharmacokinetic (PBPK) Modeling Providing Insights into Fentanyl Pharmacokinetics in Adults and Pediatric Patients. Pharmaceutics 2020, 12, 908. https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics12100908
Best regards,
Lukas

title: multiple dose protocol
body_text:
Hello everyone
what pharmacokinetic parameters should i evaluate using an oral multiple administration protocol?
and what does each mean?

answer.body_text:
It depends on what you need, but per default, the choice would be the PK Properties in steady-state, so this one

(and accordingly the same for AUC and Cmax)

comments.body_text:
Dear @AParisotto ,
what do you mean, exactly?
Generally, for PO, AUC (Area under Curve), Cmax (maximum concentration) and Tmax (Time of maximum concentration) are useful metrics.
Best,
Stephan

the pharmacokinetic parameters after several administrations are those below.
which ones should be evaluated?

AUC_inf_tD1
Area under the concentration vs. time curve from the first data point extrapolated to infinity (further administrations are not
considered!)
AUC_inf_tD1_n
oArmrea under the concentration vs. time curve from the first data point extrapolated to infinity (further administrations are not
considered!) normalized to dose, with dose in drug mass per body weight
....tDi-tDj
Respective PK parameter from the administration time of the first dose until the administration time of the second dose
tDlast-tDEnd
Respective PK parameter following the last application
....tDlast-1-
tDlast
Respective PK parameter in the intervall between the (last -1) application and the last application
C_trough_dDi
Trough concentration just before the i-th dose is administered
C_trough_dlast
Trough concentration just before the last dose is administered

It depends on what you need, but per default, the choice would be the PK Properties in steady-state, so this one

(and accordingly the same for AUC and Cmax)

title: Exporting CI/PI/VPC
body_text:
Dear community,
As most people, I use post-processing software (R in this case) to convert PKSim or MoBi results into figures for e.g. a publication or a report. For single simulations, this is quite straightforward as these can be easily exported to csv and read into R again. However when I try to do the same for the results of a parameter identification step, e.g. to plot the CI or VPC, this can only be done to xlsx (in mobi) and the resulting file only shows the observed data and (in separate sheets) the median predictions. Am I missing a straightforward way to get e.g. the percentiles as well?
Thank you!
Kind regards,
Robin

comments.body_text: Does anyone have an idea? I feel this should be simple but I am not finding how to do this.

I'm not sure I understand why such output would be needed.
In PBPK models, all parameters should be uniquely and precisely identifiable - very little RSE. This should be shown through multiple optimizations from random start values ending at the exact solution.
For population simulations, you do not need complex statistics or confidence intervals. Confidence intervals for the plasma concentrations? Just simulate with N>10000 to trend to the exact median, 5th and 95th of the simulation. Output the 5th and 95th when you edit the time profile and it comes out on excel.

comments.body_text:
Hello Enrique,
looking at the error messages it seems that many parameters that do not exist in the spatial structure are nonetheless referred to e.g. in the passive transports building block. To solve this you could manually go through the error list and check that all referenced parameters (entities) exist at the specified path.
If these are standard parameters you could also have a look at how they are implemented in other default organs.
Also note that the tutorial you referenced above was developed for small molecule drugs only, but looking at the error messages, it seems that you are working with a large molecule.

André

Hi Enrique,

Also, if your MSV BB is correct, make sure that the right BB is linked to the simulation (if you have multiple) and that the MSV is linked to the right BBs. This last bit is I think difficult to check afterwards, but you can select this when you create a new MSV BB or you can check the MSV values when you generate a new simulation.

title: A warning that occurs when a mechanical inhibition is set
body_text:
Dear OSP community，I recently started running simulations after defining the mechanistic inhibitory processes of the compounds. However, in the processes interface, this warning always appears：

“Please note that for the mechanism-based inactivator no clearance process is defined via the inactivation process by default. In theory, for every inactivated enzyme molecule, also one inactivator molecule is cleared; this must be separately defined by the user in form of additional metabolization/excretion processes for the inhibitor.”
I've tried many things, but I don't know what this warning means? If there is a master know, please reply.
Tony

answer.body_text:
Hi Tony,
what this message means it that when your drug binds as inhibitor (Ligand A in the figure below) to the active site of the enzyme, the latter is inactivated. Note however, that the drug itself remains bound to the inactivated enzyme and does not dissociate. Hence, the inactivation of the enzyme also removes the drug molecule from the system; it is thus cleared.
However, the implementation of mechanism-based inhibition in PK-Sim does not automatically account for the removal of the inhibitor from the system. This may need to be added explicitly as separate clearance process.

Figure from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7557591/
I hope this makes it clear,
André

Note however, that the drug itself remains bound to the inactivated enzyme and does not dissociate. Hence, the inactivation of the enzyme also removes the drug molecule from the system; it is thus cleared.
However, the implementation of mechanism-based inhibition in PK-Sim does not automatically account for the removal of the inhibitor from the system. This may need to be added explicitly as separate clearance process.

Figure from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC7557591/
I hope this makes it clear,
André

Dear Tony,
there might be also cases were you can ignore this warning. Please check out this discussion:
#226
Best, Tobias

title: No 'Add metabolizing enzyme... (Database query)' option available after specifying the path
body_text: Dear OSP community, I'm new to figuring out PK-Sim and I'm following the Tutorials from https://www.open-systems-pharmacology.org/. I've been trying to open the protein expression database in PK-Sim to view the 'Add metabolizing enzyme... (Database query)' option. I've downloaded the 'Gene expression database (human)' from the site https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Suite/releases/tag/v11.0. I've specified the path to the species-specific (human) expression database in the utilities > Options > Application tab. Though, the database query option isn't available. I've restarted the software as well to check if this could have caused the issue. Why is the option not showing? Many thanks in advance!

answer.body_text:
Hi Joyce, the protein expression has a bit changed in PK-Sim v11 and can now be found as separate Building Block. You can add a new protein via the context menu of that Building Block:

In the menu that pops up you can either type in the enzyme name or select one from the list, e.g.:

This creates an (empty) placeholder for the enzyme without any tissue expression. You can then either add tissue expression manually by overwriting the values for relative expression or initial concentration in the list below or you click on Database Query to load it from the (human) expression database:

You can find more information in the manual:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-expression-profile#definition-of-new-expression-profile-in-pk-sim-r
I hope this helps,
André

Cabozantinib PBPK

title: cancer population parameter
body_text: Hello everyone, my project needs to establish PBPK model of cancer population, there is no default cancer population in pk-sim, I have been trying to find parameter adjustment of cancer population modeling, so far I have not found many articles, would you like to ask if you have experience in this field? Looking forward to your reply!

comments.body_text: Cancer population tends to be leaner, older patients. More variable or lower plasma protein binding. Blood cancers may affect hematocrit. Liver cancers may affect hepatic metabolic capacity. Kidney cancers may affect GFR. In general there is a state of inflammation that affects disposition and/or catabolism of proteins. CYP3A4 metabolism may be mildly inhibited along with other enzymes.
Thank you for your answer. The literature I have found so far is basically about CYP3A4, gastric emptying time, hematocrit and other parameters.

title: What is the mathematical base for the PK-Sim model simulation?
body_text: I spent a long time but did not find any information about the mathematical base of this software. Could anyone explain to me? Is it Bayesian method based?

comments.body_text:
Hm, it is probably possible to apply Bayesian methods for certain applications. But if you want to find out more about the mathematics (I think ODE based) and principles please check out the Literature given here for details:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/mechanistic-modeling-of-pharmacokinetics-and-dynamics/modeling-concepts/principles-of-pbpk-modeling#introduction-to-pbpk-modeling
Hope this might help you.
Best, Tobias

As Tobias said, the models are based on ODEs, with the amounts of different molecules (drugs, proteins, ...) serving as state variables.
You can also read this Wikipedia article on PBPK modeling, which explains the principle very nicely

title: Inhalation of a macromolecule in preclinical species
body_text:
Dear community,
I am currently working on the design of an inhalation experiment of a new biologic in non-human primates. For this I thought to use the OSP to combine the information I have at hand. I would like to pick your brains in how to combine what is already available in PKSim and MoBi in the most efficient way to predict both lung exposure and systemic exposure after an inhalation. I have IV data, disposition patterns and an idea about the fraction of the administered dose that reaches the respiratory tract. My idea was thus to use one of the two inhalation models which have been constructed in MoBi: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Inhalation-model or https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Lung-absorption-model
The problem is of course that these are completely parameterized for humans and not for NHP. Therefore I thought to create a PKSim model first including NHP and the specific characteristics of my biologic, confirm this with the IV data and then export it to MoBi. So far so good, but what would in your opinion be the best way to combine this exported model with the developed inhalation models? Is there a way to scale down spatial structures in these lung models with body weight (which would be a very crude approach but would satisfy my current purposes).
Thank you very much for your insights!
Kind regards,
Robin

answer.body_text:
Dear Robin,
I can speak to the first inhalation model (https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Inhalation-model).
You can scale down the spatial structures as you described. To do this most efficiently, I would access the current parameter values (e.g. airway diameter, airway length, etc.) and scale them using the R toolbox.
In terms of connecting this inhalation model to a PK-Sim model, the model is not yet configured to do this very easily. The inhalation model needs to be connected to the full body PK-Sim model in a way that allows for intermittent deposits of molecule into the oral pathway due to mucociliary clearance. This configuration step still needs to be done with this inhalation model and it would take some work. I'm not sure if the other inhalation model would be easier to connect to PK-Sim.
Alternatively, you could use the inhalation model connected to a two-compartment model (as it is currently configured), which would give you an estimate of both lung and systemic exposure after inhalation.
Let me know if you have any other questions or concerns about configuring or using the model.
Thank you,
Moriah Pellowe

comments.body_text:
Dear Robin,
I can speak to the first inhalation model (https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Inhalation-model).
You can scale down the spatial structures as you described. To do this most efficiently, I would access the current parameter values (e.g. airway diameter, airway length, etc.) and scale them using the R toolbox.
In terms of connecting this inhalation model to a PK-Sim model, the model is not yet configured to do this very easily. The inhalation model needs to be connected to the full body PK-Sim model in a way that allows for intermittent deposits of molecule into the oral pathway due to mucociliary clearance. This configuration step still needs to be done with this inhalation model and it would take some work. I'm not sure if the other inhalation model would be easier to connect to PK-Sim.
Alternatively, you could use the inhalation model connected to a two-compartment model (as it is currently configured), which would give you an estimate of both lung and systemic exposure after inhalation.
Let me know if you have any other questions or concerns about configuring or using the model.
Thank you,
Moriah Pellowe

title: About the formular in MOBI
body_text:

Plasma of venous blood?Plasma of the liver pericentral?Or plasma of the liver periportal?

answer.body_text:
.. refers to the parent container (related to the location of the reaction)
s. https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-mobi/mobi-documentation/model-building-components#working-with-formulas for details.
When a simulation is created from building block, reaction will be created in every container where all educts, products and modifiers of this reaction are present. .. is then replaced with the path of that container.

title: The mass balance issue in whole-body PBPK due to the use of blood reservoirs?
body_text:
A recent paper discussed the use of blood reservoirs in full PBPK causing the breaking of the mass balance principle.
What do you think its impact on PK-sim?
Please see Section 5 of A myth of the well-stirred model: Is the well-stirred model good for high clearance drugs?
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ejps.2022.106134

comments.body_text:
Hi,
yes that seems to be an interesting question. You might want to try to find an answer going along this lines: Try to describe the Lidocaine data from Pang and Rowland, 1977 with PK-sim using their Clint input. Compare simulated values to their measurements. Make a sensitivity analysis including e.g. liver, organ blood volumes and other critical parameters like BPR, lipophilicity, organ blood flow. Check which of these parameters have an impact and how it needs to be changed to get the simulation to describe measured values.
Best, Tobias

title: Which parameters to tweak in order to make an absorption compartment behave as though not present
body_text:
Dears,
I would like to shut a part of the absorption compartment in the GI such that it behaves as it is not present and has no influence on the distribution. Which parameters should I change in order to get this effect to work?
Best,
Pri

answer.body_text:
Hi Pri,
not sure I understand your question correctly, but if you want to disable absorption from a given GI compartment, you could set the effective surface area enhancement factor to zero. However, note that your GI compartment will still be present, i.e. your drug has to travel through it before it can enter the next compartment and this can obviously have an effect on e.g. tmax and other PK parameters.
If you want to completely remove a GI compartment, I would try setting organ and liquid volumes, blood flows, surface area enhancement factors etc. to zero. If this won't work, I would directly go to MoBi to cut it out...
Best,
André

title: Partition Coefficients (PCS) Definition?
body_text:
Hi,
It's unclear how the PCs - reported as Interstitial:Plasma and Intracellular:Plasma - are defined and used in the ODEs. For example, for a 3-subcompartment (vascular, interstitial fluid and intracellular) tissue you want 2 PCs; one defined as the unbound fraction in plasma/unbound fraction in the interstitial fluid, and the second PC, defined as the PC as unbound fraction in the interstitial fluid/intracellular compartment.  These PCs are used in transport or intercompartment flux terms in the ODEs.  For example, the PC defined as unbound fraction in the interstitial fluid/intracellular compartments could be obtained by dividing the 2 PC provided by PK-Sim. Is that how you envision their use? Further, in a blood-flow limited tissue, the PC (assuming linear binding) is = unbound fraction in blood (or plasma)/unbound fraction in the tissue. Can this be extracted from PK-Sim.
Thanks, jim

comments.body_text:
Hi Jim,
you can find a more detailed explanation of PCs in the manual:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#adme-properties
Also, if you export your simulation to MoBi, it will likely get clearer how the ODEs are set up, or at least how the rates (e.g. transports) that make up the ODEs are set up.
Best,
Stephan

Hi Stephan,
Thanks, but it's still unclear how the PK-Sim generated PCs (yes, I see the 5 methods of calculation) are used in the ODEs. I can't load my PK-Sim model into MoBi to investigate as you suggest; clearly I don't use these programs. If you would have time for a Zoom later this week I can share my model.

Thanks and regards, jim

title: Partition coefficient water/protein for tissue distribution model
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I have a set of concentrations in plasma, liver, lung, and spleen (in mice), but it turns out my model didn't predict the concentrations in all the tissues well. I tried to use the parameter identifier to optimize the Partition coefficient (water/protein). With the identified partition coefficient (water/protein), which is ~10 time higher than the original one, the model prediction fits my data much better. I would like to know:


Is it ok to change the partition coefficient (water/protein) manually on PKsim/Mobi ?


The partition coefficient (water/protein) is calculated from the lipophilicity by the formula "PARAM_KProt", which is equal to (0.81 + 0.11 * 10^LogMA) / 24.92 * 5.0 . It there a reference/theory for this calculation?


Is it possible to conduct an in vitro experiment to obtain the partition coefficient (water/protein) value?


And also a quick question: the name of this parameter in PKsim 8 is "Partition coefficient (water/protein)", but according to the formula, it should be the ratio between the concentration in protein over the concentration in water? So its correct name should be "Partition coefficient (protein/water)" right?
Many thanks!
Andante

title: How to determine the transit rate for each intestinal segment from intestinal transit time?
body_text:
Hi, all
In recent days, for some reasons I am trying to modify an oral PBPK model in another software. In this software, the intestinal segment transit rate is defined as, taking duodenum as example, *(kTransportSmallIntestine*organismLengthDuodenum/organismLengthSmallInstestine)Duodenum.DrugSolid and kTransportSmallIntestine is equal to 0.693/organismMeanResidenceSmallIntestine.
I think assuming duodenum transit time is equal to SmallIntestineTransittime*organismLengthDuodenum/organismLengthSmallInstestine, the transit rate constant should be the inverse of that, which result in kTransportSmallIntestine*organismLengthSmallInstestine/organismLengthDuodenum, contradicting with the equation in software.
And I think the mean residence time determins the time at which about 63.2% of initial amount having passed through the compartment and inverse of mean residence time determining the transit rate. Why does organismMeanResidenceSmallIntestine correlate with 0.693 which is often seen in half-life associated expression?
Am I wrong? Which one is reasonable?
Then, I attempt to refer to the transit rate set in PK-Sim and MoBi, but what I get is:



The small intestinal transit time is only used to calculate a factor, not used in duodenum intestinal transit rate. However, the intestinal transit rate for each GI segment is distinct. How to determine this value? Is it independent with intestine small intestinal transit time?
Thanks for any comment.

comments.body_text:
The intestinal transit rate (absolute) is calculated by intestinal transit time/ SITT factor.

The intestinal transit rate (absolute)  is used in the passive transport building block to simulate the transit in the small intestine. So eventually the real intestinal transit rate is dependent on intestinal transit rate time.
However, I also don't know what's the meaning of SITT factor and why divide the intestinal transit rate with this factor

SITT = Small intestine transit time factor
It is a divisor to the rate such that if you increase it, the transit time will be increased (and the rate reduced). It is only a scaling factor for convenience.

I am not sure what “intestinal transit rate” actually means. This parameter seems to a large degree different from “intestinal transit rate (absolute)” in value, which is eventually used in simulation, but two parameters are using similar names. I think “intestinal transit rate” is used to make transit rate of each segment distinct. However, to achieve this goal, is it possible to just use anatomic information like length and radius plus intestinal transit time to define transit rate for GI segment？

It is a divisor to the rate such that if you increase it, the transit time will be increased (and the rate reduced). It is only a scaling factor for convenience.

What makes me confused is that the SITT factor isn't 1 by default, instead, it is calculated by a linear fit with slope and intercept. The slope and intercept are both constants which are dependents on the species but not on demographic data. So the SITT factor keeps the same for all human individuals. Based on this setting, it is very hard to adjust the SITT factor to change the SITT rate or time.

So the SITT factor keeps the same for all human individuals.

This is not correct. The factor is a linear function of the small intestinal transit time. Different individuals have in general different transit time and thus different scaling factors.
Thus if you change the transit time manually, or create a population where transit time vary between individuals - individuals will have different scaling factors.
S. also #484 and #580

title: Passive Transport between two compartments with similar structure
body_text: I'm wanting to have a Passive Transport defined between ExtracellularFluid compartments within different layers (see diagram below). How can I define the source and target tags to allow for this? I know that I could simply say "In container layer 1" for the source and "In container layer 2" for target, but I'm wanting to make many, many layers, and it would be ideal to have a single Passive Transport to apply to all of them. Does anyone have any ideas on how I could do this efficiently (instead of the brute force way, i.e. a separate Passive Transport for each neighborhood)?

answer.body_text:
I don't see a possibility to solve it with only 1 transport.
You will need to create N transports. They all can share the same formula, so you don't need N formulas at least.
P.S. transferring the issue to the forum.

comments.body_text:
So you want to have the transports
Layer1/ExtracellularFluid<=>Layer2/ExtracellularFluid<=>Layer3/ExtracellularFluid<=>... ?
It's enough to set SourceCriteria = "ExtracellularFluid" and TargetCriteria = "ExtracellularFluid"
Passive transports are only created if there is a neighborhood between 2 containers, so as long as you have a neighborhood between 2 extracellular fluid compartments of 2 adjacent layers - it's fine.
It will become problematic, if you have a neighborhood between 2 ExtracellularFluid compartments where you don't want to have a transport (e.g. additionally to Layer1/ExtracellularFluid <=> Layer2/ExtracellularFluid you have a neighborhood Layer1/ExtracellularFluid <=> SomethingElse/ExtracellularFluid ). Is this the case?
EDIT: no, this would not work, because it's not clear what is the source and the target...

I don't see a possibility to solve it with only 1 transport.
You will need to create N transports. They all can share the same formula, so you don't need N formulas at least.
P.S. transferring the issue to the forum.

title: Exclude Stationary Molecules from Compartments
body_text:
Hello OSP Community!
I think I may be missing something very basic. I am currently trying to modify a model developed in PK-Sim in Mobi by adding additional compartments. My model has several stationary molecules in the form of transporters and enzymes, but they were of course added to be "active" only in specific sites. However, I noticed that all of these molecules are present under every organ in the spatial structure including the compartments that I added, and I can't seem to find a way to add a "not in container" tag or the equivalent. Unfortunately, it is resulting in errors only for the newly added compartments when trying to configure the simulation.
A partial solution that I found is to add a tag to the individual parameters of the molecules, but this is so tedious and new errors emerge as soon as I get rid of the old ones. I have also tried searching for matching paths in the Molecule Start Values block, but even after extending, I cannot find my new compartments listed as a path element. So, is it possible to exclude molecules from containers in Mobi? Thanks in advance!

answer.body_text: Do you maybe have a copy of the Spatial Structures-BB? Then the MSV-BB refers to the "old" BB and not the extended one.

comments.body_text: Molecules are excluded from compartments in the Molecules Start Values building block through the IsPresent property. I would advice to adjust the pattern in your new organ based on the pattern in any standard organ
Do you maybe have a copy of the Spatial Structures-BB? Then the MSV-BB refers to the "old" BB and not the extended one.

title: GFR fraction and renal impairment and enzyme abundance
body_text: Hello, everyone. I recently modeled a study involving people with renal insufficiency, and did not find out if GFR Fraction changes when renal insufficiency occurs. I would like to ask you about GFR fraction and the changes in the abundance of CYP2B6, UGT2B7 and CYP2C8 enzymes in response to renal impairment.

comments.body_text:
Interesting question. I think GFR fraction could stay the same, although the maximal filtration rate will be modulated through the increase in fraction unbound.
CYP2C8 changes are covered by papers by Tan et al., 2018, PMID: 28990182. You could start there.
For isozymes without convincing data, you could use the averages determined by Sayama et al., 2014, PMID: 24912798.
As always, perform a sensitivity analysis across different assumptions for these parameters to quantify the uncertainty in your conclusions that is attributed to the degree of your uncertainty in these parameterizations.
Paul

title: About the kidney

title: Drug penetration into interstitial space
body_text:
Hi PKSim users,
I'd like to ask about describing drug exposure in interstitial space in PKSim. I've optimized the plasma concentrations and then simulated concentrations in interstitial space of muscle tissue (left figure). By modifying endothelial permeability and surface area (as discussed previously in the forum), I could describe the elimination phase but couldn't describe the delay and the extent of tissue penetration (right figure).
I am looking at differential equations of passive diffusion (plasma to interstitial and interstitial to cell) in Mobi. However, I'm unsure how to modify them to prolong the drug absorption time into tissue but maintain the extent. Is there any time parameter in PKSim that I am unaware of?
Any comments would be highly appreciated. Thanks so much for your help.

My compound is a small molecule with:

Molecular weight: 1449 g/mol
logP: -3.1 or 2.2 (varies across studies and based on molecular structure)
fu: 0.5-0.7
pKa: 2.1, 7.7, 8.9

Best,
Thi

comments.body_text:
Hi Thi,
My understanding is that the plasma interstitial permeability is set really high as an assumption. This parameter should mainly change the kinetics and not the extent (in equilibrium conditions). You can change this in MoBi but not in PK-Sim I believe? An interesting option here could be to model the molecule as a small molecule in the large molecule model, where the passive diffusion is set at a much lower permeability and the distribution kinetics are determined also by the two-pore model. I am not sure, but the default distribution kinetics might be a bit slower in this condition and you have more parameters that you can modify, such as the hydrodynamic radius. The last bit is a bit artificial and probably not what you want, but it might help. Correct me if I am wrong here @PavelBal

title: Metoprolol-CYP2D6 PBPK Model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have been working on a PBPK model of the CYP2D6 substrate metoprolol. The model is able to predict and describe the effect of CYP2D6 drug-gene interactions on the pharmacokinetics of (R)-metoprolol, (S)-metoprolol and their major active metabolite α-hydroxymetoprolol.
The manuscript, including comprehensive supplementary materials, has been published online in Pharmaceutics [1] and the metoprolol PK-Sim model (including metoprolol enantiomers and metabolite α-hydroxymetoprolol) is available here.
Related publication:
[1] Rüdesheim, S.; Wojtyniak, J.-G.; Selzer, D.; Hanke, N.; Mahfoud, F.; Schwab, M.; Lehr, T. Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling of Metoprolol Enantiomers and α-Hydroxymetoprolol to Describe CYP2D6 Drug-Gene Interactions. Pharmaceutics 2020, 12, 1200.
Kind regards,
Simeon

title: PBPK/PD model of nicotine and cotinine
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have recently been working on a PBPK/PD model of nicotine and its major metabolite cotinine. The thoroughly developed parent-metabolite model is able to predict plasma concentrations, nicotine brain tissue concentrations, and heart rate profiles after intravenous, oral, transdermal, and pulmonary nicotine intake.
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Clinical Pharmacokinetics. The PK-Sim and MoBi model files presenting the PBPK/PD model including simulations of clinical studies can be found here.
Related publication:
Kovar L, Selzer D, Britz H, Benowitz N, St. Helen G, Kohl Y, Bals R, Lehr T. Comprehensive parent-metabolite PBPK/PD modeling insights into nicotine replacement therapy strategies. Clin Pharmacokinet 2020;Epub:https://doi.org/10.1007/s40262-020-00880-4.
Best regards,
Lukas

comments.body_text: Excellent! Thanks for this!

title: single dose and AUC
body_text: Hello, I recently started to use PK-SIM modeling to complete my graduation project. I found that the blood drug concentration curve of single dose simulated by software can only be simulated up to 24h, while many data in the literature are up to 72h. Will there be a big difference between the simulated AUC and that in the literature?

comments.body_text:
Hi, you can adjust the simulation time in the setting of your simulation to 72h or any value you need as shown below:

You might want to check out the comprehensive documentation here:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-simulations#pk-parameters
Best, Tobias

If your concern is that you have only data till 24h and data till 72 hours might change the AUC, a good check is to look at the difference between AUC 0-inf and AUC 0-tlast, with a simulation until 24h. This difference is also expressed in the parameter % AUC (tlast - infinity). The smaller this percentage, the lower the uncertainty in AUC induced by the limited sampling time. You can repeat this for a simulation till 72h if you really want to compare uncertainties between sampling time scenarios.

title: Uncertainty of parameter estimates on log-scale/ as geometric standard deviations
body_text:
Hi All,
In PK-Sim and Mobi, the uncertainty on parameter estimates is expressed at the parameter value +/- uncertainty.
This symmetrical uncertainty is based on the normal distribution, but with many biological parameter values, a log-normal distribution is more appropriate, as these values cannot become negative (Volumes, Flows, Amounts, etc.). This becomes especially important when parameter uncertainties become relatively large and the 95% CI stretches into the negative domain (which is not uncommon). Would it be an option to express the uncertainty as geometric standard deviation or as parameter value */÷ uncertainty? A logical implementation would be to do this for all parameters for which "scaling" was set to "log" in the PI Parameters window.
Best,
Wilbert

comments.body_text: Hi All, maybe I can rephrase this: Does anyone know how 95% CI intervals are calculated if parameter identification is performed for log-scaled parameters? I understood from earlier discussions this is derived from the FIM, is there any conversion from log to linear scale involved for parameters identified on log-scale to get the CI on linear scale? @Yuri05, do you have information on this?

title: 'Some variables become negative' when I simulate oral formulation models
body_text:
Hi, all
When I simulate some oral formualtion models, a notice appears like this:

I attach the PK-Sim file to this issue. In this file, the simulation 'iv' and 'suspension' are fitted to observed data, and the fitted parameters are used to consturct models of other formulations. Among these formulations, 'reference', 'T1' and 'T2' would result in the above notice (with different specifi  variables) .
How can I resolve this problem?
Lacidipine.zip

answer.body_text:
For all oral formulations - except for Particle dissolution -  (InVitro) dissolved fraction of dose is assumed monotonically increasing.
In all your "problematic" formulations ('reference', 'T1' and 'T2') it's not the case. E.g.:

This is not allowed and leads to negative concentrations in the lumen.

Please make sure that fraction (dose) in all table formulations is non decreasing.
@AndreDlm @KatrinCoboeken FYI

Hi,
what is your dosing scheme? po? single or multiple dose?
If it is multiple dose I can probably reproduce you issue: If I dose once daily for three days and set Simulation time to 4 days total body clearance is not calculated:

But if I reduce simulation time to two days, PK-Sim calculates it:

I am not sure if this behaviour makes sense. Probably it is a bug.
Hope this might help you. Best Tobias

The behaviour that @tobiasK2001 is observing does not make sense.
AUC_Inf and therefore CL are only calculated for Single administration. While we may change this behaviour in the future (/cc @PavelBal  and @Yuri05 ), this is the current behaviour.
@Fate-afshar CL is nothing more than TotalDrugMassPerBodyWeight/AucInf. You can calculate it yourself for instance by using AUC_inf_TD1 (considering only the first dose)
Reducing the application to only use TWO administrations should still consider the application to be multiple dosing and not show AUC_inf. This is where there is potentially a bug.  @tobiasK2001 Can you share that project with us so that we can check the behaviour?

Yes indeed. Sane behaviour for iv multiple dose as for po multiple dose.

title: Digital Signature / Hash
body_text:
Dear,
Our IT Security guidelines require that we provide either an installer that is digitally signed before being able to deploy it on a users' workstation. I wanted to have PK-Sim installed for one user, but it seems that it is missing the digital signature. In that case we would still be allowed to install it if we can provide the hash (MD5 or SHA256) from the developers.
Could I ask you to please share the hash (MD5 or SHA256) of pk-sim-portable-setup.zip available here:  (or of both PKSim.BatchTool.exe and PKSim.exe)?
I suspect that some users are also going to request the whole suite. If you don't mind, could I ask you to please share also the hash of OSPSuite-Full.11.0.139.exe ?
Thank you very much

comments.body_text: @Yuri05 Would be a good idea to include MD5 hash to downloadable assest in every release.
@Nurbinch I have added the hashes to the OSP Suite Setup / PK-Sim Setup / MoBi Setup

title: What's the differences among brain tissue, intestitial and intracellular?
body_text:
Hi, I'm working on a model for a CNS drug. Several questions bother me. Could anyone help me?

What's the unit of brain tissue in the software? The Y-axis is ng/mL but normally the tissue concentration should be normalized with ng.
In the simulation, the drug shows in brain tissue and interstitial but absolutely zero in intracellular. Is this regular? What's the difference between interstitial and intracellular concentration?
I don't know the transporters for this drug, but I got the brain/plasma concentration ratio from animal PK study. Could I set the brain/plasma ratio fixed in the software?

title: where to change fu,mic in PK Sim
body_text:
Hello, all
I wonder if there are some places to change the fu,mic in PK Sim?
Because when I modeling the DDI, I find that I have to change the Ki from 6 to 0.01 to match the clinical change, it's too exaggerated.
So I want to check the fu,mic value in PK Sim, however, I cannot find this parameter in PK Sim.
Or do you have any suggestions for when predicted value don't match the observed value in Drug drug interaction modeling.
Thanks you in advance!

answer.body_text:
Hi @1998225,
To my knowledge, there is no way to enter a fu,mic value directly in PK-Sim. However, you can adjust the respective parameter in PK-Sim (i.e. overwrite the value) to adjust for fu,mic.
For instance, if a fu,mic value was determined/calculated for your microsomal assay, you would typically try to set the value of Ki to Ki * fu,mic. Make sure beforehand that the authors of your source have not already adjusted their reported values for fu,mic. If you have no information on microsomal binding in your assay, you could try approximation methods for fu,mic; for instance, have a look at this article by Austin et al.
Hope this helps,
Simeon

body_text:

title: Can't select formulation
body_text:
I have faced an issue where I can't select my formulation after I changed it from dissolution to first order. How should I solve this issue? Is there any reason behind this? I am clueless as I am a complete newbie here. :D
Also, is there any way that I can import metabolizing enzyme variants into PK-Sim? Like what type of files does it support and how should I import it?
Thank you in advance.
Yours,
Harry

comments.body_text: hm, strange. I can reproduce the issue for first and zero order formulation. All other formulation work and I cannot see why it should not work with zero or first order. @msevestre, @Yuri05 Can this be a bug?

It's a missing feature. Currently Zero/First Order formulations cannot be combined with oral administrations.
As a workaround: approximate your first order formulation with the table formulation. For that:

Open your first order formulation, right click on the formulation graph, select "Export to Excel" and export your first order formulation to excel file:





Create new table formulation and import it from the exported First Order formulation file:





When creating your simulation: select this table formulation instead of first order.

title: PKa value
body_text: I've received an error in PK-Sim stating "pKa value should be greater than zero". Is there a way to adjust it such that a pKa slightly less than zero is accepted? "My compound is approximately -0.20"

answer.body_text:
@ibalshammari , no, negative values are not allowed in PK-Sim (not deemed necessary).
As your pKa is way out of the physiological range, you might as well set it to zero.

title: Question about the definitions of several parameters (K_water_cell, K_water_int, K_rbc, K_int_pls, permeability and SA_pls_int)
body_text:
Dear team,
I am trying to understand all the background equations embedded in the PK-Sim model. I have a few questions about the definition of some of the parameters and equations.


I really don’t understand what is the definition of the Partition coefficient (interstitial/water) K_water_int and Partition coefficient (intracellular/water) K_water_cell in the Ref[1] for the distribution of the drug instead of the absorption. In all five partition coefficient calculate methods provide in the software [2], all of them discussed either tissue/plasma or blood cell/plasma partition coefficient. However, non of them has shown a partition coefficient of each sub-compartment against water. Do I interpret anything wrong? What’s is the formula to calculate the K_water_cell and K_water_int.


I also want to clarify about the Partition coefficient (blood cells/plasma) K_rbc. Is its calculation depends on one of the five partition coefficient calculation methods we selected?  However, how about the Partition coefficient (interstitial/plasma) K_int_pls? Same question as to the previous question that all of the five methods discussed either tissue/plasma or blood cell/plasma partition coefficient but not the interstitial/plasma. Is this K_int_pls simply defined as the fraction of the extracellular drug over the plasma drug?


About the permeability (not the Specific intestinal permeability) is defined as the following equation: IsSmallMolecule ? (MWEff *1E9 / 336) ^ (-6) * 10^LogMA / 5 * 1E-4 *1E-1: 0. How do those numbers come from? Is there any referenced article to this equation?


About the surface area (plasma/interstitial) SA_pls_int, It is defined as (V_organ* 1000 / A)^0.75 * B * 1E2 . A and B are different inputs depending on the different organs. What are A and B stand for? How do those numbers come from? Is there any referenced article to this equation?


References:

https://static-content.springer.com/esm/art%3A10.1007%2Fs10928-017-9559-4/MediaObjects/10928_2017_9559_MOESM1_ESM.txt
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#adme-properties

answer.body_text:
Hi @jessechao830330 ,
excellent questions. ou can find some of the answers in the documentation: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#distribution


K_water_cell and K_water_int represent the ratio bound to non-water components in the cellular or interstitial space. Their reciprocal value basically represents the unbound fractions in cell and interstitial space (which is why you then often see expressions like [C]/K_water_cell in equations/formulas that define inputs to cellular processes)


Yes, K_int_pls is (simply) defined as the fraction of the extracellular (as interstitial) drug over the plasma drug based on the interstitial/plasma protein and water fractions:


See section "How are permeability surface-area (PxSA) products predicted in PK-Sim®?" found below the link above. There are also a couple of references listed.


Same as for 3.


Hope this helps,
Stephan

comments.body_text:
Hi @jessechao830330 ,
excellent questions. ou can find some of the answers in the documentation: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#distribution


K_water_cell and K_water_int represent the ratio bound to non-water components in the cellular or interstitial space. Their reciprocal value basically represents the unbound fractions in cell and interstitial space (which is why you then often see expressions like [C]/K_water_cell in equations/formulas that define inputs to cellular processes)


Yes, K_int_pls is (simply) defined as the fraction of the extracellular (as interstitial) drug over the plasma drug based on the interstitial/plasma protein and water fractions:


See section "How are permeability surface-area (PxSA) products predicted in PK-Sim®?" found below the link above. There are also a couple of references listed.


Same as for 3.


Hope this helps,
Stephan
Thank you for your explanation. If I have any further question, I will let you know. Take care.

@StephanSchaller, I'm still unclear as to what exactly the "Partition coefficient (water/container)" parameters represent. When examining the RHS of compartments I have noticed that this parameter multiplied by drug concentration gives drug amount (at least, I think that is correct). Why is that exactly?
Forgive me if I should have created a new topic, felt like this was related enough where it belonged on the same thread.
Thanks,
DS

@DonkeySaddle ,

I have noticed that this parameter multiplied by drug concentration gives drug amount (at least, I think that is correct).

No, this should not convert concentrations to amounts. Do you have an example of such a RHS?
Partition coefficients (PC) reflect the ratio "dissolved" within two different "volume fractions" and are dimensionless (so do not convert dimensions). In this case, the PC describes the theoretical concentration ratio of compound dissolved within water and the "fluid" of a specific container (could be "intracellular" with the cellular space fluid consisting of e.g. water, lipids, and protein).
The theory is that only compound dissolved in water (i.e., unbound) is "available" for enzymatic degradation, hence the division of concentrations within that container with the "Partition coefficient (water/container)".
Hope this helps,
Stephan

@StephanSchaller,
That makes perfect sense. I don't think I realized part of the RHS of dM/dt can be interpreted as being "unitless". Here's the Matlab code for the RHS of an Alprazolam metabolite in periportal liver intracellular compartment.
`y(818) = 0; % Xanax_Itraconazole_fed|Organism|Liver|Periportal|Intracellular|Alprazolam-CYP3A4-alpha-OH pathway Metabolite
dy(818) =  (P_13610P_5771P_10747P_15916(P_12486/(P_16403+(P_15916*P_12486))));
The above equation
dM/dt = (CYP3A4_con * Kcat * vol * Part_cof * Alpraz_con)/(Part_cof * Alpraz_con + Km)
P_13610 = CYP3A4|Concentration
P_5771 = Alprazolam-CYP3A4-alpha-OH pathway|kcat_app
P_10747 = Liver|Periportal|Intracellular|Volume
P_15916 = Alprazolam|Partition coefficient (water/container)
P_12486 = Alprazolam|Concentration
P_16403 = Alprazolam-CYP3A4-alpha-OH pathway|Km_app`
I was thrown by the Alprazolam concentration variables (P_12486) not being multiplied by the liver volume(P_10747). Of course, this could be done, you would just have to also multiply the Km parameter (P_16403) by the liver volume as well.
Is the interpretation correct?
Thanks,
DS

Hi @DonkeySaddle ,
This is a Michaelis-Menten equation:
CYP3A4_con * Kcat * vol * Part_cof  equals to the dimension amount/time (µmol/min)
and the rest Alpraz_con/(Part_cof * Alpraz_con + Km) is dimensionless as:
P_13610 = CYP3A4|Concentration --> µmol/l
P_5771 = Alprazolam-CYP3A4-alpha-OH pathway|kcat_app --> 1/min
P_10747 = Liver|Periportal|Intracellular|Volume --> liter
P_15916 = Alprazolam|Partition coefficient (water/container) --> unitless
P_12486 = Alprazolam|Concentration --> µmol/l
P_16403 = Alprazolam-CYP3A4-alpha-OH pathway|Km_app --> µmol/l
@StephanSchaller, Thank you so much!

@StephanSchaller Just a quick confirm with you that for the partition coefficient of intracellular : plasma. I selected Rodgers and Rowland method to calculate the partition coefficient. I saw that there are terms of f_ew and Ka_PR in the equation popping up from the PK-Sim software. In my knowledge, these two terms refer to the "Extracellular" part in the tissue. However, this equation is used to calculate the "Intracellular" : plasma partition. Did the software actually extract those two terms of extracellular drug when calculating? Or the equation includes those two term for some reasons? Please correct me if I make any mistake. Thank you.
I am looking forward to hearing from you.

title: Question about competitive inhibition
body_text:
Dear all,
In PK-Sim, when creating a competitive inhibition, in the description box, it is reported the following (this screenshot is taken from the itraconazole model)

As you can see, in the description it is mentioned that inhibition "Can be overcome by high substrate concentrations". But on the other side, when looking at the equations of the competitive inhibition, the substrate concentrations are not taken into account. As you can see in the formula below, the inhibitor only affects the clearance of the victim drug, but the victim concentrations are not taken into account (this is in agreement with all the formulas I have seen for DDI, so I think the implemented equations are correct).

But then, could you please help me to understand the specification "Can be overcome by high substrate concentrations", since I am trying to better understand the interaction as it is described in PK-Sim.
Thanks a lot in advance for your help!
Donato

answer.body_text:
Hi @teutonicod, the substrate concentrations are "S".
With the competitive inhibition, only the Km is increased (no effect on Vmax). This means, if your victim (i.e., substrate) concentrations are always >> Km (also >> Km,app in the inhibited case), the inhibition will not have a significant effect as the metabolic rate is running at Vmax in either case.
Let me know if this was understandable.
Cheers, Stephan

comments.body_text:
Hi @teutonicod, the substrate concentrations are "S".
With the competitive inhibition, only the Km is increased (no effect on Vmax). This means, if your victim (i.e., substrate) concentrations are always >> Km (also >> Km,app in the inhibited case), the inhibition will not have a significant effect as the metabolic rate is running at Vmax in either case.
Let me know if this was understandable.
Cheers, Stephan
Maybe "can be overcome" is not an ideal wording to paraphrase this :-)

Hi @StephanSchaller,
Thanks for your comment. Indeed it supports my impression that the wording "Can be overcome by high substrate concentrations" is not really correct. Let's hear also other opinion, but I think we should consider if changing the description if this does not reflect reality.
Anyway I was very interested in what you mention. If I understood your point, if the victim has concentrations >> Km, the metabolic rate will run at Vmax and the inhibitor should not have anymore an impact. I agree!
But I am just wondering what happens in the frequent case that you have just a linear clearance that is assigned to a specific enzyme. I checked the equations, and in this case, the inhibition factor on the slide is applied to the linear clearance instead of Km. In this case, the clearance will always be affected by the inhibitor, right?
Best,
Donato

title: population simulation ；5%-95%confidence interval
body_text: Hello everyone, I have been using PK-SIM modeling for my graduation project recently, and I found that the 5%-95% confidence interval of my group simulation was very narrow, and my senior students had a wider confidence interval than me before. What might be the reason for this?

comments.body_text:
Hi,
it is a bit hard to say why the concentration range of your model outputs are different, without knowing the used processes and population parameters.
One thing that is clear is that what you showed here from PK-sim is not a confidence interval. It is just the range of predicted concentrations based on the biometrics and number of individuals in your Population (including  interindividual variability). Your model parameters were, I guess, fitted to data using a model with a PK-Sim mean individual. In the Parameter Identification you will find the confidence interval of your estimated model parameters fitted to individual data, if you have done so and calculated sensitivity.
But when you propagate your model based on a mean individual to a Population, what you get from PK-Sim is more a "prediction interval" and this should not be confused with a Confidence interval you get e.g. from NLME models.
Hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias

title: Reference concentration
body_text:
Hi Everyone,
Is it okay to use only Km and Vmax values of different isozymes of an enzyme by keeping the reference concentration as default value of 1umol/l in PK-Sim?

comments.body_text: I am not sure I understand 100% what you are trying to do, but if you mean you can e.g. implement a number of isoenzymes with  reference concentration as default value of 1umol/l and use individually characterized Km and Vmax values, sure, you can do this, but you have to be aware, that the effective Vmax is a product of kcat and reference concentration...

Thank you Stephan,
I am working on an enzyme which has several classes that contribute towards the particular substrate metabolism. I got the required protein content of each class of the enzyme from the litrature. When I tried to put these values in the model, the simulation changes drastically and it doesnt look good. But when I keep the reference concentration of each class as a default value of 1umol/l  the Vmax (kcat) value fit good to the observed data. Also I have selected in -vitro metabolic rate in the presence of recombinant CYPs/ Michaelis-Menten enzyme process so I am using the Km and Kcat value in simulation.
Thanks

If you have selected in -vitro metabolic rate in the presence of recombinant CYPs/ Michaelis-Menten you will get specific activity and this needs then to be applied to the actual concentrations of the enzymes.

required protein content of each class of the enzyme

What type of Data is this. Do you have actual measured PROTEIN levels (for each tissue) or relative tissue mRNA expression with a reference measurement?

I have the data for the relative protein content of the enzyme in the liver which I got from the literature. The Vmax for the different isozymes are mentioned as per min value in the literature which goes quite well in the simulation. But I am not sure about the reference concentration because it is too high for the simulation

I have the data for the relative protein content

So this is not available as an absolute measure such as mol/l?

The Vmax for the different isozymes are mentioned as per min value in the literature

The in -vitro metabolic rate in the presence of recombinant CYPs/ Michaelis-Menten requires Vmax as Amount/time per Amount recombinant Enzyme... how else did you derive kcat?
If you do not have any absolute measures of enzyme amounts a feasible approach is to scale the reference concentration such that it describes your data well. If the default is a good fit, then you are all set.
But this makes it technically a FITTED model and not a bottom-up IVIVE-based prediction.

title: Numerical differences between two setups of reversible reaction in MoBi
body_text:
I've set up a reversible reaction in MoBi two different ways that I had expected to be equivalent: 1) as a single reaction and 2) as two separate reactions (one for binding and the other for disassociation). The parameters used result in numerical instability, but it seems that using separate reactions allows the simulation to run at a higher tolerance. Why would this be the case?


Both simulations will run for 24 hours, but only the 2nd one will run for 2 years (with tolerances = 1E-10). I can also lower the tolerances for the single reaction simulation to 1E-16 so that it also runs, but then the results of the two simulations are different. The simulation with separate reactions is very noisy but it can run at a tolerance of 1E-10. The single reaction needs the smaller tolerance of 1E-16 but it looks cleaner. So is the single reaction simulation more correct?


I've attached the project file below.
test.zip

answer.body_text: The values of used k_off are different..

comments.body_text: Can you try using the scaling factor features and see if it makes any difference?
I am very interested in the answer, too. I thought both ways would result in exact the same ODE system.
Can you generate the ode and see the difference? (Start using the --dev mode).

You can calculate the scale divisors using the button marked in the attached figure (if you are in a simulation). The scale divisors become part of the MSV building block after calculation (see the column there). Highly recommended if some of your state variables have very small values.

Heare the matlab output
function dy = ODERHSFunction(Time,y)

P_0 = 1;  % Test|Organism|Volume
P_1 = 6;  % Test|Organism|Rxn|k_on
P_2 = 24;  % Test|Organism|Rxn|k_off
P_3 = 1e-16;  % Test|Organism|Rxn|V
P_4 = 1e-16;  % AbsTol
P_5 = 1e-16;  % RelTol
P_6 = 1e-10;  % H0
P_7 = 0;  % HMin
P_8 = 60;  % HMax
P_9 = 100000;  % MxStep
P_10 = 1;  % UseJacobian
P_11 = (IIf((P_0 > 0),(y(1)/P_0),0));  % Test|Organism|A|Concentration
P_12 = (IIf((P_0 > 0),(y(2)/P_0),0));  % Test|Organism|B|Concentration
P_13 = (IIf((P_0 > 0),(y(3)/P_0),0));  % Test|Organism|AB|Concentration

dy(1) =  ...
            (0-((P_1*y(1)*(y(2)/P_3))-(P_2*y(3))));
dy(2) =  ...
            (0-((P_1*y(1)*(y(2)/P_3))-(P_2*y(3))));
dy(3) =  ...
            ((P_1*y(1)*(y(2)/P_3))-(P_2*y(3)));
dy = dy';


vs
function dy = ODERHSFunction(Time,y)

P_0 = 1;  % separate|Organism|Volume
P_1 = 6;  % separate|Organism|Rxn|k_on
P_2 = 24;  % separate|Organism|Rxn|k_off
P_3 = 1e-16;  % separate|Organism|Rxn|V
P_4 = 0.0166666666666667;  % separate|Organism|Disassociate|k_off
P_5 = 1e-10;  % AbsTol
P_6 = 1e-10;  % RelTol
P_7 = 1e-10;  % H0
P_8 = 0;  % HMin
P_9 = 60;  % HMax
P_10 = 100000;  % MxStep
P_11 = 1;  % UseJacobian
P_12 = (IIf((P_0 > 0),(y(1)/P_0),0));  % separate|Organism|A|Concentration
P_13 = (IIf((P_0 > 0),(y(2)/P_0),0));  % separate|Organism|B|Concentration
P_14 = (IIf((P_0 > 0),(y(3)/P_0),0));  % separate|Organism|AB|Concentration

dy(1) =  ...
            (0-(P_1*y(1)*(y(2)/P_3))) ...
            +(P_4*y(3));
dy(2) =  ...
            (0-(P_1*y(1)*(y(2)/P_3))) ...
            +(P_4*y(3));
dy(3) =  ...
            (P_1*y(1)*(y(2)/P_3)) ...
            +(0-(P_4*y(3)));
dy = dy';
The values of used k_off are different..

title: Parameter identification and optimization of unknown parameters
body_text: Hello everyone, when I read the information on the forum, I found that the Kcat value of some drug transporters or metabolic enzymes is unknown, and there is no literature or experimental source, which is obtained through parameter identification and optimization. I would like to ask you about this unknown parameter, how to set the initial value and range of parameter identification? The first picture is I find the content of the screenshots in BBS, say about unknown parameters is obtained by optimization and parameter identification, the second picture is a model screenshots, also said the unknown about Kcat, I don't have the retrieved literature value, but I don't know modeling is how to set the initial value and range, what's the basis? I have been puzzled by this question recently. I am looking forward to your answers.

comments.body_text:
Hi @WTY-tt,
Depending on the impact of the parameter, the data that you have, and the number of parameters that you are fitting, the initial parameter value and its range are more or less important. In the ideal case where you are fitting only 1 sensitive parameter with a dataset that allows its identification, you can pick a random start value and a wide range and 'Scaling' on 'Log' if you have no prior info on the possible values. It is always a good idea to first do some simulations with various values to give you a better idea of the impact of the parameter. This is also the best way to get to a proper initial value. The more parameters you are fitting simultaneously, the more data you need, and the higher the likelihood of unsatisfactory results. In the screenshot above, you have already a lot of parameters open, which might give problems or at least very long runtimes.
Best,
Wilbert

@WTY-tt could you use some assumption on the number of cells/mL to obtain pmol/min?
@noxavier When faced with too many parameters to identify each realiably, one can choose to optimise them all simultaneously and accept that their values as such are only one possible combination that result in a satisfactory fit, while the value of each separate parameter remains uncertain. Either prior information or additional data is needed to reduce the uncertainty. A sensitivity analysis can help to eliminate parameters from the identification if they have no impact on the simulation result. Their uncertainty remains, but it does not necessarily translate into uncertainty of the simulations. One has to be careful here that the sensitivity analysis is done in such a way that it covers the scenarios for which simulations are required.

title: How can we build permeability limited liver model in PKSIM
body_text:
Dear All,
Is there any option in PKSIM that can be used to build a permeability limited liver model. Most literature suggest using this model for drug transporter related PBPK model building and DDI predictions. Especially when the disposition is governed mainly by uptake and efflux transporters.

answer.body_text:
Hi @StephanSchaller and @NileshGaud ,
Thanks for the question and the discussion, since I think it is a very interesting topic. I am not sure I would define the model in PK-Sim, "permeability limited". From my understanding this type of definition come from other software, for which  the model can be based on one of the two following assumptions:

perfusion limited (and in this case from my understanding the distribution is mainly driven by Kp)
permeability limited, for which the distribution is driven by permeabilities

From my understanding, it is difficult to compare the model, but if we really want to, I would say that in PK-Sim we do not make any of the aforementioned assumptions, but we use both a distribution model and a permeability model to define organ Kp and permeabilities which both drive the distribution of a compound. Which one is more relevant, depends on the molecule phys-chem parameters.
I would not define PK-Sim a permeability limited model, since I suppose you could have a parameter set for which the distribution may become limited by perfusion (although I have to say that I never tested the molecule parameter space to check if this is possible).
Donato

comments.body_text: Hi Nilesh, PK-Sim is permeability-limited by default

See: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#adme-properties
And: https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/mechanistic-modeling-of-pharmacokinetics-and-dynamics/modeling-concepts/principles-of-pbpk-modeling#l-adme-logic-and-routes-of-administration

Hi @StephanSchaller and @NileshGaud ,
Thanks for the question and the discussion, since I think it is a very interesting topic. I am not sure I would define the model in PK-Sim, "permeability limited". From my understanding this type of definition come from other software, for which  the model can be based on one of the two following assumptions:

perfusion limited (and in this case from my understanding the distribution is mainly driven by Kp)
permeability limited, for which the distribution is driven by permeabilities

From my understanding, it is difficult to compare the model, but if we really want to, I would say that in PK-Sim we do not make any of the aforementioned assumptions, but we use both a distribution model and a permeability model to define organ Kp and permeabilities which both drive the distribution of a compound. Which one is more relevant, depends on the molecule phys-chem parameters.
I would not define PK-Sim a permeability limited model, since I suppose you could have a parameter set for which the distribution may become limited by perfusion (although I have to say that I never tested the molecule parameter space to check if this is possible).
Donato

@teutonicod, yes, all what you write is correct and I do agree and thank you for providing this additional detail. Having a case of limitation by perfusion of permeation in PK-Sim depends on the physchem, but structurally, PK-Sim ALWAYS considers permeation. Other PBPK models (often used in e.g. environmental tox) are BY STRUCTURE perfusion limited models. They do not have a concept of permeation and organ subcompartments.

Hi @StephanSchaller and @teutonicod
Thank you for providing the details it really helped me to understand the difference between the two models.
Nilesh
In the case of highly lipophilicity volatile anesthetics that are inhaled, tissue distribution would be perfusion limited I think.

title: for population of CKD stages in version 10, Did they take into account the CYP3A4 expression level variability in CKD?
body_text:
I want to ask about a Populations for CKD stages in PK-Sim version 10, did they account for CYP3A4 level variability published in literature? this paper includes the decrease in expression ''Modeling and predicting drug
pharmacokinetics in patients
with renal impairment
Expert Rev. Clin. Pharmacol. 4(2), 261–274 (2011)''
it seems that they did not account for that because the concentration in all populations was 4.3 micromole/l, and I cannot edit that even in simulation.

comments.body_text:
No decreases in nonrenal clearance are applied. The compounds in the evaluation were predominantly cleared renally. The population includes changes in foundational and systemic parameters that apply regardless of the route of elimination of the compound.
You can apply changes to nonrenal clearance manually as you see fit. To do this, make sure to set the appropriate reference concentration in the individual upon which your desired CKD population is templated. There is a ton of conflicting literature on the topic. One of my favourite methods is to use the mean changes parameterized by Sayama et al. 2014.
Also consider the impact on renal transporters. OCT transporter function is largely conserved, but OAT function may be severely decreased.

Simulation using these templates does not confirm any edits, either default or user defined for CYP3A4 concentration
please help me to use the templates and edits for CYP3A4 concentration

title: accessing a process rate parameter
body_text:
Hi All,
I would like to use the process rate parameter for endosomal clearance in the reactions building block, is this possible?
I have checked the "create process parameter" checkbox, and I can see the process rate parameters in each organ in the simulation (under neighborhoods) and I can also plot them. However I cannot find it in the reactions building block. Does anyone know how I can do this? I can find the process rate parameter in the reactions building block for the paracellular absorption, which is a transport that has already the process rate parameter generated in the default large molecule mice model that I exported to Mobi. I believe the issue is that I need to update the reactions building block in such a way that it recognizes what I have done in the passive transport building block. I have already tried to do this by creating a new simulation but no luck. Thanks in advance for the help!
Wilbert
P.S. I am working with version 8

answer.body_text:
ProcessRate parameters are created in the simulations only, not in the building blocks.
However you still can use them in the formulas, but you need to create such a reference manually.
For this: right click on the References area of the formula and select Create Reference...

This will create an empty reference like below:

Now enter the alias and the path of your ProcessRate-parameter manually and set the dimension.

Path can be absolute or relative
Dimension is either Amount per time (for amount based projects) or Concentration per time (for conc based projects)

Attached you can find an example with 3 reactions. Reactions RE2 and RE3 use the process rate parameter of the reaction RE1 in their kinetics definition (in RE2 it's referenced via absolute path, in RE3 via relative path).
Example_ProcessRate.zip
