title: Setting up a coupled Parent-Metabolite PBPK model
body_text:
Dear OSP community,
I’m extending an already developed PBPK model for a compound that is metabolised by the liver to a major metabolite, in order to predict the PK in healthy volunteers.
Firstly, I have collected the physiochemical properties of the metabolite, as well as the in vitro measurements that were used to parameterize its clearance process from the literature. Secondly, upon comparing the simulated metabolite exposure vs the observed (experimental) data, I have optimised the hepatic clearance, Lipophilicity (LogP), and the fraction unbound in plasma within the reported literature values.
Below you can see the resulting concentration-time profiles of the parent (in red) and the metabolite (in blue) vs the observed data. A strong misspecification is prominent in the first 30 min of the simulation, even after optimization of relevant parameters within the literature ranges.

Then as a trial, I did another parameter identification step with unconstrained values for the previous parameters, resulting in the curve below. While the misspecification is now gone, unrealistic parameter values were obtained, i.e. a LogP more than 10 times below the reported range and the clearance 5 times higher than the reported range.

The fact that these unrealistic parameters lead to a good fit led me to the assumption that I am missing some process in between the formation of the metabolite in the liver and its appearance in plasma, has anyone of you had a similar experience and could they point me towards a possible solution? The alternative would be to accept the alternative parameters as such but I am hesitant to do so.
Please let me know if anything is unclear or more information should be provided.
Thanks in advance,
Aya

comments.body_text:
Hi Aya
It is likely because the metabolite is being formed too quickly and too extensively.
Are you attributing 100% of parent clearance to the hepatic route, which forms your major metabolite? Consider the possibility that other clearance mechanisms are at work (minor hepatic metabolite, renal or biliary)
Paul

In addition to @prvmalik comments, consider reduced (from a default of 100cm/min) endothelial permeability (it seems you compound reached liver too quickly).
What is the reported LogP range?
Is the metabolite formed by Phase I or Phase II enzymes?
Best,
Stephan

Hi Paul,

It is likely because the metabolite is being formed too quickly and too extensively.


Are you attributing 100% of parent clearance to the hepatic route, which forms your major metabolite? Consider the possibility that other clearance mechanisms are at work (minor hepatic metabolite, renal or biliary)
Paul

I agree with you that the first assumption we made to this curve, is the fast formation of the metabolite and since the parent compound is mainly metabolized by CYP3A4, so the Kcat of CYP3A4 was changed based on the assumption of ~ 90% of the parent is metabolized to the main metabolite (and the other 10% to a sink metabolite).  Then I tried different lower values for this process but it still has this steep peak in the metabolite exposure.

Hi Stephan,

In addition to @prvmalik comments, consider reduced (from a default of 100cm/min) endothelial permeability (it seems you compound reached liver too quickly).

from what I know (as I have little experience in PK-Sim), that is already assumed in the simulation block for each compound,  but How can I change that value??

What is the reported LogP range?

The reported range of the LogP = 2.0 - 2.5 --> for the metabolite
In the first curve, LogP = 2.291 --> optimized using the observed data
While for the second curve, LogP=0.047

Is the metabolite formed by Phase I or Phase II enzymes?

The metabolite is formed by CYP3A4
Best,
Aya
WHat is the LogP for the parent?

WHat is the LogP for the parent?

Log P = 2.897 --> previously optimised for the parent from range values (2.8 - 3.53)

Permeability is probably not restrictive for a compound of logP > 2.5 especially on that short time scale you have presented.
Also check that your metabolite is being formed inside cells rather than in plasma (location of enzyme in the model).
I see you are implying that there is zero renal and zero biliary clearance?
yes,, I agree with @prvmalik

Dear Paul,

Permeability is probably not restrictive for a compound of logP > 2.5 especially on that short time scale you have presented.
Also check that your metabolite is being formed inside cells rather than in plasma (location of enzyme in the model).

Yes, the enzyme is localized intracellularly in the tissue and endosomal in the vascular endothelium.

I see you are implying that there is zero renal and zero biliary clearance?

For the metabolite, I only applied total hepatic clearance. While for the parent the GFR = 0.64 which was optimized earlier as well. and no biliary clearance for both.

Hi everyone,
I did two other parameter identification steps, the 1st one with the permeability parameter, where the original value was P(plasma <-> interstitial) = 100 cm/min, while the optimised value decreased by almost 100% to give P(plasma<->interstitial) = 0.121 cm/min, resulting in the curve below. And yet the misspecification became more prominent than before (in both early and late phases).

The 2nd P.I, with unconstrained values for the following original permeability parameters:

P (plasma<->interstitial) = 100 cm/min
P (interstitial->intracellular) = 0.007268 cm/min
P (intracellular->interstitial) = 0.007268 cm/min

While the optimised value decreased by ~ 99% for the 3 parameters, to give:

P (plasma<->interstitial) = 1 cm/min
P (interstitial->intracellular) = 0.00002714 cm/min
P (intracellular->interstitial) = 0.00001519 cm/min
Resulting in the curve below:


The early misspecification is again gone, but I still cannot interpret it physiologically, can someone guide me to what I’m not fully understanding here?
Thanks in advance,
Aya

Dear colleagues,
Would it be possible that lysosomal trapping is at the basis of this discrepancy? Our formed metabolite is not charged at physiological pH but +/- 40% ionized at pH 5, which could lead to trapping in the lysosomes subsequent slower appearance in the systemic circulation. What would be the most straightforward way to test and/or implement such a hypothesis?
Kind regards,
Robin

It is rare that a drug is metabolized 100% to one metabolite. As I mentioned before this is likely the source of the error, that your metabolite is being formed too extensively. Lysosomal trapping may occur for drugs with physicochemistry similar to doxorubicin or with high lipophilicity (yours does not have).

Dear Paul,
Thank you for your quick reply!
In vitro experiments in HLM and hepatocytes confirm >90% metabolism to this one metabolite so it would surprise me if it would be much lower. To decrease the overprediction at Cmax this would mean a stark reduction in the formation of this metabolite which would contradict a lot of other information. Furthermore, the fact that tuning the permeability-related parameters solves the problem seem to point in the direction of some kind of delay in transport from the hepatocyte to the plasma seems to be present.

Dear Robin, dear Community,
To my knowledge, lysosomal trapping is probable for compounds with:
logP >2 (maybe >1), basic nitrogen with a pKa >8 (maybe >6.5), TPSA <70 Angström² (maybe <100 A²).
This information is taken from a slide of Andrew Parkinson (Marbach DDI Workshop 2017).
As a straightforward workaround, you could try to implement a binding partner as a substitute for the trapping and see how it looks. Has somebody tried that?
Kind regards, Nina

Dear Nina & OSP community,

To my knowledge, lysosomal trapping is probable for compounds with:
logP >2 (maybe >1), basic nitrogen with a pKa >8 (maybe >6.5), TPSA <70 Angström² (maybe <100 A²).
This information is taken from a slide of Andrew Parkinson (Marbach DDI Workshop 2017).

Thank you for your suggestion, and I have looked upon the lysosomal trapping from the publication of Andrew Parkinson (https://dmd.aspetjournals.org/content/41/4/897), where Lipophilic drugs (logP > 1)  with ionizable amines (pKa > 6) can accumulate in lysosomes.

As a straightforward workaround, you could try to implement a binding partner as a substitute for the trapping and see how it looks. Has somebody tried that?

And, as per your recommendation, I implemented a Dummy binding protein intracellularly with the following assumptions:


And below you can see the resulting concentration-time profiles of the parent (in red) and the metabolite (in blue) vs the observed data. The simulation showed the same trajectory as the one produced before with the reduced cellular permeability parameters and the disappearance of the peak in the first 30 min. But I could not solve the clear underprediction of the metabolite.


So, my question now, what steps shall I take to account for that trapping effect, or what I missed here to give the same trajectory but with lower values?
Regards,

Dear Aya,
As this approach would only be a substitute for lysosomal partitioning, unfortunately there are no literature values for the binding parameters.
I would try to optimize the reference concentration and the Kd (also try koff) of your binding partner.
If you have, you could compare the results to your volume of distribution VD, as this should be impacted by binding as well as lysosomal partitioning, and you might get some information from this.
Kind regards, Nina

Dear Nina,
Thanks for your feedback, now I have tried out some optimisation steps for adjusting the reference concentration and the kinetics of the binding partner, and using the optimised values as follow:

Reference concentration = 0.62 umol/L (instead of 1 umol/L)
Relative expression of the protein is 100% in the liver periportal only (instead of all organs)
Kd = 2.2 nmol/L (instead of 1 nmol/L)
Koff = 1 1/min (fixed)
And below you can see the resulting concentration-time profiles of the parent (in red) and the metabolite (in blue) vs the observed data. The simulation has markedly improved, but am I now going in the right direction of explaining this lysosomal trapping or drifting by changing the above-mentioned parameters?




If you have, you could compare the results to your volume of distribution VD, as this should be impacted by binding as well as lysosomal partitioning, and you might get some information from this.

And one more question, how can I estimate the Vd/Vss of the metabolite? As far as I know, can I estimate it using the following equation:
Vss= CL * MRT, where the CL is the plasma CL used as input parameter and MRT from the simulated concentration-time profile of the metabolite?
Thanks in advance,
Aya

Dear Aya,
Your fit looks really nice! Unfortunately, if this is a workaround using binding as a substitute for lysosomal trapping, I think there is no way to physiologically interpret the binding parameter values that resulted from your optimization, or compare them to literature. Unless you actually have binding of your metabolite instead of lysosomal partitioning. Do you know if the metabolite is pharmacologically active? Then it might be binding to the target and you could search for literature binding parameter values (of the metabolite or as an approximation of the parent compound).
And I think the formula Vss = CL * MRT can only be applied if you have data of your metabolite administered intravenously. But you could assume a similar Vd for your metabolite as measured after iv administration your parent compound.
Kind regards, Nina

title: Problems with simulations in pregnancy
body_text:
Hello,
I tried the steps for simulations in pregnancy as discussed in issue#523, and the simulation ran but no profiles were generated
please find attached the PK-SIM
Ritonavir & Lopinavir_Pregnancy.zip

answer.body_text:
Ok as suspected the solubility of lopinavir is the issue here. Specifically, the building block templates for the pregnancy model (spatial structure/passive transports) have been created with an older version of MoBi (version 6 as described here) and do not support the pH solubility Table used for lopinavir.
When using this old spatial structure template with this SolubilityTable, the parameter intestinal solubility is set everywhere to 0 which explains why lopinavir does not dissolve in your model.
This parameter is defined by an equation and can be found under SpatialStructure | Lumen | <name of GI segment> | MoleculeProperties. In newer versions, the equation for this parameter looks as follows:

In the pregnancy spatial structure this parameter is defined as follows:

In this equation, the parameter SolubilityTable is missing.
I see three ways to solve this:

you can update the old pregnancy spatial structure by adding the parameter SolubilityTable in the equation above (and potentially also additional parameters that may be missing)
or, if you have a constant solubility, you could just overwrite the solubility equation in MoBi with your measured value
or, if the solubility is constant, you can go to the simulation in MoBi and manually set the solubility in each intestinal segment to the measured value. You find the solubility in each intestinal segment under Organism | Lumen | <name of GI segment> | <Drug Name>, e.g. here shown for the caecum:


I hope this helps!
Good luck,
André

Ok as suspected the solubility of lopinavir is the issue here. Specifically, the building block templates for the pregnancy model (spatial structure/passive transports) have been created with an older version of MoBi (version 6 as described here) and do not support the pH solubility Table used for lopinavir.
When using this old spatial structure template with this SolubilityTable, the parameter intestinal solubility is set everywhere to 0 which explains why lopinavir does not dissolve in your model.
This parameter is defined by an equation and can be found under SpatialStructure | Lumen | <name of GI segment> | MoleculeProperties. In newer versions, the equation for this parameter looks as follows:

In the pregnancy spatial structure this parameter is defined as follows:

In this equation, the parameter SolubilityTable is missing.
I see three ways to solve this:

you can update the old pregnancy spatial structure by adding the parameter SolubilityTable in the equation above (and potentially also additional parameters that may be missing)
or, if you have a constant solubility, you could just overwrite the solubility equation in MoBi with your measured value
or, if the solubility is constant, you can go to the simulation in MoBi and manually set the solubility in each intestinal segment to the measured value. You find the solubility in each intestinal segment under Organism | Lumen | <name of GI segment> | <Drug Name>, e.g. here shown for the caecum:


I hope this helps!
Good luck,
André

title: A question on formulas and in distribution model
body_text:
Hi, all,
When I learn the formula in MoBi, I find this passive transport PassiveDiffusionPl2Int is defined as "dN/dt=fu * P_endothelial * SA * (C_pls - C_int / K_int_pls)"; isn't the term "C_pls - C_int / K_int_pls" is equal to 0?

comments.body_text:
Dear @wangwei1619,
passive diffusion will only take place, if there is a difference in unbound concentration between the two spaces (plasma and interstitial).
In MoBI you can select "create process rate parameter" and "plot parameter" to have a look at the PassiveDiffusionPl2Int in one of your simulations. You will find that PassiveDiffusionPl2Int is basically never 0 (except for the turning point or when there is no drug in the system any more).
On the other hand, if you set all clearane parameter (hepatic, enzymatic & renal) in your PBPK model to 0 and simulate your system for a long time periode (1000 h or more) you will find that at some point PassiveDiffusionPl2Int will become 0. This is when the system is in equilibrium and the unbound concentration in bothe spaces (here, plasma and interstitial) are equal.
Hope this helps!
Kind regards,
Henrik

Dear Henrik,
Thanks for your advice. But I don't know how to create process rate parameter and to plot it. When I right-clicked on the simulation, I didn't find "create process rate parameter" .

Now, I get your idea that unbound concentration between the two spaces (plasma and interstitial) are not equal during equilibrium process, but, another problem comes. I thought Cu + Cb = Ctotal in a compart because V *(Cu + Cb)= V * Ctotal. However, as the PK-Sim takes account protein as another compart, I think unbound and bound drug don't share the same distribution volume, is it right? And, basing on this change, what is the relationship among Cu, Cb, C_water, C_protein, Vf(water/plasma) and Vf(protein/plasma)?
Best regards,
Wang Wei

Dear @wangwei1619,
in MoBi you have to open the "passive transport" building block:

and than go to PassiveDiffusionInt2Cell and check the boxes:

Kind regards,
Henrik

Hi, Henrik,
Thanks for your help, I got it. But I am still confusing with these parameters Cu, Cb, C_water, C_protein, Vf(water) and Vf(protein). According to the calculation method of PK-Sim standard method:

it can deduce that C_plsfu=Cu=C_water=NuV_water_pls, which means fu here is equal to (NuV_pls)/(N_plsV_water_pls). It is not identical to what is generally used that fu=Cu/(Cu+Cb)=Nu/(Nu+Nb)=Nu/N_pls. On the other hand, Cu could be assumed to equal C_water, because plasma almost consists of water.

title: Run a simulation from command shell Window or Linux
body_text:
Hello all, I have to run many simulations on Mobi/PK-Sim and I would like to automate the simulation process. I would like to know if there is a way to execute a simulation from Windows or Linux command shell ?
Thank you in advance.
Jonathan

answer.body_text:
You can do it via the OSP R-toolbox (both under Windows and Linux); s. the documentaion for details.

R-Toolbox is not part of the OSP Suite setup and must be installed separately. Installation steps depend on the operating system and are described in the documentation.
Current version of the R-Toolbox works only with R 3, not with R 4

R-Toolbox works with simulations in pkml format. If you have only PK-Sim/MoBi projects - you must export all simulations of interest as pkml.

For PK-Sim it can be done via the PK-Sim CLI (command line interface) - s. the documentaion for details. E.g.
PKSim.CLI export -p "C:\Temp\ExampleProject.pksim5" -o "C:\Temp\Output" --pkml
For MoBi you can export simulations as pkml only manually.

title: How much blood remains in organs after blood sampling via cardiac puncture?
body_text:
Hi, all,
I am wondering how much blood remains in organs after blood sampling via cardiac puncture. I have searched that nearly half of all blood can be sampled via cardiac puncture. So where is the remaining blood mainly staying, venous vein, organs, or both?
Any comment is appreciated
Wang Wei

comments.body_text:
Hi Wang,
Tricky question. This will most likely also depent on the species. Since the heart of the mice is too small for this procedure, you will most likely obtain mixed blood (wrt oxygene saturation). For larger species, the amount of blood exeeding the cardiac blood volume will most likely be removed from the venous blood pool. In this case I would keep the pulmonary blood content, arterial blood pool and the vascular space of each organ but substract the venous blood pool as far as needed. Does the sampling volume exeed the venous blood pool in your case?
Please keep in mind, that the vascular space of the heart in PK-Sim represents the blood volume of the myocadial tissue. The PBPK framework has no representation of a ventricle.
Best,
Jan

Hi Wang,
we estimated a fraction of 18% of residual blood after tissue dissection assuming the same fraction for all organs (https://doi.org/10.1007/s10928-017-9559-4).
In a more recent paper, they looked at residual blood organ specific: https://doi.org/10.1113/JP274819
These numbers are however not specific for a condition after cardiac puncture.
Best,
Christoph

title: Extracellular Membrane, Intracellular, Interstitial - What Does this mean for the lumen?
body_text:
Dear OSP community,
I am relatively new to PBPK and am currently trying to implement a simple method to mimic lysosomal trapping in a model I'm working on, using the methods found in this post: #346

When adding an unspecified protein, I have to choose if it is localized interstitially, on the extracellular membrane, or intracellularly:

If I'm adding the protein in the lumen, I wonder how the different choices affect the calculations/what the different choices (specifically interstitial and intracellular) would mean, given that the lumen is the space inside?
Any help anyone can give to help me better understand this (and which option I should then choose) is very much appreciated - thanks in advance!

answer.body_text:

this setting only affects the entries fur "tissues". Lumen is not part of the tissue and not affected by this choice.

title: Measure of variability around PK analysis?
body_text:
I'm wondering if it's possible to find--or create a new feature, if it doesn't already exist--a measure of variability (standard error, confidence interval, etc.) surrounding the PK parameter calculations within a simulation (AUC, Cmax, etc.)?
For example: when I run a parameter identification I can see the 5-95% CI for that varied parameter, but once I apply the optimized result to my simulation, there's no way to calculate uncertainty in the "new" PK analysis. It would be nice to be able to see the 5-95% estimates for AUC, Cmax, etc., especially when you're trying to compare a model result to published clinical data.
Thanks!

answer.body_text:
Of course, as always, this could be done through R: export the correlation/covariance matrix and sample from it for a population simulation
In PK-Sim sth "similar" could be achieved by just creating the confidence interval plots (one of the results plots for a parameter identification)?... this should directly display the simulations with CI against data.
To do sth. as described above in PK-Sim, we would need to create a population which captures covariance on-the-fly when applying the results to the simulations which would then have to be plugged in to the simulations... not impossible, but also not planned at this time...

The goal behind the snapshot is to be able to recreate a project from scratch using only the parameters that were changed by the user (input parameters) so that the project is "fresh" so to speak. title: DDI with large molecule model
body_text:
Dear all,
I build a model containing small and large molecule together, since the molecules are connected. I would like to make a DDI prediction, for instance with Itraconazole, on the small molecules that I have in my model, but as you all know, all the model we have for DDI are built on the small molecule model.
Do you have any idea (any trick!) to combine a small and large model together? For instance, in MoBi?
Or for instance, do you think that I could import itraconazole in my simulation with the large molecule model and then reparametrize the model to make it identical to the original one build in the small molecule model?
FYI, I already checked and changing the model from small to large on the itraconazole-midazolam interaction has a big impact, and the predictions are not accurate anymore.
Please any input is welcome!
Thanks in advance!
Donato

answer.body_text:
Hi Donato!
If you use a small molecule within the large molecule model, the only difference compared to using the small molecule model is, that you introduce an (effective) barrier between the plasma and interstitial space of the organs. If the fu is small (which is the case for e.g., Itraconazole, this matters). All you have to do is to adjust the permeability P_endothelial for your drug (and the metabolites) for all organs except brain to the value which is used for the small molecule model (100 cm/min, i.e. fast exchange) and you will end up with exactly the same model as if you had chosen the small molecule model! You can stay in PK-Sim for this. Just take care that you also change the P_endothelial (= P(plasma<->interstitial)) values for the mucosa “organs” (the latter are not accessible in the Permeability/P_endothelial group but in the Neighborhoods in the Hierarchy view).
So just using the large molecule model for all compounds (large and small molecules) and make the adjustments described above in case the PBPK model for the small molecule  was not established using the large molecule model, should do the trick.
Best, Christoph

Hi Donato!

If the fu is small (which is the case for e.g., Itraconazole, this matters). All you have to do is to adjust the permeability P_endothelial for your drug (and the metabolites) for all organs except brain to the value which is used for the small molecule model (100 cm/min, i.e. fast exchange) and you will end up with exactly the same model as if you had chosen the small molecule model! You can stay in PK-Sim for this. Just take care that you also change the P_endothelial (= P(plasma<->interstitial)) values for the mucosa “organs” (the latter are not accessible in the Permeability/P_endothelial group but in the Neighborhoods in the Hierarchy view).
So just using the large molecule model for all compounds (large and small molecules) and make the adjustments described above in case the PBPK model for the small molecule  was not established using the large molecule model, should do the trick.

title: Wrong MRT result
body_text:
After i get the results of my Rivaroxaban simulation , time values such as MRT and Thalf are highly overestimated but values like Cl are correct and as expected .one other problem is that MRT value in a single dose is so close to MRT value after administering rivaroxaban for 4 weeks.
i think kel is way underestimated how can i solve this problem ?
Sincerely

comments.body_text:
Hi @ranamjz
You need to make sure your simulation time is long enough. We calculate the MRT using the last 10% point in your simulation. If the distribution phase is not finished, it will lead to wrong results
See https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-simulations#pk-analysis-view
So in short, make sure you simulate long enough after your LAST infusion and your MRT values should be better

title: How the value of P_pls_rbc_factor and P_pls_rbc_factor are determined?
body_text:
Hello team,
I found the example of the study protocol of diclofenac on the website: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/MoBi/tree/develop/examples  However, I have a few questions about some of the parameters in the model. I found parameters called "P_rbc_pls_factor" and "P_pls_rbc_factor" in Spatial Structures-Study Protocol 50 mg Diclofenac IR Tablet Single Dose/Parameters/Molecule Properties/Parameters. In its description, it only says that "Factor modifying the permeability of acids and bases depending on their degree of dissociation". Thus, I am wondering that if these parameters came from experimental data fitting or there are some equations to determine these parameters? Could you please explain how did you come up with the value of these parameters?
Thank you very much!

answer.body_text:
This is called a "Black Box" Formula because definition will be retrieved when you build your simulation. This is unfortunately not very intuitive to find the formula.
But there is a way
1: Create your simulation and check for the instance of the parameter. You will see the formula
2: Open the AllCalculationMethods.pkml file and look for this formula
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/MoBi/blob/develop/src/Data/AllCalculationMethods.pkml

title: Area of application doesn't work properly

answer.body_text:
@jonathanlsn this is because the avobenzone dose is very large and remains on the skin since very little of it is absorbed.   When you add the new dose, the skin is still saturated with avobenzone from the previous application, so you don't see the difference in the absorption profile.
Try it with a dose of 0.01 ug/cm2 and plot the mass of the avobenzone in the vehicle (ie select vehicle_mass when choosing the outputs to plot).  With the small dose, the skin is not saturated and you will be able to see the different applications in the absorption profile (Q.)

comments.body_text:
Hello,
I'm working on a model about a molecule penetration through the skin with Mobi and I must choose different application area to compare the total penetration of this molecule. But when I change this parameter from 5000 cm^2 to 10000 cm^2 I obtain almost the same results. So I made a test with just 5 cm^2 and I obtained almost the same result again.
I would like to know if you have ever met this problem before ?
Best regards,
Jonathan
Maybe @abdullahhamadeh can help?
Hi @jonathanlsn, are you simulating Q? Because that result is normalized by area.  ie if you double the area you get the same Q since it is in dimension mass per area.

Sorry I didn't specified the type of output I want to simulate. Actually I am simulating the Amount of molecule into the plasmatic compartment at a given time point (80h for example). For each value of application area I obtained approximately 70 micromol of my compound.
Ok, can you verify what amount gets into in_vivo_sink?  Does it change when you change the area?
There is just one parameter Volume = 1.00 L and it's the same whatever the area.
In which compartment? in_vivo_sink?
Yes, the Volume is the only parameter present in in_vivo_sink and its formula type is Constant.
Yes, that is ok, because those parameters are not used.  But is the simulated amount of the permeant reaching the in_vivo_sink changing when you modify the area?

Yes, the permeant amount is changing  with the application area but not much. I checked the permeant amount at the same time point for different skin area and I made this graph below. As expected the amount increase linearly with the area but the predicted intercept is equal to 69.01 instead of 0.
Ok please upload your model.  I will look at it and your earlier issue, #684

Skin area.zip
Here are two models with 5 and 5000 cm^2 skin area.
Thank you for taking a look on my project.

@jonathanlsn the reason you are not seeing a difference when you change the area is because the amount of avobenzone entering via the dermal route is much much smaller than the amount that is entering via the 4x/day application, so you can't see the effect of the dermal application on the graph.  To see this, remove the 4x/day and then plot plasma concentration using different areas of dermal application.  You will find that, eg, if you double the area then you double the amount in plasma.
Ok, but I would like to keep 4x/day application to simulate an in vivo protocol, so the solution would be to set the dose entering via dermal route as the sum of 4x/day application dose ?

You need to add the dermal applications in MoBi as shown in the documentation in section 4.2, you can't add them in PKSim.
I have made an events building block that has dermal applications at the same times as in your original application.   You can download it from here.
Use the current model in the repository, but replace the events building block with the one in the zip file.  You can set the dose as before, from DERMAL_APPLICATION_AREA|skin_compartment|Dose.

title: Parameter Identification
body_text:
Hello everyone,
My team's research focuses on generic development, and we are having trouble predicting the in vivo performance of one of our formulations.
I'm using PK-Sim to predict the plasma-concentration of a BCS IV drug with a different amount of a certain excipient (Poloxamer) which really affects its solubility and absorption.
We have data (in vivo and dissolution profiles) from 3 other formulations (different amounts of poloxamer) but we don't have values for the formulations' permeability and clearance. As such, we are using the parameter identification tool to predict all missing values and trying to find tendencies (e.g., linear permeability with increasing excipient concentration) to predict our formulation's in vivo performance.
The problem is that we are having trouble using the parameter identification tool - our simulations do not fit the observable data by 100%, which lead us to poor trustable conclusions. Which is the best way to use the parameter identification tool?
We would also be grateful if anyone could point us in another direction if you think this is not the best way to answer our research question.

comments.body_text:
Hello @joao-reis3
I do not think it is a problem with the PI tool.
-Try to fit only key parameters or use sensitivity analysis to select the most important one.
-Make sure that you have the accurate dissolution method to IVIVE-PBPK
-Does the Polaxamer play with intestinal permeability or only solubility !?
-Did you incorporated the transporters and enzyme metabolism ?
Please contact me by email if you need more support :Frederico.martins@esqlabs.com

Hi Joao,
If you have IV in vivo data of the compound, you can get the total plasma clearance from and NCA and add it to your simulation (assuming linear PK).
Then you can optimise the permeability, you set your initial values around 1 x 10^-4 cm/min.
Hope that works.
Regards,
Ricardo

Hello @Ricardo-DO
Thank your for your answer.
I have no IV in vivo data, when I said BCS IV, I meant BCS class 4 (low solubility and low permeability).
I only have in vivo data from 3 oral formulations. I tried to calculate clearance but since my compound is low permeable, I achieved a really low value (which doesn't mimic what happens in vivo).
Regards,
João

title: Tubular secretion transporters
body_text:
Dear all,
I want to know how do we specify the renal transporters (basolateral, apical) involved in tubular secretion and how would this differ from implementing the tubular section option in renal clearance?

If we are to use the tubular secretion option, only one process kinetics is required to be provided, but what does this indicate? is this kinetics of drug transport from the tubular cells to the urine? if this is the case would it be equivalent to defining the apical transporter kinetics?
Also, if I have the information on the tubular uptake transporter involved in secretion (e.g. OAT3), but limited knowledge of the efflux transporter and I want to define it as a hypothetical efflux transporter with values for Vmax, km.. how would this be implemented given that we don't have expression data (we want to keep it exclusively expressed in the kidney in this case)?

Kind regards
Salma

comments.body_text:
Insert two separate transporters in the ADME tab under "Transport Proteins." One influx transporter that is expressed on the basolateral membrane (e.g. OAT1 or OAT3). One efflux transporter that is expressed on the apical membrane. Define Vmax and Km individually.
However there is often no way to identify the Vmax of each of these transporters through optimization and there is no way to parameterize reliably from in vitro in any PBPK modeling system to date. An in vitro Km has fairly good value in translation.
One way to identify an OAT Vmax separately from the apical transporter Vmax is by optimizing against an observed DDI with probenecid.
If there is no information that would allow separate identification of both the uptake and efflux processes, then an assumption that the apical efflux is rate limiting could be an appropriate work around. In this case only one net transporter is required.
I believe the tubular secretion option under the renal clearance process eliminates drug directly from plasma to urine with a Michaelis Menten approximation.

Hello, I am also using PK-SIM for modeling recently, which involves OAT3. The Vmax unit I searched is pmol/min/mg, and I don't know how to convert it into µmol/L/min in PK-SIM. I would like to ask how to convert the Vmax you finally used into units, is it according to the reference [24]?

answer.body_text:
Hi Enrique, two options to avoid this:

If the 'Shift' key is pressed when clicking on the “+” sign, the other compartments will not disappear.
If the other containers are not visible, right-click anywhere in the empty space of the model structure and select 'Show All Children'.

title: Variability (confidence interval) in optimized parameter value
body_text:
Dear community,
I’ve developed several PBPK tumor models in MoBi regarding radionuclides used in clinical practice and managed to validate these models based on scan data (n=41). The main process is that the drug binds to a receptor on cell membranes (interstitial) and gets internalized into the intracellular compartment.
Exact receptor expression is unknown, while all other parameters regarding the internalization process are based on literature values. Therefore we assume that we can optimize receptor concentrations based on our observed organ (scan) data. Since spleen is known to have highest receptor expression (and thus uptake) the receptor fraction is 1 for spleen. The receptor reference concentration was then optimized based on spleen data using the built-in parameter identification Monte Carlo algorithm.
Results show a reference concentration of 100.11 nmol/L, which is a reasonable estimate. However, I’d like to add variability to this estimated value, to say something about how certain we are about this specific estimation. (So, just to make things clear, I do not mean an evaluation of the model as a whole, which can be done looking at residuals and the total error.) I did check the covariance matrix tab, but didn’t manage to fully understand what is calculated there, although I read threads on the forum and the online manual. So my specific questions are:

How is the confidence interval on the identified parameter calculated (in the covariance matrix tab)?
I get a confidence interval of NaN on my reference concentration estimate, how can that be explained?
Is there another way to include a certainty to this optimized parameter?

Many thanks for your help and I’ll be happy to answer any questions that arise!
Hinke

answer.body_text: @hsiebinga Small addition to the explanation by @StephanSchaller: zero sensitivities (which result then in NaN confidence intervals) are in this particular case ("Reference concentration") just a bug (sensitivity of parameters which are used to calculate the initial amount of a molecule - in this case of a protein - are currently always returned as 0)

comments.body_text:
Dera Hinke,
I hope I can help with these perliminary answers (in a rush):

confidence intervals are calculated based on the fisher information
NaN values pop up if the information on the parameter in the data is "zero" (zero entries in the fisher information) as the inverted fisher information matrix is used to calculate these . This might happen if you have parameters fittet which are non-identifiable (e.g. highly correlated)
Not sure I get the last question, you can add certainty with additional data or increase certainty per parameter by removing parameters to be fitted (reduce parameter dimensionality)

Hope this helps?
An interesting topic (radionuclide PK). Do you use these models for dosimetry? We would be interested to exchange ideas as we are also working on this topic. Would love to have a chat if you do have time: stephan.schaller@esqlabs.com
hope my answers were helpful,
Stephan

@hsiebinga Small addition to the explanation by @StephanSchaller: zero sensitivities (which result then in NaN confidence intervals) are in this particular case ("Reference concentration") just a bug (sensitivity of parameters which are used to calculate the initial amount of a molecule - in this case of a protein - are currently always returned as 0)

Thanks for your explanations, really helpful! To clarify -  I only have one fitted parameter, but then with the explanation of the bug it makes sense that I get NaN values.
However, I would still like to add variability (probably a confidence interval) to the fitted parameter. I’ve already tried to fit the start amount instead of the reference concentration, but that didn’t solve the problem. Is there a solution to still obtain a confidence interval to the optimized parameter (e.g. is it possible to calculate it manually or in R?)?
@StephanSchaller , I’m not working on absorbed radiation doses (yet), but just on the peptide part to get insights in the distributions and (tumor) parameters that affect this distribution most. However, it would be very interesting to also include activity and absorbed doses if this would be possible. I’ll keep in touch!
Hinke

title: Particle Dissolution/ Use as suspension
body_text:
Dear all,
I am trying to reproduce a PBPK model from the paper “A physiologically based pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic (PBPK/PD) model of the histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitor vorinostat for pediatric and adult patients and its application for dose specification” by Moj et al. The model was distributed on this forum here: #102 @DanielMoj.
I have been able to reproduce intravenous adult model, however I’m having several problems with the oral adult model. Particle Dissolution was used as Formulation and I have some questions about this type of formulation
1-	What does “Number of bins” mean?
2-	In their model in MoBI, when Particle Dissolution is described, the option “Use as suspensión” appears at the end, but when I define Particle Dissolution in PK-SIM, the “Use as suspension” option does not exist. Is this problem due to a different PK-SIM version? In that case, is there any way to include “Use as suspension” option?
Thank you in advance!

comments.body_text:
Dear @sariiiiii83,
The parameter Number of  bins defines how many bins of particle sizes your tablet will be composed of; each bin will have its own particle size. More information on the particle dissolution model can be found in the OSP manual here and in the original paper here.
The parameter Use as suspension is not used in the particle dissolution model. You could check in the MoBi file whether it's used for any Formula in the Events Building Block but I wouldn't assume so...
André

title: The problem that the half-life does not decrease uniformly only by adjusting Vmax
body_text:
Hello.
I am getting a lot of help here.
The half-life of the drug being simulated is getting too long. Adjusting Vmax decreases the half-life and increases it non-constantly. When the Vmax value is increased, the half-life decreases for the first few times and then increases abruptly. What other variables besides Vmax should be adjusted to reduce the half-life?

comments.body_text:
Hi!
Half-life may also be influenced by absorption and redistribution, so, you could adjust parameters related to these processes. I would recomment to display the "fraction absorbed into mucosa" and the organ intracellular concentrations in your simulations in order to evaluate their influence.

@gapa84 , try simulating for a longer time. Terminal half-life should be close to zero of you reduce al clearance processes (i.e. set vmax, kcat, ... tp zero).
As mentioned by @TWendl , the decline you see in the beginning of drug PK is also gouerned by absorption and distribution processes (e.g. permeability and partitioning values)

title: How to create 1 observer 'total fraction of dose' for multiple molecules/enzymes
body_text:
Hi
I am working on a compound that is metabolized via multiple routes, including different UGT enzymes. The container observer 'Total fraction of dose' gives me the fraction for each of the enzymes. However, I would like to have a single observer that gives immediately the total fraction for all UGT enzymes.
I am aware of the examples in the manual, but I'm still not able to create this observer.
Thanks!

answer.body_text:
One possibility to do this is:

Caclulate the total amount for each metabolite
Define the observer as (Total_amount_metabolite_1+...+Total_amount_metabolite_N) / Total_DrugMass_of_the_parent_compound

E.g. see the attached example. TotalFractionDose_OverAllMetabolites_02.zip
Here the Compound C1 is metabolized by 2 Enzymes (Enzyme_1 and Enzyme_2) building 2 metabolites: C1-Enzyme_1-aa Metabolite and C1-Enzyme_2-bb Metabolite

In the molecules building block, I introduce the Parameter "Total drug amount in organsim" for the first metabolite and define is as the sum of all amounts of this metabolite within the organism (important: set parameter type to Property)

Same for the 2nd metabolite (here I can just use the same formula which I created for the first metabolite)

Now I define new container observer for the total fraction of all metabolites

The formula of the new observer is then:

Now you can plot the new observer

title: implementation of peripheral venous blood in Mobi for PD modeling
body_text:
Hello, i have a questions about implementation of peripheral venous plasma concentration.
I didn't find the peripheral venous plasma concentration in possible referenced objects in new parameters of spatial structures. Provided peripheral venous blood compartment were not clicked in the software.

How can i incorporating the plasma concentration-time profiles in the PD model?
When i used the table (multiple time discrete and piecewise constant numeric values) in specific time point nor the formula (an explicit formula) values, i confirmed that PD model is plausible.  So, i want to incorporate the simulated profiles in the PD model, not the specific table for improvement of our model.

Please give me the opinion and help.
Thank you.

answer.body_text:
Hi,
the "PeripheralVenousBlood" is an observer. It is mixture of the concentrations in Muscle and Fat plasma. Observers cannot be used in equations.
Instead, you can use the "VenousBlood|Plasma" concentrations, which should be very close to the PeripheralVenousBlood unless your compound is extensively metabolized in muscle and/or fat.

Age-related changes in GFR are accounted for by default in PK-Sim according to the work by Jan Schlender and team

title: Plot the Drug amount into Stratum Corneum
body_text:
Hi everyone !
I'm just started with OSP M

obi and PKsim and I got some issues I will try to explain.
I just made a model with a skin compartment which is linked to the whole body system on Mobi. I wanted to simulate the permeation of a drug through the different layers of the skin. I had selected Plasma, SC_skin (Stratum Cormeum) and DE_skin (Dermis) drug amount into simulation settings but when I launched the simulation I only see the amount of drug into the Plasmatic compartment. So there are no curves for the amount of drug into the Stratum Corneum and the Dermis even if I select them one by one.
If you have any solution please don't hesitate to reply.
Thank you in advance.

answer.body_text:
@jonathanlsn
There are no dermal applications in your merged whole body model.  To add them, please use these steps:
1 - download the events building block from here

title: Finite dose
body_text:
Dear everyone,
I was wondering whether it was possible to insert an event with a finite dose for the skin permeation model, and if so, how this can be achieved. Thank you very much in advance!!
Best regards,
Mèlanie

answer.body_text:
Hi @MelanieMartha26
Every time a dose is "added" it is assumed that any dose that was previously on the skin surface is removed.  So what you need here can be accomplished by simply adding another event_with_dose_mass_entered_manually event group where the dose is zero.
Step 1: First, save event_with_dose_mass_entered_manually as a PKML file.  Then delete event_with_dose_mass_from_spatial_structure.
Step 2: Rename event_with_dose_mass_entered_manually to dose_addition.

Step 3: Right-click on skin_application and select Load Event Group... and select the previously saved PKML file containing event_with_dose_mass_entered_manually.

Step 4:  Rename the newly-added Event Group to dose_removal.

Step 5: Set the start times and doses for the dose addition and dose removal.  For example, under dose_addition, you can add a 10 ug/sq cm dose at time = 0 hours by setting Dose_time = 0 h and Mass_dose_per_area = 10 µg/cm².  Under dose_removal, you can remove the previously-added dose at 12 hours by setting Dose_time = 12 h and Mass_dose_per_area = 0 µg/cm².

Step 6: You can verify the addition and removal works by simulation the model with this new event structure and plotting the results of the Vehicle mass per area observer, which shows the removal of the dose at 12 hours:

comments.body_text:
Hello Mèlanie,
You may set one or more dosing events in the Events building block of arbitrary size and start time, as described in section 4.2 of the user guide:

You can also set the volatility of the vehicle to model the four dosing scenarios in Dancik et al, 2013 (cases I-IV) using the "Volatile vehicle" and "Vehicle type" inputs in the favorites menu.  eg to model cases I/II you need to set Volatile vehicle = 1 or Vehicle type = 0 (where 0 corresponds to a neat application of the permeant to skin).

Hope this helps, and please let me know if any clarification is needed.

I do understand how to change an event; however, it is not yet completely clear to me how one can remove the permeant that is still left on the skin after several hours. For example, I would like to simulate what happens when you apply a dose and then after 8 hours "clean" the skin. I want to simulate the model for more than 8 hours as I am curious what the end systemic exposure will be.
I hope this is clear to you, otherwise please don't hesitate to tell me.
Best regards,
Mèlanie

Hi @MelanieMartha26

Step 1: First, save event_with_dose_mass_entered_manually as a PKML file.  Then delete event_with_dose_mass_from_spatial_structure.
Step 2: Rename event_with_dose_mass_entered_manually to dose_addition.

Step 3: Right-click on skin_application and select Load Event Group... and select the previously saved PKML file containing event_with_dose_mass_entered_manually.

Step 4:  Rename the newly-added Event Group to dose_removal.

Step 5: Set the start times and doses for the dose addition and dose removal.  For example, under dose_addition, you can add a 10 ug/sq cm dose at time = 0 hours by setting Dose_time = 0 h and Mass_dose_per_area = 10 µg/cm².  Under dose_removal, you can remove the previously-added dose at 12 hours by setting Dose_time = 12 h and Mass_dose_per_area = 0 µg/cm².

Step 6: You can verify the addition and removal works by simulation the model with this new event structure and plotting the results of the Vehicle mass per area observer, which shows the removal of the dose at 12 hours:

Dear @abdullahhamadeh
Thank you very much! I tried this earlier, but somehow I made some mistakes, now I get it, thank you!

I want to make sure that after I wash the skin after 8 hours that also the passive absorption from the SC to the vehicle stops. Now an equilibrium arises for certain compounds after 8 hours, where the test item diffuses from the SC to the vehicle outside of the skin. This, however, will not happen in real-life as you completely dry the skin.

title: Vehicle composition skin permeation model
body_text:
Dear everyone,
I am using the skin permeation model derived from the study of Dancik et al. in MoBi.
I was wondering how you would approach implementing a vehicle that is a mixture of both water and ethanol. Would you just adjust the vehicle solubility and keep the vehicle type at 1? And what would you do regarding its volatility. Normally I would keep it at 0 for water and at 1 for ethanol.
Thank you very much in advance, and if anything is unclear, I do not mind explaining my question further.
Best regards,
Mèlanie

comments.body_text:
Hi @MelanieMartha26
Yes, that is how I would normally model a volatile vehicle using Dancik's model - i.e. you assume that some of the vehicle remains on the surface due to the water component not evaporating.  Of course, there are contexts where the water may also evaporate relatively early in the course of the experiment, so I think it is important to also explore the case where volatile vehicle option = 1.
Please also see this publication by our group, where we explore the assumption (absent in Dancik's 2013 model) that the permeant ceases to flow into the SC (i.e. it remains on the skin surface) once the vehicle has evaporated completely.

title: How to model a biomarker in MoBi
body_text:
Dear all,
I am trying to model a biomarker in MoBi, so basically I would like to add a molecule and then define a baseline value, before beginning the modelling of the time course of the biomarker.
As a starting point, I used the option "Add PK-Sim molecule" in MoBi, and here I have a first question: When doing so, the molecule which is created, will it be considered a small or large molecule? In my case, I would like to have a floating (non-stationary) large molecule.
After I created my molecule, I tried to define the baseline value (e.g. 50µmol/l), but in the molecule BB there is only the option to provide an amount in µmol. I also tried to use the Molecule start values but also there I could only find amounts.
(From what I have seen, the problem in using the amounts, is that the system is not equilateral, so the simulation need a certain point to reach an equilibrium, while if I would set the same concentration in all compartments I should not have this issue).
I had the idea to use a formula as start amount of my biomarker, since in this case I could define something like:
start_amount=50*volume
By doing so, each organ, independently from the volume of the organ, should have my 50µmol/l.
After I created this formula in the compound BB, nothing happened. I suspect that I need to connect this formula to the Molecule start values but I did not manage to find a way. Could someone help with this?
In case I was not clear enough, let me know and I can try to clarify my question.
Thanks a lot in advance!
Donato

answer.body_text:
Hi Donato.

In order o use a start amount in each compartment defined by a formula, do I need to set up a formula in the Molecule start values BB

Correct, the "Start values"-building blocks always win. In order to use the formula defined in the molecules-BB, select all entries for this molecule in the MoleculeStartValues (MSV)-BB and select "Start values -> Refresh selected from source"

The values will be set to the formula defined in the Molecules-BB.

If so, how can I create a formula there?

It is also possible to create new formulas in the MSV-BB by clicking on the "+"-sign in the "Formula"-field of the entry. It appears only when you click on this field.

Now when I go there and try to use the formula I just created, I cannot find it in the scroll down menu on the right (See screenshot below).

Formulas are not shared between the BBs. So The MSV-BB has no access to the formulas created in the Molecules-BB.

When creating a molecule using the option "Add PK-Sim molecule" in MoBi, will the molecule parameterized as small or large molecule?

There are no general differences in the parametrization between small and large molecules apart from the parameter Is small molecule , which is 0 by default. However, changing this value to 1 is not sufficient. Large molecules have additional "molecules" like the molecule-FcRn complex and reactions describing the binding to FcRn.

comments.body_text:
Hi @teutonicod,
I'm not sure if I completely understood your question but let’s discuss if not.
Once I did a biomarker the following way.
I knew the clearance processes of my BM (renal & metabolic cl)
I set up the BM as compound in PK-Sim together with an "user defined" continuous infusion from t 0 to t 1000 in the plasma compartment.
Than I created artificial experimental values for the BM in plasma, let's say 50 µmol/l at 200, 250, 300 & 400 h
With that setting you can use the Parameter Estimation in PK to optimize infusion dose / rate, optional compound parameters and if not known the magnitude of the cl processes.
As a result you will have a steady-state BM model with a baseline in every compartment.
Than, I added my Drug of interest and the anticipated interaction (DDI) on the BM clearance enzyme with a dosing of the drug at 400 h (shift all experimental data points of the drug by +400h).
May this is of help.
Best,
Henrik

Hi @HenrikCordes,
Thanks a lot for your reply, and I think you got exactly the point of my question! Indeed, this would be a work around, but in this case you will need to go to PK-Sim every time you would need to make changes in your biomarker administration. You will also need some time to reach the equilibrium in the system, but yes this would definitely be a way to do it!
Since I am already working with MoBi I would like to understand how to do it in MoBi directly, since I have seen some similar approach in some MoBi model (for instance the initial concentration of FcRn receptor I think is set in a similar way). This is also an opportunity for me to better understand how MoBi works :-)
Does anybody have any suggestion on how to do this in MoBi?

Hi @teutonicod,
if you want more than a quick-and-dirty solution in PK-Sim (which I usually prefer :-D ) I would like to point you to the work of Vanessa Baier, a former and talented co-worker of mine. She developed a more sophisticated way to do it in MoBi ;-)
In one of her previous work:  physiology-based bile acid model (PBBM), she included a plasma bile acid base line.
In the supplementary material of the linked publication you can find a step-by-step manual of how to "Extend PBPK model of G-CDCA in MoBi". Here it is explained how you can estimate the steady-state of a BM (Bile acid) and set this as starting values for any other simulation.
Maybe this helps.
Best,
Henrik

Thanks @HenrikCordes for the publication (very interesting by the way) and for your replies, but unfortunately it did not solve my problem.
Probably it will be easier with some more details.
I created the molecule in MoBi using the option "Add PK-Sim molecule". After this I set the start values of the molecule in the molecule BB as a formula, as explained in my first post. Below is the screenshot of how I did it

My explanation is that also if I set a formula as starting value in the molecule BB, MoBi still use the amounts reported in the Molecule start values BB. So I think (please let me know if I am wrong), that in order to define a start amount for my molecule in the different compartments, I would need to have a formula in the Molecule start values BB.
Now when I go there and try to use the formula I just created, I cannot find it in the scroll down menu on the right (See screenshot below). I also could not find a way to create a formula directly in the Molecule start values BB.

So to recap, my questions are:

If so, how can I create a formula there?


If the start amount of a molecule is defined by the Molecule start values BB, why the user has the option to define a formula in the Molecule BB, if then this formula is not used anyway?


When creating a molecule using the option "Add PK-Sim molecule" in MoBi, will the molecule parameterized as small or large molecule?


Hope that this clarifies what I would like to do, and my questions.

title: PBPK models of Probenecid and Furosemide to Predict Transporter Mediated Drug-Drug Interactions
body_text:
Dear OSP-Community,
We have been working on models for transporter mediated DDI predictions. Two whole-body PBPK models of probenecid (clinical OAT inhibitor) and furosemide (clinical OAT substrate) were carefully developed and then evaluated by prediction of the probenecid-furosemide and probenecid-rifampicin DDIs.
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Pharmaceutical Research. The models as well as PK-Sim projects presenting the predicted DDI studies can be found here.
Related publication:
Britz H, Hanke N, Taub ME, Wang T, Prasad B, Fernandez É, Stopfer P, Nock V, Lehr T. Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Models of Probenecid and Furosemide to Predict Transporter Mediated Drug-Drug Interactions. Pharm Res. 2020 Nov 25;37(12):250. (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11095-020-02964-z)
Best regards,
Hannah

comments.body_text: Super! Thanks for that :-)

Great! Thank you!
Is it planned to create qualification reports "GitHub-Style"?

title: Question about lympha flow and operation about PBPK modeling for large molecule
body_text:
Hi, all
Recently I have read the paper "Niederalt, C., Kuepfer, L., Solodenko, J., Eissing, T., Siegmund, H. U., Block, M., ... & Lippert, J. (2018). A generic whole body physiologically based pharmacokinetic model for therapeutic proteins in PK-Sim. Journal of pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics, 45(2), 235-257."
I find only lymph flow in text and no term such as "lymph volume" is referred. Is there no compartment of lymph with volume in PBPK model for marcomolecule? Is this to say the distribution of such agent in lymph tube is trival?
Is there any step-by-step tutorial about  PBPK modeling for large molecule when target-mediated binding and clearance is concerned?
Thanks for any reply.

answer.body_text:
Dear Wang.
you are right, there is no compartment representing lymphatic vessels or lymph nodes. This does not mean that concentrations there are trivial.
For initial lymphatic segments the composition of lymph is generally considered to be similar to the interstitial fluid of the organs they drain. That also means that representing lymph with one compartment won't help much. For example IgG concentrations in hepatic lymph nodes are much higher than in e.g.  popliteal lymph nodes draining skin - reflecting the higher interstitial IgG concentration in liver.
Lymph composition can generally change along the lymphatic tree due to a) mixing of lymph along the tree from different organs, b) fluid exchange across vessel walls and c) some exchange with blood plasma in lymph nodes.
In what are you specifically interested? Using interstitial concentrations won't help?
Regarding the steps to implement TMDD:
The rough steps are

Set up a PBPK model in PK-Sim including the binding reaction to the target
Export this model to MoBi
Add a reaction representing  internalization and degradation of the drug-target-complex in MoBi
Since target is degraded by this reaction: Also add a reaction representing target turnover (target synthesis and degradation) in MoBi

These steps are explained in more detail in the following tutorial:
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/ISOP-webinar---PK-Sim-MoBi-introduction
Hope that helps.
Best regards,
Christoph

Dear Christoph.
Thanks for your detailed explanation.
When I firtst built a model for a kind of peptide (a little more than 1 KDa), I tried to do it as a small molecule. But the simulation results were accurate only when peptide was modeled to enter into cells (Clearance is fixed to values from literature; log P is -10; unbound fraction is 100%). I think it is not true because its receptor is on the surface of cells. So my initial guess is some peptide distributed into lymphatic circulation. Do you think it is resonable? If not, I may find other way such as target-mediated binding.
Thanks for your reply.
Best regards,
Wang Wei

title: Path error when including relative expression in MoBi equation (related to Discussion #1151)
body_text:
Dear community,
We are modelling a compound that binds to VEGFA and undergoes TMDD.
Maria included VEGFA expression (based on the gene expression database) in PK-Sim V11 and ticked only the “interstitial” box.

Then she exported the simulation to MoBi V11 and added a reaction for VEGFA turnover (called “turnover”).
However, when putting interstitial relative expression in the turnover equation, we get the following error for all organs:

In the Parameter Start Values building block, relative expression in interstitial space is not listed.
However, there are start values for intracellular relative expression (which should be zero):

From the discussion #1151 (comment) we understood  that there might be a way to adjust the PSVs - referring to msevestres comment:

However, we are not sure how to create a new entry for interstitial relative expression in our PSVs.
Please help!
Maria and Nina

comments.body_text:
Dear Maria, Dear Nina,
it should not be necessary to extend your PSV (if you add in MoBi just a turnover reaction of a protein which was already defined in PK-Sim, all relative expressions should already be defined in the PSV after export from PK-Sim).
Reading #1151 (comment) the NaNs still seem to be an issue ...
Since your protein is expressed only in the interstitial space, a possible workaround could be to add a condition at “Container Criteria” at the reaction, i.e. adding “tagged with Interstitial” at the turnover reaction.
Hope that helps ...
All the best, Christoph

Dear Nina,
one of the differences between the expression concepts in version 9 and 10+ is that all proteins are now present in all compartments. The relative expression parameter is always located in the intracellular space, and the resulting initial concentration is calculated from the relative expression value (which defines the expression in whole organ) and the fraction expressed in the specific compartment (in your case, fraction intracellular is 0 and fraction expressed in interstitial space is 1).
To model the turnover reaction, you can simply use the new parameter "Initial concentration" which already takes care of everything for you (including ontogeny factors etc).
Hope that helps :)

title: New compartment in MOBI - V and WT
body_text: I have created an extra compartment (by cloning blood organ) in MOBI - but cant get the V and WT parameters to be added to the body weight and plasma volume, any help appreciated

comments.body_text:
Body weight is modeled as a sum of the weights of blood and tissue organs:

If you examine the parameter Weight of tissue organs, you will see that this is the so-called "sum formula"

It sums up the values of all parameters called Weight (tissue), that are in any organ that have a tag TissueOrgan. So you have the make sure that:

The organ (compartment?) you have added has the tag TissueOrgan



The organ has a parameter Weight (tissue), see how it is defined in other organs:


For plasma volume: you have to manually add the plasma volume of your new organ to the formula of the paramter Volume (plasma) located in Organism:
It is hard to figure out what is the problem without knowing what are the errors that are thrown when you create a simulation.

title: unexpected food effect observed 24h after meal
body_text:
Dear All,
I am simulating food effect for a compound X.
When food intake is simulated before drug administration, simulations show that also food taken 24 h before drug administration unexpectedly has an impact on drug PK.
Based on what was reported  in issue  "Weibull for MealEffect TransitRate does not reach 1/GET after Meal " here, I assumed that this could be due to the fact that the intestinal transit time at 24h is still lower than the value in fasted condition (verified with simulation).
Here-below my questions:

Is there something I am not considering in terms of assumptions and parameters to set?
if not, I am afraid that food effect might be overestimated.  Is there a way to overcome this issue, i.e., with some tricks or by modifying equations in MoBi?

FYI, I am using OSPS version 7.1.0
Thank you very much in advance!
Best regards,
Letizia

answer.body_text:
@arketkar This would certainly be a workaround, but is not possible at the moment. Maybe you can create a feature request for in in PK-Sim repository?
I can think of following workaround, which only works in MoBi.
The food effect in MoBi is defined in the "Events"-building block. The rate of gastric emptying is defined by the parameter Organism|Lumen|Stomach|Intestinal transit rate (absolute). Upon meal administration, the value of this parameter is changed to a new formula:

The value is reset when when condition StomachIntestinalTransitRate > 1/GET is met:

Unfortunately, the value of the food effect formula for Intestinal transit rate (absolute) does not return to the "fasted" state quickly enough, so the condition is not met even after 24h. You could modify the condition for the Meal_StopEvent from
StomachIntestinalTransitRate > 1/GET
to something like
(StomachIntestinalTransitRate > 1/GET) OR (Time >= 12*60)
so simply assuming that after 12 hours (of simulation!) the gastric emptying is definitely at its fasted state. If you plan to simulate multiple food administrations, make sure that the Meal_StopEvent is always before the administration of the next meal.
This is how it would look like in the simulation. This a simulation of po caffeine administration with a food effect at t = 0. With the default gastric emptying event, the rate of gastric emptying (blue dashed line) remains low even after 24 hours post meal. WIth the adjusted event (pink dashed line), the rate returns to a fasted state after 12h. The concentration curves for caffeine remain identical. Keep in mind that if another meal would be administered at e.g. 11.5h, it would be "reset" half an hour later.

comments.body_text:
Dear Letizia,
unfortunately, this is a known problem of the current implementation of the food effect - the gastric emptying rate does not return to the fasted state after administration of food. I am also not aware of any solution.

Dear Pavel, 
thank you very much for your reply.  I'd like to know your opinion about the following possibilities:


to empirically (manually) change 1 or more parameter values in the food effect formula until the intestinal transit rate reaches the 1/GET value once the stomach is empty again.


to integrate the system "step by step" on different time intervals by using different simulations,  as follow
simulation 1: [time_food_administration  time*] --> in this time interval the transit rate in presence of food will be used 
simulation 2: [time* time_end] --> in this time interval a transit rate equal to 1/GET will be used, and the initial condition of each state will be updated to the value assumed by the corresponding state of the system at the end of the previous simulation.
Of course, depending on the problem, more than 2 time intervals might be needed.


Thanks again,
Letizia
@PavelBal Can there be an option of setting up 'fasted state' as an 'event' in PK-Sim?

@arketkar This would certainly be a workaround, but is not possible at the moment. Maybe you can create a feature request for in in PK-Sim repository?
I can think of following workaround, which only works in MoBi.
The food effect in MoBi is defined in the "Events"-building block. The rate of gastric emptying is defined by the parameter Organism|Lumen|Stomach|Intestinal transit rate (absolute). Upon meal administration, the value of this parameter is changed to a new formula:

The value is reset when when condition StomachIntestinalTransitRate > 1/GET is met:

Unfortunately, the value of the food effect formula for Intestinal transit rate (absolute) does not return to the "fasted" state quickly enough, so the condition is not met even after 24h. You could modify the condition for the Meal_StopEvent from
StomachIntestinalTransitRate > 1/GET
to something like
(StomachIntestinalTransitRate > 1/GET) OR (Time >= 12*60)
so simply assuming that after 12 hours (of simulation!) the gastric emptying is definitely at its fasted state. If you plan to simulate multiple food administrations, make sure that the Meal_StopEvent is always before the administration of the next meal.
This is how it would look like in the simulation. This a simulation of po caffeine administration with a food effect at t = 0. With the default gastric emptying event, the rate of gastric emptying (blue dashed line) remains low even after 24 hours post meal. WIth the adjusted event (pink dashed line), the rate returns to a fasted state after 12h. The concentration curves for caffeine remain identical. Keep in mind that if another meal would be administered at e.g. 11.5h, it would be "reset" half an hour later.

@PavelBal Thank you very much for your suggestion .
I just ask for a clarification about it: why resetting the gastric emptying rate after 12 hours?
This is a crucial parameter that impacts the duration of food effect I am predicting.
Since in feed condition gastric emptying time is around 3-4 hours (with some variability depending on food type and quantity), does resetting the gastric emptying rate to the fasten state value after 3-4 hours make sense?
Thank you very much in advance,
Letizia
@LetiziaCarrara 12h was just an example as I did not have any reliable values at hand. 4 hours indeed makes more sense.

@PavelBal thank you very much.
Letizia

@PavelBal  sorry if I bother you again. The solution you have suggested works, but it is quite steep. Having a smoother profile would be great.
I obtained it by changing the GET_alpha from the default value of 792 to 147 (I obtained 147 by implementing the formula in Matlab, but I may be wrong -a dimensional check was not easy to be performed-). This applies when GET=15 min (default value).


In my model I increased GET to 35.5 min reference. When GET_alpha = 147 the gastric emptying rate returns to the fasted state after 30 min, which seems too short to me.  A more physiologically return to the fasted state after 4 hours was obtained by multiplying 147 by the ratio  between the two different GET, i.e. 147*(35.5/15).
What do you think?
Thank you very much,
Letizia

If the 4h are as expected it would make sense. Be aware, that the meal effect is now static and will not change if a different meal (energy content and fraction solid) is defined.
Ideally, the constants in the GET_alpha/beta formula should be defined as variables that the user could adjust in order to customize the meal effect formula.
But as @PavelBal mentioned, this is certainly possible in MoBi.
thank you very much @StephanSchaller

title: Small intestinal transit time.
body_text:
Dear community,
I have been exploring the effect of reduced intestinal transit time on drug absorption. In concrete the effect of excipients on transit time and absorption.
The Pk-Sim default value for small intestinal transit time is 126 mins,  However in this publication:
https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1016256619309
they report median transit times of 242 mins. Does somebody know the PK-Sim reference for the default value.
Thank you!
JD

comments.body_text:
I found several publications about intestinal transit in the OPS instructions. Below is one of them, in which it reviewed the results and translate the results into PK-Sim. Even though I don't find where the 126 min come from, it may be helpful to you.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=10.1002%2Fjps.22726

I found several publications about intestinal transit in the OPS instructions. Below is one of them, in which it reviewed the results and translate the results into PK-Sim. Even though I don't find where the 126 min come from, it may be helpful to you.
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=10.1002%2Fjps.22726

Yep, this is the correct reference. Literature data from several sources was approximated by the sigmoidal function as shown in the paper. Small intestinal transit time was calculated then based on the definition below.
Small intestinal transit time: the time required for the passage of a non-absorbable compound through the small intestine, defined as the time span between gastric emptying (i.e. the point at which 63% of the drug mass has entered the duodenum) and the time at which 90% has entered the caecum.
As you can see from the figure in the paper, 90% of dissolved drug pass the small intestine at around 2.35h, giving (based on the default gastric emptying time = 15min) small intestinal transit time = 2.35h - 0.25h = 2.1h = 126min

...approximated by the sigmoidal function as shown in the paper.

The paper does not describe how the (simulated) function is fitted (at least I could not find it), i.e., which parameters are fitted and what the simulated output actually represents.
The data shows e.g. the cumulated distribution of transit times across a population of 315 individuals (Data: Kim et al). Is this what the simulated curve also shows (with an underlying population simulation)? Or is it a single (mean representative) simulation showing the amount of a single administration reaching the caecum over time?
In the paper by Thelen et al. it states, that "(Fig. 1) According to this (sigmodial) function, transit through the small
intestine is almost complete after 3–4 h.". But the data from Kim et al. has a slightly different meaning and states, that 100% of individuals had transit times between 15 min and 4-5h.
While the outcome for the fitted function would be the same, it still does not explain how P1 and P2 from the SITT_factor and the TransitVelocity (or rate) for each segment is fitted/derived?

Please let me know if anything is unclear or more information should be provided.
Thanks in advance,
Aya

title: Modeling saturating partitioning into BloodCells
body_text:
For Cyclosporin A, partitioning into blood cells saturates for high exposure (Fahr 1993):

Is there any experience in how this could be implemented in MoBi as partitioning coefficients are not directly available in Building Blocks to edit their formulas.
Any workarounds / other solutions?
Best,
Stephan

answer.body_text:
Interesting phenomenon. Thanks for sharing Stephan.
I did a brief read on cyclosporine, and it seems saturable intracellular binding occurs in the whole body (and blood cells). Organ-specific data is available from Tanaka, Kawai and Rowland, 1999. This data could be used to optimize the relative expression of a "protein binding partner" in each organ and the Kd globally. You could add an observer for the fudged B/P ratio and include the data from Fahr in the optimization using steady-state simulations. The partition coefficients calculated by the existing algorithms (R-R, PK-Sim Standard) are probably going to be wrong because of how much the binding contributes to distribution. What do you think?
Paul

comments.body_text:
Interesting phenomenon. Thanks for sharing Stephan.
I did a brief read on cyclosporine, and it seems saturable intracellular binding occurs in the whole body (and blood cells). Organ-specific data is available from Tanaka, Kawai and Rowland, 1999. This data could be used to optimize the relative expression of a "protein binding partner" in each organ and the Kd globally. You could add an observer for the fudged B/P ratio and include the data from Fahr in the optimization using steady-state simulations. The partition coefficients calculated by the existing algorithms (R-R, PK-Sim Standard) are probably going to be wrong because of how much the binding contributes to distribution. What do you think?
Paul

Thanks for your thoughts, Paul.
Yes, there also is an actual intracellular binding partner called Cyclophilin and the drug is highly lipoprotein bound (which might also affect partitioning). But Cyclophilin seems not to be the (only) reason for the cellular saturation.
The work from Tanaka is great to learn more on the multi-factorial nonlinear behaviour but uses data in rats, only, so far. We would need to establish the nonlinearity behaviour in humans.
But manually setting the calculated partitioning to a lower value and introducing another unspecific cellular binding partner (and an observer for the fudged cellular concentration) to capture the nonlinearity in partitioning could indeed be a workaround worth trying.
Thanks, I'll keep you updated.
