title: Carbamazepine PBPK model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have been working on a parent-metabolite PBPK model of carbamazepine and its main metabolite carbamazepine-10,11-epoxide.

title: Published: Development and Evaluation of an In Silico Dermal Absorption Model Relevant for Children
body_text:
The dermal modeling group at the University of Waterloo are excited to announce the publication of the following paper, which models skin penetration in children using the OSP Skin Permeation Model.
Development and Evaluation of an In Silico Dermal Absorption Model Relevant for Children
by Yejin Esther Yun, Daniella Calderon-Nieva, Abdullah Hamadeh and Andrea N. Edginton
School of Pharmacy, University of Waterloo, Waterloo, ON N2G 1C5, Canada
Abstract
The higher skin surface area to body weight ratio in children and the prematurity of skin in neonates may lead to higher chemical exposure as compared to adults. The objectives of this study were: (i) to provide a comprehensive review of the age-dependent anatomical and physiological changes in pediatric skin, and (ii) to construct and evaluate an age-dependent pediatric dermal absorption model. A comprehensive review was conducted to gather data quantifying the differences in the anatomy and physiology of child and adult skin. Maturation functions were developed for model parameters that were found to be age-dependent. A pediatric dermal absorption model was constructed by updating a MoBi implementation of the Dancik et al. 2013 skin permeation model with these maturation functions. Using a workflow for adult-to-child model extrapolation, the predictive performance of the model was evaluated by comparing its predicted rates of flux of diamorphine, phenobarbital and buprenorphine against experimental observations using neonatal skin. For diamorphine and phenobarbital, the model provided reasonable predictions. The ratios of predicted:observed flux in neonates for diamorphine ranged from 0.55 to 1.40. For phenobarbital, the ratios ranged from 0.93 to 1.26. For buprenorphine, the model showed acceptable predictive performance. Overall, the physiologically based pediatric dermal absorption model demonstrated satisfactory prediction accuracy. The prediction of dermal absorption in neonates using a model-based approach will be useful for both drug development and human health risk assessment.

comments.body_text: @abdullahhamadeh , great! Congratulations. Can you please also post this here, and if you send me a quick line, I can post it on the OSP LinkedIn Page!

title: Uniform time and concentration chart on x and y axis

title: How to convert this units?

comments.body_text:
Clearance is in volume of reference fluid (mL) per unit time (min).
This clearance is accomplished per amount (pmol) of enzyme in a recombinant enzyme assay. To parameterize this you should have a rough idea of the amount in pmol of the enzyme (e.g. CYP3A4) that is in your assay. This can be difficult as recombinant assays tend to be quite variable even within labs.
Converting from your screen shot above, the uL/min/pmol enzymes for CYP2D6 can be converted directly.
The measurement per mg of Cyp enzymes likely refers to a hepatic microsomal assay which is handled by one of the other options in PK-Sim.
As an aside, it is extremely unlikely that these values will translate from in vitro to in silico/in vivo. Vmax values have very low translation potential. However KM values have great translation potential.

title: Relative expression out
body_text:
Hello,
I am including TMDD in my model in Mobi and a question came up about protein expression:
What is the difference between „Relative expression out” and „Relative expression normalized”?
Why can I change the Formula to calculate „Relative expression out” in “Parameter start values” and what does it mean if I change it e.g. to “RelExpOutFromNorm”?
Thank you :)

comments.body_text:
Normalized expressions are the relative expressions normalized to the maximum relative expression.
MoBi allows changing anything if you want. This does not mean you should.
Do not change the formula assignments. What happens is that a wrong formula will be used for the parameter.
Hope this helps!
Stephan

title: Dextromethorphan PBPK model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
Today, we want to share our newly published PBPK model of dextromethorphan and its metabolites. Our corresponding article has been published in CPT: Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol. [1]. The dextromethorphan model was used to describe and predict dextromethorphan, dextrorphan and dextrorphan O-glucuronide plasma concentrations, as well as the effect of CYP2D6 drug-gene interactions on plasma concentrations. Additionally, the model was used to explore the interindividual variability in CYP2D6 activity score groups by estimating individual kcat values for 72 genotyped individuals. Model snapshots for aggregated and individual profiles have been uploaded to the OSP-repo.
Related publication:
[1] Rüdesheim S, Selzer D, Fuhr U, Schwab M, Lehr T. Physiologically-based pharmacokinetic modeling of dextromethorphan to investigate interindividual variability within CYP2D6 activity score groups. CPT Pharmacometrics Syst Pharmacol. 2022;00:1- 18.
Kind regards,
Simeon

title: How to know the fraction unbound (assay)?
body_text:
When input the hepatic clearance, the fraction unbound (assay) proportionally influences the specific clearance with either microsome or hepatocytes and. Apparently, the fraction unbound in tissue is also compound-dependent like fraction unbound in plasma. title: Fraction metabolized for 'last dose' at steady state
body_text:
Hi, I was wondering if there is a way to see the fractions for the last dose only, when simulating multiple doses (steady state).
If I use the "total fraction of dose", I see the cumulative fraction metabolized via each enzyme (from all doses). In literature, I have the fractions metabolized via each enzyme at a certain time point for a 'single' dose given at steady-state. However, in this study, there is also a fraction of the parent medicine (i.e. not metabolized); and there is auto-induction for some enzymes, thus I cannot compare to the 'total' fraction metabolized over all doses.
Or is that only possible if I create a new observer for this in MoBi?

comments.body_text:
Or is that only possible if I create a new observer for this in MoBi?

Yes. Here one possible solution: FractionLastDose.zip

Add parameters "Total amount" and "Total amount before last application" (const zero) to all metabolites of interest



In the last application: set Total amount before last application = Total amount for each metabolite (make sure to activate "Use as value" option.)



Create the new observer "Total fraction of last dose" and define it as

(Total metabolite amount - Total metabolite amount before the last application ) / Drug mass of the last application


Voilà

https://ascpt.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/action/downloadSupplement?doi=10.1002%2Fpsp4.12300&file=psp412300-sup-0001-suppinfo01.pdf

answer.body_text:
If you open your simulation and navigate to the SC-organ, you will see that no molecules are present in the subcompartments.

Open Molecules Start Values Building Block (BB)
Click on "extend". You will get new entries for all molecules of the project in the new organ. By default, all molecules are present there. I would only set the molecule you want to dose as "Is Present" in this organ.


At this stage, you will get an error that a paramter "Partition coefficient (interstitial/water) for the molecule is not found. Please refer to the manual you cite in the previous post how to add this parameter to the model.

You have either to select a specific molecule or, in case you want your Administartion protocol to work for different molecules, export the Application to pkml, open the pkml with a text editor, and replace the name of the molecule in the path with "MOLECULE". After that, you can load the pkml as an application in MoBi.

https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3828004/

title: Oral Absorption
body_text: How to set F and ka when known in PK-Sim?

answer.body_text:
Hi @jmgallo,
the F and ka parameters of empirical PK models with usually 1 to 3 commpartments describe how fast (ka) a fraction (F) of a given dose enters the systemic circulation.
Please keep in mind, that this is a strong simplification of the various processes (drug liberation, dissolution, permeation, metabolism...) that happen in a body after drug administration.
The physiological based PK models in PK-Sim explicitly consider the above processes. Therefore, there is no direct translation of these empirical parameters into the physiological based modeling concept in PK-Sim.
However, one can influence oral absorbtion for example by modifying formulation parameters for example in the Weibull formulation the Dissolution time / shape:

But keep in mind that (almost) all parameters in PK-Sim have a physiological meaning!
So just tuning parameters to come up with a good fit is not the way to go here.
In order to come up with meaningfull models, getting familiar with the PBPK concept is essential. Here a good starting point could be these publications:
https://ascpt.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/full/10.1038/psp.2013.41
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/27653238
Kind regards,
Henrik

title: PKSIM Beginner, How to add first order Absorption Rate Constant?
body_text:
Hi, I'm a student who is learning how to use PKSIM. I am interested in taking data for a simple linear 1 compartment drug and modeling it in PKSIM. Where can I input a Ka value for the drug's oral absorption?
Thanks!

comments.body_text:
Hi,
Generally speaking this cannot be done. PK-Sim is a mechanistic modeling tool and therefore requires you to describe oral absorption within a mechanistic framework of the gastrointestinal tract (called the ACAT model).
For a BCS class 1 compound, try this to get the ball rolling:
Add formulation building block "Dissolved" and name it "Solution" (representative of administering an oral solution, or representative of administering an oral tablet that dissolves very quickly)
Add administration protocol and specify an Oral dose
Create a simulation, configure it to include your oral dose administration protocol and your new formulation building block called "solution."
Play around with the parameter "specific intestinal permeability" to see if you can describe your Cmax & Tmax correctly. Optimize using the parameter identification tool.
Alternatively you can describe the dissolution of an oral formulation with a weibull function or power function that approximates Ka

title: Does inclusion of a meal event automatically change the physiology to fed?
body_text:
Dear all,
I want to do a simple comparison of a fasted versus fed profile in PK Sim. For this I included a meal event at the time of drug administration as sole change (therefore did not consider a change in solubility). Parameters such as gastric pH and transit time (GET) did not change as a consequence of the inclusion of this high caloric meal event.
I then changed the solubility: pH and GET remained unchanged.
From #638 I understood that those parameters would be changed automatically to the reference values under fed conditions. Did I get this wrong and should we change actively those parameters, or did I miss a step?
I thank you very much for any thoughts regarding this.
Best regards
Angèle

answer.body_text:
@Angelefleury A meal event changes not the GET itself (the GET parameter can be in fact interpreted as "GET fasted") but the normalized velocity of drug transfer from stomach into duodenum.
If you look at the drug transport from stomach to duodenum, it's defined like this:

Intestinal transit rate (absolute) is the normalized (to the amount of a molecule in stomach) velocity of drug transfer.
Which is defined as per default (= in fasted state) as 1/GET:

A meal event changes (among other things) this velocity and sets it to a function.

This new (Weibull-like) velocity function is based on the parameters of a meal (volume, caloric intake, etc.) and is constructed the way that it's smaller than 1/GET at the beginning and is increasing till it reaches the old 1/GET(fasted) value.
Thus the meal effect "stops" when the normalized velocity is greater than 1/GET.
Then the normalized velocity is set back to its original formula 1/GET:

comments.body_text:
Hi Angèle, the parameters do not change visibly in the parameters within the simulation, but will then dynamically change during the simulation (MoBi will provide insights here).
Did the simulation curves change?
Best,
Stephan

Hi Stephan,
Thank you very much for your time and efforts to help.
Yes: the shape of the curve changes, with similar AUCinf but a shift to a later Tmax and lower Cmax, suggesting indeed that some changes in GET and/or pH occurs although not visible in PK Sim (again for this exercise I consider the same solubility input value fed or fasted).
In MoBi: unfortunately the changes are not visible here neither (GET = 15min and Stomach pH = 2.00 in both cases). Does the user have a way of monitoring this?
Best wishes
Angèle

@Angelefleury If this is a parameter that you want to monitor, you can simply set the flag "Plot parameter" to true, rerun the simulation and you should be able to see the change of parameters over time

Dear @msevestre ,
Thank you very much for the tip. After some attempts it seems that GET are really equal between fed and fasted (the only difference in the simulation is the presence or absence of a meal event):

so there is definitely a difference between the two curves, but I can't see its origins form the model parameters.
I believe I still miss a step.

Hi Angèle,
the is definitely a difference. @PavelBal can certainly help ;-)
Enjoy the weekend,
Stephan

Which is defined as per default (= in fasted state) as 1/GET:

Then the normalized velocity is set back to its original formula 1/GET:

title: How to use PK-SIM to set the genotype of enzyme or transporter？
body_text:
Dear Community,
I am working on a PBPK model I have a problem. As shown in the picture, how to set the genotype of enzyme or transporter as in the picture？
Thank you in advance :)

comments.body_text:
Dear @noxavier,
In the repaglinide model, the polymorphic enzymes and transporters were implemented as follows:

To mimic the two alleles, the polymorphic enzyme (or transporter) was implemented in the individual with "halved" reference concentration compared to the reference concentration for the enzyme/transporter from the database.
In the compound, two metabolism/transport processes were created per "allele" (e.g., CYP2C8 *1 and CYP2C8 *3) and respective Km and kcat values were implemented according to the values in the table.
In the simulation, the appropriate processes for both "alleles" were selected to match the desired geno-/phenotype.

The repaglinide model file can also be downloaded here, if you want to have a look at it yourself: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Repaglinide-Model
Best,
Denise

Dear @noxavier,
PK-Sim works with the kcat value, which is calculated as Vmax / Transporter concentration. When you insert values for Transporter concentration and Vmax in the upper row, kcat will be calculated by the software. If you don't insert a value for Vmax (e.g., if no information is provided in the literature), you can enter values for kcat directly. In this case, the original formula will be overwritten with this value, which is indicated by the yellow exclamation mark.
Best,
Denise

title: how to explain Kidney volume between PK-Sim and article ？
body_text:
Dear all,
I found a paper which should be published by PK-Sim team that normalize total renal volume with respect to the subject's body surface area。But if I set a person whose information is  European race ,30 years old, male, height is 176cm in PK-sim, the total kidney volume is 0.44 l, which is very different from the results of this article, how to explain this situation?

answer.body_text:
The paper explains how to use the formula. You would take the person's automatically generated healthy kidney volume (Healthy KV) and healthy GFR (GFR_o) from PK-Sim. First normalize the PK-Sim value for GFR (mL/min) to 1.73m2 BSA (I know we have talked about this before).
To get the CKD kidney volume (Diseased KV) for a desired CKD GFR (GFR_t):

You can see from this formula that the equations on the graph are used to calculate the fraction of KV that should remain 'healthy' in someone who has kidney disease as a function of their GFR vs. their expected healthy GFR for a person of their age.
The fraction represents the theoretical 'chronic kidney disease burden.' Using this method allows you to apply CKD to any individual and conserves the information generated previously for healthy individuals and populations in PK-Sim (Willmann, Schlender, others in various papers).

comments.body_text:
This paper is not by the PK-Sim team nor reviewed by them. It is by me and my team.
The PK-Sim kidney volume includes the total volume of two kidneys including their blood volume, arteries, veins, glands and all fluids, etc. Stephan Willmann or Jan Schlender may be able to tell you more about this measurement. I merely take it as the healthy reference point.
In the graph above, this value (440 mL/two kidneys) has been normalized to the ultrasound-measured adult reference value for one kidney (150 mL). This is why the y-axis is the 'PK-Sim normalized Kidney volume.'
The purpose of the graph is to understand the fractional change in kidney volume, rather than the absolute value. These fractions are applied as a 'fraction of healthy value' when implementing kidney disease.
There is no normalization by body surface area here.

@prvmalik，
Thank you for your answer,but I think I don't know what is your meaning well. if a person's GFR is 50 ml/min/1.73m2, can I use this formula to calculate kidney volume? Or do I need to make additional changes to this?
Yours,
Sincerely！

The paper explains how to use the formula. You would take the person's automatically generated healthy kidney volume (Healthy KV) and healthy GFR (GFR_o) from PK-Sim. First normalize the PK-Sim value for GFR (mL/min) to 1.73m2 BSA (I know we have talked about this before).
To get the CKD kidney volume (Diseased KV) for a desired CKD GFR (GFR_t):

You can see from this formula that the equations on the graph are used to calculate the fraction of KV that should remain 'healthy' in someone who has kidney disease as a function of their GFR vs. their expected healthy GFR for a person of their age.
The fraction represents the theoretical 'chronic kidney disease burden.' Using this method allows you to apply CKD to any individual and conserves the information generated previously for healthy individuals and populations in PK-Sim (Willmann, Schlender, others in various papers).

@prvmalik，
I understand it well now, thank you for your patient answer, please forgive me for my mistakes I just made.Merry Christmas for you in advance！
Yours,
Sincerely!

title: Individual or population DDI simulation?
body_text:
Dear colleagues
My work is based on drug interaction, evaluating chronic use for 6 months.
Is it necessary to perform simulation using a population?
Or can only an individual's pharmacokinetics be used?
Thank you

title: Is there a way to change the population script in PK Sim?
body_text:
Hi,
I am trying to change and create my own Asian population in PK Sim, and while I understand that I can set up variability within a specified population for most parameters (eg. lymph flow, gastric pH etc) by using the 'User Defined Variability' function under the population building block, there are certain parameters in which their distribution within a population is fixed to the population database in PK Sim (eg. age, weight and height). I would like to change the way that PK Sim generates the virtual individual's age, height and weight but I do not see anywhere that I could change this.
As an example, I would like to change the way an individual's height and weight is simulated based on his/her age and gender using the following equations:
If Male, then Height = 175.32 + (0.1113 * age) - (0.0025 * age ^ 2), &
Weight = math.exp (2.643 + 0.0099 * Height)
If Female, then Height = 161.66 + (0.1319 * age) - (0.0027 * age ^ 2), &
Weight = math.exp (2.7383 + 0.0091 * Height)
Is there anywhere which I could overwrite the original population script with this new one? Appreciate anybody who can offer their help with this!

answer.body_text:
@kohhc

However, I was expecting the age distribution in my population to resembles more of a normal distribution.

I don't know anything about physiology etc.. but I would not expect age to be normally distributed.  In PK-Sim, it is uniformly distributed  (e.g. you have has many 20-25 years old as 45-50 years old as 70-75 years old).
This cannot be changed as it is part of the actual algorithm.
2/ There are no equations. It is all based on population data. By setting an equation like above as a function of BSA, you are losing variability etc...
What you would have to do in that case is generate the population  (in PKSim or R). Then load the population in R and adjusted the liver volume (and of course compensate for the difference with for instance fat volume). This can only be done in R.
However, such a population is by no means qualified. Is there any reason why you do not want to use the Japanese population that comes out of the box?

comments.body_text:
You should be able to do this in R and then re-import the population to PK-Sim...
Will have to get back with a quick how-to later.
Agreed with @StephanSchaller . That's the only way to do it at the moment

I see thank you both for the suggestion. I am not familiar with using R and the OSP Suite Manual did not mention anything about it. Is there a guide/tutorial video that I could follow to get a grasp of how it works or would it be possible if you could give a quick crash course on how I could go about changing the population script to my desired equations using R? Thank you.

There is a section in the manual specifically for the R toolbox;-)
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-r/r-introduction
Check it out. Once you have it running and you can create a population from R, get back to us and we'll proceed with the next steps

I've followed the instructions under the Section for Creating and Running a Population in R. However, I seem to be having an issue with running the simulation. For some reason, the population that I've created via R (myPopulation) is saved as a list type and I get the following error when I try to execute the following command:

simulationResults <- runSimulation(simulation = sim,population = myPopulation)
Error in validateIsOfType(population, Population, nullAllowed = TRUE) :
runSimulation: argument 'population' is of type 'list', but expected 'Population'!

On the other hand, there was no issue if I load a .csv file that was created directly from PK Sim (which I termed myPopulation1). Seeing that my original goal was to create a custom population using R instead of relying on the Asian_Tanaka databases' age/height/weight distribution, is there any way to transform myPopulation created in R into the correct data type?

I added a new line (3rd line) such that the population is the one that is being ran instead of the list:
print(PopulationCharacteristics)
myPopulation <- createPopulation(populationCharacteristics = PopulationCharacteristics)
myPopulation1 <- myPopulation$population
print(myPopulation1)
I'm not sure if this is the correct way to rectify the issue but it doesn't seem to give me any more error when I run the simulation command. Below is the script that I've written:
PopulationCharacteristics <- createPopulationCharacteristics(species = Species$Human,
population = HumanPopulation$Asian_Tanaka_1996,
numberOfIndividuals = 49,
proportionOfFemales = 65,
weightMin = NULL,
weightMax = NULL,
weightUnit = "kg",
heightMin = NULL,
heightMax = NULL,
heightUnit = "cm",
ageMin = 20,
ageMax = 30,
ageUnit = "year(s)",
BMIMin = 20,
BMIMax = 25,
BMIUnit = "kg/m²",
gestationalAgeMin = NULL,
gestationalAgeMax = NULL,
gestationalAgeUnit = "week(s)",
moleculeOntogenies = NULL)
print(PopulationCharacteristics)
myPopulation <- createPopulation(populationCharacteristics = PopulationCharacteristics)
myPopulation1 <- myPopulation$population
print(myPopulation1)
simFilePath <- file.path(getwd(),"..","Documents","4-Hydroxy-Diclofenac.pkml",fsep = .Platform$file.sep)
sim <- loadSimulation(simFilePath)
simulationResults <- runSimulation(simulation = sim,population = myPopulation1)
print(simulationResults)
simulationResults$allQuantityPaths
resultsPath <- simulationResults$allQuantityPaths[[1]]
print(resultsPath)
resultsData <- getOutputValues(simulationResults, quantitiesOrPaths = resultsPath)
resultsTime <- resultsData$data$Time
resultsValues <- resultsData$data$Organism|PeripheralVenousBlood|4'-Hydroxy-Diclofenac|Blood Cells
plot(resultsTime,resultsValues, type = "l",xlab = "Time [min]", ylab = "Concentration [µM]")
That being said, this population that I've created is still using the age/height/weight distribution from the Asian_Tanaka database in PK Sim. @StephanSchaller @msevestre you mentioned that it is possible to create a custom population with my own age/height/weight distribution in R and re-import the population back to PK Sim. May I know how I could go about doing that? Greatly appreciate the help! :)

@kohhc Well done in getting the R tool box to run.
Now, there is no DIRECT way to change the algorithm as this is not something that was ever requested (e.g. we use body weight and height based on population data instead of some fitted formula)
In order to accomplish what you want to do, you will have to trick the system a bit and create single individuals. You can do this by setting min== max
So for instance let say your formula returns BW = 70kg and height  = 175cm
then you could try creating a population with one individual (weightMin=70, weightMax=70 etc..) and iterate over the number of individuals you want to create.
Then once you have created your N populations each with one individual, I would advise you to create a new population with N individual and simply overwrite each individual values with the values created above. That way you should have one unique population matching exactly what you need.
This should not be too complicated to get going

@msevestre I see. Manually defining the population sounds rather tedious but it does seem like the only solution if there's no way to change the internal algorithm. In this case I realized that there's actually no need for me to use R since the solution you proposed can be done using PK Sim.
Nonetheless thank you to both for your help! I should be able to get my desired population with your proposed method. :)

The big difference is that you can script the creation of the population => if you need to recreate it, add new individuals, change the formula etc ..you simply need to run the script by hand.
Good luck

While it is possible to manually create my desired population using the method you proposed,  it may not be a very feasible solution in the long term

@kohhc That's why I suggested scripting so that you can always adjust and recreate on the fly. Doing it manually in PK-Sim is definitely not sustainable
You can of course change the DB directly...but this is a dangerous path.  You would OVERRIDE the default behavior of the tool... It was never intended of the user to mess around with the DB and if you do,  I cannot predict what may go wrong. Also, any update you do will be lost when installing a new version of PKSim (e..g v10 coming in the next few weeks)
Assuming you are brave enough, this should be fairly simple: Since you only want to change  Weight and Height as a function of Age, you would only need to update parameters MeanBW and MeanHeight parameters (140 entries for the Tanaka population)

You would set the STD to 0 and for each age of interest, set the value of your method above
e.g age = 1, mean_value = xx
age = 2, mean_value=yy etc

@msevestre Thank you for your advice noted that that there are certain risks involved with this method. For now, I have tried adjusting the values within the database file and it seems to be working for the most part. However, I do encounter 2 parameters where I have issues trying to change the distribution:

Age: Even before making any changes to the database file, I've noticed that the age distribution in my simulated population seems to be rather random (as seen in picture below). However, I was expecting the age distribution in my population to resembles more of a normal distribution. Can I ask how PK Sim generates individual age in a population (is it randomly selecting a number based on your defined range or does it try to follow a normal distribution)? Is there also any way that one might be able to change this if it is not normally distributed?



Liver volume: I understand that liver volume is calculated based on the generated age, height and weight of the individual in PK Sim. May I know what is the exact equation that PK Sim uses to calculate this liver volume? Asking this because I am thinking of incorporating my own equation (as shown below), but I am unsure where to find and how to change the default liver volume equation in PK Sim.

Liver Volume = 0.722 * BSA^1.176

@kohhc

However, I was expecting the age distribution in my population to resembles more of a normal distribution.

I don't know anything about physiology etc.. but I would not expect age to be normally distributed.  In PK-Sim, it is uniformly distributed  (e.g. you have has many 20-25 years old as 45-50 years old as 70-75 years old).
This cannot be changed as it is part of the actual algorithm.
2/ There are no equations. It is all based on population data. By setting an equation like above as a function of BSA, you are losing variability etc...
What you would have to do in that case is generate the population  (in PKSim or R). Then load the population in R and adjusted the liver volume (and of course compensate for the difference with for instance fat volume). This can only be done in R.
However, such a population is by no means qualified. Is there any reason why you do not want to use the Japanese population that comes out of the box?

title: question of building a new population
body_text:
I am following Dr. Dallmann's 2018 paper to build a postpartatum individual. In that paper he built a pregancy individual. There was a short paragraph describing this process "To include the physiological changes during pregnancy in

title: About distribution phase in simulation
body_text:
Dear community,
Hope you are doing well. As you can see in the picture. The observed data set shows a distribution phase followed elimination phase after Cmax, but simulation curve doesn't fit it and the predicted t1/2 is shorter. I have tried to adjusted many parameters but they don't work. So could you tell me what parameters should I take into consideration? By the way, the simulation curve got to Cmax a little earlier than observed data. What should I do to slow the absorption?
Thanks,
Harris

comments.body_text:
You likely need a different partition coefficient algorithm (Schmitt for instance) if you wish to hit that terminal point. I would check how that terminal point is determined with respect to BLQs as true mean value of the population may be lower than it is depicting.
See previous discussion of mine on the forum for different ways to model a late Tmax. See which is most relevant to your scenario.
Also if still not getting the half life you wish then you could weight the terminal points more heavily in the estimation (parameter identification tool).

title: Software change request - creating virtual individuals and populations with obesity
body_text:
I’m posting to request a change to the software to better allow for creating virtual pediatric individuals and populations with obesity in PK-Sim.
Because children with obesity are defined by BMI percentile rather than raw BMI, it is difficult to create virtual children with obesity in the current software. Currently, the probability of a virtual subject being obese is irrespective of age, which means that there is an uneven age and obesity distribution.
I propose having some type of ‘Obese’ selection in the Individual and Population building blocks with filters in the back-end, as well as a user-input field for BMI percentile in the Population building block so that the user can create populations with varying body size categories (i.e. overweight, obese, morbidly obese, etc).
Note that these changes are only relevant for virtual subjects 2 years or older, below which BMI and obesity status cannot be determined.

answer.body_text:
Hi JGG,
As I know you are working on the differential and age-dependence of anatomy/physiology in obese pediatric populations, we will need to figure out how to incorporate this information when we create obese individuals.
To change the anatomy/physiology as a function of obesity would require, in my opinion, two additions:

incorporating the change [e.g. 15% increase in liver weight when BMI is > 95th percentile of the population (definition of obesity) from which the individual comes] in the algorithm to 'Create Individual'. This allows for an obese individual to be created with the associated anatomical/physiological changes.
ability to filter by BMI percentile, not absolute BMI, when creating a population. As you suggest, filtering by absolute BMI will favour those in the pediatric population with naturally lower BMI because the probability of creating them is higher. This results in a population with many young kids whose BMI is naturally lower who may not even be obese. As a result, to ensure that the probability of getting an even age distribution of actually obese individuals in the pediatric population, filtering by BMI percentile will be necessary.

Also, by doing 1) and 2), an obese population can be created for any race where there are populations in PK-Sim. It avoids having just one 'Obese Population' that is fixed and irrespective of race.
There are probably a number of ways to do this. Let's brainstorm to figure out what's best!
Andrea

ability to filter by BMI percentile, not absolute BMI, when creating a population. title: Extrapolation of Papp to specific intestinal permeability - how to activate the customized calibration method in PK-Sim?
body_text:
Hi OSP community,
Can anyone help me with advice on how to input experimental apparent permeability (Papp) values for investigated compound + reference compound in PK-Sim?
Manual states:  (https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#adme-properties  --> ADME properties --> absorption --> Calculation of Specific Intestinal Permeabilities)
"In addition to the calculated specific intestinal permeability, experimentally determined permeabilities, e.g. from Caco-2-cell permeability assays can be used. However, due to the large inter-laboratory variability in Caco-2 permeations, a proper calibration of the measured in vitro values and the calculated in silico permeabilities for a defined set of compounds is necessary. If experimentally determined values for intestinal permeabilities are available and the customized calibration method has been implemented in PK-Sim®, this option is then available in the drop-down menu in the Calculation methods window."
I am unable to find the " drop-down menu in the Calculation methods window." specified in the instructions above.
Answers to any of these questions would additionally be helpful:
Which reference compounds has the Papp calibration performed for in PK-Sim? (low - medium - high permeability)
Which cellular models have the reference PK-Sim Papp values been measured in?
What is the equation that derives intestinal permeability from Papp?
Thanks!

answer.body_text:
In fact PK-Sim has built-in calibration for CACO/PAMPA assays in the form
Intestinal permeability (transcellular) = 10 ^ (m * log10 (Specific intestinal permeability (transcellular)) + b)
(with assay-specific calibration parameters m and b)
The only problem is that the calibration parameters (m and b) are currently hidden in PK-Sim and thus cannot be changed by user. I have just created an issue in PK-Sim for this (Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#1631)
As per default calibration parameters are defined as m=1 and b=0  which corresponds to "no calibration":
Intestinal permeability (transcellular) = Specific intestinal permeability (transcellular)
If you export a simulation from PK-Sim to MoBi you can see this calculation (and parameters are accessible :))

Setting calibration parameters to visible will enable users to perform the calibration directly in PK-Sim.

comments.body_text:
Dear @irina-antonescu ,
Hope this screenshot will help:


you can access/see alternative calculated values using the drop-down (marked with green)

a mouse-over on the publication names will give you more details


you can then add your additional entries using the "+" (marked in yellow)

Required Infos on your additional questions can be found in the listed publications.
Hope this helps,
Stephan

Dear Stephan,
Unfortunately those options do not appear when I click the drop down menu.
Here is a screenshot of what I see:

Is it perhaps a different version that supports this tool? I am using PK-Sim version 9 update 1.
Thanks,
Irina

Dear @irina-antonescu ,
the different values shown in @StephanSchaller's plot are calculated from different lipophilicity values. If you add another value for the drug's lipophilicty, a second calculated value for the specific intestinal permeability will be displayed.
Currently, there's no option in PK-Sim to input experimental apparent permeability (Papp) values and convert that into a specific intestinal permeability. I remember this issue has been discussed some time ago here, maybe that helps.
In any case, the manual should be adjusted to avoid misunderstandings.
André

Dear @AndreDlm,
Thanks for the clarification. I agree it would be good to remove that.
Irina

In fact PK-Sim has built-in calibration for CACO/PAMPA assays in the form
Intestinal permeability (transcellular) = 10 ^ (m * log10 (Specific intestinal permeability (transcellular)) + b)
(with assay-specific calibration parameters m and b)
The only problem is that the calibration parameters (m and b) are currently hidden in PK-Sim and thus cannot be changed by user. I have just created an issue in PK-Sim for this (Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#1631)
As per default calibration parameters are defined as m=1 and b=0  which corresponds to "no calibration":
Intestinal permeability (transcellular) = Specific intestinal permeability (transcellular)
If you export a simulation from PK-Sim to MoBi you can see this calculation (and parameters are accessible :))

Setting calibration parameters to visible will enable users to perform the calibration directly in PK-Sim.

@irina-antonescu ,
yes, sorry for my misunderstanding. What I meant is that the info on the calculation for the specific int permeability is shown in the mouse-over info if you move over the entries in the dropdown (with the corresponding publication).
But I do not think the manual is "wrong" in that sense as mentioned above, but is a bit sloppy in describing the location correctly.
In principle it says you will need to come up with your own calibration and you can enter the values manually (as in the "yellow" indicated "+" in my screenshot) and later select them in the "calculation methods" window. The latter is wrongly described.
The "calculation method" window it refers to is actually the "Parameter Alternative" list in the Compound settings, when you CREATE or CONFIGURE your simulations:

Best,
Stephan
@StephanSchaller ok thanks for clarifying

@Yuri05 @StephanSchaller any plans for a paracellular permeation model to be added to PK-Sim any time soon? It can be an important absorption route for quite a lot of permeability-limited drugs (BCS class III and IV) and it is also biased for drugs according to ionization state.
There are some paracellular models out there where quite a lot of physiology parameters (pore size, porosity, potential drop) are refined e.g for human jejunum or Caco-2. I can provide specific examples if needed :-)

title: Creating individuals with genetic variation
body_text:
Hello,
I was just wondering is there any way for me to load a .fasta or .vca file into PK-Sim. I came across these genetic variants and decided to import them into PK-Sim. Such an example will be CYP2C9*1 variants from PharmVar (https://www.pharmvar.org/gene/CYP2C9).
If those are not the accepted file type, may I know what will be the exact types of files PK-Sim accepts?
Thank you very much.
Yours,
Harry

answer.body_text:
Dear Harry,
to my knowledge it is not possible to import genetic information like you asked for into PK-Sim, but rather the “effect” of different genetic variants, e.g., increased protein expression or decreased activity, can be modeled.
The current practice to model pharmacokinetics in different genetic variants is to create individuals and add the enzyme/transporter of interest as well as a respective process in the compound building block. When simulating your drug’s pharmacokinetics, the reference concentration can be changed in the individual or the activity, i.e., Km, vmax or kcat, can be changed in the compound according to literature (if available) or to parameter optimizations. If you want to model heterogenous allele carriers, you can add two versions of your enzyme/transporter of interest with halve the reference concentration each. Doing this, combinations with different expressions or activities "per allele" can be combined.
Here are some examples of drug-gene interaction modeling in PK-Sim:
https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics12121200
https://doi.org/10.3390/pharmaceutics13030331
Best regards,
Denise

title: Calculation of AUCR and CmaxR for DDI simulations
body_text:
Could you please explain how exactly the calculations of the AUC and Cmax ratios in the PK-Analysis tab are performed? I am especially interested in what steps are performed for the Intraconazole project, where the CYP3A4 metabolization chain is
Itraconazole -> Hydroxy-Itraconazole -> Keto-Itraconazole -> N-desalkyl-Itraconazol
and all 4 compounds are inhibitors of CYP3A4.

comments.body_text:
The calculation is performed for one simulation and one compound,
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim/blob/develop/src/PKSim.Core/Services/GlobalPKAnalysisTask.cs#L276
even though the knowledge of the compound name is not very useful because we are de-facto turning off ALL inhibitions
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim/blob/617264dee8e040bb5f71d46f74efc891641eea6c/src/PKSim.Core/Model/SimulationFactory.cs#L208
This is the place here
_interactionTask.AllInteractionContainers(ddiRatioSimulation)
            .SelectMany(c => c.AllParameters())
            .Each(p => p.Value = disabledDDIValueFor(p.Name));

In short, we create a clone of the original simulation, we turn off all interaction processes (ALL of them) and we simulate.
So in your case, inhibition is switched off for all Itra* compounds of the model
Does it help?

Does it help?

Yepp, definitely. I am not sure yet how to interpret this, but at least the technical part is clear.
For bioavailability this is clear. You already click on a given drug. For DDI, this could be a good option if required...
@StephanSchaller We do not model auto-inhibition explicitly in PK-Sim. I thought this was already included in the Ki/Km values. So how would this solve the problem at hand?

title: drug-drug interaction by protein displacement in plasma in PK-Sim
body_text:
Hello
Does PK-Sim allow for accounting for drug-drug interaction via displacement of plasma protein binding?
What settings should be done to account for such phenomena in PK-Sim?

answer.body_text:
Hi @mmalsmadi, this is not a functionality that PK-Sim provides by default but can be achieved by customization through MoBi.

title: Specific organ permeability formula
body_text:
Hello,
I am trying to find the source for the formula used to compute the specific organ permeability parameter which is the following :
IsSmallMolecule ? (MWEff *1E9 / 336) ^ (-6) * 10^LogMA / 5 * 1E-4 *1E-1: 0
Thanks in advance,

answer.body_text: Dear @Vincent-AC , Andre's answer was fully correct. Actually I developed the equation many years ago, but never found the time to publish the approach. It is simply based on the assumption that permeability of cell membranes is propertional to the partition coefficient of the lipid core of these membranes, i.e. P = 10^logP and proportional to MW to the power of six. The large power of six is the upper end of the range of experimentally determined values you may find in the literatur. However, it turned out, that for many different compounds I worked with, this power led to the best results as compared to smaller values. The rest of the approach is then simply a recalculation of permeability values determined from fitting to detailed PK-data for Ciprofloxacin according to the rule of proportion. Admittedly the equation is based on only limited data and certainly lacks a stringent statistics based derivation. However, it worked extremely well in many projects, why we decided not to detain it from the community, when developing PK-Sim.

comments.body_text:
Hi @Vincent-AC, I think the source of this formula is not well documented, but if I'm not wrong it was developed using ciprofloxacin as reference compound; therefore, you can find its molecular weight and lipophilicity in the denominator of the equation.
More generally, lipophilicity (logMA) as input variable can be deduced from Fick's law of diffusion (by expressing the concentration in the membrane in terms of the free concentration and partitioning into the membrane with logMA as partition coefficient) and the effective molecular weight MWEff is a surrogate for molecular volume which also affects the diffusion coefficient according to the Einstein-Stokes equation. I would have to check where the exponent ^(-6) comes from, but I believe it from this book chapter here.
And for large molecules, passive diffusion across cell membranes was neglected as described in this paper.

Dear @Vincent-AC , Andre's answer was fully correct. Actually I developed the equation many years ago, but never found the time to publish the approach. It is simply based on the assumption that permeability of cell membranes is propertional to the partition coefficient of the lipid core of these membranes, i.e. P = 10^logP and proportional to MW to the power of six. The large power of six is the upper end of the range of experimentally determined values you may find in the literatur. However, it turned out, that for many different compounds I worked with, this power led to the best results as compared to smaller values. The rest of the approach is then simply a recalculation of permeability values determined from fitting to detailed PK-data for Ciprofloxacin according to the rule of proportion. Admittedly the equation is based on only limited data and certainly lacks a stringent statistics based derivation. However, it worked extremely well in many projects, why we decided not to detain it from the community, when developing PK-Sim.

Dear @AndreDlm and @wschm
Thank you very much for your answers I do understand the formula now !
I mark Walter's comment as answer but I would tag all your answers if I could.
Best regards,
Vincent

title: Fitting ontogeny including variability using PK-Sim Ontogeny Database approach
body_text:
Hi all,
I am trying to recreate the approach of fitting ontogeny including variability used in the PK-Sim Ontogeny Database.
I found the documentation for v7.3 here: https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSPSuite.Documentation/blob/master/PK-Sim%20Ontogeny%20Database%20Version%207.3.pdf
My proposal to recreate these methods:

Fit the Hill equation (bottom of page 4) to the literature data. This line doubles as the median of pop fit and the geoMean A across PMA. Some adjustment to the starting point on x-axis may need to be made to allow better fitting (i.e., a higher n).
Create upper and lower bounds defined by Hill equations with coefficient "k" to allow A>1 an A<1 to capture the observed data. Simulate 10,000 individuals with randomly selected k, A0.5, and n within the defined upper and lower bounds.
Check whether 1.645x geoSD of the fitted line of geoMean A indeed capture 90% of the simulated individuals (50th to 95th percentile above the median pop fit, and 5th to 50th percentile below the median pop fit). If it does not, then try different upper and lower bounds (repeat step 2).
Once 90% of the simulated individuals are captured within the bounds, record the geoSD n, geoSD A0.5, and geoSD adult obtained from this population.

However, I received a second opinion on the above approach and we came to conclusion that it was more likely that the bounds were not defined in such a prescribed way; and k, A0.5, and n were likely not chosen at random within those bounds. Based on the following statement in the documentation:
"For fitting the ontogeny including variability, constituting variability in ontogeny as well as activity, a virtual population with 10000 individuals was created".
It sounds like the virtual population was made based on variability in ontogeny and variability in activity. These variabilities could have been drawn from normal distributions with some SD, but I am unsure as to whether it would be literature or another criteria informing these.
I would greatly appreciate if anyone has more insight on recapitulating the documentation methods.
One further question: I want to confirm that "SD" in the PK-Sim Table is the "geoSD around the fitted line" (it appears to be so judging by CYP3A5 and UGT1A4 in liver). I also ask this because most of the SDs in the tablet for younger ages are higher than later ages and I have difficulty corresponding that to the plots visually - see CYP2C8 in liver as an example.
Thanks!
Cindy

comments.body_text:
One further question: I want to confirm that "SD" in the PK-Sim Table is the "geoSD around the fitted line" (it appears to be so judging by CYP3A5 and UGT1A4 in liver). I also ask this because most of the SDs in the tablet for younger ages are higher than later ages and I have difficulty corresponding that to the plots visually - see CYP2C8 in liver as an example.

Yes SD in the table represents geoSD in total activity/expression. GeoSD has a proportional relationship to the geomean value, rather than additive (in the case of arithmetic SD). Even with higher geoSDs at young ages, the final upper and lower bounds are smaller than at older ages because the absolute activity value at an early age (e.g. 40 weeks PMA) is quite low.

@prvmalik Thanks for the explanation on that Paul, it now makes sense to me.
Interested in hearing if anyone has feedback on the fitting approach I mentioned or any further insight.

Interested in hearing if anyone has feedback on the fitting approach I mentioned or any further insight.

Maybe @Incei ?

@choitingy may I ask why you want to change the approach to fit the variability of the ontogeny profiles?
Indeed the Geomean and GeoSD of the ontogenies were estimated based on reported variability in ontogeny and variability in activity in the literature that are referenced for each enzyme in the documentation.

@Incei Not so much to change the approach, but rather to employ similar methods used in the PK-Sim documentation where possible. Essentially, I am building a model for a drug that is metabolized by UGT1A4 and would like to update the existing ontogeny profile by incorporating data from a recently published study.
Thanks for your clarification. Just as an example, for UGT1A6 in the liver it appears that the serotonin glucuronidation data from Figure 2A from Miyagi & Collier, 2011 (doi: 10.1124/dmd.110.037192) were digitized and used for the fitting. Would the used reported variability in ontogeny and variability in activity be found from Figure 2A or were the estimates also informed by elsewhere in this paper?

@Incei, I also want to understand the Methods in the ontogeny database documentation (as in the original post), but I am not clear on all of the details. The methods in the documentation says the following:
"For fitting the ontogeny including variability, constituting variability in ontogeny as well as activity, a virtual population with 10000 individuals was created. Fitted were then the geometric mean and geometric standard deviation of the Hill coefficient (GeoSD n), the geometric mean and geometric standard deviation of PMA at 50% activity compared to adult (GeoSD A0.5), and a geometric standard deviation as an activity variability factor (GeoSD adult)."

So what variability is used to build the virtual population? Is there any variation in enzyme expression or concentration in these individuals? Does the virtual population use the Hill equation that is fit to the ontogeny data?
I don't understand how the geometric mean and geometric standard deviation of the parameters is fit. Was a Hill function first fit to each individual, then the geometric mean and geometric standard deviations were calculated from that distribution of parameters?

So what variability is used to build the virtual population? Is there any variation in enzyme expression or concentration in these individuals? Does the virtual population use the Hill equation that is fit to the ontogeny data

The Tables in the documentation are uploaded into PKSim. I believe that PK-Sim linearly interpolates between the points in the table. This interpolated function outputs the "Ontogeny factor" as a function of age. The geometric standard deviation is also inputted into the table and describes the variability for that age bin around the mean ontogeny factor. When an individual is generated, the individual receives a lognormally-distributed ontogeny factor using the mean and geoSD information from the interpolated function and table. The ontogeny factor is used to get enzyme concentration in the following equation:
Final Enzyme Concentration = Reference Enzyme Concentration * Organ Relative Expression * Ontogeny Factor
Therefore, by default the individuals have variability in enzyme concentrations, even though there is no variability in the reference concentration (yes it's quite misleading).
I'd also like to know the answer to point 2.

So @prvmalik, the tables in the documentation were used as input for the creation of the virtual population? Then the tables come from taking the geometric mean and geometric standard deviations of experimental enzyme activity results?

Yes. But as I'm learning it's rather difficult to get a geometric mean line from an optimization. It's either the mean or the median line, and the median is a decent approximation of the geometric mean. To get median I am doing L1 optimization with the absolute  value of error rather than sum of squared error as the error function.

I don't understand how the geometric mean and geometric standard deviation of the parameters is fit. Was a Hill function first fit to each individual, then the geometric mean and geometric standard deviations were calculated from that distribution of parameters?

The created population of 10000 individuals had the following parameters:
•	Age (range depending on available data)
•	Random number (between 0-1)  for calculation of the Hill coefficient
•	Random number  (between 0-1) for calculation of PMA
•	Random number (between 0-1) for calculation of  activity variability factor
In each step, new distributions were created using the random numbers of the population and the geo-mean and geo-sd of the fitparameters (hill function). These ontogeny distributions over age were than compared to the literatur data to evaluate the loss function (wiki). The best parameter fit was then used to create the ontogeny table.
Note: In case of an update, or new ontogeny function, we are not applying this method anymore. E.g. for AGP we applied MCMC, which can also be found in this documentation.

Thank you, @Incei, for that explanation. I have a few follow-up questions:

So a new virtual population was created at each step of the fitting process?
And each individual was given a random value for the Hill coefficient [n], the PMA at 50% activity [A_0.5], and the activity variability factor on the entire expression [adult]?
Why would the random numbers generated for each of these three parameters be limited to the range [0,1]?

title: The Fraction absorbed value was exceed 1 when it was simulated using EHC parameters.
body_text:
Dear community,
I have PK observed data which has double peak. So I optimized many parameters such as EHC, gallbladder, transporters, biliary clearance and meal...… and then I made double peak simulation.
Simulated PK parameters (Cmax, AUC…) fitted well with it’s observed data. But I wonder why Fraction absorbed was over 1. My Fraction absorbed value was 2.11 and I think it is impossible to make sense. The PK-sim manual said that "Absorbed fraction of applied oral dose. Please note that, e.g. in the case of enterohepatic circulation, this fraction may exceed 1".
Is it just limitation of PK-sim?
Please share your opinions.... Thanks

answer.body_text:
Hi, Sorry for answering so late.
But I don't think your fraction absorbed of 2.11 is a limitation of PK-Sim. It just tells you that your compound molecules are absorbed more than once due to your EHC assumption. And it is quite physiological to assume that in case of enterohepatic cycling you have a reuptake of excreted compound. It might prompt you to think about your assumption. I am not sure if EHC has been found for Glipizide? Is your compound a substrate for bilary transporters?
You might think of other possible explanation for double peaks. E.g. check this out https://doi.org/10.1208/s12248-020-00548-8 and look at figure 3. Nefadozone-HCl. Pepin et al could rule out EHC and discuss two other possibilities coming from the absorption side:
A) Since both nefazodone-HCl solubility and permeability are high, this drug could be a marker of gastric emptying and partial gastric emptying has been reported for other products in the literature.
(B) Wettability issues and consequently slow dissolution rates are reported for nefazodone-HCl tablets, despite the high measured equilibrium solubility.
Hope that might help you.
Best, Tobias

title: Curious Trajectories in IntraCellular Mucosa
body_text:
Using the model provided in the Git library for Itraconazole, I plotted trajectories for drug amount in various mucosa intracellular compartments of the lower intestine. The trajectories seem rather strange, and I am trying to understand what's going on. Could anyone explain how such a precipitous drop occurs?
Ive attached a photo with plots of the lower intestine mucosa colon sigmoid, and the lower intestine mucosa colon descendens, but I believe all of the lower intestine mucosa intracellular compartments have similar trajectories. The corresponding plasma trajectories are also strange, I've attached a photo with those as well.
Thanks,
DS

answer.body_text:
This is the result of solubility limitation.
If you look at the plot below you will see that the passive absorption rate Lumen|ColonDescendes ==> Mucosa|ColonDescendes|Intracellular (dashed curve) rapidly decreases at ca. t=81h - which leads to the rapid decrease of the intracellular concentration in Mucosa|ColonDescendes|Intracellular at the same time point.
Passive absorption rate is proportional to the "Effective concentration in Lumen" - and if you look at it in the plot (dotted curve) - you will see again the rapid decrease - which is the reason for the change of passive absorption rate.

"Effective concentration in Lumen" in case of "no supersaturation" (which is the default setting - also for Itraconazole) is defined as
Min (Drug amount in lumen segment / Liquid volume in lumen segment; Solubility in lumen segment) - this is the solubility limitation.
Now if you plot the Effective concentration vs. Drug amount in lumen segment / Liquid volume in lumen segment: it looks like this

Here you can perfectly see how the solubility cut off leads to this bend in the effective concentration curve, finally leading to what you observed.
And if you disable the solubility limitation (by setting Enable supersaturation = YES for Itraconazole) - you will see smooth curves without this effect:
(CAUTION: for the formulations in the Itraconazole project it does not make any sense - it is just to demonstrate the effect)

title: PBPK Modelling - Neonates
body_text:
Dear community,
I have questions regarding the derived organ volumes (L) displayed in PKSim for reference subjects as also reflected in the source publication ICRP2002 and the GFR.
Assuming the organ and tissue masses (g) provided in the ICRP and assuming the density values to derive the corresponding values in L (1.3 g/cm3 for bone, 1 g/cm3 for muscle and 0.92 < 1 for fat/adipose tissue) the resulting organ values (L) to me appear not fully in line with what can be calculated from ICRP2002 (the given source). See also the attached screenshot.
For example, ICRP stated for a male adult reference subject the following organ masses: skeleton (10500 g), muscle (2900 g),  fat (14600 g) PKSim displays as organ volumes 11.82L for bone, 32.64L for muscle and 14.66L for fat (overall greater that expected having ICRP as source data).
How are the organ volumes derived in PKSim - in general and in particular for the very young subjects (density values might change)? I would be very thankful to get to know the assumptions used for these calculations and your help in this regard.
This also leads to my second question regarding GFR.
When letting the physiology being displayed  for a neonate reference subject in PKSim, GFR is stated to be 3.95 ml/min (with no further reference) and a specific GFR  (10.27 ml/min/100g organ) is in addition provided with source publication (Schlender et al, 2016) that however is more focussed to the elderly population. I am wondering whether maturation will be considered that way, when one would use this for simulations for the neonate population. How could this be achieved (considering allometry + maturation) in the PKSim framework?
Thank you for any reply, thoughts and discussion on these topics.

comments.body_text:
If you export a simulation to Mobi, you can see the density parameter that is used for each organ.
The derivation of the GFR (mL/min) in PK-Sim that includes both Maturation and Aging is posted here #791
Maturation of the GFR in neonates is assigned by the Rhodin et al. method.

Hi GabyWa,
Regarding tissue density, in OSP, irrespective of age, tissue density is standard set to 1 g/cm3 in all organ tissues. It can be found using the search function in MoBi using ‘density’ in the search term. The tissue density parameter is located in the spatial structures building block, and within this building block under ‘organism’ you can find all organs under which the tissue density parameter is stored.
It is assumed that tissue density is not really different than 1 g/cm3 irrespective of the organ tissue or age, that is used for e.g. the calculation of the total body weight (sum of all organs volumes * tissue density). (Creating Populations - Open Systems Pharmacology (open-systems-pharmacology.org))
The calculated weights of children that resulted from the simulations using OSP has been demonstrated to adequately describe of the pharmacokinetics of drugs from preterm newborns to adolescents. (https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34185905/) The software does include age dependent information on fraction fat and muscle volumes in the interstitial space, whereas for other organs it is age-independent, and is used for the simulation of newborn children. This has however not been used to calculated tissue densities. More detailed information on tissue fractions and organ weights was published in 2006 by Edginton et al. (https://link.springer.com/article/10.2165/00003088-200645100-00005).

title: MAC
body_text: why isn't the suite available for MAC???

comments.body_text:
The suite is implemented using .net technology. While the core can be migrated to another environment (which we did already as it is working on Linux), the user interface is only available on windows.
If you absolutely need to use a mac, you can easily used the suite by using a solution such as parallel, allowing you to install a Windows environment on a mac os. Once this is done, simply install the suite on your Windows instance.
@retepterhorst , we always take on sponsors to implement new features. If you want MAC, find someone with a budget and we will be happy to make it a reality.

Another option you might try: https://www.codeweavers.com/crossover
No windows license required. You could use the free trial to check if OSP Suite runs in this environment.
And if the OSP Suite is not running yet - you could submit a request to the developers: https://www.codeweavers.com/compatibility/submit

title: How to calculate the GMFE?
body_text:
Hi everyone,
Is there a function for calculating the GMFE and/or plotting the GOFMergedPlots embedded in PKsim/Mobi? If there is any other parameters to evaluate/compare the fitness between different models?
Many thanks!

answer.body_text:
At present, there is no automated way to do this. I use 2 manual methods, depending on the amount of data that I am working with.
A. Export the simulation results to .csv and manually calculate predicted/observed.
B. Run a parameter identification with all the desired simulations for evaluation. Set 0 identification runs, linear scaling on the datasets and linear scaling on the parameters (very important!). Then you can export the time profile analysis and the residual analysis to get the data that you need for GMFE on a larger scale.
When doing this, you must be extremely careful that the units of the concentration are all the same for the observed datasets and the simulations.

title: Calculation of the total error in parameter estimation
body_text:
Dear all,
I am currently trying to reproduce the total error that is reported after the parameter estimation. For testing, I am using measurements of two compounds at the same time point in blood. I use linear scaling and corresponding weights. If I run the estimation using only compound 1 or 2, I get the expected errors of 0.62 and 0.48. If I use both measurements in the same run, the total error is reported as 0.72.
So I am wondering how the individual errors are summed up to the reported total error.
Thank you in advance!
Best,
Bastian

answer.body_text:
Total error is the square root of squared weighted residuals, where each residual is either simulated-observed or log10(simulated)-log10(observed) (depending on the scaling settings - s. Mapping Simulation Outputs to Observed Data‌).
Weights (which are all = 1 as per default) can be set per observed data set and per observed data point as described in Mapping Simulation Outputs to Observed Data‌
For the small values below LLOQ residual calculation is done depending on the LLOQ handling settings as described in Handling of LLOQ values‌

Biovailability can be controlled/defined with/by multiple properties. limited solubility, limited intestinal permeability (or active efflux), intestinal metabolism (to name the major ones). Thanks and appreciate your inputs.

I am trying to do something similar. I am trying to create an observer that will sum up two metabolites in the whole body. I created an observer and am trying to use a sum formula (just like in the Total fraction of dose observer). I set the molecule to the metabolites of interest, set the match tag condition to MOLECULE and left the sum formula as it is by default (since I just want to add up the amounts). This gives me the total amount of metabolite for each of them indvidually, but how do I get it to add two molecules together into the same value? I tried adding a reference to the other metabolite but MoBi cannot resolve the path.

There are several ways to do this. One possibility: use the new OR operator for container criteria in MoBi 11.
In the attached project, I have a compound C1 and 3 metabolites.
P1.zip
I defined compound specific parameter Total amount metabolized which sums up all 3 metabolites:

In a simulation, this will be expanded to:


Then I defined the total fraction metabolized as Sum_of_all_metabolites_amounts / Total_drugmass:

(here I defined it as a global compound parameter, which I then set plottable. Alternatively, I could define an observer instead).

Another way to do this (and that's what Michael mentioned above) is in this project: P2.zip
Here you sum up only the first metabolite via conditions, and in the formula you add all other metabolites.
The important point is adding _#i to all other metabolites (otherwise MoBi tries to locate them not relative to the first metabolite but relative to the parameter (or in you case: to the observer), which cannot work.
Please also check the explanation of how sum formulas are working (s. screenshot)

In a simulation, this will be expanded to:
Same formula as in the 1st approach, just written differently:


=================================================================================
In general, the first approach is more flexible. If you have a container where e.g. the first metabolite exists, but the 2nd metabolite does not - the sum formula from the 2nd approach would crash, because it explicitly looks for all metabolites in every container where the 1st metabolite is present
Such a situation is never the case for models exported from PK-Sim, but theoretically it could happen if you extend your model in MoBi.

title: Definition of intraperitoneal injection
body_text:
Dear team,
If I want to use an intraperitoneal injection, which organ tissue do I need to select in User defined? Is it just a matter of choosing the part that defines the intraperitoneal injection?

answer.body_text:
Dear @Carrie-nothing ,
An IP Administration is injected into the abdominal cavity. The capillaries from there drain into the portal vein.
With the IP route one can exclude strong delays in absorption processes and reduced bioavailability like after PO, but still have an first pass effect from the liver.
Thus I would choose the portal vein as input compartment and an formulation that allows to model potential small absorption delays.
Maybe we get some more comments or recommendation from the community.
Hope this helps
Henrik

comments.body_text:
Dear @Carrie-nothing ,
An IP Administration is injected into the abdominal cavity. The capillaries from there drain into the portal vein.
With the IP route one can exclude strong delays in absorption processes and reduced bioavailability like after PO, but still have an first pass effect from the liver.

title: Published: A workflow to build PBTK models for novel species
body_text:
A workflow to build PBTK models for novel species
Open-Systems-Pharmacology/OSP-based-publications-and-content#266
Physiology-based pharmacokinetic and toxicokinetic (PBPK/TK) models allow us to simulate the concentration of xenobiotica in the plasma and different tissues of an organism. PBPK/TK models are therefore routinely used in many fields of life sciences to simulate the physiological concentration of exogenous compounds in plasma and tissues. The application of PBTK models in ecotoxicology, however, is currently hampered by the limited availability of models for focal species. Here, we present a best practice workflow that describes how to build PBTK models for novel species. To this end, we extrapolated eight previously established rabbit models for several drugs to six additional mammalian species (human, beagle, rat, monkey, mouse, and minipig). We used established PBTK models for these species to account for the species-specific physiology. The parameter sensitivity in the resulting 56 PBTK models was systematically assessed to rank the relevance of the parameters on overall model performance. Interestingly, more than 80% of the 609 considered model parameters showed a negligible sensitivity throughout all models. Only approximately 5% of all parameters had a high sensitivity in at least one of the PBTK models. This approach allowed us to rank the relevance of the various parameters on overall model performance. We used this information to formulate a best practice guideline for the efficient development of PBTK models for novel animal species. We believe that the workflow proposed in this study will significantly support the development of PBTK models for new animal species in the future.

title: PK-Sim: Gastric pH and meal
body_text:
Hi,
I have a couple of PK-Sim suggestions related to the gastric pH and meal event

Include Lumen│Stomach│pH as a selectable simulation output (observer) in PK-Sim. This to enable visualization of the dynamics without needing to import an observer for this.
Add the display-and-adjust possibility to the parameter (Meal_pH_exponent) that determine the pH dynamics after a meal. Together with the observer (1) this would allow to adjust the pH dynamics according to the nature of simulated meal as seem most appropriate.
Change the current default value of Meal_pH_exponent so to mimic a more realistic time to return to a gastric pH<2.5.
The current setting can have rather large implications for fed state simulations of poorly soluble bases/acids when applying the particle dissolution function.
-The current default value of 1 --> 2 min
-An alternative lower value of 0.01 --> 3.25 h

I look forward to hearing your thoughts on this.
Many thanks for all great work and efforts.
/Erik

body_text:
Hello PK-Sim community,
I was trying to to show the effect of change of a parameter on the output result in the concentration-time profile curve. I wanted to overlay the results to show the changes in a single graph. How can I be able to do that? Is there any way to include multiple curves generated my changing a single parameter on a single graph?

comments.body_text:
Hi Gautam,
For the following examples, I used the midazolam template and changed the logP from ~3 to 0.
If you are simulating and individual, just click on the top in Compare results Individual Simulations

Then just drag and drop the simulations you want to compare


You can do the same with populations.
When you have one of the Populations you would like to compare, click on Compare Results

Then select the populations you wish to compare and assign them colours and a Reference one, if you have it

Then if you would like to show some area, you can click on Range 5% - 95%

And that's it
