title: How to make  the goodness of fit plot
body_text:
Deear all,
I plan to make  the goodness of fit plot,so I wonder if I should use individual or population models?

answer.body_text:
At present, goodness of fit plots can only be made with individual simulations using the parameter identification tool.
However, you can export the results of population simulations to .csv files and make the graphs on your preferred choice of graphing software without any problems.

comments.body_text:
At present, goodness of fit plots can only be made with individual simulations using the parameter identification tool.
However, you can export the results of population simulations to .csv files and make the graphs on your preferred choice of graphing software without any problems.

There's another workaround that has been suggested some time ago by @PavelBal (I can't find the issue entry anymore):
Just set up a parameter identification with your simulation(s) and all observed data sets, set the maximum number of iterations in the configuration window to zero and click on run. Then you can select GOF plots, residual plots etc...
^^ Yes this is an example of using individual simulations in the parameter identification tool to get a GOF plot. But this does not work with population simulations.

title: Role and localization of P-gp in lungs
body_text:
Hi,
I am trying to model an intranasal administration in PK-sim, I used different absorption routes for the dose. For around 20% of the dose I used "User defined administration type" with lung as target organ and blood cells as  target compartment. My compound is a P-gp substrate and I have seen P-gp is expressed in lungs in PK-sim.
My question is, does P-gp influence the entry of my compound in the systemic circulation using this particular route ? and if yes, how ?
Thanks in advance
Best,
Nathalie

comments.body_text:
Hi Nathalie
Proteins, enzymes, transporters etc. are only functional in PKSim when you have added them into your 'Individual' building block and parameterized a relevant ADME process in your 'Compound' building block. So unless you have added P-gp, it will not be affecting your compound in the model.
By default, passive movement of the drug between the blood cells and the plasma is determined by the 'Blood-to-Plasma' ratio, the permeability of the drug across the blood cell membrane and the surface area of the blood cells.
Paul

Hi Paul,
Thanks for your answer. I added P-gp in my 'Individual' and I added in my compound a Vmax and Km for P-gp.
As I used P-gp as an efflux transporter, the compound should be transporter from intracellular space to interstitial space or lumen. So in my case I am not sure of the impact or the no-impact.
I made DDI prediction with a P-gp inductor and the effect is an Cmax ratio > 1 (I don't understand why because my dose is split in oral dose, infusion dose and user defined with lung. So for the oral dose I think the Cmax ratio should be lower than 1, there is no impact on the infusion, so I am wondering if my user defined administration could be responsible for this Cmax ratio > 1 ?).
Thanks
Nathalie

PK-Sim does not allow transporters in blood cells (for now). So there will be no effect on the movement of the drug from blood cell space to plasma space.
I recommend using the "P-gp like" transporter type, rather than the "Efflux" transporter type for P-gp. Changing this may solve your problem.
As you search for the source of this phenomena, be aware that P-gp is present in many organs in the PK-Sim model and drives efflux in key areas, such as into the intestinal lumen, into the bile and into the urine. Inducing P-gp usually lowers absorption and increases clearance.
Thanks Paul !

title: How to  convert transporters  parameters from literature?
body_text: Dear to all, I found such a problem in the forum,  and it is also confuses me at present, how to  convert transporters  parameters from literature? MW of hOCT2 is 62.59 kDa, 1 mg protein = 1(mg)/62590*10e9=15.98 pmol protein, so Vmax 11.9 nmol/min/mg = 7.4E-4 nmol/min/pmol. Is this right ?

answer.body_text:
See issue #343
The trouble with transporter studies is that they frequently use HEK or MDCK cells that are artificially transfected with certain transporters. So, at present we have no idea how many transporters (e.g. OCT) are present per cell - nor can we be confident that the cells in one experiment have the same transporter expression as the cells in another experiment.
In a typical transporter experiment, drug is incubated with the cells expressing the transporter, and then we check how much of the drug has moved to the intracellular space after a certain amount of time has elapsed. The cells are isolated and lysed (broken apart). The unit: nmol/min/mg protein is measured after the cells are lysed. The amount of drug in the resulting fluid is taken, and then plotted against the total amount of protein in the lysed fluid. This "mg protein" reflects all the protein in the cell. "min" reflects the time of incubation, usually.
Translating this to a PBPK model therefore requires three pieces of information:

What is the concentration of the transporter per L of transfected HEK or MDCK cells?
What is the concentration of the transporter per L of human renal epithelium?
What proportion of the kidney is made up of human renal epithelium?

Basit et al. 2019 have made some progress toward quantifying transporter expression across the whole kidney volume. doi: 10.1124/dmd.119.086579.
p.s. An accepted standard is that there is 200mg protein per mL cells, by the way.

per liter? I was under the impression it should be around 200-300 mg per mL?
But in general I really don't understand, why (most of the time) these publications don't characterize their systems by providing all of these relevant (reference) information ;-)
That's right! Good catch

@prvmalik,your reply is helpful for me!
In cell experiments, the quality of transporters can be measured by the corresponding test kits of transporters. I want to know that when the Vmax is obtained from vitro experiment, it is converted to the units in the software,  is the above calculation correct?

@Wenjing1018
Unfortunately the calculation won't work. The Vmax of 11.9 nmol/min/mg protein is per mg protein in the lysate solution. This includes all protein in the cells - not just the protein associated with the transporter.

@prvmalik
The test kit can measure the mass of a single transporter. Why  consider the mass of a total protein rather than a transporter?

title: Kinetic constants of an enzyme in reversible drug metabolism
body_text: I am trying to include a reversible drug metabolite reaction in PKsim (A ⇌ B). I have Vmax and Km value of enzyme for the forward reaction and only Km value for backward reactionwhich I obtained from literature. How can I optimized the desired Vmax value of the same enzyme in reversible reaction?

answer.body_text:
@shapoo1, you simply add another saturating (michaelis menten) metabolizing enzyme reaction for the metabolite with the parent as its metabolite.
You can then fit the Vmax on observed PK data.
You might need to consider some kind of interaction between parent and metabolite on the metabolizing enzyme
best,
Stephan

You can then fit the Vmax on observed PK data.

best,
Stephan

title: Do we have to account for a time-shift after fed dosing?
body_text:
Dear Community,
please share your opinion for the following problem:
when we simulating fed clinical studies, we can add a food event prior to or simultaneous with drug dosing. In study protocols  you typically find statements like:
"a breakfast was served 30 min before drug administration". In PK-sim you cannot add a food event with negative times. So I wonder if we should design the simulation either by include a food event at t=0 and the drug administration at t=30 ( = S1). In that case to compare the simulation to measured data, the time axis of the data has to be transformed accordingly.
Or would it be enough to input both events at t=0 ( = S2) use the data as is and ignore a time difference between food intake and drug dosing.
To check if there could be some deviation expected, I simulated a typical BCS Class2 compound with the scenarios S1 and S2. To directly compare S1 with S2 I esported the simulation results, shifted the time 30 min ahead and reimported the result for a direct overlay.
The results are as expected slightly different.

The differences are somewhat visible but small if looking at the deviation especially for t-max and Cmax:



 
S1 Tabled-fed_dosing at t=30
S2 Tabled-fed_dosing and food   event at t=0
S2 with time-shift

However, I am wondering if the difference justifies the changed simulation design and the extra effort to modify the measured data? Or can it just be ignored? As there is to much interindividual variability in measured fed data anyway?
Please shre your thoughts on this
Best,
Tobias

answer.body_text:
@tobiasK2001
My thoughts would be that the characterization (and exact knowledge) of food composition and the exact way it is then consumed is too uncertain that the exact time of the start of food consumption matters in your case example.
This might be different if you have explicit food effects (as we often have in e.g. diabetes where food uptake is then related to nutrient (e.g. glucose) appearance in the system).
So it is really a case-by-case decision depending on the relevance of (i.e. sensitivity of your scenario towards) food effects.

title: What's the mechanism behind the current modeling of food effect?
body_text: Hi, everyone. I'd like to ask for some advice on food effect.  For example, we already know that food intake does not affect absorption and bioavailability of clarithromycin. But when using clarithromycin model from the OSP templates, simultaneous food intake significantly reduce its plasma concentration (under multiple doses). This introduces inaccuracy in some simulation scenarios such as DDI, if a victim drug should be administered with food.

comments.body_text:
Can you show a graph of the model mismatch to data?
Absorption may be delayed due to an increased gastric emptying time. This does not necessarily reduce AUC but possibly Cmax and extend Tmax.
What are you expecting and how is the model not delivering?
Thanks for clarifying,
Stephan
Besides, I don't figure out why food intake has an effect even 20 hours later. Please see the example of efavirenz (also from template) below.

Most template inducers/inhibitors are administered under the fasted state. We are attempting to provide DDI predictions for a new drug candidate. While this drug should be co-ministered with food, I'm worried that current template models may have predictive bias with food effects.

title: Particle dissolution model in Spatial Structure instead of Events?
body_text: I want to recreate the particle dissolution model for a custom MoBi model but have the solid particles contained within a single compartment. Since the solid particles are staying in a single compartment, is there any benefit to having the particle dissolution model within the Events building block? Or is it better to recreate the particle dissolution "module" within the Spatial Structure? My understanding is that the main benefit of having the original particle dissolution model within the Events building block is you can track the transit of solid particles through the intestinal tract, but that is not needed for my model. Does anyone have any thoughts on this?

answer.body_text:
The main reason is in fact the Building Block principle: something like drug amount, particles radius distribution, number of particles etc. are properties of formulation/administration, not of a spatial structure.
In MoBi formulation/administration properties are stored in Events building block (which is in fact Events and Applications building block). Actually the Events building Block in MoBi is implemented not in the optimal way, e.g. separating formulations into their own Building block (like in PK-Sim) would be much better.
Technically you could place the dissolution into the spatial structure (e.g. by the definiton of a "solid drug"-subcontainer for each administration and each particle size). The effort would be even higher in this case (e.g. you need to define the neighborhoods between those subcontainers and your dissolution compartment).
And if you would use this spatial structure with another dosage/formulation form: it would contain many unused containers/neighborhoods.
But let me ask you the other way around: what are the disatvantages/problems of defining the particles dissolution in a single compartment via the Events/Applications Building Block?

title: Adjusting solubility for morning vs evening doses over course of multiple dose regimen
body_text:
I have a drug with a bioavailability that is highly influenced by the food effect. I am trying to simulate a multiple dose study in humans where the drug was administered as a single dose in the morning in a fasted state and then another dose was given in the evening but in a fed state. This bid dosing was repeated for another 6 days.
The food effect appears to greatly affect solubility for this drug. To capture this, I am wondering if there is a way to adjust solubility for the morning vs evening over the course of the multiple dosing regimen in PK-Sim?

comments.body_text:
Hi @choitingy, do you have a solubility-limited absorption?
You might also play with the "volume" you administer with your oral application and food, which will define the liquid volume in which the drug dissolves. If you reduce or increase it, less/more of the drug will get dissolved?
Otherwise, a solution to switch solubility in between doses could be done in MoBi, but might need some expert MoBi knowledge.

Hi Cindy,
if you have only first order processes, you could do the following if you want to stay in PK-Sim for model development: Clone your compound and just adjust the solubility. Then you apply both compounds for fasted and fet state. Further you have to define an observer summing up the concentrations of both compounds in MoBi. This observer you can import into PK-Sim.
Since I'm not aware of a general soultion, I just made a feature request:
Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#1885 (comment)

Hi @StephanSchaller and @Christoph27! Thanks for both of your help.
Indeed, it is solubility-limited absorption. I ended up using Christoph's suggestion since it was best fit for my case - this dataset was used for evaluation and I wanted to cleanly use a previously optimized solubility value for the fed state. The only caveat was that by using this method I had to apply the meal event to both fasted and fed versions of the molecule in the simulation. However, this doesn't appear to make that big of a difference so overall it works well. Thanks for the help and neat to see a feature request resulting from it!

title: Error in a model in MoBi
body_text:
Hello,
We are working in a PBPK model for a drug which causes renal vasodilatation and it has a renal elimination. We are trying to optimize our model in MoBi considering the effect of plasma concentration of the drug on its renal excretion by the change in the renal blood flow rate.
We found an equation that estimates the Specific blood flow rate as a function to our drug's concentration. So in order to implement it in our model, we change the Parameter Specific blood flow rate from Formula type: Constant to Formula type: Formula (an explicit formula) and we wrote the equation linking the specific blood flow rate to the total drug concentration.
We previously added the total venous concentration of the drug as a new parameter to use it as part of our equation that estimates the Specific blood flow rate, and we did it in the following way:

‘add parameter’ of type: Global, Dimension: Concentration, Group: Mobi, Formula type: Formula (an explicit formula)
Then right click in the white space prepared for adding equation parameters and ‘add reference’ and there included the total venous blood concentration using the path: Organism|VenousBlood|Plasma|MOLECULE|Concentration

(we follow the step by step of discussion #907)
Then, when we update our simulation with the new version of our spatial structure but the following error appears:

We don't know what it means or how to fix this error. If anyone can help us, we will appreciate it very much!
Thank you in advance
María Luisa

comments.body_text:
@MaLuisaEti I cannot read the message. Can you upload a higher resolution?
Also maybe upload the mobi file if possible (or a modified version/simplified) where we can reproduce the problem.

Hi @MaLuisaEti , you would have to define this parameter Cfuro in a Molecule properties Container. E.g.: ..|Kidney|Plasma|Molecule Properties:

Otherwise the keyword MOLECULE cannot get assigned.
If it is a global parameter, you can also define it at the root level:

(Don't forget to adapt the path to the parameter wherever it is used)
Let me know if this works!

title: How to simulate intermittent dialysis?
body_text:
I see the discussion for simulating renal impairment:
How to simulate renal impair #479
However, how does one simulate intermittent hemodialysis in PK-Sim/MoBi?

comments.body_text:
You might use the same approach as this publication on adding external life support to a PK-Sim model :
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC6202466/
Basically, you model the "external process" as a compartment/organ in MoBi with fluxes coming in and going out.
Additional processes (e.g. filtration) might then be added in this "organ".
Hope this helps,
Stephan

I did suspected that I will need to add the external process and was worried that I might struggle with it.
I am glad the paper shared the MoBi file, and I will try to see if I can make it work for dialysis instead of pulmonary ECMO.
One part of my question is that I will have to figure out how to make the compartment available intermittently.
I hypothesized that I can use time in the equation, so that I can code the timing of dialysis in the new container?
I am guessing MoBi uses C#, am I correct? I am planning to use remainder (it seems like the operator is %) and nested if else statement to determine the timing of dialysis in the container.

MoBi is GUI-based.
It allows a number of math expressions (see here).
There you could use a time-dependent formula (directly in the flow formula or with an Event) to switch flow on or off, depending on when dialysis is connected (similar to an administration protocol).
Hope this helps,
Stephan

The list of formula does not have a function for calculating the remainder in the list. Does that mean that it is not capable of calculating the remainder?
The reason I want to calculate the remainder is that dialysis is usually on a week based schedule, e.g. MWF. I was thinking to get the remainder from dividing the time by 168 h to get 24-28 h, 72-76 h, and 120-124 h.
But you mentioned the word event. It seems like it may be a better approach.
For me, tissue distribution rather than the clearance is the focus. Would it be reasonable to "clamp" the renal and metabolism processes to reduce clearance at all times except the dialysis event?

Here's my progress so far:
1, Added a Dialysis container similar to the ECMO example, with blood cells, plasma, and dialysate waste compartments
2. Added passive transport processes which is zero when dialysis is not active
3. Created dialysis events similar to the gallbladder processes
I got it to work. Will share the details later.

title: how to interpret the IVIVE  for the midazolam model??
body_text:
Hello,
Now I'm replicating the midazolam PBPK model using IV bolus data from healthy individuals, and I have couple of questions regarding the model:
Regarding the IVIVE interpretation of the values implemented in the PK-Sim, like the metabolism by CYP3A4:

In vitro Vmax = 850 pmol/min/mg microsomal protein
Content of CYP protein in liver microsomes = 108 pmol/mg microsomal protein --> E
Km = 4 umol/L --> obtained from In vitro  literature
Kcat= 8.761 1/min --> obtained by parameter identification using PK clinical data

1st Step:


Vmax = Kcat*E = 946.2 pmol/min/mg microsomal protein


CLint  = Vmax/Km = 946.2 *10 ^-6 (umol/min/mg) / 4 umol/L = 0.000237 L/min/mg microsomal protein


CLint = CLint * MPPGL *average Liver weight = 0.000237 * 40 * 1500 = 14.2 L/min


where:
MPPGL is the amount of microsomal protein per gram of liver = 40 mg microsomal protein/gm liver --> implemented in PK-Sim
Assumed  average Liver weight= 1500 gm
2nd step, if I assumed a well-stirred model for liver (assumed):
fraction unbound in plasma= 0.031
Assuming hepatic blood flow rate = 1.4 L/min

Hepatic clearance = Q* fu * CLint / Q + (fu * CLint) = 1.4 * 0.031 * 14.2 / 1.4 + (0.031 * 14.2) = 0.335 * 60 = 20.12 L/hr

So this value is hepatic clearance of midazolam due to the metabolism by CYP3A4, right or I'm missing something??
Thanks in advance,
Aya

title: Scaling Hepatocyte Clearance (Under-prediction & Offset Method)
body_text:
Hi all,
It is generally acknowledged in the literature that in vitro hepatocyte metabolic stability assays under-predict in vivo hepatic clearance. To compensate for this under-prediction, ADME teams (including those at Merck) will apply an empirical offset correction as part of their best-practices for in-vitro/in-vivo extrapolation. See below for two references that explain this method.
I am curious if PKSim applies some similar correction method to account for underprediction when using in vitro hepatocyte data.
Or is this concern not relevant to the PKSim approach for some reason?
Yamagata, T., Zanelli, U., Gallemann, D., Perrin, D., Dolgos, H., & Petersson, C. (2017). Comparison of methods for the prediction of human clearance from hepatocyte intrinsic clearance for a set of reference compounds and an external evaluation set. Xenobiotica, 47(9), 741–751. https://doi.org/10.1080/00498254.2016.1222639
Sohlenius-Sternbeck, A. K., Jones, C., Ferguson, D., Middleton, B. J., Projean, D., Floby, E., Bylund, J., & Afzelius, L. (2012). Practical use of the regression offset approach for the prediction of in vivo intrinsic clearance from hepatocytes. Xenobiotica, 42(9), 841–853. https://doi.org/10.3109/00498254.2012.669080

comments.body_text:
HI @paconius,
Thanks for your comment.
Indeed, applying an empirical offset correction for in-vitro/in-vivo extrapolation is standard practice, and the same holds for PK-Sim.
But this correction can vary between essays and labs, making it hard to provide a "global" value.
Thus PK-Sim does not provide such a value.
It is upon the user to "calibrate" a value (or take one from literature) based on the assays and lab procedures of the vendors which were contracted to get such data.
I hope this helps,
Stephan

title: Weight-based Dose Brackets in PK-Sim
body_text:
Hi All,
I am working on recreating a clinical study simulation for model validation purposes and the study utilized an weight-based dose bracketing approach; e.g. >60kg = 100mg, 45-60 kg = 75mg, 35-44 kg= 50 mg, etc.
Is there a way to create this type of dosing schema for a PK-Sim population simulation?
Thank you!
Brian

comments.body_text:
Hi @Bcicali
I don't think there is an easy way to do this in PK-Sim....
But you can easily do this in R using ospsuite-r by creating a user-defined variability for the Dose parameter of your application

As @msevestre said, there is no easy way do to it in PK-Sim (would be a nice feature I think).
One possibility would be to create an individual simulation in PK-Sim with arbitrary dose, export it to MoBi and set Bodyweight-range-based dose in MoBi; then import this simulation in PK-Sim and combine it with your population.
For this:

Create an individual simulation in PK-Sim with the dose e.g. 1mg/kg and send this simulation to MoBi (after that, delete this simulation in PK-Sim)
In MoBi: open the Events building block, go to the first application and go to the parameters of the ProtocolSchemaItem container

Add new dosing parameters for each weight range (e.g. Dose_60, Dose_45, etc.):


Then define the Dose formula like below using the newly introduced parameters (make sure you modify the Dose parameter and NOT DosePerBodyWeight etc.)


The notation Condition ? X : Y means: if Condition applies: calculate X; otherwise calculate Y
Thus the formula above is a nested conditional statement:

     IF BodyWeight > 60
       Dose = ...
     ELSE IF BodyWeight >= 45
       Dose = ...
     ELSE IF BodyWeight >=35
     ...


Activate the "Advanced parameter" checkbox for parameters Dose, DosePerBodyWeight and DosePerBodySurfaceArea

Update the simulation from the changed events building block

Save simulation as pkml
Go back to PK-Sim, go to Import/Export and select Import population simulation

Select the pkml simulation exported from MoBi and combine it with your population of interest

Now you can use you weight-range-based dosing in PK-Sim.


Attached are example projects in PK-Sim and MoBi using this approach
Example.zip

Another possibility: use the R package ospsuite.reportingengine and use the "Study Design" feature.
For that, you would need to create a "Study Design" file in the CSV format - which allows arbitrary conditional extensions of a population (e.g. weight-range-based dosage)

For each application you need to add a target column with the Dose of the corresponding application, e.g. for the example from the previous post it would be just 1 target column Applications|App1|Application_1|ProtocolSchemaItem|Dose
Then:

export your population from PK-Sim as csv
start R and

load the exported population via loadPopulation
extend your population with created study design via addStudyParameters
save the modified population to CSV


import the modified population from CSV in PK-Sim

Thank you very much for this method @Yuri05 . I face some problems when I try to install the ospsuite.reportengine.
After installing the required packages and downloading the ospsuite.reportengine, I can't find it in the library (error).
With which version did you run this package? I work with R 4.1.2
Thank you,
Yannick

title: What are your approaches for modeling oral absorption with late Tmax?
body_text:
Delayed Tmax is something PBPK modeling has a problem with. It could be because the compound has poor solubility, is a substrate for a unknown intestinal transporter, is subject to intestinal wall metabolism, and a variety of other reasons. I typically trial a series of approaches when faced with this scenario and I can't say one works reliably - sometimes none of them work! But I think there are others out there. I thought we could share our methods for tackling delayed Tmax. I often work in this order:

Try an empirical weibull function dissolution profile. I optimize spec intestinal permeability, 50% diss time +/- lag time. I find that diss shape is not uniquely identifiable when optimized together with spec intestinal permeability. If I can use a Caco-2 permeability as a fixed parameter, then I can fix spec intestinal permeability and optimize 50% diss time, diss shape +/- lag time. Of course, run optimizations at least x6 from randomized start values to confirm identifiability
Optimizing the surface area enhancement factors (x5) for the small intestine mucosal compartments
If there is evidence of an intestinal transporter or segment-dependent absorption, add an apical influx transporter and basolateral efflux transporter on the proposed segments (e.g. upper and lower ileum). I assume that the influx transporter is rate limiting to absorption and set basolat transport to be fast (Km = 5000 uM and Vmax = 5000 umol/min/L or whatever the units are). Set drug to be dissolved, optimize spec intestinal permeability, influx transporter Vmax + Km if there is dose-dependent absorption or set Km = 5000 uM to have linear absorption and optimize Vmax.
Optimize solubility in intestinal segments, last resort, rarely works or is relevant.

Let me know if you have good/reliable approaches for handling this, that maintain some sort of physiologic relevance. It's the biggest challenge in PBPK modeling for me at the moment!

comments.body_text:
Dear Paul,
thanks for raising this interesting point. In general I can agree with your workflow. Depending on the compounds I might have some different experience. In my cases the delayed Tmax seems to come most often due to solubility issues (supersaturation, reprecipitation and redissolution. e.g for typical lipophilic BCS class 2 compounds with basic PKA).  I don#t have so much experience with cases were I could assume high intestinal gut wall metabolism or unknown intestinal transportes.
I used for example this workflow:

set disposition with iv data from absolute F study (if available)
optimize spec. intestinal permeability with po data from solution formulation (if available) or use Caco-2 permeability fixed if it describes in vivo data
Try empirical weibull function dissolution profile were 50% diss time is fitted per each dose level and shape is fitted over all doses.
Depending on the curve shape / Tmax fit enhance solubility in intestinal segment.

I also tried a more mechanistic approaches with particle dissolution, but that was not always successful and for some compounds I had the feeling the OSP-suite is a bit limited. E.g. I had a compound with a similar profile like aprepitant describe by Pepin et 2021 with a "bump" around 24 hours (Fig.6). I thought I could use a somewhat similar approach like the authors describe in the supplement. I tried to split the dose in a "liquid" fast absorption part and a "colonic absorbed part" with using a "Advanced protocol" were 50% of the dose is administered oral and the other 50% is administered in the large intestinal lumen (as particle or dissolution function). That should kind of mimic the absorption coming from redissolution of precipitated parts of the drug.  But technically it seems not to be possible to create this kind of advanced protocols. Maybe there's some way and I could not find it. But in the end I could at least empirically describe the data with an empirical weibull function and assuming enhanced colonic solubility.
Generally it seems that the dissolution, precipitation and redissolutions processes in the intestine are quite complex and it is  difficult to explain luminal absorption mechanistically even for compounds with wealth of data (e.g. Itraconazole as described by very interesting review of [ Boyed et al 2019]).
So I guess it will still remain a huge challenge.
Hope that might helps nevertheless.
Best, Tobias

Hi all, thanks for the very interesting Discussion.
Yes, a multi-phase release could be modeled with the table formula release or hopefully the double-Weibull in the future (a current feature request: Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim#1701). A phenomenon like the double-peak absorption which is sometimes observed could also be empirically described by this (although the phenomenon might be due to a phasic gastric emptying or efflux transporter expression along the gut wall pushing absorption to a lower gut segment).
Late 24h peaks could also be due to EHC involving conjugation (liver --> bile) and deconjugation (large intestine) followed by re-absorption.
Further factors to influence Tmax are of course the gastric emptying and gut transit times which might be affected by some drugs.
Cheers,
Stephan

Dear all,
Thanks for raising the point of the late tmax modelling, very interesting points from all of you.
If we consider a highly lipophilic basic compound, a non-specific gut binding could be taking place (e.g. lysosomal trapping).
How can this add be added to the model?
Could it be by adding a protein expressed in the gut wall and optimise the kd and kdiss to match the tmax?
Thanks,
Ricardo

title: Drug-biomarker interaction PBPK models of creatinine and N1-methylnicotinamide
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have been working on PBPK models of the endogenous compounds creatinine and N1-methylnicotinamide (NMN), both substrates of OCT2 and MATE1 and proposed as biomarkers for investigations on renal transporter-mediated interactions. The models were evaluated for drug-biomarker interaction (DBI) predictions within an interaction network including the OCT2 and MATE1 perpetrators trimethoprim, pyrimethamine and cimetidine.
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Clinical Pharmacology & Therapeutics and the biomarker and DBI MoBi files can be found here.
Related publication:
Türk D, Müller F, Fromm MF, Selzer D, Dallmann R, Lehr T. Renal transporter-mediated drug-biomarker interactions of the endogenous substrates creatinine and N1 -methylnicotinamide: a PBPK modeling approach. Clinical Pharmacology & Therapeutics. 2022. doi: 10.1002/cpt.2636. Online ahead of print.
Best regards,
Denise

title: Help with validating new model structure/set-up after addition of new organ compartment?
body_text:
Hello all,
Using MoBi, I modified the original PBPK model structure to include a new "Lymph Node" organ. This compartment represents all lymph node structures within the body, and collects lymphatic fluid through new neighborhoods connecting each organ's interstitial to the lymph node interstitial. The lymph is then returned to the blood stream by connection of the lymph node interstitial to the venous blood plasma.
If possible, would someone be able to take a look at the model structure and verify that I've set everything up correctly?
TFV_LN_NHP zip.zip
Happy to answer any other questions/provide details.
(I utilized the forum discussions github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/MoBi/issues/1 and github.com//issues/302 to set up the new compartment).

comments.body_text:
Hi @eburg, I think this looks almost right.
I believe you might have an (accidental) error in the total lymph flow calculation. While the sum formula is correct, it will also add the "Lymph flow rate" from the Lymph not which is again defined as an explicit sum of all organ lymph flows:

So the transporter referencing the sum formula of "total lymph flow" in the neighborhood effectively calculates 2x total lymph flow for the lymph flow "lymph node to plasma".
You do not have to set the lymph flow of the lymph node organ to the explicit sum of all lymph flows as this parameter is not used for the transporter. Just set the lymph node lymph flow to a constant value (if you have a value from literature) reflecting the actual lymph fluid flow from lymph node plasma to lymph node intestitial (as in all other organs).
Then your model should be correct.
Hope this helps,
Stephan

title: How to simulate the effect of food on drugs?
body_text:
Dear to all,
I intend to simulate the effect of food on the pharmacokinetics of a drug, which may require modification of certain physiological and anatomical parameters, could you provide some literatures for me? Can this process be achieved in the PK-SIM?
Sincerely！

answer.body_text:
With the start time you can define at which time point in your simulation the event starts.
I would recommend to simulate the time differences with regards to your administration protocol. If you use an advanced protocol, you can define start times of your drug intake.
For your examples:
i: Start time event and start time administration both at the same time ( i.e. 0 h)
ii: Start time event at 0 h and start time administration at 0.33 h
iii: Start time administration at 0 h and start time event at 0.33 h
one advice: Keep in mind to time your observed data accordingly, if you change the start of your administration.
I hope this helps!

comments.body_text:
Yes, this is possible. Please refer to the documentation:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-events#definition-of-new-events-in-pk-sim-r

@StephanSchaller，thank you for your reply.
I wonder if the parameters caused by food will change accordingly after adding meals to the event? Or do I need to manually modify the physiological and anatomical parameters?

Which parameters are you referring to?
For the meal, a few parameters such as gastric emptying time are changed and return back to normal after some time:
"For the various meals provided, the pH in the stomach and the gastric emptying function will change significantly. Irrespective of the meal chosen, the gastric pH will be increased to 5.5 and then decay exponentially. The rate of gastric emptying, which controls the transport of the drug to the absorption sites in the intestine, will change according to a function that is based on the Weibull equation. The Weibull function was parameterized based on about 100 datasets for gastric emptying profiles in humans following ingestion of various meals [81]."

@StephanSchaller，
the changes in parameters brought by food, in addition to changes in gastric PH、 gastric emptying time, may also lead to changes in parameters such as stomach volume, duodenum PH、 liver blood flow and so on. I wonder if these parameters need to be obtained from the literature?If so, could you provide some literature that involved in change of parameters?
Thank you very much！
Sincerely!

In addition to changes in gastric pH and emptying time, stomach lumen volume also increases (the fluid volume of the meal is added).
Other changes e.g. in liver blood flow are not accounted for, but you could manually change them if you have good reason to believe that these parameter should be different
When you export your model to MoBi, you will find the Weibull functions to describe the change in gastric pH and transit time in the Events building block under the defined food intake, e.g.:

Dear AndreDlm
I got it. Thank you for your patient reply!

Dear to all,
Sorry to bother you again！
I want to know what the parameter values in the formula are and what the reference source for the formula is？

That's a good question and the introduction of these functions dates long back!
The food effect on gastric emptying rate is described here. Unfortunately, I am not aware of a pdf of that poster that is available somewhere, but I'll have again a closer look...
However, this poster presents several functions incl. exponential, Hill, Weibull and others that were fitted to experimental data on gastric emptying - if I remember correctly to data reported in this study. The Weibull function scored best and was therefore chosen to describe gastric emptying rate.
It was also found that meal energy content and the meal's solid fraction affected gastric emptying rate. The relationship between these factors and T50 (time at which 50% of the initial meal volume has left the stomach) was then analyzed resulting in the current implemented equation where β is calculated from the meal's fraction solid and α from the meal's energy content and β.
I hope this helps a bit...

Dear to all,
I wonder what is the meaning of start time in the event ?
For example,
i：drugs were taken with breakfast, what is the value of start time?
ii：Breakfast was served at 8:40 a.m., the drug was taken  at 9:00 a.m.,what is the value of start time?
iii：the drug was taken  at 8：40 a.m.，Breakfast was served at 9:00 a.m.,what is the value of start time?
Sincerely!

With the start time you can define at which time point in your simulation the event starts.
I would recommend to simulate the time differences with regards to your administration protocol. If you use an advanced protocol, you can define start times of your drug intake.
For your examples:
i: Start time event and start time administration both at the same time ( i.e. 0 h)
ii: Start time event at 0 h and start time administration at 0.33 h
iii: Start time administration at 0 h and start time event at 0.33 h
one advice: Keep in mind to time your observed data accordingly, if you change the start of your administration.
I hope this helps!

@Fatima-Marok
Your answer is very helpful to me，Thank you for your reply！

title: Structure formula question.
body_text:
I have a two questions about structure formula.
First, i didn't find biliary clearance formulation in the Mobi software although i incorporated the renal and biliary clearance both in the model. I only found the formula of renal clearance
(dN/dt=VCLspecC_pls*fu) i wonder that same equation is applied in biliary clearance. if not, where can i find the biliary clearance formula.
Second, i confused the specific clearance formula
fu * (Q * (1 - HCT) - CL_pls * BW)>0 ? CL_pls * BW * Q * (1 - HCT) / (fu * (Q * (1 - HCT) - CL_pls * BW) * V) : 1000000
In above formula, is "fu * (Q * (1 - HCT) - CL_pls * BW)>0 ?" part indicates condition of this formula?
(i understood that only  fu * (Q * (1 - HCT) - CL_pls * BW)>0 condition is appropriate in the model)
Also, "CL_pls * BW * Q * (1 - HCT) / (fu * (Q * (1 - HCT) - CL_pls * BW) * V) : 1000000" part, i wonder that ":1000000" what is this.
I'm sorry to confuse you by not influent english skill.
Thank you.

answer.body_text:
Hi,

The biliary transport is an active transporter, located in the molecules building-block



Yes, it is a conditional formula of type

Equation1 ? Equation2 : Equation3
meaning "If Equation1 is true, then Equation2, otherwise Equation3".
In this particular case, Equation1 checks if the blood flow rate to the kidney is larger than the entered plasma clearance. If it is true, the blood flow rate is not the limiting factor and Equation2 is used, which calculates the plasma clearance. If the blood flow rate is lower than the entered plasma clearance (perfusion limited case), Equation3 which is just an arbitrary high value.

comments.body_text:

Equation1 ? Equation2 : Equation3
meaning "If Equation1 is true, then Equation2, otherwise Equation3".
In this particular case, Equation1 checks if the blood flow rate to the kidney is larger than the entered plasma clearance. If it is true, the blood flow rate is not the limiting factor and Equation2 is used, which calculates the plasma clearance. If the blood flow rate is lower than the entered plasma clearance (perfusion limited case), Equation3 which is just an arbitrary high value.

title: concentration-dependent effect model
body_text:
Hello,
We are working in a PBPK model for a drug which causes renal vasodilatation and it has a renal elimination. We are trying to optimize our model in MoBi considering the effect of plasma concentration of the drug on its renal excretion. What strategy do you recommend to model this using an Emax model?
Thank you in advance
Best regards
María Luisa

comments.body_text:
Hi @MaLuisaEti, the thing is, you can model this pretty much any way you like in MoBi. So the answer depends on how much you are prepared to customize.
A simple question would be to make renal blood flow and GFR concentration-dependent. You can use Emax or any other kind of transfer function...
Best,
Stephan

title: Expresssion level of metabolizing enzymes in normal vs obese patients
body_text:
Hi everyone,
I am working on obese vs normal patients data in PK-Sim. So I am facing an issue wherein PK-Sim I am not able to justify in PK-Sim the difference between normal and obese patients in terms of expression of metabolizing enzymes as in obese patients the drug is not able to pass to the plasma. The expression is relative to metabolizing enzymes i.e it is a gene database that selects the %expression in the tissue which will be the same in normal and obese. While in obese patients there has to be greater metabolism than in normal patients. How can we show that in PK-Sim?
Any suggestions?
Thank you,
Sanjana

comments.body_text:
Dear Sanjana,
I do hope I understand your issue correctly.
You could create two different baseline individuals with different expressions of the same enzyme (you can manually edit the loaded expression data) and then create your populations for each of these individuals and their distinct expression profiles.
Hope this helps,
Stephan

title: Plasma stability
body_text: I want to input information on the PKsim & Mobi regarding plasma stability. So I need to implement that metabolism occurs in plasma. But I'm not sure about that information. There seems to be no implementation method in PKsim's Metabolism tab or transport & excretion. Can you do this with PKsim & mobi?

comments.body_text:
Dear Jenny,
that should be possible in PK-Sim as well. Just create and enzyme called e.g."PlasmaMetabolism" define that it is only expressed in plasma by setting relative expression in Plasma to 1 and in all other organs to zero.
Add in you compound BB a metabolism process that you can connect to your enzyme and than you should be ready to go.
Best, Tobias

Hi,
in that case I would fit the process e.g. the Specific clearance to plasma concentration time profile of your compound in a middle out way. That would kind of ignore your in vitro data and you would use the presence of plasma metabolism as assumption for the reason for the fast disappearance of your compound.
As a quick thought, if you believe your in vitro data are predicting the in vivo situation I think your rate constant is most similar to the Specific clearance. As the compound is doing it by itself you can use you compound concentration in plasma as "enzyme concentration" start value. But that would be a simplification. Otherwise probaly you really might have to go to Mobi and include a decay reaction for your compound. But maybe there are more ideas from others...

title: How to better this fitting "lacidipine"
body_text:
Hi, all
I am trying to duplicate the simulation of lacidipine on beagles from two papers (DOI: 10.1039/c4ra16017g, and DOI: 10.1016/j.ajps.2016.03.003).

title: about food effect
body_text:
hello team,

when I use the default,my figure like this

when I change it ,

the figure comes to

so I want to make sure what the tablet time delay factor means?
and can it use to explain the food effect?(like the food delays drug  release?)

answer.body_text:
Dear @Carrie-nothing,
the Tablet time delay factor delays the transfer of the solid dosage form along the GI tract relative to water (but the dissolution rate itself is not directly affected). You can find the parameter when exporting your simulation to MoBi and navigating to the Events Building Block:

You can also visualize the tablet location in the GI tract by selecting Plot parameter in each of the Tablet_Location_{GI-segment} parameters and plot it in the simulation (e.g. Tablet_Location_cae will be 1 when the tablet is located in the cecum and 0 otherwise).
Note that this only works when you have have set IsSuspension = No. In the default case (where IsSuspension is activated), the suspension fraction will be changed.
Anyway, in your example the transfer of the tablet along the GI tract is slowed resulting a greater Tmax.
When simulating a food effect (via an added food intake in the Events building block in PK-Sim), the gastric emptying time is automatically increased, resulting in a slower transfer of all drug amount from stomach to duodenum.
In contrast, decreasing the Tablet time delay factor slows down the transfer of the solid tablet (i.e. undissolved drug amount) for all GI compartments.
I hope this helps
André

I would like to know where I can find the equations for each physiological parameter. For example, equations that describe organ volume as a function of age, weight, gender, etc. Also, where can I find the differential equations for the PBPK model. Thank you.

Maybe this discussion is of help to your question:
#360 (comment)

Hi Jan,
Thank you for the information. However, I still have questions about how the software generates physiological parameters. Let's take organ volume as an example. I know that organ volume is from literature reported values for a specific age (i.e., ICRP report brain weight for different age groups). Do you use body weight-based organ volume (i.e., mL/kg) or other methods? If so, how do you get this body weight-based organ volume from the literature (they usually reported total volume but not body-weight based organ volume). In addition, how about user-defined age that is not reported in the literature (i.e., 5.6 yr)? Do you do interpolation to get the physiological parameter or other methods, and how? Thank you.
Best regards,
Ping

Hi Ping,
What do you mean by body weight-based organ volumes? The Create Individual algorithm is discribed here:

https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17431751/

Jan

Jan

title: Simulations with CKD Stages templates AUC_inf relative to Pop healthy simulation AUC_inf Ratio
body_text:
I have no explanation for the decrease in ratio for stage 4 and 5 relative to stage 3 in comparison to control healthy, my drug have non-renal clearance with <1% of drug excreted unchanged in urine, Can anyone give me an explanation for that or even give me the head of the thread to search for.
Thank you

answer.body_text:
A number of parameters are changed vs. healthy
Fraction unbound
Intestinal transit
Gastric emptying
Hematocrit
Kidney volume
Kidney blood flow
Likely fraction unbound is responsible for the greatest change in a scenario with no renal CL. A sensitivity analysis would be enlightening.
Increasing fraction unbound lowers plasma concentrations.
But a model should account for changes in nonrenal clearance in CKD stages. Generally speaking for a compound as you describe AUCR should be less than 2 however.

Fraction unbound is scaled up by the plasma protein scale factor in the CKD pop.
Increased distribution into cellular spaces will enable faster clearance in addition to the volume of distribution being larger, which is why your AUC might change (though fundamental PKers will disagree on this, quite a complex topic).
For a sensitivity analysis, change your parameter of interest by +/- 10% and record the change in the output of interest.

title: some questiones about the meaning of parameters in the software
body_text:
hi, all
I 'm confused about the meaning of  some parameters in the software, such as "percentile "about physiological parameter,

when I adjust the value, the percentile changes correspondingly, how to understand the meaning of percentile?

comments.body_text:
@xixi-1995
When you create an individual and select the species Human, you are also asked to select a Population
see Creating Individual
More specifically
For humans, the population parameters database that comes with PK-Sim® includes information on the dependence of anatomical and physiological parameters relevant for PK-Sim® such as organ weights, blood flow rates, or tissue composition on age, gender, body weight, body mass index, which have been collected in a comprehensive literature search. Thus, when creating an individual of a given Age, Weight, and Height, the parameters will be adjusted based on the information included in the underlying database. There are three classes of parameters that will be adjusted:

Organ volumes
Further parameters for which distribution data are available in the database, e.g. the hematocrit value.
The volume fractions, which are scaled using a global scale factor taken from the database

In your case, Gastric Emptying time is one of those parameters where distribution data is available. As such, the percentile shown here represents the percentile of the value in the population (based on your Age, Height and Weight selection)
When you change the value, the percentile updates automatically based on the underlying distribution.
Hope that clarifies things,

Dear  @msevestre ,thanks for your patiently explanation, actually I'm still confused.
Taking Gastric Emptying time as an example, when the value is set for 30min,the percentile is 50%, is that mean people with 30min Gastric Emptying time occupy for all 50% population I selection? but when I change the value to 50min,the percentile is 99%? from where I see, the percentile of each value should be 100% when sum them all up, is that right?
on the other hand, when I select different population with different age ,height or weight, the percentile of the same value seems to be the same, is there something wrong?

title: PK-Parameter in Time Profile Analysis - multiple questions
body_text:
While going through the documentation (https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-simulations#pk-analysis-view) and comparing it with the actual output of the pk-parameters in PK-Sim, I realized I have multiple questions.
Following use case: I have a model with two compounds - Esomeprazole and R-Omeprazole (these are the two enantiomeres of Omeprazole). A container observer for the molecule Esomeprazole calculates the concentration of the sum of Esomeprazole and R-Omeprazole in Pervipheral Venous Blood Plasma. This observer is called "Omeprazole Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)"
In an iv simulation (both enantiomeres are administered via iv route), I select only the observer "Omeprazole Plasma (Peripheral Venous Blood)" for the output, but not the concentrations of the single compounds. When I open the PK-Analysis, I see this:



For the general understanding: the PK-parameters in the lower table are calculated for the selected outputs (and observed data, if available), and the PK-parameters in the upper table are calculated for all molecules in the model, even if I do not select their outputs?


How are the "_norm"-parameters (e.g. AUC_tEnd_norm) calculated in this case? For the observer which sums up concentrations of two compounds, I assume it only accounts for the dose of the molecule for which the observer is calculated (Esomeprazole in this case). If so, the calculated value is not correct. In fact, allowing user-defined observers introduces a problem here.


Why is "AUC Ratio (AUCR)" calculated for the compounds only, and not for the observer? Here actually it could be calculated (as opposed to 2) ). Same for C_max ratio.


I cannot find parameter "Total body clearance".


Instead, I see parameter  "Total body clearance/F", which I cannot find in the documentation at all.


Also parameters "Vd(plasma)/F" and "Vss(plasma)/F", which should not appear for the iv simulation.


@msevestre @Yuri05 @StephanSchaller

comments.body_text:
Now, a po simulation. Same setting - both enantiomeres are administered, and the observer for the total concentration is selected as output.



How are the parameters "Vss(plasma)/F", "Vd(plasma)/F", "Total plasma clearance/F", and "Total Body Clearance/F" calculated? I did not press "Calculate Bioavailability" so far, so which values does the calculation use?


The parameter "Total body clearance/F" cannot be found in the documentation.


After calculating bioavailability, I see this:



Could it be, that the "/F" and not "/F" captions are mixed-up?

Why, after calculation bioavailability, the non- /F parameters (which are labeled as /F but probably wrong...) are not shown any more?

Complex administration protocol: a compound is administered as iv at time 0, and as po at time 120h.

Why cannot the "Fraction absorbed" be calculated?
Same question for "Vss (plasma)", "Vd (plasma)", and "Total plasma clearance"
AUC_tEnd, AUC_inf, %AUC(tlast-inf), AUC_tEnd_norm, AUC_inf_norm, C_tEnd are not calculated (though stated in documenation as "for all simulations")

Some quick feedback:
The definition of the parameter/F (for instance Total plasma clearance/F) does not require F. The way it is calculated is based on the IV définition.
CL/F = Dose/AUC
If you want to know the real value, you need to multiply by F
That's why Total plasma clearance is only available once you have the bioavailability
Value of parameter P = P/F x F
So there is no inversion of parameters whatsoever

For the general understanding: the PK-parameters in the lower table are calculated for the selected outputs (and observed data, if available), and the PK-parameters in the upper table are calculated for all molecules in the model, even if I do not select their outputs?

This is correct. Some outputs are ALWAYS used, even if not selected. Those are
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim/blob/develop/src/PKSim.Core/Services/SimulationPersistableUpdater.cs#L35

Venous Blood Plasma
Peripheral Venous Blood Plasma
F abs oral


How are the "_norm"-parameters (e.g. AUC_tEnd_norm) calculated in this case? For the observer which sums up concentrations of two compounds, I assume it only accounts for the dose of the molecule for which the observer is calculated (Esomeprazole in this case). If so, the calculated value is not correct. In fact, allowing user-defined observers introduces a problem here.

I think this is a bug. I need to think about it

Why is "AUC Ratio (AUCR)" calculated for the compounds only, and not for the observer? Here actually it could be calculated (as opposed to 2) ). Same for C_max ratio.

This is calculated for all compounds (and only once using peripheral venous blood except for mouse and rat where it is using venous blood). This is how it was specified. We have 100s of observers so I am not quite sure what you mean with calculated for observer

I cannot find parameter "Total body clearance".

This should be Total body clearance/F

Instead, I see parameter "Total body clearance/F", which I cannot find in the documentation at all.

See above

Also parameters "Vd(plasma)/F" and "Vss(plasma)/F", which should not appear for the iv simulation.

I don't think those parameter makes sense for oral simulation either... (at least they make sense in the global view but not for each selected output)..

Why, after calculation bioavailability, the non- /F parameters (which are labeled as /F but probably wrong...) are not shown any more?

The rest if I have explained. Here I guess we could show the /F parameters as well. not sure why we are hiding them
moving this to the forum as discussion so that it can be found later on

Why cannot the "Fraction absorbed" be calculated?

It can be calculated but I am not sure if this is meaningful. Let say you have oral and iv and iv starts before the oral application ends etc.. I am not sure that we would be able to differentiate the part that comes from the oral application and the one coming from the iv.  At any rate, in code, it is explicitly shown for oral application(s) only. Maybe @Yuri05  can comment on this
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/PK-Sim/blob/develop/src/PKSim.Core/Services/GlobalPKAnalysisTask.cs#L117

Same question for "Vss (plasma)", "Vd (plasma)", and "Total plasma clearance"

Those parameters are only define for single dosing

AUC_tEnd, AUC_inf, %AUC(tlast-inf), AUC_tEnd_norm, AUC_inf_norm, C_tEnd are not calculated (though stated in documenation as "for all simulations")

They are also only defined for single dosing applications
FYI

This is calculated for all compounds (and only once using peripheral venous blood except for mouse and rat where it is using venous blood). This is how it was specified. We have 100s of observers so I am not quite sure what you mean with calculated for observer

I mean the observers that are selected by the user. I might be interested in the AUC ratio in muscle intracellular (why not?).

It can be calculated but I am not sure if this is meaningful. Let say you have oral and iv and iv starts before the oral application ends etc.. I am not sure that we would be able to differentiate the part that comes from the oral application and the one coming from the iv. At any rate, in code, it is explicitly shown for oral application(s) only. Maybe @Yuri05 can comment on this

The observer for fraction absorbed uses the parameters MOLECULE|Total oral drug mass and Neighborhoods|Lumen_sto_Lumen_duo|MOLECULE|Oral mass absorbed, so I do not see how the amount administered via iv route would interfere here...

Those parameters are only define for single dosing

Yes, but why? :D
C_tEnd is just the concentration at the end of simulation... It should not matter how many applications are simulated? The same for AUC - I may be very much interested what the AUC in a multiple dose simulation is.

Those parameters are only define for single dosing

lol, even more confusing! I have a simulation with two IV (0 and 24h) and and PO (129h) administrations. Simulation time is 140h:

I get the long list for of PK-parameters for simulations with multiple administrations... Now, change the simulation time to the time of administration of the last dose (129h):
I get the PK parameters for the single dose! i.e., the are calculated even for multiple dosing!

Fluvoxamine-Omeprazole-DDI_light.zip
Just change the simulation time to 139h

Another point I found: the 'C_trough_tDlast' actually calculates the 'C_end' and not the 'C' BEFORE the last dose.
E.g., if I finish simulating just 1h after the last dose it takes the last concentration value:

title: Protein binding to other protein than Albumin or alpha1-glycoprotein
body_text:
Dear all,
Im working on a model for endogenous secretion and distribution of sex steroids in man. The main sex steroids of interest (Testosteron, DHT and Estradiol) are mainly and stronger bound to Sexual hormon binding globulin (SHBG) than to Albumin and there is a belief that the Albumin bound fraction is also biolicigal active. However when I set the fu to the reported literature values of total unbound sex steroids (Neither bind to Albumin nor to SHBG) the Simulated values are in good agreement with observed data. But my question is if there a possibility to set up a protein binding  partner different then albumin or alpha1-glycoprotein? I tried to integrate SHBG as a specific binding partner in Molecule building block and set the fu to the value that reflected the absolute unbound + the SHBG bound fraction. Unfortunatly this resulted in to low plasma concentration values of the sex steroids. Is there maybe an opportunity to create SHBG as a compund and then modeling the binding interaction between SHBG and the sex steroids?
Best wishes and thank you for help!

comments.body_text:
Unfortunatly this resulted in to low plasma concentration values of the sex steroids

total or unbound?... if you create SHBG as a protein binding partner (via expression in the individual building block) it will also be relevant to set the concentration there (reference and relative expression value) correctly.

Is there maybe an opportunity to create SHBG as a compund and then modeling the binding interaction between SHBG and the sex steroids?

Certainly, you create the binding partner as a second compound in PK-Sim and then export the simulation to MoBi and define the binding in MoBi.
Hope this helps.
Stephan

title: Plasma protein scaling factor(PPSF)
body_text:
Dear All,
Kindly tell me how to use option of plasma protein scaling factor in PK-Sim(R) ? Concentrations of Albumin./AAG have reduced in diseased state which results in increase of Fu of molecule. By default value of plasma protein scaling factor in PK-Sim individuale is 1.00. What to do if con. of Albumin/AAG reduced? how to simulate this situation in PK Sim?


Change the default value of plasma protein scaling factor from 1.00 to fraction of Albumin/AAG in diseased state e.g. in liver cirrhosis it reduced upto the fraction  0.3
or
Calculate the value of Fu in diseased state by using equation explained by Edginton etal., 2008. The fractional increase in Fu of diseased population from healthy will be used as plasma protein scaling factor.


or
3) Changing the Altered Fu value in molecule and leaving the scaling factor 1.00 by default.

answer.body_text:
@nasirkalam This parameter is nothing more than an easy way for you to adjust the fu based for example on disease state etc..
Look at the description of the parameter

In my example, I have the following fu

If  I multiply the scale factor by 2, the fu will also be multiplied accordingly

so I'd suggest using option 1.

title: How does the log(Ko/w) influence the fraction unbound to Albumin ?
body_text:
Hello,
I made two models for the same molecule on Mobi. The Only difference is the log(Ko/w) value I set to 4.7 for the first one and to 3.8 for the second one. I saw that the Fraction Unbound to Albumin was different either : 0.01 and 0.03 respectively.
I also tried to modify the parameter of the second model by setting the Fraction unbound to 0.01 (as the first one) and I obtain very different results. So I suppose this fraction unbound has a great influence on the simulation and I was wondering how does the log(Ko/w) influence in the calculation of the fraction unbound to Albumin ?

answer.body_text:
I was wondering how does the log(Ko/w) influence in the calculation of the fraction unbound to Albumin ?

It doesn't.
Fraction unbound (fu) is a measured input parameter. But of course, log(Ko/w) and fu both influence the distribution of the molecule.
To learn more, I suggest to check out the literature on the basics of compound distribution and compartmental partitioning:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#adme-properties
Hope this helps.
Best,
Stephan

comments.body_text:
I was wondering how does the log(Ko/w) influence in the calculation of the fraction unbound to Albumin ?

It doesn't.
Fraction unbound (fu) is a measured input parameter. But of course, log(Ko/w) and fu both influence the distribution of the molecule.
To learn more, I suggest to check out the literature on the basics of compound distribution and compartmental partitioning:
https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-compounds-definition-and-work-flow#adme-properties
Hope this helps.
Best,
Stephan

Indeed, I checked the equation for the calculation of fu and the Log(ko/w) doesn't appear but have an indirect influence on fu through the calculation of logD_pH_7p4.
Thank you for your help !
Best,
Jonathan

title: Meaning of plasma protein scaling factor in the context of macromolecules
body_text:
Dear community,
I am currently working on a pre-clinical PBPK model for a therapeutic protein (including IgG) after IV administration. Using parameters based on in vitro data I obtained a descent description of the Cmax, elimination and the half life, but not of the initial distribution, thus leading to overprediction of the concentration-time profile. Doing a sensitivity analysis showed (not surprisingly) Kd(FcRn) and the solute radius to be important, as well as the plasma protein scaling factor. Running parameter identification with those parameters leads to a perfect fit but also to a value of 20 for the PPSF, which seems to be very high? How would we interpret this value? Or would this point to something else being wrong/missing in the model?
Thank you very much for your insights!

comments.body_text: Plasma protein scaling factor scales fraction unbound. Fraction unbound should be 1, and the plasma protein scaling factor should not be different from 1 in a model for a therapeutic protein.

As in the previous answer, PPSF should not have a large influence if fu = 1. Can you repeat the fit without fitting PPSF and check if you can still get an optimal fit?
What is your protein binding to? Any specific targets (abundantly / highly expressed? Any TMDD?), ...?

Thanks Stephan and Paul for your insights,
I agree that PPSF should stay 1 in this kind of setting, which is why I was so surprised by the results and I assume there is something else that I am missing. The therapeutic protein has a target-binding domain and an IgG-Fc part (thus binding to FcRn), but these data are from animals where the target is not present. Perhaps another binding partner is present that has not been considered before, but I would expect this to give lower PPSF rather than higher?
Could it be that the administration of the compound leads to some shifts in blood volumes that skew the first measurements? Administered volume is between 100 and 200 µL in mice, so up to 10% of the blood volume.

Dear Robin,
you write that you have a  therapeutic protein "including IgG". Could you indicate how large your protein is?

Hi Paul,

That would be exactly what I am looking for, I will try it out asap.
To confirm, that would be like adding a factor F2 on the convective flow
in the two pore diffusion model right?

Hi everyone,
I would like to update you regarding this topic if you would be interested.
After all discussions here and having an implausibly high PPSF in PKSim or F2 estimate in MoBi, I went back to the compound itself and took a.o. @Christoph27 's comment to heart. As stated originally the compound includes IgG in its structure, but this is not the only part and until now only antibody-related processes were considered in the model. The inclusion of a non-specific binding process, assumed to be related to other parts of the molecule, completely solved the misfit, allowing for a rapid decrease in plasma concentrations after administration after which the PK starts following expected antibody-like kinetics. So now it's back to the lab to figure if this binding partner would indeed be related to the target or is something else entirely. There are some plausible pistes to follow which were not considered before, again showcasing the beautiful hypothesis-generating power of PBPK modeling :)
Thanks all for you input!
Robin

title: Partition coefficient between cells and plasma
body_text:
Hello,
I work with mobi and I have a question about the K_cell_pls partition coefficient. When I select the rowland and rodgers formula I find that the equation is the one that rowland and rodgers use for determining K_tissue (doi :  10.1002/jps.20322 )
The issue is that K_cell_pls being the partition coefficient between intracellular and plasma should not be equal to K_tissue (which includes extracellular water in addition to intracellular), unless I misunderstand the equations in Mobi ?
Best regards,
Vincent

comments.body_text: The calculated constants will not be equal as K_cell_pls will use the volume fraction from cell, while K_tissue used the fractions for cell + interstitial. Maybe the publication from Schmitt et al.  will be of help here.

title: Unbound interstitial concentrations
body_text: Hello, how are unbound interstitial concentrations of compounds computed in PK Sim? Is it using the interstitial fluid volume, or from free fraction and total organ concentration?

comments.body_text:
Hi Paul,
the interstitial space is its own compartment in PK-Sim. The output concentration will give you total concentration in interstitial Space, e.g.:

However, there is no output for "unbound interstitial" (you could build an observer in MoBi, though).
"unbound interstitial" Could be derived by multiplying the default (total) interstitial concentration and multiplying it with the water/interstitial partition coefficient, which you can access through MoBi:

@StephanSchaller is correct. This is the way to calculate.
However, we already have an observer for you out of the box called "Interstitial Unbound". If you export to MoBi, you will see it under Container Observer

title: Molecule parameters only in simulation or also in building blocks?
body_text: I transferred a simulation to MoBi, and I'm noticing that there are some parameters that show up under the molecule within the simulation but are not in the molecule building block (e.g. Ka (acidic phospholipids), Ka (proteins)). Are those parameters only found within the simulation or are they somewhere in the building blocks?

answer.body_text:
Those are supporting parameters, which are used only in some calculation methods (e.g. for partition coeffcients, cellular permeabilities etc.) and are stored in C:\ProgramData\Open Systems Pharmacology\MoBi\X.Y\AllCalculationMethods.pkml

So you will not find any of those parameter in the building blocks
Depending on the selection of different calculation methods for a molecule, supporting parameters are added during the simulation creation.

title: Total fraction metabolized by CYPs with large molecule model
body_text:
Hi all,
I am building a model for a large and a small molecule using the protein model. The small molecule is metabolized by two CYPs, and I would like to know the total fraction metabolized by each CYPs. When using the small molecule model, there is an observer for this, but it seems that the observer is not available with the protein model.
I tried then to find the observer in a small molecule model in MoBi, so that I could load it in the large molecule model, but I am not sure I was able to locate the right observer.
Could someone tell me the procedure to have this observer also for the large molecule model? Or is there an easier way to get this output? I am using PK-Sim 9.
(By the way, is there a reason why this observer is not available by default with the protein model? Since this model can also be used for small molecules).
Thank you in advance for your help!
Donato

comments.body_text: Hi try outputting the concentration of the respective metabolites as molar unit in the liver intracellular space to get a first idea. Obviously the error here will be if you have the enzyme present in tissues beyond the liver, but you get a preliminary idea.

Hi Donato,
Total fraction metabolized observer is defined in large molecule models in exactly the same way as in small molecule models.
So if you don't see it in your large molecules simulation - it might be due to other reasons.

title: Pathway contribution to total body clearance
body_text: We are trying to build a model that describes the elimination pathways of a compound which has low clearance in vivo.  There is evidence of biliary elimination as well as renal excretion of the parent compound.  The compound is a substrate for efflux transporters as determined by MDCK-MDR1 and Caco2 studies, and has very low hepatic metabolic clearance in vitro.  We have been able to model the fraction eliminated in bile and urine (and understand their contribution to overall clearance from the simulation by looking at percent excreted), however, please could you advise if there is a way to model excretion of the compound from blood into the gut via efflux from the enterocytes (and particularly extract the fraction cleared by this pathway from the model / simulation)?  Additionally is there a way to extract the fraction metabolised from the simulation.  Is their a way to create a table of all clearance pathways and there relative contribution to total body clearance?

answer.body_text:
Hi Harri,
No, enterohepatic recirculation is not enabled by default. You must enable gall-bladder emptying.


Create an 'Event' to enable continuous gall bladder emptying into the duodenum and then subsequent reabsorption



Set the EHC continuous fraction to 0.4 or 1.0, whichever you feel is more appropriate for your modeling purposes. I have seen both done. This parameter is available under the 'ADVANCED' menu.



A word of warning: the fraction of oral drug mass absorbed accounts for all the drug that is absorbed: on first administration plus all the drug that is absorbed during EHC. For this reason it can sum over 100%.

comments.body_text:
Dear @harridickinson ,
that is already great progress.
Have you tried implementing an efflux transporter using the protein expression database (e.g. MDR1/Pgp)?
This will put efflux transporters in enterocytes, bile and kidney amongst others.
Regarding displaying the fractional contribution of these transporters to total elimination:
this is not possible in PK-Sim at this time as this varies with the types and number of transporters implemented and is quite challenging to implement as a generic feature, but there are workarounds e.g. by using the R-package and summing up different pathways and putting them in relation to total flux.
Hope this helps,
Stephan

Thanks @StephanSchaller for the really quick response,
We have been using the transporters which is really good, how do we extract the amount of compound that has gone into the gut via enterocytes. Also is there an easy way to calculate hepatic metabolic clearance or extract from the simulation the amount of drug removed by hepatic metabolism.
Many thanks,
Harri

how do we extract the amount of compound that has gone into the gut via enterocytes

You could try using the Lumen > Fraction of Oral Drug Mass Absorbed observer.

is there an easy way to calculate hepatic metabolic clearance or extract from the simulation the amount of drug removed by hepatic metabolism

You can use the Liver > Intracellular > 'DRUG-METABOLITE-COMPLEX' > Fraction of Dose observer if you have a metabolic enzyme set up.

Thanks @prvmalik, when plotting the fraction excreted in bile/gallbladder is it then available for reabsorption in the lumen if it has the correct properties?
Cheers,
Harri

Hi Harri,
No, enterohepatic recirculation is not enabled by default. You must enable gall-bladder emptying.


Create an 'Event' to enable continuous gall bladder emptying into the duodenum and then subsequent reabsorption



Set the EHC continuous fraction to 0.4 or 1.0, whichever you feel is more appropriate for your modeling purposes. I have seen both done. This parameter is available under the 'ADVANCED' menu.



A word of warning: the fraction of oral drug mass absorbed accounts for all the drug that is absorbed: on first administration plus all the drug that is absorbed during EHC. For this reason it can sum over 100%.
Thanks @prvmalik, just implemented it, was very helpful.

is there an easy way to calculate hepatic metabolic clearance or extract from the simulation the amount of drug removed by hepatic metabolism
Hello @prvmalik, is this step is done on the simulation in PK-Sim or in MoBI?? and if possible can you screenshot from where can I get it, as I looked around but I could not get it as well.

title: PK-sim sensitivity analysis blank
body_text:
Hi, I have created a simulation in PK-Sim. I want to carry out sensitivity analysis. However, when I try to do it, the parameters section on the sensitivity analysis page is blank; actually, nothing can be selected. I was looking at the documentation which states "Mark a simulation for the Sensitivity Analysis in the Simulation Explorer" - how can I mark a simulation for SA? I don't see that option.
I think I am missing something basic in setting up the simulation. I was wondering if anyone help me with this issue.
Thanks,
John
