Starting May 7th, 2021, the OSP issues will not be used anymore for discussions. Instead, the Discussions feature from Github will be used
Please see the announcement here:
#894
To start a discussion, simply go to
https://github.com/Open-Systems-Pharmacology/Forum/discussions and click on New Discussion

comments.body_text:
Hi @learnCode-cpu ,

However, when I try to do it, the parameters section on the sensitivity analysis page is blank; actually, nothing can be selected.

Can you please show us a screenshot? It is very hard to diagnose what your problem might be if we do not have more information

It's because sensitivity only works with individual simulation. The only simulation in your project seems to be a population Simulation.
So simply create a simulation with the same building blocks, swapping out the population for the individual, and you'll be good to go.

Expression values are defined in the building block called "Expression profile". One individual uses one or more expression profiles. So if you want to have multiple profiles for MDR1, you need to create multiple expression profiles for this transporter and use the corresponding profile in your individual.
When you change a value in the simulation, it it saved in the simulation. As you can see the simulation turns red, indicating that there is at least one difference between the building block in the project and the values in the simulation. To update your individual building blocks, right click on the individual in the simulation, with the red icon, and click Commit. This will commit your changes to the project individual.
I would strongly suggest that you spend some time reading the documentation. The section on building blocks will help you get a better grasp as to how all the pieces are fitting together.
Hope that helps,

title: ELF concentrations of large molecules using MoBi?
body_text:
Dear community,
Another question from my side regarding macromolecules and the lung. In a different project I am trying to predict how much of a macromolecule ends up in the lung after systemic administration. Using the macromolecule model in PKSim works very well to predict the plasma concentrations after IV administration, and also the total lung concentrations coincide with lung homogenate data that I have (preclinical species). However, in some follow-up experiments BAL samples will be taken, which to my knowledge sample the epithelial lining fluid. Sadly, ELF is not (yet?) accessible as a container in PKSim, so it is difficult to corroborate PKSim predicitons to these data. Therefore, I thought to use MoBi again, but am a bit stuck on what the best approach is here. As I would need to expand the model to include ELF, two options seemed plausible for me.


Would be to export the macromolecule IV model I already have in PKSim to MoBi and then add an ELF compartment to the lung manually, using parameter values from the recently published lung absorption model by Johanna Eriksson et al. However, I am not at all experienced in adding new tissue parts and am afraid I will miss a process and therefore wrongly predict the ELF concentrations; is there any material or tutorial on how to easily expand the spatial structures in MoBi?


Would be to start from the abovementioned lung model and expand it to accomodate large molecules; this would mean adding the endosome and FcRn recycling pathways. I tried to load in the large molecule standard file on top of this lung absorption MoBi file, but this was not succesfull so far and I again feel some tutorial on how to implement large molecules in MoBi would be very beneficial.


My question is thus which of these two paths would be the most straightforward and most scientifically sound to follow; in essence I want to 'merge' the macromolecule model and the lung absorption model into one, which in theory should be feasible but in practice I feel I am missing the finesse to connect the correct processes.
Thank you very much for you insights!
Kind regards,
Robin

answer.body_text:
Hi Robin,
I would suggest that you choose option 1, i.e., get inspiration from the Johanna et al model in terms of physiological structure and parameters and add this to your already established IV model.
If you are not planning to simulate any pulmonary administration this should be quite feasible. However, depending on your molecule you may need to add some translocation processes etc. depending on how you anticipate your molecule to reach ELF from blood.
Good luck
Erik

If you are not planning to simulate any pulmonary administration this should be quite feasible. However, depending on your molecule you may need to add some translocation processes etc. depending on how you anticipate your molecule to reach ELF from blood.

title: extending spatial structures in MoBi
body_text:
I want to expand the lung model.
I referenced the paper(Applied Concepts in PBPK Modeling: How to Extend an Open Systems Pharmacology Model to the Special Population of Pregnant Women(2018)) but failed.
help me......
lung model.zip

answer.body_text:
Dear @csungeun,
the errors tell you what went wrong - you can display them by displaying the Notification panel:

The first error tells you that one input variable needed for the the reaction via the unknown liver enzyme is missing (namely the Partition coefficient (water/container) in the compartment ELF in the lung).
In the current implementation, this reaction occurs in all compartments where the unknown liver enzyme is present. This is defined in the MSV building block:

When setting up a new simulation, this enzyme is per default also present in the new lung compartment. So to solve this you would either need to add the parameter Partition coefficient (water/container) in all sub-compartments of the new the lung or you define in the MSV building blocks that the unknown liver enzyme is not present in these compartments. To do so, you need to switch to the MSV building block, click on Extend in the ribbon bar menu and deselect the unknown liver enzyme in all compartments of nLung:


The second error in the model is that the transport equation PassiveDiffusionInt2Cell refers to a parameter that cannot be found, namely the Partition coefficient (interstitial/water) under the path Neighborhoods|nLung_int_nLung_cell|Digoxin|Partition coefficient (interstitial/water). To solve this, you just have to navigating there and correct the typos, then it should work:

I hope this helps

title: Partition coefficient (water/container) reference to Partition coefficient (interstitial/water)
body_text:
Hi All,
I have been running into a problem a few times lately and I can't get an idea of what is going on.
In two different models in Mobi (both large molecule models, default human exported from PK-Sim) after a few additions/deletions of molecules or MSVs I get an error when I try to create a simulation that says: "Partition coefficient (water/container) with path simulation|Organism|Bone|Interstitial|mydrug|Partition coefficient (water/container) references an entity with path simulation|Neighborhoods|Bone_int_Bone_cell|mydrug|Partition coefficient (interstitial/water) that cannot be found" I get this message for all organs
I tried to find out where this comes from, and if I look at e.g. bone interstitial molecule properties I indeed see this reference (to be sure, I recreated a default large molecule human model in PK-Sim, so I have made no changes to this model).

When I check this reference in the Neighbourhoods, I don't see the Partition coefficient (interstitial/water)

In the default case, there is no problem of course, and I can check the simulation parameters where I see that for my specific molecule, this Partition coefficient (interstitial/water) is present and refers to the pls_int neighbourhood to another Partition coefficient (interstitial plasma) (which depends on organism parameters and the fu) and the fu.

From all of this, I don't get many clues but only more questions. How are these parameters generated and how does that depend on MSV/PSV? Anybody who can help me out? Much appreciated!
Best,
Wilbert

answer.body_text:
Here's a workaround. Save your project before you do this. The value for the K_water_int is always 1.04 for a large molecule.
Open Spatial Structure|Formulas in your building block. Sort by Name by clicking on "name." Look for the formula "PARAM_K_Water_INT_organ."

Press the X next to the reference to K_water_int to delete it. In the bottom panel, enter the constant "1.04" as shown. Do this for all organs.

Let me know if that works.

title: Where could I find the metabolism fraction of total hepatic clearance?
body_text:
Hello all,
If I defined a "total hepatic clearance" for a compound, where could I find the fraction of the compound eliminated by "total hepatic clearance"?
Thanks.

comments.body_text:
Dear @Harris728
Can you please elaborate the question?  Do you mean, you gave hepatic plasma clearance along with other clearance mechanism or hepatic clearance alone in your model?? Usually if specific enzyme data  is not available. Then we can use mass balance data to quantitate the hepatic, renal and bile excretion from total clearance reported or calculated NCA (non compartmental analysis )clearance.  In worst case, you can use the NCA calculated clearance as total plasma clearance if mass balance data is not available.

Try using the observer under "Define Settings and Run" > "Organism" > "Total Fraction of Dose - Metabolite" or a similar name to this effect.
I would have to check, but if this doesn't work then I would avoid using the 'total hepatic clearance' parameterization and instead insert a dummy enzyme in the liver intracellular space. An observer would certainly work in that case.

title: Kidney Intracellular Protein Concentration Calculation
body_text:
In PKSim v9, the simulation parameter "Kidney|Intracellular|volume of protein container" retains the value of the whole kidney volume, while the parameter "Kidney|Intracellular|Volume" is calculated from the product of "Fraction intracellular" and total kidney volume. Each is used to calculate intracellular transporter (efflux, apical) and intracellular enzyme concentrations differently than calculations for interstitial (influx) transporter concentrations. Why is this? Thank you!
The attached .txt file shows annotated examples from PKSim "structure to file" export.
Kidney_Intracellular_Protein_Concentration_Calculations_StructuretoFile.txt

answer.body_text:
If I'm understanding correctly, the easiest way to do this is to add another "...|" to the parameter path so that "Volume" calls the volume of the organ instead of the interstitial compartment.

That's correct.

I haven't confirmed that this bug is corrected automatically in a new v10 project, but unless I'm misunderstanding, it isn't automatically corrected when a project created in v9 is opened in v10. Thank you again for your explanations!

This is only corrected when you re-create your project in PK-Sim v10 (e.g. using the snapshot function (https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/importing-exporting-project-data-models#exporting-project-to-snapshot-loading-project-from-snapshot). MoBi does not do any conversions in the models (or only in very rare cases).

comments.body_text:
I believe expression data is normally derived from whole tissue samples  which is why the total organ volume is used but the amount is then allocated to the site of transporter expression.
@PavelBal can likely link the specifications here which should explain how this was implemented.

There is no specification available how it was implemented in V9.
Basically, the statement "relative expression of 100% with reference concentration 1 µmol/l" means that the whole organ (inkl. plasma and RBC) has the particular concentration. The amount in the whole organ is therefore "1 µmol/l times volume of whole organ", and the concentration IN intracellular space is "1 µmol/l times volume of whole organ divided by intracellular volume".
For the interstitial space, this has not been taken into account and was a bug in the implementation. Version 10 corrects this shortcoming, the specification of how protein expression is calculated in V10 can be found https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/pk-sim-expression-data

Thank you again for your great answers @StephanSchaller and @PavelBal! I'll post what I've found so far. The bug can be corrected by hand in MoBi. If I'm understanding correctly, the easiest way to do this is to add another "...|" to the parameter path so that "Volume" calls the volume of the organ instead of the interstitial compartment.

I haven't confirmed that this bug is corrected automatically in a new v10 project, but unless I'm misunderstanding, it isn't automatically corrected when a project created in v9 is opened in v10. Thank you again for your explanations!

If I'm understanding correctly, the easiest way to do this is to add another "...|" to the parameter path so that "Volume" calls the volume of the organ instead of the interstitial compartment.

That's correct.

I haven't confirmed that this bug is corrected automatically in a new v10 project, but unless I'm misunderstanding, it isn't automatically corrected when a project created in v9 is opened in v10. Thank you again for your explanations!

This is only corrected when you re-create your project in PK-Sim v10 (e.g. using the snapshot function (https://docs.open-systems-pharmacology.org/working-with-pk-sim/pk-sim-documentation/importing-exporting-project-data-models#exporting-project-to-snapshot-loading-project-from-snapshot). MoBi does not do any conversions in the models (or only in very rare cases).

Is the concentration of albumin in the brain interstitial space available in PK-Sim or Mobi, and would this help in determining the bound fraction?

comments.body_text:

The container/water partitioning value for interstitial brain will give you the answer on the fraction unbound.
Best,
Stephan

title: A question about sensitivity analyses!
body_text:
Dear all,
Could you tell me how to determine the values of "number of steps" and "variation range"? When I set "number of steps" at 9, and "variation range" at 10, I found the results were significant different from that of the default settings, and it would exaggerate sensitivity.
For example, when I use default settings, n=2 and a=0.1, the sensitivity values of fu and pKa were -0.91 and -0.82, respectively. When I set "number of steps" at 9, and "variation range" at 10, the sensitivity values of fu and pKa were -3.36 and -0.1, respectively.
I want to know which one is correct and how to determine the values of "number of steps" and "variation range".
Thanks.

comments.body_text:
you will have to think about how parameters affect your model.
The pKa might be sensitive (also depending on other drug properties) if it is set at physiologic relevant values (pH 2 or 5 to 7.5 or maybe 8). also, it will have no real meaning > 14  etc. So starting at e.g. 5 and increasing it 10-fold ("variation range" at 10) will not make much sense.
For a local sensitivity analysis, it is best to keep the "variation range" at very small values (I would even say 0.1 could be considered a sufficiently large).
Setting the "variation range" to larger values (and 10 might be too extreme (see example above)) might help to identify non-linearities, if it returns a different value...

title: PhD project available on Subcutaneus administration modelling
body_text:
Title of the PhD project:
Development of a PBPK model to quantitatively describe absorption and bioavailability after subcutaneous administration of therapeutical biologics
At Sanofi, we currently have an opening for a talented and innovative PhD student to join our physiologically-based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) project realized in collaboration with the consulting company Pharmetheus (www.pharmetheus.com) and the COMPO group (https://team.inria.fr/compo/) in Aix Marseille University.  This setting will allow the student to gain experience in PBPK modelling within pharma industry, consulting, and academia.
Description:
New modalities of drugs, like therapeutic proteins and monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), have changed the paradigm in the treatment landscape of many therapeutic areas/diseases like immuno-oncology and immuno-inflammation. Subcutaneous administration is increasingly becoming a more flexible and patient-friendly option for the administration of such treatment options. The integration of this administration route in a PBPK modeling framework is crucial, in order to access the full potential of this modeling technique and to optimize the subcutaneous administration of mAbs. The objective of this project is to extend the PBPK model available in the open-source platform Open Systems Pharmacology (OSP) Suite in order to integrate the subcutaneous administration of therapeutical proteins, with particular focus on mAbs. The model will be developed and qualified for different mAbs format, such as nanobodies and bi-specific mAbs.
Location and duration
With the PhD project been conducted in collaboration with Pharmetheus, and Aix Marseille University, the student is expected to spent part of the PhD duration in the three different partner locations: i) at Sanofi, within the Translational Medicine & Early Development department located at our research and development site in Chilly-Mazarin, France (near Paris), ii) at Pharmetheus, primarily the main site situated in the vibrant student town of Uppsala, Sweden (near Stockholm) and/or at the new office planned to open in Paris (Montparnasse), and iii) at the School of Pharmacy of Marseille, within the COMPO group. The time spent within each site will be defined and agreed together with the candidate.
Skills

Master or equivalent degree in Pharmaceutical sciences, Mathematics, Statistics, Engineering or similar disciplines with demonstrated expertise in modelling and simulation
Previous training or experience with PK-Sim and R are an asset
Good understanding of physiology and in vitro readouts related to pharmacokinetic processes
Ability to conduct deep bibliographic searches
Strong problem-solving skills
Excellent oral and written communication skills in English
Knowledge of French language is an advantage but not a requirement

Compensation
The student will receive a market-level salary for PhD students, as well as social benefits including paid vacations days. Accommodation and living costs are the responsibility of the candidate.
To apply, please contact donato.teutonico@sanofi.com

title: Tissue concentration
body_text:
Hello everyone
I have a question, I was doing a simulation of an IV administration.
First I adjusted the model to the observed concentrations in blood (red dots and line) and it was fairly acceptable.
Liver and spleen were removed at 6h, homogenised and the compound quantified.
What I noticed is that the simulated concentration in "Tissue (observer)", for both liver and spleen, is lower than blood (liver, blue dot and line; spleen, green dot and line).
At least with the liver, which is a highly vascularized tissue, the concentrations should be higher, right?
So my question is, what is considering the "Tissue observer" to calculate concentration?
To get the concentration as in the tissue homogenate, do I have to create an observer to sum the concentrations of intracellular, interstitial, plasma, RBC...?
Thanks in advance
Ricardo

answer.body_text:
Dear @Ricardo-DO , tissue to plasma concentration ratio is governed by the plasma/tissue partitioning properties of the drug (depending on mostly lipophilicity of the drug). Perfusion is only a means of how fast this ratio might be reached but does not define it.
The "Tissue" observer provides the corresponding concentration from a BLOODLESS tissue homogenate (no blood or plasma).
If you still have blood left in the sample, use the "Whole Organ" observer and adjust the "Residual Blood Fraction for Sampling" to account for lost blood (bleeding out) in the sampling procedure.
Hope this helps.
Stephan

comments.body_text:
Dear @Ricardo-DO , tissue to plasma concentration ratio is governed by the plasma/tissue partitioning properties of the drug (depending on mostly lipophilicity of the drug). Perfusion is only a means of how fast this ratio might be reached but does not define it.
The "Tissue" observer provides the corresponding concentration from a BLOODLESS tissue homogenate (no blood or plasma).
If you still have blood left in the sample, use the "Whole Organ" observer and adjust the "Residual Blood Fraction for Sampling" to account for lost blood (bleeding out) in the sampling procedure.
Hope this helps.
Stephan

Just a note: The steep decline in your plasma concentration profile is caused by rapid uptake and sequestration in cells. It will be clearer to see when viewing the profile on a linear scale. For small molecule drugs, rapid uptake and sequestration can be driven by high lipophilicity, active transporters or binding to cellular components. One or more of these processes are missing in the model. Elimination is much too fast in your model. When you have updated the model accordingly, the tissue concentrations in the liver and spleen will be much closer to your observed data.

Hi I just have a quick question maybe not related to the issue. How do you calculate your tissue concentrations in ng/mL? Because all the tissue concentrations I got are in the unit of ng/g.
Thanks in advance!
@andanteyang  In general we assume that the density of tissues is 1.00 - 1.04 g/mL

Dear All
Thank you for your kind and illustrative replies.
Stephan, sorry I haven't replied before, I work on my job's laptop and I need to tell IT to update my PKSim version, I am still working with the v8. Are these options available on v9, right?
Paul, thanks for the advice. Yes, now we are thinking that there might be some active transport and sequestration or tissue binding as the clogP is around 0.5 and the clogD 7.4 ~-1.
A weird compound, the Caco experiments show no permeation and I was thinking that it might be the same for tissues and it was the blood inside of them what it was making the concentration look high.
andanteyang, adding to Paul's comment. Yes, we homogenise our tissue with water for quantification and then we assume the tissue density as 1 g/mL.
Thanks!
Ricardo

title: Improve workflow to extend the structure of an existing PBPK Model (e.g. add Tumor Organ)
body_text:
The current workflow to extend a PK-Sim structure in MoBi (for example in order  to add an Tumor organ to a model) could be simplified greatly.
At the moment, the workflow consists of several steps, most of which could be automatized by MoBi:

Save a representative organ as .pkml file (for example Heart)
Import the saved organ as container and name it Tumor
Rename all internal neighborhoods between sub-containers of imported organs
Modify tag of new organ & sub-containers (e.g. Rename Heart tag to Tumor)
Create neighborhoods for new organ (i.e, "wire" it to the blood flow)
Update sum parameter Weight (tissue) in Organism
Update sum parameter Volume(plasma) in Organism
Update sum parameter Blood Flow Rate in Lung
Define the partition coefficient (int/water)

I believe that steps 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9 should be performed automatically:

Step 3: Rename of internal neighborhoods should happen automatically. If Heart was renamed to Tumor, then heart_bc_heart_pls neighborhood should also be renamed to tumor_bc_tumor_pls etc..
Step 4: Renaming of Tags in subcontainers should also happen automatically
Steps 6,7,8 are very similar. The definition of parameters Weight (tissue) in Organism,  Volume(plasma) in Organism, Blood Flow Rate in Lung should be changed to used a generic SumFormula instead of listing the list of parameters explicitly. This would have to be implemented in PK-Sim
Step 9: The parameter is added automatically by the calculation method. Instead, this parameter should be defined all the time in the spatial structure. If it is not used, the overhead is minimal. This would have to be implemented in PK-Sim

What do you think?

comments.body_text: This is a crucial feature for extending models. So far, the workflow is error-prone and takes much time and effort. Implementing your suggestions would help a lot.

Even more steps are required in case of protein model. All of them can be also automated via sum formulas

Volume of Endosome in  EndogenousIgG/Endosome: Add endosomal tumor volume
Volume of Interstitial in  EndogenousIgG/Interstitial: Add interstitial tumor volume
Volume of Plasma in  EndogenousIgG/Plasma: Add plasma tumor volume
Lymph flow rate in  EndogenousIgG: Add tumor lymph flow rate
Fluid recirculation flow rate in EndogenousIgG: Add tumor fluid recirculation flow rate
Surface area (plasma/interstitial) in the Neighborhood EndogenousIgG_int_EndogenousIgG_pls: Add respective surface area from tumor.

Another additional steps are not related to sum formulas:

Add new equation for the volume of endosomal space in tumor
Note also that additional neighborhoods are needed with the protein model:
a) Tumor endosomal space <-> EndosomalClearance compartment
b) Tumor interstitial space <-> VenousBlood plasma
The property is present has to be adapted in the Molecule Start Values if the tumor is added manually.
@Yuri05 I really think that we should try to tackle step 9 Define the partition coefficient (int/water) asap. This is something that is confusing for everybody and just does not make sense.
Great!

Hi All,
Thanks for this excellent thread! I have been looking for a while why my tumor interstitial concentrations were different compared to the organ which was duplicated (muscle) while only the volume had changed. Turned out to be a combination of one of the points from Yuri and a new one:

Add new equation for the volume of endosomal space in tumor
Add new equation for the volume of vascular endothelium in tumor (i.e., refer to the tumor neighborhood rather then the original organ)

In addition if you change the volume of the tumor compared to the duplicated organ, the following is also relevant for the correct calculation of the isogravimetric flow if I am not mistaken:

Make sure the "Volume" parameter value of the tumor is identical to the "Organ volume mouse" parameter value of the tumor

If anyone has additional points/different experience or if there is a more recent thread, I would be happy to hear.
P.S. I cannot easily find this thread either via the issues or discussions search functionality, maybe possible to move this issue to the discussions?
Best,
Wilbert

Make sure the "Volume" parameter value of the tumor is identical to the "Organ volume mouse" parameter value of the tumor

Hmm, not sure... @Christoph27 Can you comment on this?

I cannot easily find this thread either via the issues or discussions search functionality

title: Clopidogrel PBPK Model
body_text:
Dear OSP-community,
We have been working on a parent-metabolite PBPK model of the CYP2C19/CYP3A4 victim drug and CYP2B6/CYP2C8/CYP2C19 perpetrator drug clopidogrel and its four metabolites clopidogrel carboxylic acid, clopidogrel acyl glucuronide, 2-oxo-clopidogrel, and clopidogrel thiol H4. The model is able to predict and describe the drug–gene–interaction (DGI) involving CYP2C19 as well as drug–drug–interactions (DDIs) with rifampicin (CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 perpetrator drug), omeprazole (CYP2C19 victim and perpetrator drug), bupropion (CYP2B6 victim drug), as well as repaglinide, pioglitazone, and montelukast (CYP2C8 victim drugs).
The corresponding manuscript, including comprehensive documentation, has been published online in Pharmaceutics and the clopidogrel model as well as PK-Sim projects presenting the predicted DD(G)I studies can be found here.
Related publication:
Loer, H.L.H.; Türk, D.; Mantilla, J.D.G.; Selzer, D.; Lehr, T. Physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling of clopidogrel and its four relevant metabolites for CYP2B6, CYP2C8, CYP2C19, and CYP3A4 drug-drug-gene interaction predictions. Pharmaceutics 2022, 14, 915.
Best regards,
Helena

title: using copy to clipboard or exporting a simulation to excel: Pksim v9.1
body_text:
Dear colleagues
I have a couple of questions regarding the use of the copying to clipboard or exporting the simulated data feature for a particular simulation- right click on the chart area under the tab "Time profile Analysis"

When copying to clipboard, the figure has a grey border that is not very aesthetically appealing. Is there anyway i can alter the colour/appearance of this border?
In case I export the simulation to pdf, in the pdf output all the observed data points overlaid on the simulation disappear and only the simulated curves are visible in the figure.

Any advice on the aforesaid is welcome. Unfortunately i cannot share the graphs for now  given confidentiality reasons.
Thanks
Amit Taneja
Galapagos Pharma

comments.body_text:
Dear Amit,
the grey border can be removed by changing the color here:

Edit: For the PDF Report, I believe this issue was reported as a bug. Unfortunately, due to challenges with updating the PDF reports creation for new features and releases (LaTeX), these have been discontinued for the newest release (V11) and may be replaced by markdown-based solutions in the future.
Best,
Stephan

title: Do you use the Export for cluster computation/Import PK-Analyses features in PK-Sim?
body_text:
Dear community,
PK-Sim supports the concept of offloading the calculation of a population simulation to another engine (a cluster maybe) and loading the simulation results back into the application. This feature is called Export for cluster computation and was implemented at a time when the scripting capabilities of the OSPSuite were very limited. With v9, the OSPSuite started to offer a very powerful interface with R (OSPSuite-R) which rendered this feature irrelevant.

The code required to maintain this feature is fairly complicated and we would like to remove it entirely.
The same goes with the Import PK-Analyses feature where a user could potentially calculate some user-defined PK-Parameters outside of PK-Sim and import them for analyses. OSPSuite-R now fully supports user-defined PK-Parameters
Please select Yes in the poll below if you use at least one of these features regularly (e.g more than once in the last 8 years). If you select yes, don't hesitate to leave a message explaining the use case.
Otherwise, please select No
Thanks for your feedback

comments.body_text: I have used import PK-Analyses some years ago after exporting, adjusting and re-importing back into PK-Sim. I have not used it for a long time, but it was quite useful. If the R interface renders this feature irrelevant, I don't see why we would need double features.
I can image that "Import PK-Analyses..." is still a useful feature. You could do PK-calculation in R but import in PK-Sim in order to visualize and compare.

I have not used this feature but can see the merit of being able to run simulations, estimations or sensitivity analyses (partly) on a cluster. Especially more complex mobi models quickly become computationally expensive. With that in mind, I feel that a good guide of what is exactly possible with the osp suit r package would be very beneficial, I hear from many that they try to use it for their work but get turned off by the lack of documentation. Just as a small feedback to this user poll, thank you for the amazing work!
